QBC is more sensitive than thick smear but requires a fluorescence microscope and cannot reliably distinguish Plasmodium species.
highYield: QBC = Acridine orange dye + fluorescence microscope. Sensitive but needs special equipment. Cannot reliably identify species. Better sensitivity than thick smear but less practical in field.
pyqNote: QBC is asked as 'which dye is used' = Acridine orange. Also tested: what it detects (nucleic acids of parasites). Sensitive but NOT species-specific = key limitation.

subject: Microbiology
stem: During a kala-azar field survey in Bihar, health workers use a rapid diagnostic test based on the rK39 antigen. Which of the following BEST describes the rK39 test?
options: Detects Leishmania amastigotes directly from blood
An immunochromatographic test detecting anti-rK39 IgG antibodies (specific for visceral leishmaniasis)
A PCR-based test for Leishmania DNA in urine
explanation: rK39 is a recombinant antigen derived from the kinesin region of L. chagasi/donovani complex. The rK39 rapid immunochromatographic test detects anti-rK39 IgG antibodies in patient serum/whole blood with >97% sensitivity and specificity for visceral leishmaniasis. It is the recommended field test for kala-azar diagnosis in India. The formol-gel (Aldehyde) test is the non-specific test for hypergammaglobulinemia.
highYield: rK39 = specific rapid test for visceral leishmaniasis = immunochromatographic strip test = detects anti-rK39 IgG. Sensitivity >97% in India. Best field diagnostic tool for kala-azar.
pyqNote: rK39 vs Formol-gel: rK39 = specific (antibody detection). Formol-gel = non-specific (hypergammaglobulinemia). rK39 is the recommended test for kala-azar in NVBDCP guidelines.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A newborn is evaluated for congenital toxoplasmosis. Which combination of findings would MOST strongly suggest this diagnosis?
options: Sabre tibia + saddle nose + Hutchinson teeth
Cerebral calcifications + port-wine stain + leptomeningeal angioma
explanation: The classic congenital toxoplasmosis triad is: (1) Intracranial calcifications (scattered, periventricular/parenchymal), (2) Hydrocephalus, (3) Chorioretinitis. Option B describes congenital syphilis (Hutchinson triad). Option C describes tuberous sclerosis. Option D describes Sturge-Weber syndrome. Maternal primary infection during pregnancy is teratogenic; prophylactic treatment with spiramycin is used in pregnant women with acute infection.
highYield: Congenital Toxoplasma triad = Hydrocephalus + Chorioretinitis + Intracranial calcifications (+ psychomotor retardation). Part of TORCH. Treatment = Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin.
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG: 'Triad of congenital toxoplasmosis' = must memorize. Contrast with CMV (periventricular calcifications + deafness + petechiae) and congenital rubella (cataracts + heart defects + deafness).

subject: Microbiology
stem: A patient is diagnosed with lymphatic filariasis due to Wuchereria bancrofti. Diethylcarbamazine (DEC) is prescribed. Which of the following BEST describes the mechanism of action of DEC in filariasis?
options: Immobilizes microfilariae and enhances their phagocytosis; has adulticidal activity
Blocks acetylcholine at neuromuscular junction causing paralysis
Inhibits ergosterol synthesis in adult worms
explanation: Diethylcarbamazine (DEC) works by: (1) Immobilizing microfilariae (rapidly clearing them from blood) by interfering with arachidonic acid metabolism and altering their surface, making them susceptible to host immune attack, and (2) Has slow adulticidal (kills adult worms) activity with repeated courses. It does not directly kill microfilariae by DNA inhibition. Ivermectin (alternative) causes sustained microfilarial suppression. Mass Drug Administration = DEC + Albendazole ± Ivermectin.
highYield: DEC = DOC for lymphatic filariasis. Immobilizes microfilariae + slow adulticidal activity. MDA in India = DEC + Albendazole (2-drug) or + Ivermectin (3-drug in some areas).
pyqNote: DEC mechanism is asked. Also: Mazzotti reaction = severe reaction after DEC in onchocerciasis (NOT given in oncho). Ivermectin = DOC for Onchocerca. DEC = DOC for W. bancrofti.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 6-year-old immunocompetent child from a rural area develops acute watery diarrhea lasting 10 days after drinking from an untreated stream. Stool modified acid-fast staining shows small (4-6 µm) pink-staining oocysts. The diarrhea resolves spontaneously. Which of the following statements about this parasite is CORRECT?
options: Infection is always severe and life-threatening regardless of immune status
Oocysts are NOT acid-fast and require special fluorescent staining
Disease is self-limiting in immunocompetent hosts; nitazoxanide can shorten duration
Treatment with metronidazole for 10 days is the standard of care
explanation: Cryptosporidium parvum/hominis causes self-limiting watery diarrhea in immunocompetent individuals (resolves in 1-2 weeks). The oocysts ARE acid-fast (pink on modified ZN stain) — a key identifying feature. Nitazoxanide reduces duration and severity in immunocompetent patients. Metronidazole has NO efficacy against Cryptosporidium. In immunocompromised patients (AIDS, CD4<50), it causes severe, profuse, life-threatening diarrhea.
highYield: Cryptosporidium = acid-fast oocysts (4-6 µm). Self-limiting in immunocompetent. Life-threatening in AIDS. Metronidazole ineffective. Nitazoxanide = drug of choice. Waterborne transmission.
pyqNote: Two contrasting scenarios: (1) Self-limiting in normal child vs (2) Severe/chronic in HIV. Metronidazole = common wrong answer trap. Acid-fast = critical staining feature.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A NEET PG aspirant is comparing features of different Plasmodium species. Which of the following correctly matches the Plasmodium species with its UNIQUE characteristic?
options: P. vivax — Maurer's clefts + banana-shaped gametocytes
P. falciparum — Schuffner's dots + enlarged RBCs + amoeboid trophozoite
P. malariae — Band-form trophozoite + Ziemann's dots + 72-hour fever cycle
P. ovale — Enlarged RBCs with Maurer's clefts + diurnal periodicity
explanation: P. malariae = Band-form (basket-form) trophozoites + Ziemann's dots + quartan fever (72-hour cycle) + NO RBC enlargement + NO relapse (no hypnozoites) + associated with nephrotic syndrome. Option A incorrectly assigns falciparum features to vivax. Option B incorrectly assigns vivax features to falciparum. Option D incorrectly assigns Maurer's clefts (falciparum) to ovale and diurnal periodicity (incorrect for any Plasmodium).
highYield: P. malariae unique: Band forms + Ziemann dots + 72-hr quartan fever + Nephrotic syndrome. Only P. falciparum has Maurer's clefts. Only P. vivax has amoeboid trophozoite + enlarged RBC + Schuffner dots.
pyqNote: Comparison table MCQ is very common in NEET PG. Memorize: Schuffner = vivax/ovale; Maurer's = falciparum; Ziemann = malariae. Band form = malariae only.

subject: Microbiology
stem: Miltefosine is approved for treatment of visceral leishmaniasis in India. Which of the following statements about miltefosine is CORRECT?
options: It is the first intravenous drug approved for kala-azar
It is the first oral drug approved for visceral leishmaniasis
It acts by inhibiting the ergosterol synthesis pathway
It cannot be used in women of childbearing age
explanation: Miltefosine (hexadecylphosphocholine) was the first oral drug approved for visceral leishmaniasis. It is an alkylphosphocholine that acts by inhibiting the phosphatidylcholine biosynthesis pathway and disrupting cell membranes. It was originally developed as an anticancer drug. Key limitations: teratogenic (contraindicated in pregnancy; requires contraception for 3 months post-treatment), vomiting, and potential resistance. Option C is incorrect — ergosterol inhibition is the mechanism of amphotericin B and azoles.
highYield: Miltefosine = first ORAL drug for kala-azar. Mechanism = inhibits phospholipid synthesis. Teratogenic = avoid in pregnancy. Duration = 28 days oral. Resistance emerging.
pyqNote: Miltefosine milestone: first oral treatment for VL. NEET PG tests its mechanism and teratogenicity. Note: amphotericin B targets ergosterol, NOT miltefosine.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 50-year-old male presents with colicky abdominal pain and blood-mixed diarrhea. Colonoscopy reveals multiple discrete ulcers with flask-shaped appearance — narrow neck at the mucosal surface and wider base in the submucosa. Biopsy of ulcer edge shows trophozoites with ingested red blood cells. What is the pathological basis of this characteristic ulcer shape?
options: E. histolytica secretes hyaluronidase that spreads superficially across mucosa
Trophozoites penetrate the mucosa and proliferate in the submucosa, undermining the edges
Immune complex deposition causes transmural inflammation with stricture formation
Cysts germinate in the submucosa producing radial tissue destruction
explanation: E. histolytica trophozoites penetrate the intestinal mucosa via proteases (including cysteine proteinases), spread laterally through the submucosa, and proliferate beneath the mucosa. This undermines the edges of the initial mucosal breach, creating a flask-shaped ulcer with a narrow mucosal neck and wide submucosal base. The trophozoites secreting proteases, galactose-inhibitable lectin for adherence, and amoebapores for cell lysis are key virulence factors.
highYield: Flask-shaped ulcer = hallmark of intestinal amoebiasis. Narrow neck (mucosa) + wide base (submucosa) = trophozoites penetrate down then spread laterally. Contrast: TB ulcers are transverse (circumferential).
pyqNote: Flask-shaped ulcer = E. histolytica (frequently drawn/described in MCQs). Contrast with linear/circumferential TB ulcer. Trophozoite with RBCs = pathognomonic for pathogenic E. histolytica.

subject: Microbiology
stem: An HIV-positive patient has a CD4 count that drops to 85 cells/µL. His anti-Toxoplasma IgG is positive (indicating past exposure/latent infection). He is currently on ART. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate prophylaxis to prevent Toxoplasma encephalitis?
explanation: Trimethoprim-Sulfamethoxazole (TMP-SMX/Co-trimoxazole) is the drug of choice for primary prophylaxis against Toxoplasma encephalitis in HIV patients with CD4 <100 cells/µL who are Toxoplasma IgG-positive. It also provides concurrent PCP (Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia) prophylaxis. Prophylaxis can be discontinued when CD4 rises above 200 cells/µL on sustained ART for >3 months. Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine is used for TREATMENT, not prophylaxis.
highYield: Toxoplasma prophylaxis in HIV (CD4 <100): TMP-SMX (double strength daily). Same drug as PCP prophylaxis. Discontinue when CD4 >200 on ART >3 months. Dapsone + pyrimethamine = alternative if TMP-SMX intolerant.
pyqNote: CD4 thresholds: PCP prophylaxis = CD4 <200. Toxoplasma prophylaxis = CD4 <100 (if IgG positive). MAI prophylaxis = CD4 <50. These thresholds are repeatedly tested.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 28-year-old patient is tested for HIV. The laboratory reports detection of p24 antigen. Which component of HIV does p24 represent?
options: Envelope glycoprotein responsible for CD4 binding
explanation: p24 is the core capsid protein of HIV. The HIV envelope contains gp120 (binds CD4 receptor) and gp41 (transmembrane fusion protein). The viral core contains p24 (capsid), p7 (nucleocapsid), and the viral enzymes: reverse transcriptase, integrase, and protease. p24 antigen is the earliest detectable marker of HIV infection, appearing approximately 2 weeks after exposure, making it critical in 4th generation ELISA testing.
highYield: p24 = capsid protein; gp120 = CD4 binding; gp41 = transmembrane fusion; earliest detectable = p24 at ~2 weeks
pyqNote: NEET PG 2019 asked about earliest detectable marker in HIV — p24 antigen appears 2 weeks post-exposure before antibodies

subject: Microbiology
stem: HIV primarily uses gp120 to attach to CD4 receptors on T helper cells. Which coreceptor is predominantly used by macrophage-tropic (R5) HIV strains?
explanation: Macrophage-tropic (R5) HIV strains use CCR5 as coreceptor along with CD4. T-tropic (X4) strains use CXCR4. Dual-tropic strains (R5X4) can use both. This distinction is clinically important: individuals homozygous for the CCR5-delta32 deletion are highly resistant to HIV infection. The sequence is: gp120 binds CD4 → conformational change → gp120 binds coreceptor (CCR5 or CXCR4) → gp41 mediates membrane fusion.
highYield: R5 (macrophage-tropic) = CCR5; X4 (T-tropic) = CXCR4; CCR5-delta32 deletion = HIV resistance
pyqNote: Frequently tested: coreceptor used by HIV — CCR5 for R5 strains (early/macrophage-tropic), CXCR4 for X4 strains (late/T-tropic)

subject: Microbiology
stem: A blood donor is tested using a 4th generation ELISA. What does this test detect that a 3rd generation ELISA does NOT?
options: Both p24 antigen and anti-HIV antibodies simultaneously
HIV DNA in peripheral blood mononuclear cells
explanation: 4th generation ELISA (combo test) detects both p24 antigen AND anti-HIV antibodies (IgG + IgM) simultaneously. 3rd generation ELISA detected only antibodies. Since p24 appears ~2 weeks post-exposure (before antibodies develop), 4th gen tests narrow the window period significantly. This is now the recommended screening test. The window period for 4th gen ELISA is approximately 18-45 days (vs 3-12 weeks for antibody-only tests).
highYield: 4th gen ELISA = p24 antigen + antibody combo; reduces window period; recommended for HIV screening
pyqNote: NEET PG 2020 pattern: 4th gen ELISA detects p24 + antibody; this is the gold standard screening test currently

subject: Microbiology
stem: A high-risk individual tests negative for HIV antibodies 6 weeks after potential exposure. He is anxious about being in the 'window period.' What is the window period for 3rd generation antibody-based ELISA?
explanation: The window period for 3rd generation antibody-based ELISA is 3–12 weeks (approximately 45–90 days). During this period, the person is infected and infectious but antibody tests are negative. p24 antigen appears earlier (~2 weeks). 4th generation combo test (p24 + antibody) has a shorter window period of ~18–45 days. HIV RNA PCR can detect infection even earlier (~10–12 days). A negative test at 6 weeks on antibody-only test does not rule out infection; retesting at 12 weeks is recommended.
highYield: Window period antibody ELISA = 3-12 weeks; p24 = 2 weeks; RNA PCR = 10-12 days; 4th gen = 18-45 days
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG question: window period = HBsAg/HIV tests negative despite active infection; counseling point for high-risk individuals

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 34-year-old HIV-positive man has a CD4 count of 160 cells/μL. He is not on ART yet. Which prophylaxis should be started MOST urgently to prevent the commonest AIDS-defining illness?
explanation: TMP-SMX (co-trimoxazole) prophylaxis is started when CD4 count falls below 200 cells/μL. It prevents Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia (PCP) — the commonest AIDS-defining illness — and also provides cross-protection against toxoplasmosis (Toxoplasma gondii encephalitis). Azithromycin for MAC prophylaxis is given when CD4 <50 cells/μL. Fluconazole for cryptococcal prophylaxis is context-dependent (high-prevalence settings). CMV retinitis prophylaxis is not routinely given. With a CD4 of 160, TMP-SMX is the most urgent prophylaxis.
highYield: TMP-SMX when CD4 <200 → prevents PCP + toxoplasmosis; azithromycin when CD4 <50 → prevents MAC
pyqNote: NEET PG PYQ: OI prophylaxis thresholds are high-yield — TMP-SMX <200, azithromycin for MAC <50

subject: Microbiology
stem: According to current WHO/India NACO guidelines, ART should be initiated in HIV-positive patients:
options: Only when CD4 count falls below 350 cells/μL
Only when CD4 count falls below 200 cells/μL
In all HIV-positive patients regardless of CD4 count
explanation: Current WHO (2016) and India NACO guidelines recommend 'Test and Treat' — ART for ALL HIV-positive individuals regardless of CD4 count or clinical stage. This replaced earlier threshold-based approaches (CD4 <350, then <500). The benefits include reducing transmission, preventing OIs, and improving survival. The first-line regimen in India is TDF (tenofovir) + 3TC (lamivudine) + DTG (dolutegravir), replacing the older TDF+3TC+EFV (efavirenz) regimen. DTG has higher barrier to resistance and better tolerability.
highYield: ART = ALL patients regardless of CD4; India first line = TDF+3TC+DTG; 'Test and Treat' policy
pyqNote: NEET PG 2021 pattern: WHO 'Treat All' policy; current India first-line ART = TDF+3TC+DTG (dolutegravir replaced efavirenz)

subject: Microbiology
stem: A pregnant woman is found to be HIV-positive at 20 weeks of gestation. What is the recommended approach to prevent vertical transmission?
options: Start ART only after delivery to avoid teratogenicity
Start ART immediately regardless of CD4 count and continue lifelong
Give single-dose nevirapine only at delivery
Avoid breastfeeding only; no ART needed if CD4 >500
explanation: All HIV-positive pregnant women should receive ART (PMTCT - Prevention of Mother to Child Transmission) regardless of CD4 count or gestational age, and should continue lifelong. This is WHO Option B+ strategy. Without intervention, vertical transmission rate is 15-45%. With ART, it can be reduced to <1-2%. Breastfeeding on ART carries ~1-2% risk vs ~15% without ART; WHO recommends continued ART during breastfeeding. In resource-rich settings, formula feeding may be advised. Single-dose nevirapine alone (old Option A) is no longer recommended.
highYield: PMTCT = ART all pregnant HIV+ women lifelong (Option B+); reduces vertical transmission from 45% to <1-2%; breastfeeding on ART allowed
pyqNote: PMTCT is frequently tested — WHO Option B+ = ART all pregnant women; vertical transmission occurs in-utero, intrapartum, and via breastfeeding

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 38-year-old HIV-positive patient with CD4 count of 45 cells/μL develops blurring of vision. Fundoscopy shows 'pizza pie' appearance with hemorrhages and exudates. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
explanation: CMV (Cytomegalovirus) retinitis presents with the classic 'pizza pie' or 'cottage cheese with ketchup' appearance on fundoscopy — white fluffy exudates with perivascular hemorrhages. It occurs when CD4 falls below 50 cells/μL. It is painless and can lead to blindness if untreated. Treatment is with valganciclovir or ganciclovir. Toxoplasma chorioretinitis can occur but typically shows focal necrotizing lesions without the pizza pattern. Kaposi sarcoma is caused by HHV-8 and presents as violaceous skin/mucosal lesions. CMV retinitis is an AIDS-defining illness.
highYield: CMV retinitis = CD4 <50; 'pizza pie' fundoscopy; treat with valganciclovir; AIDS-defining
pyqNote: NEET PG pattern: AIDS-defining illnesses with CD4 thresholds — CMV retinitis <50, PCP <200, Toxoplasma encephalitis <100-200

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 32-year-old HIV-positive patient presents with severe headache, fever, and neck stiffness. CSF India ink preparation shows encapsulated yeast. What is the causative organism and its typical CD4 threshold?
explanation: Cryptococcal meningitis is caused by Cryptococcus neoformans, an encapsulated yeast identified by India ink staining (negative staining showing clear halo around yeast cells). It typically occurs when CD4 <100 cells/μL (most commonly <50). CSF shows: high opening pressure, lymphocytic pleocytosis, high protein, low glucose. Diagnosis: India ink, latex agglutination for cryptococcal antigen (most sensitive), culture. Treatment: induction with amphotericin B + flucytosine → consolidation with fluconazole → maintenance fluconazole until CD4 >200. It is an AIDS-defining illness.
highYield: Cryptococcal meningitis = C. neoformans; India ink = capsule; CD4 <100; treat amphotericin B + flucytosine → fluconazole
pyqNote: NEET PG: India ink + HIV patient = Cryptococcus neoformans; most common cause of fungal meningitis in AIDS

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 35-year-old HIV-positive male develops multiple painless violaceous skin lesions on the lower extremities and palate. Biopsy shows spindle cell proliferation with vascular slits. Which virus is responsible for this AIDS-defining malignancy?
explanation: Kaposi sarcoma is caused by HHV-8 (Human Herpesvirus 8, also called Kaposi Sarcoma-associated Herpesvirus/KSHV). It presents as violaceous, non-tender macules, papules, or nodules involving skin, oral mucosa, lymph nodes, and viscera. Histology shows spindle cell proliferation with slit-like vascular spaces. It is the most common AIDS-defining malignancy, occurring at any CD4 level but more aggressive at low CD4. EBV causes primary CNS lymphoma and hairy leukoplakia in AIDS. Treatment: ART (lesions may regress), chemotherapy for visceral disease.
highYield: Kaposi sarcoma = HHV-8; violaceous skin/mucosal lesions; spindle cells + vascular slits on histology; AIDS-defining malignancy
pyqNote: HHV-8 = Kaposi sarcoma; EBV = primary CNS lymphoma + hairy leukoplakia; both are AIDS-defining conditions

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 25-year-old presents with jaundice 8 weeks after receiving a blood transfusion. His serological profile shows: HBsAg positive, HBeAg positive, anti-HBc IgM positive, anti-HBs negative. What does this profile indicate?
options: Chronic HBV infection with low infectivity
Acute HBV infection with high infectivity
explanation: This profile indicates ACUTE HBV infection with HIGH infectivity. HBsAg positive = active infection (first marker to appear, 1-10 weeks post-exposure). HBeAg positive = active viral replication, high infectivity (HBV DNA being actively produced). Anti-HBc IgM positive = acute infection (IgM appears in acute phase, replaced by IgG in chronic/past infection). Anti-HBs negative = no immunity yet. In chronic infection, anti-HBc IgM becomes IgG. HBeAg seroconversion to anti-HBe indicates reduced replication and lower infectivity.
highYield: HBsAg = active infection; HBeAg = high infectivity/active replication; anti-HBc IgM = acute; anti-HBs = immunity/recovery
pyqNote: NEET PG most tested: HBV serological markers — HBsAg first to appear, HBeAg = infectivity, anti-HBc IgM = acute marker

subject: Microbiology
stem: A blood donor's sample shows: HBsAg negative, anti-HBs negative, anti-HBc IgM positive. Which phase of HBV infection does this represent?
options: Early acute HBV infection before seroconversion
explanation: This is the WINDOW PERIOD of HBV infection. HBsAg has cleared (no longer detectable) but anti-HBs has not yet appeared. During this window, the ONLY positive marker is anti-HBc IgM, making it the key diagnostic marker of the window period. This period can last weeks to months. This is critical for blood banking — window period blood is HBsAg-negative but still infectious. Anti-HBc IgM distinguishes window period from vaccination (vaccinees have ONLY anti-HBs positive, anti-HBc negative). HBV DNA testing can detect infection even in the window period.
highYield: Window period = HBsAg gone + anti-HBs absent + ONLY anti-HBc IgM positive; vaccination = only anti-HBs positive
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG: window period HBV = only anti-HBc IgM positive; vaccinated = only anti-HBs positive; both HBsAg negative

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 30-year-old nurse had hepatitis B vaccination 2 years ago. Which serological profile would be EXPECTED in her?
options: HBsAg positive, anti-HBs positive, anti-HBc positive
HBsAg negative, anti-HBs positive, anti-HBc negative
HBsAg negative, anti-HBs negative, anti-HBc IgM positive
HBsAg negative, anti-HBs positive, anti-HBc IgG positive
explanation: A vaccinated individual who has never been infected shows: HBsAg negative (no infection), anti-HBs POSITIVE (vaccine induces antibody response to HBsAg), anti-HBc NEGATIVE (vaccine only induces anti-HBs, not anti-HBc; anti-HBc is only produced in response to natural infection). This is the key distinction: naturally immune individuals (past infection + recovery) show anti-HBs positive + anti-HBc IgG positive + HBsAg negative. Vaccinated individuals show ONLY anti-HBs positive. Option D describes naturally resolved infection.
highYield: Vaccinated = only anti-HBs positive; naturally immune = anti-HBs + anti-HBc IgG positive; anti-HBc not induced by vaccine
pyqNote: NEET PG: distinguishing vaccinated from naturally immune — anti-HBc absent in vaccinees; only anti-HBs present after HBV vaccine

subject: Microbiology
stem: Among all serological and molecular markers for Hepatitis B, which is the MOST sensitive indicator of active viral replication?
explanation: HBV DNA is the most sensitive marker of active viral replication. While HBeAg indicates high infectivity and active replication, HBeAg-negative HBV mutants (precore mutants) can have active replication without HBeAg. HBV DNA by PCR detects replication even in HBeAg-negative patients and in the window period when HBsAg is undetectable. HBV DNA is also used for monitoring treatment response (antiviral therapy with tenofovir or entecavir) and to guide decisions about treatment initiation. HBV DNA >2000 IU/mL with elevated ALT generally warrants treatment.
highYield: HBV DNA = most sensitive replication marker; HBeAg = high infectivity but misses precore mutants; HBV DNA guides antiviral therapy
pyqNote: NEET PG: most sensitive test for HBV replication = HBV DNA; HBeAg-negative HBV = precore mutant, still replicating

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 40-year-old chronic HBV carrier suddenly develops fulminant hepatitis. He is found to have a new superinfection with Hepatitis D virus (HDV). Which statement about HDV is CORRECT?
options: HDV can infect individuals who are anti-HBs positive (immune to HBV)
HDV requires HBsAg for its replication and transmission
HDV is a DNA virus that can replicate independently
HDV infection clears spontaneously in most cases of superinfection
explanation: HDV (Hepatitis Delta Virus) is a defective/satellite virus that REQUIRES HBsAg for its envelope — it cannot replicate or be transmitted without HBV co-infection. This means: only people with HBV infection (HBsAg positive) can get HDV. HBV vaccination therefore ALSO prevents HDV. HDV infection can occur as co-infection (simultaneous with HBV — often self-limiting) or superinfection (HDV in chronic HBV carrier — more severe, higher risk of fulminant hepatitis and cirrhosis). HDV superinfection has poor prognosis with low spontaneous clearance rate.
highYield: HDV = defective virus; needs HBsAg; superinfection in HBV carrier = fulminant hepatitis; HBV vaccine prevents HDV
pyqNote: HDV requires HBsAg (HBV) for replication — classic NEET PG point; coinfection vs superinfection outcomes differ

subject: Microbiology
stem: A pregnant woman is found to have HBsAg positive and HBeAg positive at 28 weeks. What is the recommended management for her NEONATE immediately after birth?
options: HBV vaccine + HBIG within 12 hours, followed by completion of vaccine series
Wait for HBsAg testing of neonate before any intervention
explanation: When mother is HBsAg positive AND HBeAg positive (high infectivity), the neonate must receive BOTH HBV vaccine AND Hepatitis B Immunoglobulin (HBIG) within 12 hours of birth (given at different sites). HBeAg-positive mothers have vertical transmission risk of 80-90%. HBIG provides immediate passive immunity while the vaccine induces active immunity. The vaccine series is then completed at 1 and 6 months. This combination reduces vertical transmission by >95%. Vaccine alone (without HBIG) is used when mother is HBsAg positive but HBeAg negative (lower risk). Delaying is not acceptable.
highYield: HBsAg+ + HBeAg+ mother → neonate: HBIG + vaccine within 12 hours; >95% transmission prevention; HBeAg+ = 80-90% vertical transmission risk
pyqNote: NEET PG: neonatal HBV prophylaxis = HBIG + vaccine within 12 hours; timing is critical — asked multiple times

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 22-year-old presents with high fever, retro-orbital headache, severe myalgia, and maculopapular rash for 4 days. Which is the MOST appropriate diagnostic test at this stage?
explanation: NS1 (Non-Structural protein 1) antigen is detectable from days 1–5 of fever (early viremic phase) and is the best test in the first 5 days. IgM antibody appears from day 5 onwards (and peaks at 2 weeks), making it unreliable in the first 4 days. IgG appears later and indicates past infection or secondary dengue. The patient is on day 4, making NS1 the most appropriate test. After day 5, IgM becomes the test of choice. Dengue is transmitted by Aedes aegypti (day-biting mosquito), and also by Aedes albopictus.
highYield: NS1 antigen = days 1-5; IgM = from day 5; IgG = secondary dengue/past infection; Aedes aegypti = vector
pyqNote: NEET PG: NS1 = early dengue (day 1-5); IgM = after day 5; classic clinical: fever + myalgia + retro-orbital pain + rash

subject: Microbiology
stem: In a dengue patient, a sphygmomanometer cuff is inflated to mean arterial pressure for 5 minutes. After deflation, how many petechiae in a 2.5cm x 2.5cm area constitutes a POSITIVE tourniquet test?
explanation: The tourniquet test (Rumple-Leede capillary fragility test) is positive when >10 petechiae appear per 2.5 cm x 2.5 cm (1 inch square) area on the forearm after inflating BP cuff to mean arterial pressure for 5 minutes. It assesses capillary fragility and thrombocytopenia. A positive test supports a diagnosis of dengue hemorrhagic fever (DHF) but is not specific. The test reflects the thrombocytopenia and capillary leakage characteristic of dengue. Positive tourniquet test + fever + thrombocytopenia <100,000/μL + evidence of plasma leakage = DHF criteria.
highYield: Tourniquet test positive = >10 petechiae per 2.5x2.5 cm; cuff inflated to MAP for 5 minutes; indicates capillary fragility
pyqNote: NEET PG: >10 petechiae in 1 inch square area = positive tourniquet test; used in DHF diagnosis criteria

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 16-year-old with dengue has fever, positive tourniquet test, platelet count of 80,000/μL, and pleural effusion on chest X-ray. His pulse pressure is 30 mmHg. What is the CORRECT WHO classification?
explanation: This patient meets WHO criteria for Dengue Hemorrhagic Fever (DHF — older WHO 1997 classification): (1) Fever, (2) Hemorrhagic manifestations (positive tourniquet test), (3) Thrombocytopenia <100,000/μL, (4) Evidence of plasma leakage (pleural effusion). Pulse pressure of 30 mmHg (>20 mmHg) excludes DSS (which requires PP ≤20 mmHg or frank hypotension). Under WHO 2009 classification, this would be 'Severe Dengue' with plasma leakage. DHF grades: I (tourniquet positive only), II (+spontaneous bleeding), III (+circulatory failure/pre-shock), IV (profound shock = DSS).
highYield: DHF = fever + hemorrhage + thrombocytopenia <100K + plasma leakage; DSS = PP ≤20 mmHg or hypotension; DHF grade III/IV = DSS
pyqNote: NEET PG: DHF criteria all 4 must be present; DSS = PP <20 or hypotension; know WHO 1997 vs 2009 classification

subject: Microbiology
stem: In dengue fever, when does platelet count typically reach its NADIR?
explanation: Platelet count in dengue typically reaches its nadir (lowest point) on days 5–7 of illness. The critical phase of dengue (when plasma leakage, thrombocytopenia, and shock risk are highest) occurs during days 4–6, corresponding to the period when fever begins to defervese (the 'defervescence phase'). Paradoxically, the period when the patient feels better (fever drops) is when the danger is greatest. Platelet count begins to recover from day 7–8 onwards. Management during this period requires careful monitoring for bleeding and hemodynamic instability. Platelet transfusion is generally not required unless <10,000 or active bleeding.
highYield: Dengue platelet nadir = day 5-7; critical phase = day 4-6 (defervescence); platelet recovery from day 7-8
pyqNote: NEET PG: dengue critical phase = defervescence day 4-6; platelet nadir day 5-7; paradox = feels better but most dangerous time

subject: Microbiology
stem: According to WHO 2009 dengue classification, which of the following would classify a patient as having 'Severe Dengue'?
options: Pulse pressure of 25 mmHg with cool extremities
Dengue with lethargy and liver enlargement >2 cm
explanation: WHO 2009 classifies dengue as: (1) Dengue without warning signs, (2) Dengue with warning signs (abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, clinical fluid accumulation, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, liver >2cm, rising hematocrit + rapid fall in platelets), (3) Severe Dengue. Severe Dengue requires at least one of: severe plasma leakage leading to shock (PP ≤20 mmHg, cold clammy extremities), severe bleeding (clinically significant hemorrhage), or severe organ impairment (liver AST >1000, CNS involvement, cardiac, kidney failure). Pulse pressure 25 mmHg (>20 mmHg) with compensatory tachycardia and cool extremities indicates dengue shock syndrome, qualifying as Severe Dengue.
highYield: WHO 2009 Severe Dengue = shock (PP ≤20/hypotension) OR severe bleeding OR severe organ involvement
pyqNote: WHO 2009 tripartite classification: dengue / dengue with warning signs / severe dengue; warning signs ≠ severe dengue

subject: Microbiology
stem: A patient who died from rabies encephalitis undergoes autopsy. Which histopathological finding is PATHOGNOMONIC of rabies?
options: Lewy bodies in substantia nigra neurons
Negri bodies in Purkinje cells of cerebellum and pyramidal cells of hippocampus
Cowdry type A inclusions in hepatocytes
explanation: Negri bodies are pathognomonic (100% specific) for rabies. They are eosinophilic, intracytoplasmic inclusion bodies found in neurons, classically in: (1) Purkinje cells of cerebellum, and (2) Pyramidal cells of the hippocampus (Ammon's horn). They represent viral replication complexes. On Seller's stain: magenta/red Negri bodies on blue background. Lewy bodies = Parkinson's disease. Cowdry type A = herpesvirus (HSV, CMV). Guarnieri bodies = vaccinia/smallpox. Detection of Negri bodies in brain biopsy/autopsy is diagnostic for rabies.
highYield: Negri bodies = pathognomonic rabies; Purkinje cells (cerebellum) + hippocampal pyramidal cells; eosinophilic intracytoplasmic inclusions
pyqNote: NEET PG: Negri bodies location = Purkinje cells of cerebellum and pyramidal cells of hippocampus; Seller's stain = magenta on blue

subject: Microbiology
stem: In the context of rabies research and vaccine production, which statement about 'street virus' vs 'fixed virus' is CORRECT?
options: Street virus has a short incubation of 4-6 days in animals; fixed virus has variable incubation
Fixed virus is isolated from naturally infected animals; street virus is laboratory-adapted
Street virus is naturally occurring wild-type; fixed virus is laboratory-adapted with fixed short incubation and cannot cause natural rabies
Both street and fixed viruses form Negri bodies with equal frequency
explanation: Street virus = naturally occurring wild-type rabies virus isolated from infected animals/humans. It has: variable and long incubation period (weeks to months in animals), can produce Negri bodies, and causes natural rabies. Fixed virus = laboratory-adapted strain (e.g., Pasteur strain — Louis Pasteur adapted it by serial intracerebral passage in rabbits). Fixed virus has: fixed short incubation (4-6 days), does NOT produce Negri bodies, CANNOT cause natural rabies infection via bite, and is used to produce vaccines. The Pasteur original strain was passed through rabbit spinal cords to create fixed virus for early vaccines.
highYield: Street virus = wild-type, long incubation, Negri bodies present; Fixed virus = lab-adapted, 4-6 day incubation, no Negri bodies, used for vaccines
pyqNote: Street vs fixed virus comparison is a classic NEET PG virology question — key difference is incubation and Negri body production

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 10-year-old child is bitten on the face by a stray dog. The dog has run away. What is the CORRECT sequence of post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP)?
options: Wound wash only; vaccinate after 48 hours
Wound wash with soap and water + rabies vaccine only (5-dose Essen regimen)
Wound wash + Rabies Immunoglobulin (infiltrated into wound) + rabies vaccine starting day 0
Rabies Immunoglobulin IV infusion + vaccine after 7 days
explanation: WHO category III exposure (bite on face/head/neck, multiple bites, or unprovoked bite) requires FULL PEP: (1) Immediate wound washing with soap and water for 15 minutes (most important first step — reduces viral load by 90%), (2) Rabies Immunoglobulin (RIG): HRIG 20 IU/kg or ERIG 40 IU/kg — as much as anatomically possible infiltrated INTO and around the wound, remainder given IM at distant site, (3) Rabies vaccine: 4-dose regimen (days 0,3,7,21 — Zagreb) or 5-dose Essen (days 0,3,7,14,28). RIG provides immediate passive immunity while vaccine induces active immunity. RIG and vaccine must NOT be given at the same site.
highYield: PEP = wound wash (15 min) + RIG infiltrate wound + vaccine day 0,3,7,21 or 28; face bite = Category III; RIG not at same site as vaccine
pyqNote: NEET PG: PEP sequence — wound wash FIRST, then RIG into wound, then vaccine; head/face = high risk = always give RIG

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 25-year-old man presents with fever, agitation, hydrophobia, and aerophobia 6 weeks after a dog bite he did not report. He develops spasms on seeing water. Which form of rabies does he have, and what is the prognosis?
options: Paralytic (dumb) rabies; may recover with ICU support
Encephalitic (furious) rabies; uniformly fatal once symptomatic
Encephalitic rabies; 50% survival with aggressive treatment
explanation: Hydrophobia (fear/spasm on seeing water due to pharyngeal spasm) and aerophobia (spasm on exposure to air) are PATHOGNOMONIC of encephalitic (furious) rabies — the most common form (~80%). Paralytic (dumb) rabies (~20%) presents with ascending flaccid paralysis resembling Guillain-Barré syndrome without hydrophobia. BOTH forms are 100% FATAL once clinical symptoms develop. Only a handful of survival cases have been reported (Milwaukee Protocol, highly controversial). Prognosis is uniformly fatal. This underscores the critical importance of PEP after any suspected exposure. The incubation period is 20 days to several months (average 1-3 months).
highYield: Hydrophobia + aerophobia = furious (encephalitic) rabies; 100% fatal once symptomatic; paralytic rabies = ascending paralysis without hydrophobia
pyqNote: NEET PG: rabies = 100% fatal once symptomatic; hydrophobia = furious form; paralytic form mimics GBS; pre-exposure = 3 doses

subject: Microbiology
stem: A veterinarian working in a high-risk area requests pre-exposure prophylaxis for rabies. What is the recommended pre-exposure vaccination schedule?
options: 5 doses as in the Essen regimen
explanation: Pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP) for rabies consists of 3 doses of rabies vaccine given on days 0, 7, and 21 or 28, administered intramuscularly (or intradermally). It is recommended for: veterinarians, animal handlers, lab workers handling rabies virus, cave explorers, travelers to endemic areas. After PrEP, if exposure occurs, only 2 booster doses are needed (days 0 and 3) WITHOUT RIG. This is the major advantage — PrEP primes the immune system so that booster doses are sufficient without the immediate need for RIG (which may be unavailable). Antibody titer check every 2 years is recommended for high-risk individuals.
highYield: PrEP = 3 doses (day 0,7,21/28); after exposure in PrEP-vaccinated = 2 boosters only, no RIG needed
pyqNote: Pre-exposure rabies = 3 doses; post-exposure in pre-vaccinated = 2 boosters + no RIG; post-exposure naive = 4/5 doses + RIG

subject: Microbiology
stem: An HIV-positive patient has a CD4 count of 38 cells/μL. In addition to TMP-SMX, which additional prophylaxis should be given to prevent disseminated Mycobacterium avium complex (MAC) infection?
explanation: Azithromycin 1200 mg weekly (or clarithromycin 500 mg twice daily) is recommended for MAC prophylaxis when CD4 falls below 50 cells/μL. This patient with CD4 of 38 qualifies. Disseminated MAC presents with fever, night sweats, weight loss, diarrhea, elevated ALP, and anemia. Diagnosis is by blood culture (mycobacterial culture). Treatment of active MAC requires combination therapy (azithromycin/clarithromycin + ethambutol ± rifabutin). Isoniazid is for TB/LTBI prophylaxis (not MAC). Fluconazole is for fungal prophylaxis. TMP-SMX covers PCP and toxoplasma but NOT MAC.
highYield: MAC prophylaxis when CD4 <50 = azithromycin 1200 mg weekly; MAC = fever + high ALP + anemia; diagnosed by blood culture
pyqNote: CD4 thresholds: <200 = TMP-SMX (PCP/toxo); <50 = azithromycin (MAC); <50 = CMV retinitis risk; memorize these thresholds

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 29-year-old HIV-positive woman with CD4 count of 85 cells/μL complains of painful swallowing (odynophagia) and dysphagia to solids. Endoscopy shows white plaques in the esophagus. What is the MOST likely diagnosis and treatment?
options: Esophageal candidiasis (AIDS-defining); treat with oral fluconazole
Esophageal Kaposi sarcoma; treat with chemotherapy
explanation: Esophageal candidiasis is an AIDS-defining condition typically occurring when CD4 <100-200 cells/μL. It presents with odynophagia (painful swallowing) and dysphagia. Endoscopy shows white pseudomembranous plaques that can be wiped off (unlike HSV/CMV ulcers). Treatment: oral or IV fluconazole (first-line). Note: oral thrush (oropharyngeal candidiasis) is NOT AIDS-defining but is a clinical indicator. ESOPHAGEAL candidiasis IS AIDS-defining. CMV esophagitis shows large shallow ulcers. HSV esophagitis shows multiple small shallow ulcers with vesicular lesions. The endoscopic appearance of white plaques plus the CD4 level strongly points to Candida.
highYield: Esophageal candidiasis = AIDS-defining; white plaques on endoscopy; treat with fluconazole; oropharyngeal thrush = not AIDS-defining
pyqNote: NEET PG: esophageal (not oral) candidiasis = AIDS-defining; fluconazole treatment; differentiate from CMV and HSV esophagitis

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 45-year-old asymptomatic man's serology shows: HBsAg negative, anti-HBs positive (titre 150 mIU/mL), anti-HBc IgG positive, HBeAg negative, anti-HBe positive. What is the MOST likely interpretation?
options: Active HBV infection with seroconversion in progress
Past HBV infection with natural immunity and recovery
Window period of acute HBV infection
explanation: This profile represents PAST HBV INFECTION with NATURAL IMMUNITY and RECOVERY: HBsAg negative (infection resolved), anti-HBs positive (immune — can be from vaccine or natural recovery), anti-HBc IgG positive (indicates past NATURAL infection — not from vaccine), anti-HBe positive (seroconversion occurred, replication ended), HBeAg negative. The KEY distinguisher from vaccination is anti-HBc IgG POSITIVE — vaccines only induce anti-HBs, NOT anti-HBc. This person had HBV infection in the past that resolved naturally. The window period shows only anti-HBc IgM (not IgG), HBsAg negative, anti-HBs negative.
highYield: Natural immunity (past infection) = HBsAg neg + anti-HBs pos + anti-HBc IgG pos; Vaccinated = HBsAg neg + anti-HBs pos + anti-HBc NEGATIVE
pyqNote: NEET PG favourite: anti-HBc IgG distinguishes natural immunity (positive) from vaccination (negative); both have anti-HBs positive

subject: Microbiology
stem: An HIV-positive patient with CD4 count of 65 cells/μL presents with focal neurological deficits and seizures. MRI brain shows multiple ring-enhancing lesions with surrounding edema in the basal ganglia. What is the MOST likely diagnosis and first-line treatment?
options: Primary CNS lymphoma (EBV); treat with whole brain radiotherapy
Cryptococcal meningitis; treat with amphotericin B + flucytosine
Cerebral toxoplasmosis; treat with pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + leucovorin
explanation: Cerebral toxoplasmosis (caused by Toxoplasma gondii) is the most common cause of mass lesions in HIV patients, typically occurring when CD4 <100-200 cells/μL. MRI shows multiple ring-enhancing lesions (30-50% in basal ganglia). Treatment: pyrimethamine (loading 200 mg then 50-75 mg/day) + sulfadiazine + leucovorin (folinic acid — to prevent bone marrow toxicity from pyrimethamine). Alternative: TMP-SMX. Clinical improvement within 2 weeks of empirical treatment is diagnostic. Primary CNS lymphoma (EBV) shows similar MRI but typically single lesion; diagnosed by CSF EBV PCR or thallium SPECT scan. TMP-SMX prophylaxis (when CD4 <200) also prevents toxoplasmosis.
highYield: Cerebral toxoplasmosis = multiple ring-enhancing lesions; treat pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + leucovorin; TMP-SMX prevents it at CD4 <200
pyqNote: Ring-enhancing lesion in HIV: toxoplasmosis (multiple) vs CNS lymphoma (single); empirical Toxo treatment first; improvement confirms diagnosis

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 19-year-old with confirmed dengue fever has a platelet count of 65,000/μL but no active bleeding. Which statement about management is CORRECT?
options: Platelet transfusion should be given immediately to prevent bleeding
Aspirin should be given for fever and myalgia
Treatment is supportive; platelet transfusion not indicated without active bleeding or count <10,000
Corticosteroids reduce thrombocytopenia and should be given
explanation: Dengue treatment is ENTIRELY SUPPORTIVE — there is no specific antiviral. Key points: (1) Platelet transfusion is NOT indicated for thrombocytopenia alone; it is given only when platelets <10,000/μL OR with active clinically significant bleeding. Prophylactic platelet transfusion worsens outcome. (2) Aspirin and NSAIDs are CONTRAINDICATED (worsen bleeding risk, can cause gastritis). (3) Corticosteroids are NOT beneficial and not recommended. (4) Paracetamol for fever/pain. (5) Adequate oral/IV hydration is the cornerstone. (6) WHO warning signs require hospitalization. Avoid unnecessary platelet transfusions — they carry risks and are not evidence-based in uncomplicated dengue.
highYield: Dengue = supportive; no antivirals; aspirin CONTRAINDICATED; platelet transfusion only if <10K or active bleeding; paracetamol for fever
pyqNote: NEET PG: dengue management = supportive; aspirin contraindicated; platelet transfusion NOT for thrombocytopenia alone

subject: Microbiology
stem: HIV is classified as a retrovirus because it uniquely converts its genetic information in a reverse manner. What enzyme is responsible for this process, and what is its significance?
options: DNA-dependent DNA polymerase; replicates HIV DNA in the nucleus
RNA-dependent RNA polymerase; creates mRNA from viral RNA
Reverse transcriptase (RNA-dependent DNA polymerase); converts viral RNA genome to double-stranded DNA for integration
Integrase; inserts viral RNA directly into host chromosomal DNA
explanation: Reverse transcriptase (RT) is the hallmark enzyme of retroviruses including HIV. It is an RNA-dependent DNA polymerase that converts the single-stranded HIV RNA genome into double-stranded DNA (proviral DNA). The process: HIV RNA → (RT: RNA-dependent DNA pol) → single-stranded DNA → (RNase H activity of RT degrades RNA) → double-stranded DNA → (integrase) → integrated into host chromosome (provirus). HIV has THREE key enzymes: RT (for genome conversion), Integrase (for chromosomal integration), and Protease (for post-translational processing of viral polyproteins). NRTIs and NNRTIs inhibit RT; INSTIs inhibit integrase; PIs inhibit protease.
highYield: Reverse transcriptase = RNA→DNA; integrase = DNA integration; protease = polyprotein cleavage; all three = drug targets in ART
pyqNote: HIV = retrovirus = RNA genome + reverse transcriptase; 3 enzymes as drug targets: RT, integrase, protease

subject: Microbiology
stem: A neonate is protected against infections in the first few months of life by maternal antibodies. Which immunoglobulin is primarily responsible for this passive immunity through placental transfer?
explanation: IgG is the only immunoglobulin that crosses the placenta via FcRn (neonatal Fc receptor). It is also the most abundant immunoglobulin in serum (~75% of total Ig). IgA is found in secretions and colostrum but does not cross the placenta. IgM is a pentamer too large for transplacental passage. IgE is present in lowest concentration in serum.
highYield: IgG: most abundant, crosses placenta, 4 subclasses (IgG1 most abundant; IgG2 against polysaccharide antigens; IgG3 best complement activator; IgG4 non-inflammatory).
pyqNote: Frequently asked in NEET PG and AIIMS PG. The concept of placental transfer = IgG is a classic one-liner.

subject: Microbiology
stem: During an acute bacterial infection, which immunoglobulin is the FIRST to be produced and is an indicator of recent/current infection?
explanation: IgM is the first immunoglobulin produced during a primary immune response (appears within days of infection). It exists as a pentamer (10 antigen-binding sites) and is highly efficient at activating complement via the classical pathway. Elevated IgM indicates recent or current infection. IgG appears later and persists long-term as immunological memory.
highYield: IgM: pentamer, first produced in acute infection, best complement activator, does NOT cross placenta. Elevated IgM = current/recent infection; elevated IgG = past infection or memory.
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG pattern: 'which Ig is first produced in primary immune response?' Answer = IgM.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 30-year-old breastfeeding mother asks about the protective factors in her colostrum. Which immunoglobulin predominates in colostrum and secretions like saliva and tears, protecting mucosal surfaces?
explanation: Secretory IgA (sIgA) is the predominant immunoglobulin in mucosal secretions including colostrum, saliva, tears, intestinal secretions, and bronchial secretions. It exists as a dimer in secretions, held together by a J chain and secretory component (SC) which protects it from proteolytic digestion. It is the first line of defense at mucosal surfaces. Colostrum is rich in sIgA, providing passive mucosal immunity to the neonate.
highYield: IgA: secretory dimer in mucosa, J chain + secretory component, found in colostrum/saliva/tears/milk/intestinal secretions. Protects mucosal surfaces. Monomer in serum.
pyqNote: Asked as 'predominant Ig in colostrum/secretions/saliva'. Answer = IgA (secretory).

subject: Microbiology
stem: A patient presents with severe anaphylaxis after a bee sting. Which immunoglobulin mediates this Type I hypersensitivity reaction by binding to mast cells and basophils?
explanation: IgE is responsible for Type I (immediate) hypersensitivity reactions including anaphylaxis, urticaria, asthma, and allergic rhinitis. IgE has the LOWEST serum concentration of all immunoglobulins but has the highest affinity for its Fc receptor (FcεRI) on mast cells and basophils. Cross-linking of mast cell-bound IgE by allergen triggers degranulation releasing histamine, prostaglandins, and leukotrienes. IgE also plays a role in defense against parasites (helminths).
highYield: IgE: lowest serum concentration, highest affinity for mast cells/basophils via FcεRI, mediates Type I hypersensitivity (allergy/anaphylaxis) and anti-parasite defense.
pyqNote: Frequently tested: 'Ig with lowest serum concentration' = IgE. 'Ig mediating anaphylaxis' = IgE.

subject: Microbiology
stem: Which immunoglobulin functions primarily as a B-cell surface receptor for antigen and plays a role in B-cell activation, but is present in negligible amounts in serum?
explanation: IgD is expressed on the surface of mature naive B cells along with IgM as part of the B-cell receptor (BCR) complex. It plays a crucial role in B-cell maturation and activation. IgD is present in extremely low concentrations in serum and its function as a secreted antibody is not well defined. IgM is also a B-cell surface receptor but has a well-defined serum role as a pentamer. IgD's primary role is as a membrane-bound antigen receptor on B cells.
highYield: IgD: B-cell surface receptor, present on mature naive B cells along with IgM, negligible serum concentration, function as secreted antibody poorly understood.
pyqNote: Low-frequency but high-discrimination question in NEET PG. IgD = B-cell surface receptor marker of mature naive B cells.

subject: Microbiology
stem: The complement cascade is activated by antigen-antibody complexes binding C1q. Which pathway of complement activation is described here?
explanation: The Classical pathway is activated by antigen-antibody (immune) complexes, specifically when C1q binds to the Fc region of IgG or IgM antibodies in the complex. The sequence is: C1q → C1r → C1s → C4 → C2 → C3 → C5-C9 (MAC). The Alternative pathway is activated by microbial surfaces directly (no antibody needed), using factors B, D, and Properdin. The Lectin pathway uses MBL (mannose-binding lectin) to recognize mannose on pathogens.
highYield: Classical: C1q→C4→C2→C3→C5-C9; triggered by IgG/IgM-antigen complexes. Alternative: microbial surfaces, factors B/D/Properdin. Lectin: MBL on mannose residues.
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG: 'complement activation by antigen-antibody complex' = Classical pathway. Order of activation is frequently tested.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A child presents with recurrent pyogenic infections by encapsulated bacteria such as Streptococcus pneumoniae and Haemophilus influenzae. Complement levels show a specific deficiency. Which complement component deficiency is most likely?
explanation: C3 is the most important complement component and is the central convergence point of all three pathways. C3 deficiency leads to inability to opsonize bacteria (C3b is the main opsonin) and form the MAC. This results in recurrent infections with encapsulated/pyogenic bacteria such as S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, and S. aureus. C5-C9 deficiency specifically leads to recurrent Neisseria infections (meningococcemia, gonococcemia) due to inability to form MAC. C1q deficiency increases susceptibility to SLE-like disease.
highYield: C3 deficiency: recurrent pyogenic bacterial infections (encapsulated organisms). C5-C9 deficiency: recurrent Neisseria infections. C3 = most important complement component.
pyqNote: High-yield distinction: C3 deficiency vs C5-C9 deficiency. C3 = pyogenic bacteria; C5-C9 = Neisseria.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 25-year-old man presents with recurrent episodes of Neisseria meningitidis infections. Which complement component deficiency is most likely responsible?
explanation: Deficiency of terminal complement components C5-C9 (which form the Membrane Attack Complex, MAC) leads to inability to lyse gram-negative organisms, particularly Neisseria species. Patients with MAC deficiency suffer from recurrent meningococcal (N. meningitidis) and gonococcal (N. gonorrhoeae) infections. C3 deficiency causes recurrent pyogenic infections. C1 inhibitor deficiency causes hereditary angioedema. C4 deficiency is associated with SLE-like illness.
highYield: C5-C9 (MAC) deficiency = recurrent Neisseria infections (meningococcemia, disseminated gonococcal infection). These patients need meningococcal vaccination.
pyqNote: Repeatedly asked: 'recurrent Neisseria infections' = C5-C9/MAC deficiency. Always pair with 'recurrent pyogenic bacteria' = C3 deficiency.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 20-year-old woman develops recurrent episodes of non-pitting, non-pruritic swelling of the face, lips, and larynx that resolves spontaneously over 72 hours. She has a family history of similar episodes. Which deficiency is responsible?
explanation: C1 inhibitor (C1-INH) deficiency causes Hereditary Angioedema (HAE). C1-INH normally inhibits C1r, C1s, kallikrein, and factor XII. Its deficiency leads to uncontrolled bradykinin generation, causing episodic, non-pitting, non-pruritic angioedema affecting skin, GI tract, and larynx (life-threatening). It is autosomal dominant. Unlike allergic angioedema, HAE does not respond to antihistamines or steroids. Treatment: C1-INH concentrate, icatibant (bradykinin antagonist), or fresh frozen plasma for acute attacks.
highYield: C1 inhibitor deficiency = Hereditary Angioedema; non-pruritic, non-pitting; autosomal dominant; bradykinin-mediated; unresponsive to antihistamines/steroids.
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG scenario: recurrent non-pruritic angioedema + family history = C1 inhibitor deficiency = Hereditary Angioedema.

subject: Microbiology
stem: During complement activation, which complement fragment is the most potent chemotactic agent that also acts as an anaphylatoxin?
explanation: C5a is the most potent anaphylatoxin and also the most potent chemotactic factor in the complement system. It recruits neutrophils and monocytes to the site of infection (chemotaxis). C3a and C4a are also anaphylatoxins (causing mast cell degranulation, smooth muscle contraction, increased vascular permeability) but are weaker than C5a and lack significant chemotactic activity. C3b is the main opsonin. The order of anaphylatoxin potency: C5a > C3a > C4a.
highYield: Anaphylatoxins: C3a, C4a, C5a (in increasing potency). C5a = most potent anaphylatoxin AND chemotaxin. C3b = opsonin. C5a also primes neutrophils.
pyqNote: High-yield distinction: most potent chemotaxin in complement = C5a. Opsonin = C3b. All anaphylatoxins listed in order of potency.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 22-year-old student develops urticaria, angioedema, and bronchospasm within minutes of a penicillin injection. Which type of hypersensitivity reaction is this?
explanation: Type I hypersensitivity (immediate/anaphylactic) is IgE-mediated. On initial exposure, IgE is produced and binds to FcεRI on mast cells and basophils. On re-exposure, the allergen cross-links IgE → mast cell degranulation → release of histamine, leukotrienes, prostaglandins → immediate (within minutes) symptoms: urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, anaphylaxis. Examples: anaphylaxis to penicillin/bee stings, allergic rhinitis, asthma, food allergy.
highYield: Type I: IgE-mediated, immediate (minutes), mast cells/basophils, anaphylaxis, urticaria, asthma. Mediators: histamine (early), leukotrienes (late).
pyqNote: Most commonly asked hypersensitivity in NEET PG. Type I = IgE + mast cells + immediate reaction.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A patient with myasthenia gravis has autoantibodies that block acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction. Which type of hypersensitivity reaction best describes this mechanism?
explanation: Type II (cytotoxic) hypersensitivity involves IgG or IgM antibodies directed against antigens on cell surfaces or extracellular matrix. Mechanisms include complement-mediated lysis, ADCC (antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity), and receptor blockade/stimulation. Myasthenia gravis (anti-AChR antibodies), Goodpasture syndrome (anti-GBM antibodies), autoimmune hemolytic anemia, and Graves disease (stimulating TSH receptor) are all Type II. Graves is unique as the antibody stimulates rather than destroys.
highYield: Type II: IgG+IgM against cell-surface antigens. Examples: hemolytic anemia, Goodpasture, myasthenia gravis (receptor blockade), Graves (receptor stimulation), transfusion reactions.
pyqNote: NEET PG frequently gives myasthenia gravis or Goodpasture to identify Type II hypersensitivity. Key = antibody against cell surface/extracellular antigen.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A patient develops fever, joint pain, skin rash, and lymphadenopathy 7-10 days after receiving horse antiserum for snake bite. Complement levels are low and immune complexes are found in the blood. What type of hypersensitivity is this?
explanation: Type III hypersensitivity is caused by deposition of soluble antigen-antibody (immune) complexes in tissues. These complexes activate complement and recruit neutrophils, causing tissue damage. Serum sickness (7-10 days after antiserum injection) is the classic systemic Type III reaction. Other examples: Arthus reaction (local), SLE, post-streptococcal GN, hypersensitivity pneumonitis, polyarteritis nodosa. Low complement levels are characteristic due to complement consumption.
highYield: Type III: immune complex deposition, complement activation, neutrophil recruitment. Examples: SLE, serum sickness, post-streptococcal GN, Arthus reaction. Low complement levels.
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG: serum sickness after antiserum = Type III. SLE = Type III (DNA-anti-DNA complexes). Low complement = Type III clue.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A healthcare worker develops an itchy, erythematous rash with vesicles on her hands 48-72 hours after wearing latex gloves. A patch test is positive at 48 hours. Which type of hypersensitivity is this?
explanation: Type IV hypersensitivity (delayed-type hypersensitivity, DTH) is T-cell mediated and does NOT involve antibodies. It develops 48-72 hours after antigen exposure. Contact dermatitis (to latex, nickel, poison ivy) is a classic example. The Mantoux tuberculin test (positive at 48-72 hours) is another classic DTH reaction. Other examples: granuloma formation in TB/leprosy/sarcoidosis, allograft rejection. The patch test used to diagnose contact dermatitis is read at 48-72 hours.
highYield: Type IV: delayed (48-72 hours), T-cell mediated (no antibody), contact dermatitis, tuberculin test, granuloma. CD4+ Th1 cells and CD8+ CTLs are involved.
pyqNote: Mantoux test = Type IV. Contact dermatitis = Type IV. Graft rejection = Type IV. Key = 48-72 hour delay + T-cell mediated, no Ig involved.

subject: Microbiology
stem: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis occurs 2-3 weeks after streptococcal throat infection. The renal biopsy shows 'lumpy-bumpy' granular deposits on immunofluorescence. Which hypersensitivity mechanism is responsible?
explanation: Post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis (PSGN) is a Type III hypersensitivity reaction. Immune complexes of streptococcal antigens and IgG antibodies are deposited in the glomerular basement membrane. This causes complement activation, neutrophil recruitment, and glomerular damage. On immunofluorescence, 'lumpy-bumpy' (granular) deposits of IgG and C3 are seen (vs. Goodpasture's Type II which shows linear deposits). PSGN follows pharyngitis with nephritogenic streptococcal strains (M types 1, 2, 4, 12) and presents with hematuria, edema, and hypertension.
highYield: PSGN = Type III (immune complex). Lumpy-bumpy granular deposits on IF. Goodpasture = Type II (anti-GBM) = linear deposits. This distinction is frequently tested.
pyqNote: High-yield: 'lumpy-bumpy' granular IF = Type III (PSGN, SLE); 'linear' IF = Type II (Goodpasture). The IF pattern distinguishes Type II from Type III GN.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 28-year-old man presents with chronic low back pain worse in the morning with stiffness that improves with exercise. X-ray shows sacroiliitis. Which HLA antigen is most strongly associated with his condition?
explanation: HLA-B27 is strongly associated with the seronegative spondyloarthropathies (SpA). Ankylosing Spondylitis (AS) has the strongest association (90% of AS patients are HLA-B27 positive). Other HLA-B27-associated conditions include Reactive arthritis (formerly Reiter syndrome), Psoriatic arthritis (axial involvement), and IBD-associated arthritis. The clinical scenario describes classic AS: young male, morning stiffness improving with activity, sacroiliitis on X-ray.
highYield: HLA-B27: AS (strongest, 90%), Reactive arthritis, Psoriatic arthritis (axial), IBD-associated arthritis. Mnemonic: PAIR (Psoriatic, AS, IBD-arthritis, Reactive).
pyqNote: Most commonly tested HLA association in NEET PG. HLA-B27 = AS is a must-know. Also remember the full PAIR group.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with a butterfly-shaped facial rash, joint pain, oral ulcers, and positive ANA. Which HLA is most associated with her condition?
explanation: SLE (Systemic Lupus Erythematosus) is associated with HLA-DR3. HLA-DR3 associations include: SLE, Graves disease, Sjögren syndrome, and Myasthenia gravis (without thymoma). HLA-DR4 is associated with Rheumatoid arthritis, Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus, and Pemphigus vulgaris. HLA-Cw6 is associated with Psoriasis. HLA-B27 is associated with spondyloarthropathies.
highYield: HLA-DR3: SLE, Graves, Sjögren, Myasthenia gravis. HLA-DR4: RA, T1DM, Pemphigus vulgaris. These are the two most tested DR associations in NEET PG.
pyqNote: Pattern: 'SLE + female + ANA positive' → HLA-DR3. Frequently paired with 'RA' → HLA-DR4 to distinguish.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with chronic diarrhea, weight loss, and iron deficiency anemia. Duodenal biopsy shows villous atrophy. Which HLA haplotype is most associated with this condition?
explanation: Celiac disease (gluten-sensitive enteropathy) is strongly associated with HLA-DQ2 (found in ~90% of celiac patients) and HLA-DQ8 (found in most remaining patients). Almost all patients with celiac disease carry at least one of these alleles. Duodenal biopsy showing villous atrophy is the gold standard for diagnosis. Anti-tissue transglutaminase (tTG) IgA is the best initial screening test. The condition is treated with a gluten-free diet.
highYield: Celiac disease = HLA-DQ2 (90%) and HLA-DQ8. Remember: 'DQ2 for celiac' is the highest yield HLA association for celiac in NEET PG.
pyqNote: Frequently tested: villous atrophy + chronic diarrhea + HLA association = HLA-DQ2/DQ8. Anti-tTG IgA is the serological test.

subject: Microbiology
stem: During flow cytometry analysis of peripheral blood lymphocytes, which CD marker would be used to identify ALL T lymphocytes regardless of their subtype?
explanation: CD3 is the pan-T cell marker present on all T lymphocytes (both helper and cytotoxic). It is part of the T-cell receptor (TCR) complex and is essential for TCR signal transduction. CD4 is found only on helper T cells; CD8 on cytotoxic T cells. CD19 and CD20 are B-cell markers. To identify all T cells in flow cytometry, CD3 is used as the gating marker. The CD4:CD8 ratio is normally ~2:1 and is reduced in HIV/AIDS.
highYield: CD3 = pan-T cell marker (all T cells). CD4 = helper T cells. CD8 = cytotoxic T cells. CD19/CD20 = B cells. CD56 = NK cells. CD14 = monocytes.
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG: 'marker for ALL T cells' = CD3. CD4 = helper only; CD8 = cytotoxic only.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A patient with HIV is monitored using CD4 cell counts. CD4+ T cells are which functional subtype of T lymphocytes?
explanation: CD4+ T cells are helper T cells (Th cells). They are the primary targets of HIV (via CD4 receptor + co-receptors CCR5/CXCR4). CD4+ Th cells play a crucial role in orchestrating immune responses by secreting cytokines that help activate B cells, CD8+ cytotoxic T cells, and macrophages. Normal CD4 count is 500-1500 cells/µL. AIDS is defined as CD4 < 200 cells/µL or the presence of an AIDS-defining illness. CD8+ cells are cytotoxic T cells. CD25 is expressed on regulatory T cells (Tregs) and activated T cells.
highYield: CD4 = helper T cells (HIV target). CD8 = cytotoxic T cells. CD25 = IL-2 receptor, expressed on Tregs and activated T cells. AIDS: CD4 < 200 cells/µL.
pyqNote: HIV + CD4 count is a recurring topic. CD4 = helper T cells. The co-receptors CCR5 (macrophage tropic) and CXCR4 (T-cell tropic) are also frequently asked.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A patient has a genetic deficiency in cells bearing both CD16 and CD56 markers. Which immune cells are affected, and what would be the primary immunological consequence?
options: Loss of B cells; no antibody production
Loss of NK cells; impaired innate immunity against virus-infected and tumor cells
Loss of helper T cells; impaired adaptive immunity
Loss of monocytes; impaired antigen presentation
explanation: CD56 and CD16 are both expressed on Natural Killer (NK) cells. CD56 is the defining NK cell marker; CD16 (FcγRIII) is the Fc receptor that mediates ADCC (antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity). NK cells are innate immune lymphocytes that kill virus-infected cells and tumor cells without requiring prior sensitization. They use the 'missing self' mechanism: if a cell lacks MHC class I (as in many tumor cells and virus-infected cells), NK cells kill it. NK cell deficiency leads to severe, recurrent herpesvirus infections.
highYield: NK cells: CD56 (defining marker) + CD16 (Fc receptor for ADCC). Kill targets via perforin/granzyme and ADCC. 'Missing self' surveillance. CD16 also on neutrophils.
pyqNote: High-yield: CD56 = NK cells. CD16 = NK cells + neutrophils. NK cell deficiency = recurrent herpesvirus infections. ADCC via CD16 is a key mechanism.

subject: Microbiology
stem: An autoclave is the gold standard for sterilization. What are the correct parameters for a standard autoclave cycle?
explanation: Autoclave (moist heat/steam under pressure) is the gold standard method of sterilization. Standard cycle parameters: 121°C (250°F), 15 psi (103 kPa), 15 minutes. A rapid cycle uses 134°C for 3 minutes. Moist heat is more effective than dry heat because steam penetrates better and denaturation of proteins occurs at lower temperatures in the presence of water. The Bowie-Dick test is used to check steam penetration in pre-vacuum autoclaves. Biological indicator: Bacillus stearothermophilus (now reclassified as Geobacillus stearothermophilus) spores.
highYield: Autoclave: 121°C/15psi/15min (or 134°C/3min). Gold standard for sterilization. Moist heat destroys by protein denaturation + disruption of cell wall.
pyqNote: Most fundamental sterilization question: autoclave parameters 121°C/15psi/15min. Must know. Frequently asked as one-liner.

subject: Microbiology
stem: To verify that an autoclave has achieved sterility, which biological indicator uses bacterial spores and is the most reliable test?
explanation: Biological indicators (BIs) are the gold standard for monitoring sterilization efficacy. For autoclave (moist heat), the biological indicator is Bacillus stearothermophilus (Geobacillus stearothermophilus) spores, as these spores are the most heat-resistant in nature and require proper autoclave conditions to be killed. If the spores are killed (no growth in culture after 48-72 hours at 55-60°C), the cycle was successful. The Bowie-Dick test checks steam penetration and air removal in pre-vacuum autoclaves but is a physical/chemical test, not biological. Chemical tape changes color but only confirms temperature exposure, not sterilization.
highYield: Biological indicator for autoclave = B. stearothermophilus spores. Bowie-Dick test = steam penetration test (physical). For EO gas: B. subtilis spores. For dry heat: B. subtilis var. niger.
pyqNote: Always asked: 'biological indicator for autoclave' = B. stearothermophilus. Compare with EO gas = B. subtilis. This distinction is NEET PG gold.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A microbiology laboratory needs to sterilize glassware, oils, and anhydrous powders. Which sterilization method is most appropriate?
options: Dry heat oven at 180°C for 1 hour
explanation: Dry heat (hot air oven) is used for materials that cannot be sterilized with moist heat or would be damaged by steam. Items sterilized by dry heat include: glassware, oils, waxes, anhydrous powders, and cutting instruments (which may be blunted by autoclave steam). Standard dry heat cycle: 160°C for 2 hours, or 180°C for 1 hour, or 170°C for 30 minutes. The mechanism is oxidation of cell components. Dry heat requires higher temperatures than moist heat because water facilitates protein denaturation. Biological indicator for dry heat: B. subtilis var. niger spores.
highYield: Dry heat: 180°C/1hr (or 160°C/2hr). Used for glassware, oils, powders, sharp instruments. Biological indicator: B. subtilis var. niger. Mechanism: oxidation.
pyqNote: NEET PG pattern: 'glassware/oils/powders' = dry heat. 'Instruments/surgical supplies' = autoclave. Know temperature differences.

subject: Microbiology
stem: An endoscope used for upper GI endoscopy needs to be processed between patients. Which chemical agent is the most appropriate for high-level disinfection of heat-labile endoscopes?
explanation: 2% Glutaraldehyde (Cidex) is the agent of choice for high-level disinfection of heat-labile items like endoscopes, bronchoscopes, and cystoscopes. At 20-30 minutes, it achieves high-level disinfection (kills all organisms except high numbers of spores). At 10 hours, it achieves sterilization (kills spores too; sporicidal). It is effective against bacteria, viruses, fungi, and mycobacteria. Key points: it is alkaline-activated ('activated Cidex'), toxic (irritates mucous membranes), requires thorough rinsing before patient use, and must be replaced regularly (every 14-28 days based on MEC testing).
highYield: Glutaraldehyde 2%: high-level disinfection in 20-30 min; sterilization (sporicidal) in 10 hours. Used for endoscopes. Alkaline-activated. Toxic to mucous membranes.
pyqNote: Classic question: 'high-level disinfection of endoscopes' = 2% glutaraldehyde/Cidex. Time matters: 20-30 min for HLD; 10 hours for sterilization.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A hospital needs to sterilize single-use plastic syringes, cardiac catheters, and other heat-labile medical devices. Which method is most suitable?
explanation: Ethylene oxide (EO) gas sterilization is used for heat-labile items that cannot withstand the high temperatures of autoclave or dry heat. These include: plastics, rubber, electronic equipment, pre-packaged single-use items, cardiac catheters, and some implants. EO works by alkylation of nucleic acids and proteins. Disadvantages: mutagenic, carcinogenic (occupational hazard), requires long aeration time (8-12 hours) to remove toxic residues, flammable, and the cycle is long. Biological indicator: B. subtilis var. globigii (B. subtilis var. niger) spores.
highYield: EO gas: heat-labile items (plastics, catheters, electronics). Mutagenic/carcinogenic occupational hazard. Long aeration needed. Biological indicator: B. subtilis spores.
pyqNote: NEET PG: 'heat-labile plastics/catheters' = EO gas. 'Mutagenic/carcinogenic' sterilization agent = EO. Know the occupational hazard aspect.

subject: Microbiology
stem: UV radiation is used for disinfection in operation theatres and laminar flow cabinets. What is the most germicidal wavelength of UV light and what is its primary mechanism of microbial killing?
options: 260 nm; formation of thymine dimers in DNA
explanation: UV radiation at 260 nm (ultraviolet-C range) is the most germicidal wavelength, corresponding to the peak absorption by DNA. The mechanism of microbial killing is the formation of thymine dimers (cyclobutane pyrimidine dimers) between adjacent thymine bases in the DNA strand. This distorts the DNA double helix, preventing replication and transcription. Important limitations: UV does NOT penetrate solid objects (surface disinfection only), is NOT sporicidal, can cause eye damage (photokeratitis) and skin damage (erythema), and its germicidal effect decreases with distance. Uses: operation theatres, laminar flow hoods, water purification.
highYield: UV radiation: most germicidal at 260 nm. Mechanism: thymine dimer formation (DNA damage). NOT sporicidal. NOT penetrating (surface only). Used in OTs and laminar flow.
pyqNote: NEET PG: 'most germicidal wavelength of UV' = 260 nm. 'Mechanism of UV' = thymine dimers. 'UV is NOT' = sporicidal. These negatives are high-yield.

subject: Microbiology
stem: The Bowie-Dick test is performed in a hospital CSSD (Central Sterile Supply Department). What does this test specifically evaluate?
options: Efficacy of biological sterilization by monitoring spore destruction
Steam penetration and air removal in pre-vacuum autoclaves
Temperature accuracy of the dry heat oven
Concentration of glutaraldehyde in Cidex solution
explanation: The Bowie-Dick test is a specific test for pre-vacuum (high-vacuum) autoclaves that checks the efficiency of air removal from the autoclave chamber and steam penetration into porous loads. A standardized test pack with a special chemical indicator sheet is placed in the autoclave and run through an empty cycle. Uniform color change of the indicator across the sheet indicates adequate steam penetration and air removal. If air pockets remain, sterilization will be incomplete even if temperatures are reached. This is a physical/chemical test, not a biological test. It should be performed daily in pre-vacuum autoclaves.
highYield: Bowie-Dick test: evaluates steam penetration and air removal in pre-vacuum autoclaves. Run daily, empty chamber. Chemical indicator - uniform color change = pass. Not a biological test.
pyqNote: Less commonly asked but high-discrimination: 'Bowie-Dick test purpose' = steam penetration/air removal in pre-vacuum autoclaves. Do not confuse with biological indicators.

subject: Microbiology
stem: The lectin pathway of complement activation is initiated by which molecule binding to mannose residues on the surface of pathogens?
explanation: The Lectin pathway (Mannose-binding lectin pathway) is initiated when MBL (Mannose-Binding Lectin), a pattern recognition molecule produced in the liver, binds to mannose residues on the surface of pathogens (bacteria, fungi, viruses). MBL is structurally similar to C1q and activates MASPs (MBL-Associated Serine Proteases), which cleave C4 and C2, similar to the classical pathway. The alternative pathway is triggered by microbial surfaces via spontaneous C3 hydrolysis and factors B, D, and Properdin. C1q initiates the classical pathway.
highYield: Lectin pathway: MBL → mannose on pathogens → MASPs cleave C4 and C2 → C3 convertase. MBL deficiency = increased susceptibility to infections in early childhood.
pyqNote: Distinguishing point for NEET PG: Classical = C1q + Ag-Ab complex; Lectin = MBL + mannose on pathogens; Alternative = C3b + microbial surface + factors B,D,P.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 45-year-old man is found to have hereditary hemochromatosis on screening. His HLA typing would most likely show which association?
explanation: Hereditary hemochromatosis (HH) is associated with HLA-A3. The HFE gene (C282Y mutation most common) responsible for HH is located close to the HLA region on chromosome 6p, creating a linkage disequilibrium with HLA-A3. HLA-Cw6 is associated with psoriasis. HLA-DR4 is associated with RA, T1DM, and pemphigus vulgaris. HLA-B27 is associated with spondyloarthropathies. Hemochromatosis is an autosomal recessive disorder of iron overload, presenting with liver cirrhosis, diabetes ('bronze diabetes'), cardiomyopathy, and hypogonadism.
highYield: HLA-A3 = hereditary hemochromatosis. HLA-Cw6 = psoriasis. These are less common HLA associations that differentiate high-scorers in NEET PG.
pyqNote: Rare but asked in NEET PG: hemochromatosis HLA = HLA-A3. Also remember HLA-Cw6 = psoriasis (most common chronic skin condition). These are the unusual HLA associations.

subject: Microbiology
stem: A laboratory technician uses a chemical disinfectant that kills all organisms including bacterial spores, but only after prolonged exposure (10 hours). This agent is routinely used for 20-30 minutes to achieve high-level disinfection of endoscopes. However, it is noted to be mutagenic and irritating to the skin and mucous membranes. Identify this agent.
explanation: 2% Glutaraldehyde (Cidex, activated glutaraldehyde) fits all the described characteristics: it is sporicidal at 10 hours (true sterilant), achieves high-level disinfection (kills all organisms except some spores) in 20-30 minutes, is alkaline-buffered (pH 7.5-8.5), is effective against a broad spectrum including mycobacteria, and is used for heat-sensitive endoscopes.
