options: Recurrent laryngeal nerve injury; treat with voice rest
Hypoparathyroidism due to parathyroid devascularization; give IV calcium gluconate
Thyroid storm; give propranolol and Lugol's iodine
explanation: Post-thyroidectomy hypocalcemia is the most common complication after total thyroidectomy, occurring due to inadvertent removal or devascularization of parathyroid glands. Symptoms include perioral tingling, paresthesias, carpopedal spasm, Chvostek sign (facial muscle twitch with tapping over facial nerve), and Trousseau sign (carpal spasm with BP cuff inflation). Serum calcium <8 mg/dL confirms hypocalcemia. Immediate management is IV calcium gluconate (10 mL of 10% solution over 10 minutes), followed by oral calcium and vitamin D supplementation. Bilateral RLN injury causes stridor/respiratory distress, not hypocalcemia.
highYield: Post-thyroidectomy hypocalcemia: Chvostek + Trousseau signs, perioral tingling. Treat with IV calcium gluconate acutely, then oral Ca + Vit D.
pyqNote: Complications of thyroidectomy (hypocalcemia, RLN injury) are perennial NEET PG favorites. Know signs and immediate management.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 55-year-old woman presents with a solitary thyroid nodule. FNAC shows follicular cells arranged in a microfollicular pattern. She undergoes thyroid lobectomy, and histopathology reveals capsular invasion and vascular invasion. What is the diagnosis and what distinguishes this from follicular adenoma?
options: Papillary thyroid carcinoma; distinguished by Orphan Annie nuclei
Follicular thyroid carcinoma; distinguished by presence of capsular and/or vascular invasion
Medullary thyroid carcinoma; distinguished by calcitonin staining
Hurthle cell adenoma; distinguished by oxyphilic cells
explanation: Follicular thyroid carcinoma (FTC) cannot be distinguished from follicular adenoma on FNAC - both show follicular cells and the cytology is reported as 'indeterminate' (Bethesda Category IV or V). The distinction is made ONLY on histological examination of the surgical specimen by demonstrating capsular invasion (tumor cells penetrating the entire capsule) and/or vascular invasion. FNAC showing follicular cells mandates surgical excision (lobectomy) for definitive diagnosis. FTC spreads hematogenously to bones and lungs (unlike PTC which spreads via lymphatics).
highYield: FTC vs follicular adenoma: ONLY distinguished by capsular/vascular invasion on histology, NOT on FNAC. FNAC = indeterminate → surgery needed.
pyqNote: The limitation of FNAC in follicular lesions and hematogenous spread of FTC are classic NEET PG exam points.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 60-year-old woman with follicular thyroid carcinoma presents with a pathological fracture of the femur. X-ray shows a lytic expansile lesion with a 'blow-out' appearance. Which of the following best explains the spread pattern of this tumor?
options: Lymphatic spread to regional lymph nodes
Hematogenous spread to bone and lung
Direct extension to surrounding soft tissue
explanation: Follicular thyroid carcinoma characteristically spreads via the hematogenous route, most commonly to bones (producing lytic 'blow-out' lesions, especially in skull, spine, pelvis, and femur) and lungs. This is in contrast to papillary thyroid carcinoma, which spreads via lymphatics to central and lateral neck lymph nodes. The hematogenous spread of FTC explains why patients may present with bone pain or pathological fractures as the first manifestation. The 'blow-out' lytic bone metastasis from thyroid carcinoma is a classic radiological finding.
highYield: FTC = hematogenous spread → bone (lytic blow-out lesions) and lung. PTC = lymphatic spread → central + lateral neck LN.
pyqNote: Spread pattern differences between FTC (hematogenous) and PTC (lymphatic) are directly tested in NEET PG.

subject: Surgery
topic: Thyroid Cancer - Hurthle Cell & Special Variants
stem: A 65-year-old woman undergoes total thyroidectomy for a thyroid tumor. Histology shows a tumor composed predominantly of large polygonal cells with abundant granular eosinophilic cytoplasm (oxyphilic cells/Askanazy cells) with capsular and vascular invasion. Post-operatively, she is planned for radioiodine therapy but the nuclear medicine physician notes poor iodine uptake. Which thyroid cancer variant does this represent?
options: Papillary thyroid carcinoma, tall cell variant
explanation: Hurthle cell carcinoma (oncocytic carcinoma) is an aggressive variant of follicular thyroid carcinoma composed of oxyphilic (Hurthle/Askanazy) cells - large polygonal cells with abundant granular eosinophilic cytoplasm due to mitochondria-packed cytoplasm. It is distinguished from Hurthle cell adenoma by capsular and vascular invasion. A key characteristic is that it poorly concentrates radioiodine (I-131) compared to regular FTC, making RAI therapy less effective. It has a worse prognosis than regular FTC with higher rates of lymph node metastasis and distant spread.
highYield: Hurthle cell carcinoma: oxyphilic cells + capsular/vascular invasion + POOR radioiodine uptake + aggressive behavior.
pyqNote: Hurthle cell carcinoma's poor RAI uptake distinguishing it from regular FTC is a high-yield surgical oncology point.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 35-year-old man presents with a thyroid nodule. His brother recently died of thyroid cancer. FNAC shows sheets of polygonal and spindle cells with amyloid deposits in the stroma. Immunohistochemistry is positive for calcitonin. Which cells give rise to this tumor?
explanation: Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) arises from parafollicular C-cells (clear cells) of the thyroid gland, which are neural crest-derived cells that produce calcitonin. The tumor cells are positive for calcitonin on immunohistochemistry, and serum calcitonin is used as a tumor marker for diagnosis, screening family members, and monitoring for recurrence. Amyloid deposits in the stroma (derived from procalcitonin) are a characteristic histological feature. Unlike differentiated thyroid cancers (which arise from follicular cells), MTC does not take up radioiodine.
highYield: MTC: C-cells → calcitonin marker + amyloid stroma + IHC calcitonin positive + NO radioiodine uptake.
pyqNote: Cell of origin and tumor marker of MTC (C-cells, calcitonin) are basic but frequently tested NEET PG questions.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 30-year-old woman is diagnosed with medullary thyroid carcinoma. Genetic testing reveals a germline RET proto-oncogene mutation (C634F). Her 8-year-old son has no symptoms. What is the most appropriate next step for the son?
options: Annual ultrasound thyroid starting at age 10
Serum calcitonin stimulation test with pentagastrin or calcium
RET genetic testing; if positive, prophylactic total thyroidectomy
No action needed until symptoms develop
explanation: Hereditary MTC (25% of all MTC cases) is associated with germline RET proto-oncogene mutations and is autosomal dominant. In MEN2A, prophylactic thyroidectomy timing is based on the specific RET codon mutation (risk category): high-risk codons (e.g., C634) - thyroidectomy by age 5 years. For a child of an affected parent, RET genetic testing should be performed. If positive, prophylactic total thyroidectomy is performed at an age dictated by the mutation risk level. Calcitonin stimulation testing (pentagastrin or calcium infusion) was previously used before genetic testing was available and is now less commonly used for screening.
highYield: Hereditary MTC: RET germline mutation → screen family with genetic testing → prophylactic thyroidectomy based on mutation risk category.
pyqNote: Prophylactic thyroidectomy for RET mutation carriers in MEN2 is a high-yield clinical scenario in NEET PG surgery.

subject: Surgery
stem: A patient is diagnosed with MEN2B syndrome. Which of the following combination of features would you expect?
options: Medullary thyroid carcinoma + primary hyperparathyroidism + pheochromocytoma
Medullary thyroid carcinoma + pheochromocytoma + mucosal neuromas + marfanoid habitus
Medullary thyroid carcinoma + pheochromocytoma + parathyroid hyperplasia + cafe-au-lait spots
Pituitary adenoma + pancreatic tumors + parathyroid adenoma
explanation: MEN2B (Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia type 2B) is characterized by: (1) Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) - most aggressive, develops earliest, (2) Pheochromocytoma (50%), (3) Mucosal neuromas (lips, tongue, eyelids), (4) Marfanoid habitus (tall, long limbs), and (5) Intestinal ganglioneuromatosis. Unlike MEN2A, there is NO hyperparathyroidism in MEN2B. MEN2A includes MTC + pheochromocytoma + primary hyperparathyroidism. MEN1 (Wermer syndrome) includes pituitary + pancreatic + parathyroid tumors. MEN2B is associated with RET codon 918 mutation (highest risk) and MTC appears in infancy.
highYield: MEN2B: MTC + Pheo + Mucosal neuromas + Marfanoid habitus (NO hyperparathyroidism). MEN2A: MTC + Pheo + Hyperparathyroidism.
pyqNote: Distinguishing MEN2A from MEN2B features is a classic NEET PG multiple-choice trap question.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 75-year-old woman presents with a rapidly enlarging neck mass of 6 weeks duration causing dysphagia and stridor. She has had a goitre for 20 years. CT scan shows a large heterogeneous thyroid mass with tracheal compression, lymph node enlargement, and lung nodules. Biopsy shows bizarre giant cells and spindle cells with numerous mitoses. What is the most likely diagnosis and prognosis?
options: Papillary thyroid carcinoma; excellent prognosis with total thyroidectomy
Follicular thyroid carcinoma; good prognosis with radioiodine
Anaplastic (undifferentiated) thyroid carcinoma; median survival <6 months
Lymphoma of thyroid; curable with chemotherapy
explanation: Anaplastic thyroid carcinoma (ATC) is the most aggressive thyroid malignancy with the worst prognosis of all thyroid cancers. It typically presents in elderly patients (>65 years) with a pre-existing goitre. Features include: rapid growth, early invasion of adjacent structures (trachea, esophagus), bizarre pleomorphic cells with spindle and giant cell morphology, high mitotic rate, and early distant metastases. It does not take up radioiodine (dedifferentiated). Median survival is 3-6 months; 1-year survival <20%. Treatment is palliative (external beam RT ± chemotherapy with doxorubicin). It is classified as Stage IV at diagnosis by AJCC.
highYield: ATC: Elderly + rapidly enlarging goitre + worst prognosis (median survival <6 months) + Stage IV at diagnosis + no RAI uptake + palliative treatment.
pyqNote: ATC as the thyroid cancer with worst prognosis in elderly patients is a frequently tested NEET PG fact.

subject: Surgery
stem: During total thyroidectomy, a 40-year-old woman's right recurrent laryngeal nerve is inadvertently divided. She recovers from surgery but develops hoarseness. Two months later, if the left RLN is also injured during a re-exploration, what would be the most dangerous complication?
options: Permanent hoarseness and change in voice quality
Bilateral vocal cord paralysis causing respiratory obstruction requiring tracheostomy
explanation: Unilateral RLN injury results in ipsilateral vocal cord paralysis causing hoarseness (the contralateral cord compensates). Bilateral RLN injury causes both vocal cords to assume the paramedian position (adducted), leading to severe respiratory distress and stridor requiring emergency tracheostomy. This is the most dangerous complication of bilateral RLN injury. The RLN supplies all intrinsic laryngeal muscles except the cricothyroid (supplied by superior laryngeal nerve). The right RLN hooks around the subclavian artery; the left hooks around the arch of aorta at the ligamentum arteriosum - making it longer and more vulnerable.
highYield: Bilateral RLN injury → both cords in paramedian position → acute airway obstruction → emergency tracheostomy. Unilateral = hoarseness only.
pyqNote: Consequences of unilateral vs bilateral RLN injury and the need for tracheostomy are high-yield surgical anatomy questions.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 45-year-old woman undergoes screening mammography. The radiologist reports a 2 cm spiculated mass with microcalcifications and skin thickening, classified as BIRADS 5. What does this classification indicate and what is the recommended next step?
options: Probably benign; repeat mammogram in 6 months
Highly suspicious for malignancy; tissue biopsy is required
Known malignancy; correlate with prior biopsy results
explanation: The BIRADS (Breast Imaging Reporting and Data System) classification: BIRADS 0 = incomplete, BIRADS 1 = negative (routine screening), BIRADS 2 = benign (routine screening), BIRADS 3 = probably benign (<2% malignancy risk, 6-month follow-up), BIRADS 4 = suspicious (2-95% risk, biopsy recommended), BIRADS 5 = highly suspicious (>95% malignancy risk, biopsy required), BIRADS 6 = known malignancy (biopsy-proven, awaiting treatment). BIRADS 5 indicates a highly suspicious lesion requiring tissue biopsy (core needle biopsy preferred for histology and receptor status). Spiculated mass with microcalcifications is a classic malignant mammographic appearance.
highYield: BIRADS 5 = highly suspicious (>95% malignancy) → tissue biopsy mandatory. BIRADS 3 = probably benign → 6-month follow-up.
pyqNote: BIRADS classification and management at each level are standard NEET PG surgical oncology questions.

subject: Surgery
topic: Breast Cancer - FNAC and Core Biopsy
stem: A 50-year-old woman has a palpable breast lump classified as BIRADS 4 on mammography. FNAC is performed and reported as C4 (suspicious). The next step is core needle biopsy. What additional information does core needle biopsy provide that FNAC cannot?
options: Determination of tumor size on imaging
Histological architecture, ER/PR/HER2 receptor status, and Nottingham grade
explanation: FNAC (C1-C5 classification): C1=inadequate, C2=benign, C3=atypia/probably benign, C4=suspicious, C5=malignant. FNAC provides cytology only - it can suggest malignancy but cannot determine: (1) histological architecture (invasive vs in-situ), (2) ER/PR/HER2 receptor status, (3) Nottingham histological grade (tubule formation + nuclear pleomorphism + mitotic count), (4) lymphovascular invasion. Core needle biopsy (Tru-Cut) provides histology with tissue architecture, enabling definitive cancer typing, receptor status (essential for treatment planning - tamoxifen for ER+, trastuzumab for HER2+), and grading. All invasive breast cancers must have ER/PR/HER2 testing on core biopsy.
highYield: Core needle biopsy > FNAC: provides histology, ER/PR/HER2 status, Nottingham grade - all essential for treatment planning.
pyqNote: Advantages of core biopsy over FNAC for receptor testing in breast cancer is a modern high-yield NEET PG topic.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 48-year-old woman has a 2.5 cm, Grade 2, ER+/PR+/HER2- invasive ductal carcinoma in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast with no palpable axillary lymph nodes. She is suitable for either mastectomy or breast conservation. Regarding surgical options, which statement is most accurate?
options: Modified radical mastectomy gives better survival than breast-conserving surgery
Wide local excision (WLE) with postoperative radiotherapy is oncologically equivalent to mastectomy
WLE alone without radiotherapy is sufficient for early-stage breast cancer
Sentinel lymph node biopsy is contraindicated in clinically node-negative patients
explanation: Multiple randomized controlled trials (NSABP B-06, Milan trials) have established that breast-conserving surgery (wide local excision/lumpectomy) with postoperative radiotherapy provides equivalent survival to mastectomy for early-stage breast cancer (Stage I-II). The key is achieving clear margins. WLE alone without RT is NOT adequate - radiotherapy reduces local recurrence from ~30% to <10%. Therefore, the combination of WLE + RT is the standard of care for early breast cancer when feasible. Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is the standard of care for axillary staging in clinically node-negative patients, avoiding routine axillary clearance.
highYield: WLE + RT = mastectomy in terms of survival for early breast cancer. RT is mandatory with breast conservation. SLNB for node-negative patients.
pyqNote: Equivalence of breast conservation + RT vs mastectomy is a landmark concept repeatedly tested in NEET PG.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 55-year-old woman undergoes wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy for a T2 invasive breast cancer. The sentinel lymph node (SLN) shows micrometastasis (0.2-2 mm). She is planned for whole-breast radiotherapy. According to ACOSOG Z0011 trial criteria, what is the appropriate management of the axilla?
options: Axillary lymph node dissection (ALND) of all levels
No further axillary surgery; complete planned whole-breast RT
Axillary radiation therapy as alternative to ALND
Repeat sentinel node biopsy to confirm micrometastasis
explanation: The ACOSOG Z0011 trial established that in patients with T1-2 breast cancer undergoing breast-conserving surgery with whole-breast radiotherapy who have 1-2 positive SLNs, completion ALND does NOT improve overall survival or disease-free survival compared to observation. Therefore, for patients meeting Z0011 criteria (1-2 positive SLN, BCS + whole-breast RT planned, clinically node-negative preoperatively), ALND can be omitted. This avoids lymphedema and arm morbidity. ALND remains indicated if: >2 positive SLN, mastectomy without RT, matted nodes, or gross extracapsular extension.
highYield: Z0011 trial: 1-2 positive SLN + BCS + whole-breast RT → no ALND needed. ALND if >2 positive nodes or mastectomy without RT.
pyqNote: ACOSOG Z0011 trial and its implications for axillary management are increasingly tested in NEET PG surgical oncology.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 42-year-old premenopausal woman undergoes surgery for ER+/PR+/HER2- Stage II breast cancer. She receives adjuvant chemotherapy (AC-T regimen). What endocrine therapy should she receive, and for how long?
options: Tamoxifen for 2 years then aromatase inhibitor for 3 years
No endocrine therapy needed since she received chemotherapy
explanation: For premenopausal women with hormone receptor-positive (ER+/PR+) breast cancer, the standard endocrine therapy is tamoxifen (a selective estrogen receptor modulator, SERM). Current ASCO guidelines recommend 5-10 years of tamoxifen for premenopausal women (extended adjuvant therapy for 10 years reduces recurrence further per ATLAS and aTTom trials). Aromatase inhibitors (anastrozole, letrozole, exemestane) are NOT effective in premenopausal women unless combined with ovarian suppression, because they only block peripheral estrogen synthesis but not ovarian estrogen production. Tamoxifen remains the standard for premenopausal ER+ breast cancer.
highYield: Premenopausal ER+ breast cancer → Tamoxifen 5-10 years. Postmenopausal ER+ → Aromatase inhibitor 5-10 years. Key distinction.
pyqNote: Tamoxifen for premenopausal vs aromatase inhibitor for postmenopausal ER+ breast cancer is a classic NEET PG differentiator.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 50-year-old postmenopausal woman has HER2-positive (IHC 3+), ER-negative breast cancer. After surgery and chemotherapy, which targeted agent should be included in her adjuvant regimen?
explanation: Trastuzumab (Herceptin) is a monoclonal antibody targeting HER2 (human epidermal growth factor receptor 2). It is indicated for HER2-positive breast cancer (IHC 3+ or FISH amplified). Adjuvant trastuzumab for 1 year significantly improves disease-free survival and overall survival in HER2+ early breast cancer (HERA trial, NSABP B-31). Key side effect: cardiotoxicity (cardiomyopathy) - requires baseline ECHO/MUGA scan and monitoring. CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) are used for HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer. Tamoxifen/AI are for ER+ disease. Bevacizumab is not a standard adjuvant breast cancer treatment.
highYield: HER2+ breast cancer → Trastuzumab for 1 year. Monitor cardiac function (LVEF). HER2 tested by IHC/FISH on all invasive tumors.
pyqNote: Trastuzumab for HER2+ breast cancer and its cardiotoxicity monitoring are high-yield NEET PG pharmacology-surgery interface questions.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 52-year-old woman presents with a T4b (skin involvement) N2 (fixed axillary nodes) breast cancer (locally advanced). She is ER-/PR-/HER2- (triple negative). What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate modified radical mastectomy followed by chemotherapy
Neoadjuvant chemotherapy first, then surgery, then radiotherapy
explanation: Locally advanced breast cancer (LABC) - T3/T4 or N2/N3 - is managed with neoadjuvant (preoperative) chemotherapy first, typically with an anthracycline-taxane regimen (AC-T). Benefits of neoadjuvant chemotherapy: (1) Downstages the tumor enabling surgery (sometimes converting inoperable to operable or enabling breast conservation), (2) Tests in-vivo chemo sensitivity, (3) Pathological complete response (pCR) is a surrogate endpoint for prognosis, (4) For triple-negative breast cancer, pCR correlates with excellent survival. After neoadjuvant chemo, surgery (mastectomy or BCS if downstaged), then adjuvant RT. This patient is inoperable initially due to T4b disease.
highYield: LABC (T3/T4, N2/N3): Neoadjuvant chemotherapy first → surgery → adjuvant RT. Never primary surgery for LABC.
pyqNote: Neoadjuvant chemotherapy as the initial treatment for locally advanced breast cancer is a frequently tested clinical scenario in NEET PG.

subject: Surgery
stem: Pheochromocytoma is described by the 'Rule of 10s'. Which of the following correctly represents these features?
options: 10% occur in children, 10% are associated with MEN1, 10% cause hypertension, 10% are benign
10% are right-sided, 10% secrete cortisol, 10% occur in females, 10% cause Cushing syndrome
10% are located in the organ of Zuckerkandl, 10% present with weight gain, 10% are ACTH-secreting
explanation: The classic 'Rule of 10s' for pheochromocytoma: 10% are malignant, 10% are bilateral (25-50% in hereditary cases), 10% are extra-adrenal (paragangliomas, most commonly at the organ of Zuckerkandl at aortic bifurcation), 10% are familial (associated with MEN2A, MEN2B, VHL syndrome, NF1, SDH mutations), and 10% are calcified. Extra-adrenal pheochromocytomas are more commonly malignant. Bilateral pheochromocytomas should raise suspicion for hereditary syndromes (MEN2, VHL). Malignancy is defined by metastasis (local invasion alone is not sufficient for diagnosis of malignancy).
highYield: Rule of 10s: 10% bilateral, 10% extra-adrenal, 10% malignant, 10% familial, 10% calcified. Extra-adrenal = more often malignant.
pyqNote: The Rule of 10s for pheochromocytoma is one of the most commonly tested facts in NEET PG surgery and medicine.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 38-year-old man with hypertension, episodic headaches, sweating, and palpitations has elevated urine metanephrines. CT scan shows a 4 cm right adrenal mass. He is scheduled for laparoscopic adrenalectomy in 2 weeks. What is the correct sequence of preoperative pharmacological preparation?
options: Start beta-blocker (propranolol) first for 10-14 days, then add alpha-blocker (phenoxybenzamine)
Start alpha-blocker (phenoxybenzamine) first for 10-14 days, then add beta-blocker if needed for tachycardia
Start both alpha and beta blockers simultaneously on day 1
No preoperative medication needed; control BP intraoperatively with sodium nitroprusside
explanation: The correct preoperative preparation for pheochromocytoma is ALPHA-BLOCKADE FIRST with phenoxybenzamine (non-selective, irreversible alpha-blocker) for 10-14 days before surgery, THEN add beta-blocker (propranolol) if tachycardia occurs. The critical reason: if beta-blockers are given FIRST (or alone), they block beta-2 mediated vasodilation, leaving alpha-1 mediated vasoconstriction unopposed, causing a hypertensive crisis. Only after adequate alpha-blockade can a beta-blocker be safely added. Phenoxybenzamine causes reflex tachycardia (due to decreased BP), requiring beta-blockade after alpha-blockade is established. Doxazosin (selective alpha-1 blocker) is an alternative. High-salt diet and increased fluid intake are also recommended to correct volume depletion.
highYield: PHEO pre-op: Alpha-block FIRST (phenoxybenzamine 10-14 days) → THEN beta-block if tachycardia. Never beta-first = hypertensive crisis (unopposed alpha).
pyqNote: Alpha before beta blockade in pheochromocytoma is one of the most tested pharmacological principles in NEET PG surgery.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 25-year-old woman presents with bilateral pheochromocytoma. She also has hemangioblastomas of the cerebellum and retinal angiomas. Genetic testing reveals a germline mutation. Which syndrome does this represent and which gene is mutated?
options: Von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) disease; VHL tumor suppressor gene mutation
Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1); NF1 tumor suppressor gene mutation
Succinate dehydrogenase (SDH) mutation syndrome; SDHB gene mutation
explanation: Von Hippel-Lindau (VHL) disease is an autosomal dominant condition caused by germline mutations in the VHL tumor suppressor gene (chromosome 3p25). Features include: hemangioblastomas (cerebellum, brainstem, spinal cord, retina), renal cell carcinoma (clear cell type), pheochromocytoma (often bilateral), pancreatic cysts and neuroendocrine tumors, and endolymphatic sac tumors. MEN2A (RET mutation) has MTC + pheo + hyperparathyroidism. NF1 has cafe-au-lait spots, neurofibromas, and pheo. SDH mutations (SDHB especially) are associated with extra-adrenal paragangliomas and higher malignancy risk. The combination of cerebellar hemangioblastoma + retinal angiomas + bilateral pheo = VHL.
highYield: VHL disease: VHL gene mutation → hemangioblastoma (cerebellum/retina) + bilateral pheo + RCC + pancreatic lesions.
pyqNote: VHL syndrome associations and distinguishing it from MEN2A and SDH mutations is a high-yield NEET PG genetics-surgery question.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 45-year-old hypertensive woman presents with paroxysmal episodes of headache, sweating, and palpitations. Blood pressure during an episode is 220/130 mmHg. Which investigation has the highest sensitivity for diagnosing pheochromocytoma?
explanation: Plasma free metanephrines (normetanephrine and metanephrine) have the highest sensitivity (>95%) for detecting pheochromocytoma and are now the recommended first-line biochemical test. Metanephrines are the stable O-methylated metabolites of catecholamines, continuously produced by the tumor independent of episodic release, making them more reliable than episodic catecholamine measurements. 24-hour urine metanephrines also have high sensitivity (~97%). 24-hour urine VMA has lower sensitivity (~65%) and more false positives. Urinary catecholamines have moderate sensitivity. CT/MRI are for tumor localization AFTER biochemical confirmation. MIBG scintigraphy is used for extra-adrenal/metastatic disease.
highYield: Plasma free metanephrines = highest sensitivity for pheo diagnosis (>95%). Biochemical confirmation before imaging. VMA = low sensitivity (historical).
pyqNote: Biochemical diagnosis of pheochromocytoma and the superiority of plasma metanephrines are high-yield NEET PG investigation questions.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 50-year-old man with a large multinodular goitre is scheduled for total thyroidectomy. He denies symptoms of hyperthyroidism. Preoperative TFTs show TSH 0.02 mIU/L, free T4 elevated, free T3 elevated. What is the most appropriate preoperative management?
options: Proceed directly to surgery; thyroid function is irrelevant for surgical planning
Render euthyroid with antithyroid drugs (carbimazole/propylthiouracil) before surgery
Start propranolol only and proceed to surgery within 48 hours
Cancel surgery; hyperthyroid patients can never undergo thyroidectomy
explanation: All patients undergoing thyroid surgery must be rendered euthyroid (normal thyroid function) before surgery to prevent thyroid storm (thyrotoxic crisis), which can be precipitated by anesthesia, surgical manipulation of the thyroid, or perioperative stress. Antithyroid drugs (carbimazole or propylthiouracil) are used for 6-8 weeks. Potassium iodide (Lugol's iodine) for 10 days before surgery reduces vascularity of the gland and decreases intraoperative bleeding. Beta-blockers (propranolol) control symptoms. Thyroid storm (fever >40°C, severe tachycardia, agitation, hypotension) is a life-threatening complication prevented by achieving euthyroid state preoperatively.
highYield: Preop thyroid surgery: MUST be euthyroid. Give antithyroid drugs → euthyroid state → surgery. Lugol's iodine 10 days before to reduce vascularity.
pyqNote: Preoperative euthyroid requirement and thyroid storm prevention are classic NEET PG anesthesia-surgery interface questions.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 60-year-old postmenopausal woman with HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer develops progression on letrozole monotherapy. Which class of targeted agents combined with an aromatase inhibitor has shown significant improvement in progression-free survival in this setting?
explanation: CDK4/6 inhibitors (palbociclib, ribociclib, abemaciclib) in combination with endocrine therapy (letrozole or fulvestrant) have revolutionized treatment of HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer. Multiple trials (PALOMA-2, MONALEESA-2, MONARCH-3) demonstrated doubling of progression-free survival from ~14-15 months to ~24-27 months. CDK4/6 inhibitors block the cell cycle at G1-S transition by inhibiting CDK4/6-cyclin D1 complex, preventing RB phosphorylation and E2F transcription factor release. Key side effects: neutropenia, fatigue, nausea. mTOR inhibitors (everolimus + exemestane: BOLERO-2) are another option. PARP inhibitors are for BRCA1/2-mutated HER2- metastatic breast cancer.
highYield: HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer: CDK4/6 inhibitor (palbociclib/ribociclib/abemaciclib) + AI = standard of care. Doubles PFS.
pyqNote: CDK4/6 inhibitors in HR+/HER2- metastatic breast cancer represent the latest high-yield pharmacology additions to NEET PG oncology.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 35-year-old man undergoes total thyroidectomy for a 3 cm papillary thyroid carcinoma. Intraoperative frozen section of a central neck (level VI) lymph node is positive. What is the standard recommendation regarding lymph node dissection?
options: Lateral neck dissection (levels II-V) only, preserving central neck nodes
Central neck dissection (level VI) at time of thyroidectomy for node-positive central neck
No lymph node dissection; treat with radioiodine alone
Radical neck dissection including all levels I-VI and sternocleidomastoid muscle
explanation: PTC spreads via lymphatics, first to central neck (level VI - pretracheal, paratracheal nodes) and then to lateral neck (levels II-V). ATA 2015 guidelines recommend therapeutic central neck dissection (level VI) when central neck LN involvement is documented preoperatively or intraoperatively. Prophylactic central neck dissection may be considered for T3-T4 tumors even without clinical nodal involvement. Lateral neck dissection (levels II-V, compartment-oriented modified radical neck dissection) is performed for documented lateral neck nodal metastasis. Classical radical neck dissection (sacrificing IJV, SCM, spinal accessory nerve) is no longer standard - modified functional neck dissection preserves these structures.
highYield: PTC nodal spread: central neck (level VI) first → lateral neck (levels II-V). Therapeutic CND for documented central neck involvement at thyroidectomy.
pyqNote: Central vs lateral neck dissection patterns in PTC and indications for prophylactic vs therapeutic dissection are increasingly tested in NEET PG.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 40-year-old woman with biochemically confirmed unilateral adrenal pheochromocytoma (4 cm, right sided) has undergone adequate preoperative alpha-blockade for 14 days. She is now prepared for surgery. What is the preferred surgical approach?
options: Medical management only with phenoxybenzamine lifelong
explanation: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy is now the gold standard surgical approach for pheochromocytoma, provided tumors are <6-8 cm and not locally invasive. Advantages include: less blood loss, shorter hospital stay, faster recovery, less postoperative pain, and equivalent oncological outcomes to open surgery. The retroperitoneoscopic approach may be preferred for smaller tumors as it avoids intraperitoneal organ manipulation. Open surgery is reserved for: tumors >6-8 cm, suspected malignancy with local invasion, previous abdominal surgery making laparoscopy difficult, or failure of laparoscopic approach. Intraoperative hypertensive crises are managed with sodium nitroprusside or phentolamine; hypotension after tumor removal is managed with IV fluids and vasopressors (beta-blockade effect on BP).
highYield: Pheochromocytoma surgery: laparoscopic adrenalectomy (gold standard) after 10-14 days alpha-blockade. Open for >6-8 cm or invasive tumors.
pyqNote: Laparoscopic adrenalectomy as the preferred approach for pheochromocytoma and the role of preoperative alpha-blockade are standard NEET PG questions.

subject: Surgery
stem: A pathology report for invasive ductal carcinoma of the breast describes: tubule formation score 3 (poorly formed), nuclear pleomorphism score 3 (marked), mitotic count score 2 (moderate). What is the Nottingham histological grade?
options: Grade cannot be determined from this information
explanation: The Nottingham grading system (Elston-Ellis modification of Scarff-Bloom-Richardson) grades invasive breast carcinoma based on three components, each scored 1-3: (1) Tubule formation (1=majority, 2=moderate, 3=minimal), (2) Nuclear pleomorphism (1=small uniform, 2=moderate, 3=marked), (3) Mitotic count per 10 HPF (scored based on absolute counts). Total score: 3-5 = Grade 1 (well differentiated, best prognosis), 6-7 = Grade 2 (moderately differentiated), 8-9 = Grade 3 (poorly differentiated, worst prognosis). This patient: 3+3+2 = 8, which is Grade 3 (poorly differentiated). Grade 3 tumors are associated with higher Ki-67, triple-negative phenotype, and worse prognosis.
highYield: Nottingham grade: Tubules + Nuclear pleomorphism + Mitosis, each 1-3. Total 3-5=Grade1, 6-7=Grade2, 8-9=Grade3.
pyqNote: Nottingham grading calculation and the three components are directly tested in NEET PG breast cancer pathology questions.

subject: Surgery
topic: Thyroid Cancer - Medullary Thyroid Carcinoma Calcitonin
stem: A 45-year-old man is found to have a thyroid nodule on ultrasound. Serum calcitonin is markedly elevated at 850 pg/mL. FNAC shows cells positive for calcitonin and CEA. After total thyroidectomy confirms medullary thyroid carcinoma, what is the most appropriate next step for his family members?
options: Annual thyroid ultrasound for all first-degree relatives
Serum calcitonin stimulation test with pentagastrin for all relatives
RET proto-oncogene germline genetic testing for all first-degree relatives
Prophylactic total thyroidectomy for all relatives regardless of test results
explanation: Approximately 25% of MTC cases are hereditary (associated with germline RET mutations: MEN2A, MEN2B, or Familial MTC). All newly diagnosed MTC patients should undergo RET proto-oncogene germline testing. If a pathogenic RET mutation is identified in the index patient, all first-degree relatives should be offered RET genetic testing. Positive family members should undergo prophylactic total thyroidectomy at an age determined by the specific codon mutation (ATA risk category: highest risk - codon 918 in MEN2B = infancy; high risk - codon 634 = age 5; moderate risk - other codons = age 5-10). Calcitonin and CEA are tumor markers used for post-treatment surveillance, not primary family screening.
highYield: MTC diagnosis → RET germline testing for patient → if positive, screen all first-degree relatives → prophylactic thyroidectomy age based on mutation risk.
pyqNote: Family screening protocol after hereditary MTC diagnosis using RET testing is a high-yield clinical management question in NEET PG.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 65-year-old man presents with a 2 cm subareolar breast lump. Core biopsy confirms invasive ductal carcinoma, ER+/PR+/HER2-. He has a family history of breast cancer. Which genetic mutation is most commonly associated with male breast cancer?
explanation: BRCA2 mutation is the most common hereditary predisposition for male breast cancer, accounting for approximately 10-15% of male breast cancers. Unlike female breast cancer where BRCA1 and BRCA2 contribute more equally, BRCA2 has a much stronger association with male breast cancer (lifetime risk ~6-8% with BRCA2, vs ~0.1-0.2% population risk). BRCA1 mutations can also predispose but less commonly. Male breast cancer is treated similarly to female breast cancer: modified radical mastectomy (mastectomy + SLNB/ALND), tamoxifen for ER+ disease (aromatase inhibitors less effective due to residual androgen-to-estrogen conversion). Male breast cancer represents <1% of all breast cancers.
highYield: Male breast cancer: BRCA2 mutation most common genetic risk. Treatment: mastectomy + tamoxifen for ER+. BRCA2 > BRCA1 for male breast cancer risk.
pyqNote: BRCA2 as the predominant genetic risk in male breast cancer, contrasting with female breast cancer genetics, is an emerging NEET PG topic.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 22-year-old male presents with 18 hours of periumbilical pain that has migrated to the right iliac fossa, anorexia, and nausea. On examination: temperature 37.9°C, tenderness at McBurney's point, and rebound tenderness. WBC = 13,500/mm³ with 78% neutrophils. What is his Alvarado (MANTRELS) score and the recommended management?
options: Score 7 — CT abdomen before any decision
explanation: MANTRELS scoring: Migration to RIF (1) + Anorexia (1) + Nausea/Vomiting (1) + Tenderness RIF (2) + Rebound tenderness (1) + Elevated temperature (1) + Leukocytosis (2) + Shift left (1) = 10 possible. This patient scores: Migration(1)+Anorexia(1)+Nausea(1)+Tenderness RIF(2)+Rebound(1)+Temp(1)+Leukocytosis(2)+Shift(1) = 10 but the described findings give 9. Scores 7-10 indicate high probability and patient should proceed to surgery. Score 5-6 = admit for observation; 0-4 = discharge.
highYield: Alvarado score 7-10 = surgery; 5-6 = observe; 0-4 = discharge. Maximum score is 10, not 9.
pyqNote: MANTRELS mnemonic is a classic NEET PG favourite — know the exact weightings (tenderness and leukocytosis score 2 each, rest score 1 each).

subject: Surgery
stem: A patient with suspected appendicitis is found to have pain in the right iliac fossa when the examiner applies pressure to the LEFT iliac fossa. Which sign is this and what is its anatomical basis?
options: Psoas sign — due to irritation of the iliopsoas muscle
Rovsing's sign — due to gaseous distension transmitted across the colon causing peritoneal irritation at the inflamed appendix
Obturator sign — due to proximity of appendix to the obturator internus
Cope's sign — due to hip flexion compressing the inflamed appendix
explanation: Rovsing's sign is positive when pressure applied to the left iliac fossa causes pain in the right iliac fossa. The mechanism is retrograde distension of the colon causing peritoneal irritation at the site of the inflamed appendix. It indicates peritoneal irritation adjacent to the inflamed appendix.
highYield: Rovsing: LIF pressure → RIF pain. Psoas: hip extension → pain (retrocaecal appendix). Obturator: internal hip rotation → pain (pelvic appendix).
pyqNote: Classic exam question — distinguish Rovsing, psoas and obturator signs with their clinical implications about appendix position.

subject: Surgery
stem: A patient with acute appendicitis has pain that is exacerbated by passive extension of the right hip while the patient lies on their left side. Which position of the appendix is most likely responsible?
explanation: The psoas sign (pain on passive extension of the right hip) indicates that the inflamed appendix lies in a retrocaecal position, posterior to the caecum, where it lies over the iliopsoas muscle. Extension of the hip stretches the psoas muscle and causes pain. The obturator sign (pain on internal rotation of the right hip) is positive with a pelvic appendix. Retrocaecal is the most common position (65%).
highYield: Retrocaecal appendix = most common position (65%) = positive psoas sign. Pelvic appendix = positive obturator sign.
pyqNote: Know the frequency: retrocaecal (65%) > pelvic > pre-ileal > post-ileal. Each position has a characteristic clinical sign.

subject: Surgery
stem: McBurney's point, the point of maximum tenderness in appendicitis, is located at:
options: Junction of the medial 1/3 and lateral 2/3 of a line from the umbilicus to the ASIS
Junction of the lateral 1/3 and medial 2/3 of a line from the ASIS to the umbilicus
Midpoint of the right inguinal ligament
Junction of the medial 2/3 and lateral 1/3 of the right costal margin to the ASIS
explanation: McBurney's point is located at the junction of the lateral 1/3 and medial 2/3 of a line drawn from the right anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS) to the umbilicus. This corresponds to the base of the appendix at the confluence of the taeniae coli on the caecum. It is the point of maximum tenderness in acute appendicitis.
highYield: McBurney's point = 1/3 of the way from ASIS to umbilicus (i.e., lateral third from ASIS side).
pyqNote: The direction of the line matters — from ASIS to umbilicus, point is 1/3 from the ASIS. Frequently tested in exact measurement questions.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 30-year-old female presents with uncomplicated acute appendicitis confirmed on CT scan. She has no perforation, no abscess, and no peritonitis. Which of the following statements about her management is MOST accurate?
options: Laparoscopic appendicectomy is the gold standard surgical treatment
Open appendicectomy through Lanz incision is mandatory
Antibiotics alone are not a valid option under any circumstances
CT scan is contraindicated in females of reproductive age
explanation: Laparoscopic appendicectomy is the current gold standard for surgical treatment of acute appendicitis, offering faster recovery, less wound infection, and better cosmesis. However, for uncomplicated appendicitis in selected patients, antibiotic-only treatment (e.g., IV amoxicillin-clavulanate followed by oral) is a recognized alternative with acceptable short-term outcomes, though with higher recurrence rates at 5 years. CT abdomen is the gold standard imaging modality and is not absolutely contraindicated in women of reproductive age.
highYield: Laparoscopic appendicectomy = gold standard surgery. Antibiotic-only = valid for selected uncomplicated cases (informed consent essential).
pyqNote: Recent NEET PG trend: antibiotic-only management for uncomplicated appendicitis is now a tested concept. Know when it applies.

subject: Surgery
stem: Which imaging modality is considered the gold standard for diagnosis of acute appendicitis in adults?
explanation: CT abdomen is the gold standard imaging for diagnosing acute appendicitis in adults, with sensitivity of 94-98% and specificity of 94-97%. It can identify an inflamed non-compressible appendix >6mm in diameter, periappendiceal fat stranding, and complications like perforation or abscess. Ultrasound is the first-line investigation in children and pregnant women (no radiation), but is operator-dependent. MRI is preferred in pregnancy. Plain X-ray may show a faecolith in 10% of cases.
highYield: CT abdomen = gold standard imaging for appendicitis in adults. Ultrasound = first-line in children/pregnancy. MRI = pregnancy if ultrasound equivocal.
pyqNote: Frequently asked: gold standard imaging = CT. Ultrasound is preferred in pregnancy, not CT.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 45-year-old male presents with a 4-day history of right iliac fossa pain, now with fever (39.2°C), diffuse peritonitis, and WBC 22,000/mm³. CT shows perforated appendicitis with diffuse faecal peritonitis. What is the correct sequence of management?
options: Emergency laparoscopic appendicectomy immediately without resuscitation
Resuscitate with IV fluids + broad-spectrum antibiotics, then proceed to surgical intervention
Manage with IV antibiotics alone for 6 weeks, then interval appendicectomy
ERCP to decompress the appendix followed by elective surgery
explanation: Perforated appendicitis with peritonitis requires: (1) Resuscitation with IV fluids (correct fluid and electrolyte balance), (2) Broad-spectrum antibiotics covering gram-negatives and anaerobes (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam or cefuroxime + metronidazole), and (3) Surgical intervention — either laparoscopic or open peritoneal lavage and appendicectomy. The key principle is resuscitate FIRST, then operate. Immediate surgery without resuscitation is dangerous. Antibiotics alone are not adequate for free perforation with faecal peritonitis.
highYield: Perforated appendicitis with peritonitis = Resuscitate + Broad-spectrum antibiotics + Surgery. Never skip resuscitation phase.
pyqNote: Distinguish: appendix mass (palpable mass, subacute) = conservative Ochsner-Sherren regimen. Diffuse peritonitis = surgery after resuscitation.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with lower abdominal pain, urinary symptoms, and a positive obturator sign (pain on passive internal rotation of the right hip with the hip flexed). What position of the appendix and its proximity to which structure explains this sign?
options: Retrocaecal appendix lying over the psoas muscle
Pelvic appendix lying in close proximity to the obturator internus muscle
Pre-ileal appendix compressing the terminal ileum
Post-ileal appendix irritating the posterior peritoneum
explanation: The obturator sign is elicited by passive internal rotation of the right hip with the hip flexed to 90 degrees. It is positive in pelvic appendicitis when the inflamed appendix lies in the pelvis, in close contact with the obturator internus muscle. The rotation stretches and irritates the obturator internus, causing right iliac fossa or hypogastric pain. In women, pelvic appendicitis can mimic ovarian pathology. Urinary symptoms may occur due to irritation of the ureter or bladder.
highYield: Obturator sign = pelvic appendix. Internal rotation of flexed hip = obturator internus stretch = pain in pelvic appendicitis.
pyqNote: Pelvic appendix can mimic gynaecological pathology in females — classic trap in NEET PG clinical vignettes.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 45-year-old male underwent upper GI endoscopy for epigastric pain. Antral biopsies were taken. Which endoscopic test for H. pylori is the most commonly used rapid bedside test that relies on the organism's urease activity?
explanation: The Rapid Urease Test (RUT), also called the CLO test (Campylobacter-Like Organism test), is the most commonly used endoscopic test for H. pylori. A biopsy sample is placed in urea-containing medium; H. pylori's urease splits urea to ammonia, raising pH and turning the indicator from yellow to pink/red (positive within 1-24 hours). Histology with Giemsa/Warthin-Starry silver stain is the gold standard but takes longer. Urea breath test is the best non-invasive test of cure. Serology detects IgG antibody (exposure, not active infection — NOT used to confirm eradication).
highYield: CLO/RUT = most common endoscopic test (rapid urease). Histology = gold standard. UBT = non-invasive test of CURE. Serology = NOT test of cure.
pyqNote: The 'most common endoscopic test' = RUT/CLO. The 'gold standard' = histology. Do not confuse the two — both are frequently asked.

subject: Surgery
topic: Peptic Ulcer Disease - Test of Cure
stem: A 38-year-old male completed 14-day triple therapy for H. pylori-associated duodenal ulcer. Which test should be used to confirm successful eradication of H. pylori?
options: Stool antigen test or urea breath test (UBT)
Complete blood count for anaemia resolution
explanation: The urea breath test (UBT) and stool antigen test (HpSA) are the preferred tests for confirming H. pylori eradication (test of cure) — done at least 4 weeks after completion of therapy and 2 weeks after stopping PPIs. Serology (IgG) detects antibodies that may persist for months to years after successful eradication and thus CANNOT confirm eradication — it only confirms prior exposure. CLO test requires repeat endoscopy (invasive, not first-line for test of cure). UBT is the most widely recommended non-invasive test of cure.
highYield: Test of cure: UBT (urea breath test) or stool antigen. Serology = CANNOT confirm eradication (antibodies persist after treatment).
pyqNote: Serology is NOT a test of cure — classic NEET PG trap. UBT is the non-invasive gold standard for confirming eradication.

subject: Surgery
stem: The standard first-line triple therapy regimen for H. pylori eradication consists of:
options: H2 blocker + Clarithromycin + Metronidazole for 10 days
PPI + Bismuth + Metronidazole + Tetracycline for 14 days
explanation: Standard first-line triple therapy: PPI (omeprazole/pantoprazole) + Clarithromycin + Amoxicillin for 14 days. The duration of 14 days is superior to 7 days in eradication rates. In penicillin allergy, amoxicillin is replaced by metronidazole. Bismuth-based quadruple therapy (PPI + Bismuth + Metronidazole + Tetracycline) is used as second-line or in areas with high clarithromycin resistance. H2 blockers are less effective than PPIs in creating the alkaline environment needed for antibiotic activity.
highYield: Triple therapy: PPI + Clarithromycin + Amoxicillin x 14 days. Penicillin allergy: replace amoxicillin with metronidazole.
pyqNote: 14 days is now the recommended duration (not 7). Clarithromycin is the key antibiotic in first-line regimen.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 52-year-old male with a history of NSAID use presents with massive upper GI bleed. Endoscopy reveals a posterior wall duodenal ulcer with a visible vessel at the base. Which artery is most likely eroded?
explanation: The posterior wall of the first part of the duodenum is in close anatomical relationship with the gastroduodenal artery (GDA). A posterior wall duodenal ulcer erodes the GDA, causing massive haemorrhage. This is the most feared vascular complication of duodenal ulcer. Management includes endoscopic haemostasis (adrenaline injection + thermal coagulation or clipping) and, if failed, surgical ligation of the GDA (Nissen approach through a longitudinal duodenotomy, with under-running of the vessel). Anterior wall DU causes perforation (free air under diaphragm).
highYield: Posterior wall DU = erodes Gastroduodenal Artery (GDA) = massive bleed. Anterior wall DU = perforation = free air under diaphragm.
pyqNote: Anterior = perforation; Posterior = bleeding (GDA erosion). This distinction is a guaranteed NEET PG surgical anatomy question.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 40-year-old male with known peptic ulcer disease presents with sudden onset severe epigastric pain ('knife-like'), followed by board-like rigidity of the abdomen. Erect chest X-ray shows free gas under the right hemidiaphragm. Which complication has occurred and from which wall does this classically arise?
options: Bleeding from posterior wall duodenal ulcer
Perforation from anterior wall duodenal ulcer
Gastric outlet obstruction from pyloric channel ulcer
explanation: Free gas under the diaphragm (pneumoperitoneum) on erect CXR is pathognomonic of hollow viscus perforation. The most common cause is perforation of an anterior wall duodenal ulcer. Anterior wall DU perforates into the peritoneal cavity, causing pneumoperitoneum and chemical peritonitis from gastric acid leakage. Posterior wall DU bleeds (erodes GDA). Management: resuscitation + NGT + urinary catheter + urgent laparotomy (Graham's omental patch repair) or laparoscopic repair + H.pylori eradication.
highYield: Free air under diaphragm = pneumoperitoneum = perforated anterior wall DU. Posterior wall DU = GDA erosion = bleeding.
pyqNote: Graham's patch repair is the operation for perforated duodenal ulcer. Free air under diaphragm = classic exam X-ray finding.

subject: Surgery
stem: Which of the following is the MOST COMMON complication of peptic ulcer disease?
explanation: Haemorrhage (bleeding) is the most common complication of peptic ulcer disease, accounting for approximately 40% of all complications. It manifests as haematemesis, melaena, or both. Perforation is the second most common (less frequent but more dramatic presentation). Gastric outlet obstruction occurs due to scarring/oedema at the pylorus or duodenum. Malignant transformation is a complication of gastric ulcer ONLY (not duodenal ulcer). Any GU that fails to heal after 6-8 weeks of treatment must be re-endoscoped and biopsied to exclude malignancy.
highYield: Most common complication of PUD = Haemorrhage. Malignancy risk = Gastric ulcer only (DU does NOT undergo malignant change).
pyqNote: DU does NOT undergo malignant transformation — a key differentiating fact. GU must always be biopsied to exclude malignancy.

subject: Surgery
stem: What percentage of duodenal ulcers are associated with H. pylori infection or NSAID use, and where is the most common site of a duodenal ulcer?
options: 95%; anterior wall of the first part of the duodenum
60%; posterior wall of the second part of the duodenum
explanation: Approximately 95% of duodenal ulcers are associated with H. pylori infection or NSAID use (the two major aetiological factors). The most common site of duodenal ulcer is the anterior wall of the first part (D1) of the duodenum. Posterior wall D1 ulcers are less common but cause bleeding from GDA erosion. Gastric ulcers are most common on the lesser curvature at the angulus (junction of body and antrum). DU is 4x more common than GU overall.
highYield: DU = 95% H.pylori/NSAID. Most common site = anterior wall D1. DU:GU ratio = 4:1.
pyqNote: The 95% figure for DU association with H.pylori/NSAID is a direct exam answer. Lesser curvature = GU site (not DU).

subject: Surgery
stem: A 60-year-old male presents with deep jaundice, clay-coloured (acholic) stools, dark urine, and weight loss. Serum bilirubin is 12 mg/dL with predominantly conjugated (direct) fraction elevated. What type of jaundice is this and what causes the clay-coloured stools?
options: Pre-hepatic jaundice; excess unconjugated bilirubin in stool
Obstructive (post-hepatic) jaundice; absence of bile salts reaching the intestine
Hepatocellular jaundice; impaired conjugation of bilirubin
Haemolytic jaundice; overflow of unconjugated bilirubin
explanation: This is obstructive (surgical/post-hepatic/cholestatic) jaundice characterised by: elevated DIRECT (conjugated) bilirubin, clay/pale stools (acholia = absence of stercobilin, formed from bile pigments in gut), dark urine (bilirubinuria — conjugated bilirubin is water-soluble, excreted in urine), steatorrhea (fat malabsorption due to absence of bile salts), and pruritus (bile salt accumulation). The cause is obstruction of biliary outflow preventing bile from reaching the intestine, hence no stercobilin = clay stools and urobilinogen = dark urine.
highYield: Obstructive jaundice = direct hyperbilirubinemia + clay stools (no stercobilin) + dark urine (bilirubinuria) + steatorrhea + pruritus.
pyqNote: The triad: direct bilirubin high + clay stools + dark urine = obstructive jaundice. Required for every surgical jaundice question.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 65-year-old male presents with progressive painless jaundice and significant weight loss. On examination, the gallbladder is palpable (non-tender). Ultrasound confirms a distended gallbladder with dilated intrahepatic bile ducts. What does Courvoisier's law state and what is the most likely diagnosis?
options: A palpable gallbladder in painless jaundice is likely due to gallstones; diagnosis = choledocholithiasis
A palpable gallbladder in painless jaundice is unlikely due to gallstones and suggests malignancy; diagnosis = carcinoma head of pancreas
A palpable gallbladder always indicates empyema; diagnosis = cholecystitis
A palpable gallbladder in jaundice suggests primary biliary cirrhosis; diagnosis = autoimmune hepatitis
explanation: Courvoisier's law states: 'In the presence of painless obstructive jaundice, a palpable non-tender gallbladder is unlikely to be due to gallstones.' This is because gallstones cause chronic inflammation and fibrosis of the gallbladder wall, making it unable to distend. A palpable gallbladder in painless jaundice therefore suggests malignant obstruction — most commonly carcinoma of the head of pancreas compressing the common bile duct. Other causes include cholangiocarcinoma and ampullary carcinoma.
highYield: Courvoisier's law = palpable GB + painless jaundice = NOT gallstones = MALIGNANCY (carcinoma head of pancreas most likely).
pyqNote: The word 'painless' is key in the law. Gallstone jaundice is typically PAINFUL (biliary colic). Painless + palpable GB = suspect pancreatic head cancer.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 55-year-old female with obstructive jaundice is found on CT to have a 1.2 cm calculus in the common bile duct (CBD). Which of the following is the most appropriate next investigation/intervention?
options: MRCP — for therapeutic removal of the CBD stone
ERCP — for definitive therapeutic management with sphincterotomy and stone extraction
explanation: ERCP (Endoscopic Retrograde CholangioPancreatography) is the investigation of choice when BOTH diagnosis AND therapy are required for bile duct pathology. For a confirmed CBD stone, ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction is the definitive treatment. MRCP (Magnetic Resonance CholangioPancreatography) is a purely DIAGNOSTIC tool — excellent for imaging bile ducts non-invasively but cannot perform any therapeutic intervention. PTC is used when ERCP fails or is not accessible. The key distinction: ERCP = therapeutic; MRCP = diagnostic.
highYield: ERCP = therapeutic (sphincterotomy, stone extraction, stent placement). MRCP = diagnostic only (non-invasive imaging of biliary tree).
pyqNote: ERCP vs MRCP is a classic one-liner: therapeutic vs diagnostic. Know when each is indicated — tested multiple times in NEET PG.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 68-year-old male with painless progressive jaundice, weight loss of 8 kg, and a new onset of diabetes mellitus. CT abdomen shows a 3 cm mass in the head of pancreas with dilation of both the pancreatic duct and common bile duct. The CA 19-9 is 1200 U/mL. What is the CT finding called and what operation is performed for resectable disease?
options: Double duct sign (dilation of both CBD and main pancreatic duct); Whipple's pancreaticoduodenectomy
explanation: The 'double duct sign' refers to simultaneous dilation of both the common bile duct (CBD) and the main pancreatic duct (Wirsung's duct), seen on imaging when a mass at the head of pancreas obstructs both ducts simultaneously. It is highly suggestive of pancreatic head carcinoma or periampullary cancer. CA 19-9 is the tumour marker for pancreatic cancer (though not specific). Whipple's pancreaticoduodenectomy (removal of pancreatic head, duodenum, distal CBD, gallbladder, and sometimes distal stomach) is the operation for resectable carcinoma of the head of pancreas. Only 15-20% of cases are resectable at presentation.
highYield: Double duct sign = CBD + PD both dilated = head of pancreas cancer. CA 19-9 = tumour marker. Whipple's = operation for resectable disease.
pyqNote: Double duct sign + painless jaundice + CA 19-9 elevation = classic vignette for pancreatic head carcinoma. Whipple's operation details are frequently tested.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 62-year-old male with jaundice is found on MRCP to have a stricture at the confluence of the right and left hepatic ducts, with bilobar intrahepatic duct dilation but a normal CBD and gallbladder. Which tumour is this and how is it classified?
options: Carcinoma head of pancreas; classified by TNM staging only
Klatskin tumour (perihilar cholangiocarcinoma); classified by Bismuth-Corlette classification
Ampullary carcinoma; classified by AJCC staging
Mirizzi syndrome; classified by Csendes classification
explanation: A cholangiocarcinoma at the hepatic duct confluence (hilar cholangiocarcinoma) is called a Klatskin tumour. The Bismuth-Corlette classification describes the extent of ductal involvement: Type I = below confluence; Type II = at confluence without extension into intrahepatic ducts; Type IIIa/b = extending into right or left intrahepatic ducts; Type IV = extending into both right and left intrahepatic duct systems (unresectable). Features: bilobar IHD dilation + normal CBD + normal GB (contrast with pancreatic head cancer where GB is distended). Treatment is major hepatectomy for resectable disease.
highYield: Klatskin tumour = perihilar cholangiocarcinoma at hepatic confluence. Bismuth-Corlette I-IV. Type IV = bilateral involvement = unresectable.
pyqNote: Bismuth-Corlette classification is a direct NEET PG exam topic. Know Type IV = unresectable. Distinguish from pancreatic head cancer (no palpable GB in Klatskin).

subject: Surgery
stem: In the MANTRELS (Alvarado) scoring system, which two features carry a score of 2 points each (rather than 1 point)?
options: Tenderness in the right iliac fossa and leukocytosis
Rebound tenderness and migration of pain
Nausea/vomiting and left shift of neutrophils
explanation: In the MANTRELS (Alvarado) score: Migration (1) + Anorexia (1) + Nausea/Vomiting (1) + Tenderness in RIF (2) + Rebound tenderness (1) + Elevated temperature (1) + Leukocytosis (2) + Shift to left (1) = Maximum 10 points. Tenderness in RIF and Leukocytosis each score 2 points because they are the most reliable and important clinical and laboratory indicators. The maximum score is 10. This must be memorised precisely as the weighting is directly tested.
highYield: MANTRELS: Tenderness RIF = 2; Leukocytosis = 2; all others = 1. Total maximum = 10.
pyqNote: The exact weightings (which features score 2) are a direct NEET PG recall question. Tenderness + Leukocytosis = 2 each.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 55-year-old male has a gastric ulcer on the lesser curvature confirmed on endoscopy. A biopsy is taken from the ulcer edge and shows intestinal metaplasia. Which statement best describes the unique management concern for gastric ulcers compared to duodenal ulcers?
options: Gastric ulcers never require biopsy as malignancy does not occur
Gastric ulcers must always be biopsied and re-endoscoped at 6-8 weeks to exclude malignancy
Gastric ulcers are always treated surgically without a trial of medical therapy
H. pylori is not associated with gastric ulcers
explanation: Unlike duodenal ulcers, gastric ulcers carry a risk of malignant transformation. Therefore, all gastric ulcers must be: (1) biopsied at diagnosis (multiple biopsies from ulcer edge and base), and (2) re-endoscoped at 6-8 weeks after treatment to confirm healing and exclude malignancy. Failure of a GU to heal after adequate therapy should raise strong suspicion of underlying gastric carcinoma. Duodenal ulcers do NOT undergo malignant transformation and do not require routine re-endoscopy. Most gastric ulcers (60-70%) are H.pylori-associated.
highYield: Gastric ulcer = biopsy mandatory + re-endoscopy at 6-8 weeks to exclude malignancy. DU does NOT undergo malignant transformation.
pyqNote: This is a high-yield surgical principle: GU requires re-endoscopy; DU does not. Malignancy in DU = essentially never.

subject: Surgery
stem: Cope's sign (also called Cope's psoas test) in appendicitis refers to pain produced by which manoeuvre?
options: Pressure on the left iliac fossa producing pain in the right iliac fossa
Passive internal rotation of the right hip in flexion
Active flexion of the right hip against resistance (or passive extension of the hip) with the patient lying on the left side
Deep palpation at McBurney's point during inspiration
explanation: Cope's sign (psoas sign) refers to pain elicited by active flexion of the right hip against resistance or passive extension of the right hip with the patient in the left lateral decubitus position. It stretches the iliopsoas muscle and is positive when the inflamed appendix lies in the retrocaecal position, overlying the psoas muscle. Note: Cope's sign is sometimes used synonymously with the psoas sign. It is distinct from Rovsing's sign (LIF pressure → RIF pain) and the obturator sign (internal rotation of flexed hip).
highYield: Cope's sign = psoas sign = hip extension causing RIF pain = retrocaecal appendix. Positive = appendix lying on psoas.
pyqNote: Cope's sign may be tested as an alternative name for the psoas sign. Know all three main signs: Rovsing, Psoas/Cope, Obturator.

subject: Surgery
stem: Which tumour marker is most commonly associated with carcinoma of the head of the pancreas and is used for monitoring response to treatment and disease recurrence?
explanation: CA 19-9 (Carbohydrate antigen 19-9) is the most widely used tumour marker for pancreatic adenocarcinoma. It is elevated in approximately 70-80% of pancreatic cancers. However, it is not specific (can be elevated in cholangiocarcinoma, colorectal cancer, gastric cancer, and benign conditions like cholangitis and pancreatitis) and not useful for screening. Its main utility is in monitoring treatment response and detecting recurrence. CEA is used for colorectal cancer; AFP for hepatocellular carcinoma and testicular cancer; CA-125 for ovarian cancer.
highYield: CA 19-9 = tumour marker for pancreatic cancer. Not specific enough for screening. Used for monitoring treatment response.
pyqNote: CA 19-9 for pancreatic cancer is a one-liner fact. Note it can be falsely negative in patients who are Lewis antigen-negative (5-10% of population).

subject: Surgery
stem: Which anatomical position of the appendix is the MOST COMMON in the general population?
explanation: The retrocaecal position is the most common position of the appendix, accounting for approximately 65% of cases. In this position, the appendix lies behind the caecum (and may be extraperitoneal). Because of this, peritoneal signs may be less obvious, the psoas sign may be positive, and diagnosis may be delayed. The pelvic position is the second most common (approximately 30%), followed by pre-ileal and post-ileal positions (approximately 5% each). Position affects clinical presentation, signs elicited, and difficulty of surgical access.
highYield: Appendix positions: Retrocaecal 65% (most common) > Pelvic 30% > Pre/Post-ileal 5% each.
pyqNote: The 65% retrocaecal figure is a direct exam answer. The clinical implication is attenuated peritoneal signs and positive psoas sign.

subject: Surgery
topic: Surgical Jaundice - MRCP vs ERCP Indications
stem: A 70-year-old male presents with obstructive jaundice. Ultrasound shows dilated intrahepatic and extrahepatic bile ducts but the lower CBD is poorly visualised. The clinician needs to define the level and cause of obstruction non-invasively before deciding on intervention. Which investigation is the best next step?
options: ERCP immediately — to visualise and intervene
MRCP — non-invasive, no radiation, excellent ductal anatomy without intervention
Endoscopic ultrasound (EUS) as the only option
explanation: MRCP (Magnetic Resonance CholangioPancreatography) is the optimal investigation when non-invasive diagnostic imaging of the biliary tree is required. It provides high-resolution images of the bile ducts, pancreatic duct, and surrounding structures without radiation or contrast injection into the bile duct. It is the investigation of choice before planning therapeutic ERCP or surgery. ERCP is reserved for when therapeutic intervention is anticipated. EUS provides complementary information (especially for ampullary lesions and lymph nodes) but is invasive. HIDA scan assesses functional excretion, not anatomy.
highYield: MRCP = non-invasive diagnostic imaging of biliary tree. ERCP = when therapy needed. MRCP comes first to plan treatment.
pyqNote: The clinical algorithm: US → MRCP (define anatomy) → ERCP (if therapeutic intervention needed). This sequence is commonly tested.

subject: Surgery
topic: Peptic Ulcer Disease - H.pylori Stool Antigen
stem: A 35-year-old female is being evaluated for H. pylori infection but is unable to undergo endoscopy and refuses the urea breath test. She is currently not on PPI therapy. Which NON-INVASIVE test can be used both for initial diagnosis AND confirmation of eradication?
explanation: The H. pylori stool antigen test (HpSA) is a non-invasive test that detects H. pylori antigen in stool using monoclonal antibody-based ELISA. It has high sensitivity and specificity comparable to UBT. Importantly, it can be used for BOTH initial diagnosis AND as a test of cure (done at least 4 weeks after completing therapy and 2 weeks after stopping PPIs). Serology (IgG) detects antibodies — useful for initial diagnosis but CANNOT confirm eradication as antibodies persist for months/years. CLO test requires endoscopy (invasive). HpSA and UBT are the two non-invasive tests recommended for test of cure.
highYield: HpSA (stool antigen) = non-invasive test for both diagnosis AND eradication confirmation. Serology = diagnosis only, NOT test of cure.
pyqNote: Two non-invasive tests of cure: UBT and stool antigen test. Serology does NOT confirm eradication. Must stop PPIs 2 weeks before both tests.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 19-year-old male has an Alvarado score of 5. He has no peritonitis, fever is 37.4°C, and WBC is 11,000/mm³. CT abdomen is equivocal. According to the Alvarado score management algorithm, the most appropriate next step is:
options: Discharge home with oral analgesia and no follow-up
Admit for active observation, serial abdominal examinations, and reassessment
Immediate open appendicectomy via Lanz incision
explanation: An Alvarado score of 5-6 represents intermediate probability for appendicitis. The recommended management is admission for observation with serial abdominal examinations, repeat clinical assessments, and repeat investigations (FBC, imaging if needed). Most patients either declare themselves over the next 12-24 hours (score increases → surgery; or score decreases → discharge). This 'active observation' policy reduces the negative appendicectomy rate (operating on patients who do not have appendicitis). Score 0-4 = low probability → discharge. Score 7-10 = high probability → surgical intervention.
highYield: Alvarado 5-6 = intermediate probability = ADMIT for active observation (not immediate surgery, not discharge). Re-assess in 12-24 hours.
pyqNote: The three management bands (0-4/5-6/7-10) are directly tested. 5-6 = observe, not operate. This prevents unnecessary surgery.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 67-year-old male with carcinoma head of pancreas is being assessed for resectability. CT shows a 2.8 cm mass in the pancreatic head with encasement of the superior mesenteric artery (SMA) and superior mesenteric vein (SMV). What is the resectability status and why?
options: Resectable — Whipple's can be performed with SMA reconstruction
Borderline resectable — trial neoadjuvant chemotherapy then reassess
Locally advanced unresectable — arterial encasement (SMA involvement) precludes curative resection
explanation: Resectability criteria for pancreatic head adenocarcinoma: RESECTABLE = no arterial involvement (SMA, celiac axis, hepatic artery) and no venous involvement or <180 degree SMV/PV contact without distortion. BORDERLINE = >180 degree venous involvement or short-segment arterial abutment without encasement. LOCALLY ADVANCED UNRESECTABLE = encasement (>180 degrees) of SMA or celiac axis, or occlusion/encasement of SMV/portal vein without reconstruction options. This patient has SMA encasement = unresectable. Arterial encasement (especially SMA) is the key contraindication to Whipple's surgery. Only 15-20% of pancreatic cancers are resectable at presentation.
highYield: SMA encasement = unresectable pancreatic cancer. Venous involvement (SMV/PV) may be resectable with vascular reconstruction. Only 15-20% are resectable at diagnosis.
pyqNote: Resectability = no arterial encasement. SMA/celiac encasement = unresectable. Portal vein/SMV involvement may still allow resection with vascular reconstruction.

subject: Surgery
topic: Peptic Ulcer Disease - Gastric Outlet Obstruction
stem: A 50-year-old male with long-standing peptic ulcer disease presents with projectile vomiting of undigested food (without bile), early satiety, and visible peristalsis in the epigastrium. Succussion splash is present 4 hours after eating. What complication has developed and what are the characteristic metabolic derangements?
options: Perforated peptic ulcer; metabolic acidosis with hypernatraemia
Gastric outlet obstruction; hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis
Upper GI haemorrhage; iron deficiency anaemia with metabolic acidosis
Malignant transformation; hyperglycaemia and raised CA 19-9
explanation: Gastric outlet obstruction (GOO) due to PUD presents with vomiting of large volumes of old undigested food WITHOUT BILE (as bile enters distally at the ampulla, proximal obstruction = bile-free vomiting), succussion splash, weight loss, and dehydration. Loss of HCl from vomiting causes: hypochloraemia, hypokalaemia (kidney compensates by excreting K+ to conserve H+/Na+), and metabolic alkalosis (loss of H+ ions). The paradox: urine is acidic despite systemic alkalosis ('paradoxical aciduria') as the kidney prioritises K+/Na+ conservation. Treatment: nasogastric decompression + IV fluid resuscitation (0.9% NaCl with KCl supplementation) + treat underlying cause.
highYield: GOO = bile-free vomiting + succussion splash + hypochloraemic hypokalaemic metabolic alkalosis + paradoxical aciduria.
pyqNote: The metabolic derangement of pyloric stenosis/GOO is a classic NEET PG biochemistry-surgery crossover question. Paradoxical aciduria is a must-know.

subject: Orthopedics
stem: A 28-year-old man falls onto his outstretched hand and sustains a mid-shaft fracture of the humerus. On examination, he is unable to extend his wrist and fingers. Which structure is most commonly injured in this fracture?
explanation: Spiral fractures of the humeral shaft at the junction of the middle and distal thirds injure the radial nerve as it winds around the humerus in the spiral groove (radial groove). The radial nerve supplies all the wrist and finger extensors. Injury produces wrist drop (inability to extend the wrist) and finger drop (inability to extend the MCP joints). The triceps is often spared because its branches arise proximal to the groove.
highYield: Humeral shaft (spiral) fracture → Radial nerve → Wrist drop + finger drop. Triceps reflex is usually preserved.
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG scenario: mid-shaft humerus fracture with wrist drop. Must distinguish from PIN injury (no wrist drop) and AIN injury (no motor weakness visible at wrist).

subject: Orthopedics
stem: A 35-year-old man sustains a fracture of the surgical neck of humerus in a road traffic accident. Which nerve is at highest risk of injury in this fracture?
explanation: The axillary nerve (C5, C6) winds around the surgical neck of the humerus within the quadrilateral space. A fracture at the surgical neck puts this nerve at greatest risk. Injury results in paralysis of the deltoid (inability to abduct the arm beyond 15°) and loss of sensation over the 'regimental badge' area — the lateral aspect of the upper arm over the deltoid.
highYield: Surgical neck of humerus → Axillary nerve → Deltoid wasting + sensory loss over regimental badge area.
pyqNote: Frequently tested pairing. Also tested in context of anterior shoulder dislocation — same nerve is at risk.

subject: Orthopedics
stem: A 10-year-old child falls on an outstretched hand and sustains a supracondylar fracture of the humerus. He develops severe forearm pain that worsens with passive finger extension. The pain is disproportionate to the injury. What is the most feared immediate complication?
options: Volkmann's ischaemic contracture due to compartment syndrome
explanation: Supracondylar fractures in children are the most common cause of compartment syndrome of the forearm (Volkmann's ischaemic contracture). Pain out of proportion to injury, especially worsened by passive stretch of the fingers, is the earliest and most sensitive sign of compartment syndrome. Immediate fasciotomy is required if compartment pressure exceeds 30 mmHg or is within 30 mmHg of diastolic pressure. Delayed treatment leads to Volkmann's ischaemic contracture with a fixed flexion deformity.
highYield: Supracondylar fracture → most common cause of compartment syndrome in children → pain on passive stretch is earliest sign.
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG: 6 P's of compartment syndrome — Pain (out of proportion, passive stretch worst), Pallor, Paresthesia, Paralysis, Pulselessness, Poikilothermia. Emergency = fasciotomy.

subject: Orthopedics
stem: A patient sustains a fracture around the elbow. On examination, he cannot flex the tip of his thumb (flexor pollicis longus) or the distal phalanx of the index finger (FDP to index), but wrist flexion is intact and there is no sensory loss. Which nerve is injured?
explanation: The anterior interosseous nerve (AIN) is a pure motor branch of the median nerve arising just below the elbow. It supplies FPL, the lateral half of FDP (index and middle fingers), and pronator quadratus. AIN palsy produces inability to flex the IP joint of the thumb (FPL) and the DIP of the index finger (FDP), giving a characteristic 'OK sign' failure — the patient makes a pinch with extended rather than flexed distal joints. There is NO sensory loss because AIN carries no cutaneous fibres.
highYield: AIN injury: FPL + FDP (index/middle) + pronator quadratus — no sensory deficit. Test: 'OK sign' (patient cannot make a circle with thumb and index).
pyqNote: Distinguishing AIN from full median nerve injury: AIN = no sensory loss, no thenar wasting. Full median at wrist = LOAF wasting + sensory loss palmar/thumb area.

subject: Orthopedics
stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with tingling and numbness over the lateral three and a half fingers, thenar wasting, and weakness of thumb abduction. Tinel's sign is positive at the wrist. Which muscles constitute the LOAF group affected in this condition?
options: Lumbricals 1&2, Opponens pollicis, Abductor pollicis brevis, Flexor pollicis brevis
Lumbricals 3&4, Opponens pollicis, Adductor pollicis, Flexor pollicis longus
Lumbricals 1&2, Opponens pollicis, Adductor pollicis, Flexor pollicis brevis
Lumbricals 1&2, Opponens digiti minimi, Abductor pollicis brevis, Flexor pollicis brevis
explanation: This is carpal tunnel syndrome (median nerve compression at the wrist). The LOAF muscles are the intrinsic muscles of the hand supplied by the median nerve recurrent branch at the wrist: Lumbricals (lateral 1st and 2nd), Opponens pollicis, Abductor pollicis brevis, and Flexor pollicis brevis. Thenar wasting occurs with chronic compression. Positive Tinel's (tingling on percussion over carpal tunnel) and Phalen's test (wrist flexion reproduces symptoms) are classic signs.
highYield: LOAF = Lumbricals (1,2) + Opponens pollicis + Abductor pollicis brevis + Flexor pollicis brevis — all median nerve (recurrent branch) at wrist.
pyqNote: NEET PG frequently tests: LOAF muscles, which muscles are thenar vs hypothenar, Tinel's vs Phalen's. Adductor pollicis is ulnar nerve — trick option.

subject: Orthopedics
stem: A 30-year-old man is brought after a fall from a motorcycle. He sustained a fracture of the medial epicondyle.
