Slit skin smear shows a high bacillary index. Skin biopsy shows a clear subepidermal zone (Grenz zone) with abundant foamy macrophages (Virchow cells) loaded with AFB. This patient belongs to which end of the Ridley-Jopling spectrum?
explanation: Lepromatous leprosy (LL) represents the anergic pole of the spectrum with absent cell-mediated immunity against M. leprae, leading to uncontrolled bacillary multiplication. Classic features: numerous symmetrically distributed lesions (macules, plaques, nodules), madarosis (loss of eyebrows and eyelashes - outer third first), leonine facies (diffuse facial infiltration), saddle nose deformity, nerve damage late and symmetrical. Histology shows Grenz zone (clear band of collagen beneath epidermis) and Virchow cells (foamy macrophages packed with AFB = globi). High bacterial index (BI 4-6+). TT pole shows few lesions, granulomas, no bacilli, strong CMI.
highYield: LL pole: anergy + diffuse symmetric lesions + Virchow cells/globi + Grenz zone + high BI + madarosis
pyqNote: Tested as histopathology description -> identify leprosy type

subject: Dermatology
stem: A 35-year-old man being treated for lepromatous leprosy develops sudden onset of fever (38.8 degrees C), malaise, and multiple painful erythematous nodules appearing on his limbs and face. Laboratory tests show elevated ESR and CRP. Iritis is also noted on slit lamp examination. What is the most appropriate treatment for this lepra reaction?
options: Increase dose of clofazimine and add dapsone
Continue MDT and add thalidomide or prednisolone
explanation: This is Type 2 lepra reaction (Erythema Nodosum Leprosum, ENL), an immune complex-mediated reaction occurring in BL/LL leprosy. It presents with systemic features (fever, malaise) and new painful erythematous nodules. CRITICAL RULE: MDT must NEVER be stopped during a lepra reaction, as discontinuing treatment allows bacillary multiplication. Treatment of ENL: Thalidomide is the gold standard (especially for recurrent/chronic ENL in males) due to its anti-TNF-alpha properties; Prednisolone for acute episodes or where thalidomide is contraindicated (e.g., women of childbearing age - severe teratogen). Clofazimine in high doses has anti-inflammatory effects and can be used for chronic ENL.
highYield: ENL = Type 2 = immune complex = continue MDT + add thalidomide/prednisolone; never stop MDT
pyqNote: Critical PYQ: 'reaction in leprosy patient -> stop or continue MDT?' -> always continue

subject: Dermatology
stem: A 68-year-old man presents with tense bullae on an urticarial base on the trunk and thighs. His oral mucosa is uninvolved. DIF of perilesional skin shows linear IgG and C3 at the basement membrane zone. Which of the following statements regarding this condition compared to pemphigus vulgaris is CORRECT?
options: It has a worse prognosis than pemphigus vulgaris
Oral mucosal involvement is almost universal
The blister forms below the epidermis at the dermoepidermal junction
Antibodies target desmoglein 3 on keratinocyte surfaces
explanation: Bullous pemphigoid (BP) features a subepidermal split - the blister forms between the epidermis and dermis at the level of the lamina lucida within the basement membrane zone. Antibodies target BP180 (type XVII collagen, BPAG2) and BP230 (BPAG1). Key differentiating features from PV: (1) BP has subepidermal split vs PV suprabasal intraepidermal split; (2) BP rarely involves mucosa vs PV almost universally involves oral mucosa; (3) BP has a milder prognosis than PV; (4) BP affects elderly vs PV affects middle-aged. Anti-desmoglein 3 targets are specific to pemphigus vulgaris.
highYield: BP: subepidermal + tense + elderly + no mucosa + anti-BP180/BP230 + milder prognosis; PV: suprabasal + flaccid + mucosa + anti-Dsg3
pyqNote: Integrated comparison question combining clinical, histology, DIF, and prognosis

subject: Dermatology
stem: A 25-year-old man with a strong family history presents with well-demarcated erythematous silvery scaly plaques on his scalp, elbows, and knees since age 20. His younger sibling also has psoriasis. Which HLA antigen is most strongly associated with early-onset (Type I) psoriasis?
explanation: HLA-Cw6 (now termed HLA-C*06:02) is the most strongly associated HLA allele with psoriasis, particularly early-onset Type I psoriasis (onset before 40 years, family history positive). It confers an approximately 10-fold increased risk. HLA-B27 is associated with ankylosing spondylitis and psoriatic arthritis (axial type). HLA-DR4 is associated with rheumatoid arthritis. Psoriasis Type I (HLA-Cw6 positive) = young onset, family history, guttate flares common, more severe. Psoriasis Type II = older onset, no family history, HLA-Cw6 negative.
highYield: Psoriasis = HLA-Cw6; Psoriatic arthritis (axial) = HLA-B27; RA = HLA-DR4
pyqNote: Single-line PYQ: 'HLA associated with psoriasis = HLA-Cw6'

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 24-year-old man presents with the belief that his thoughts are being removed from his mind by an external force, that others can hear his thoughts, and that a supernatural being is controlling his hand movements. According to Schneider's first rank symptoms, which of the following best describes the symptom where the patient believes others can hear his thoughts?
explanation: Schneider's first rank symptoms (FRS) are specific to schizophrenia (though not pathognomonic). Thought broadcasting is the belief that one's thoughts are being transmitted or broadcast so others can hear/know them - 'others can hear my thoughts.' Thought insertion = foreign thoughts being placed into one's mind by an external agency. Thought withdrawal = thoughts being removed/stolen from one's mind. Made impulses = impulses experienced as alien/externally controlled. Made acts = actions controlled by external force. Somatic passivity = bodily sensations imposed from outside. The scenario describes all three: withdrawal (thoughts removed), broadcasting (others hear thoughts), and made acts (hand movements controlled).
highYield: FRS: Thought broadcasting = others hear thoughts; Insertion = foreign thoughts placed in; Withdrawal = thoughts removed
pyqNote: FRS matching questions appear almost every NEET PG cycle

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 26-year-old woman with schizophrenia is brought to the clinic. She sits motionlessly, speaks in brief one-word answers, shows no facial expression, has lost interest in hobbies, and rarely leaves her room. She denies hallucinations or delusions. Which category of schizophrenia symptoms does this presentation represent, and what is the prognosis associated with predominance of these symptoms?
explanation: Negative symptoms of schizophrenia reflect a diminution or absence of normal functions. The classic 5 A's of negative symptoms: Alogia (poverty of speech), Avolition (lack of motivation), Anhedonia (inability to experience pleasure), Affect (blunted/flat affect), and Attention (impaired). Negative symptoms correlate with: (1) poor premorbid function, (2) predominant D1 receptor hypoactivity in prefrontal cortex (mesocortical pathway), (3) poor response to typical antipsychotics, (4) worse long-term prognosis. Positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, disorganization) reflect dopamine excess in mesolimbic pathway and generally respond better to antipsychotic treatment.
highYield: Negative symptoms (5 A's): Alogia, Avolition, Anhedonia, Affect, Attention = poor prognosis
pyqNote: PYQ pattern: distinguish positive vs negative symptoms from clinical vignette + state prognosis

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 35-year-old man with treatment-resistant schizophrenia is started on clozapine after failing two adequate trials of antipsychotics. Two months later, he develops a severe throat infection and is found to have an absolute neutrophil count of 400/uL. Which of the following best describes this complication and the required monitoring protocol?
options: Thrombocytopenia; platelet count weekly for 6 months
Agranulocytosis; CBC weekly for 18 weeks then monthly
Aplastic anemia; bone marrow biopsy monthly
explanation: Clozapine causes agranulocytosis (ANC <500/uL) in approximately 0.5-1% of patients, which can be fatal if untreated. This is the primary reason clozapine requires mandatory CBC monitoring: weekly for the first 18 weeks (highest risk period), then every 2 weeks for 1 year, then monthly thereafter. ANC thresholds: ANC 1000-1500 = increase monitoring; ANC 500-999 = interrupt clozapine, daily CBC; ANC <500 = permanently discontinue clozapine. Clozapine is the only antipsychotic with proven efficacy in treatment-resistant schizophrenia (TRS = failure of 2 antipsychotics at adequate dose/duration). It also reduces suicide risk and is used for suicidal schizophrenia.
highYield: Clozapine: agranulocytosis (0.5-1%) = CBC weekly x18 weeks then monthly; only proven drug for TRS
pyqNote: Extremely high-yield: clozapine side effects + monitoring schedule tested repeatedly

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 28-year-old man with schizophrenia, started on haloperidol 3 days ago, is brought to the emergency department with high fever (40.2 degrees C), severe generalized muscle rigidity (lead-pipe), altered consciousness, diaphoresis, and tachycardia. Laboratory tests show CPK 15,000 IU/L. Which of the following is the most appropriate immediate management?
options: Add another antipsychotic and cool the patient
Increase haloperidol dose and give IV fluids
Stop haloperidol, give dantrolene sodium and bromocriptine
Give clonazepam and continue current antipsychotic
explanation: Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS) is a life-threatening emergency caused by dopamine receptor blockade by antipsychotics. Classic tetrad: (1) Hyperthermia, (2) Severe muscular rigidity (lead-pipe), (3) Autonomic instability (tachycardia, labile BP, diaphoresis), (4) Altered consciousness. Marked CK elevation (due to rhabdomyolysis). Mortality ~10%. IMMEDIATE management: (1) STOP the offending antipsychotic immediately; (2) Supportive care (IV fluids, cooling); (3) Dantrolene sodium - reduces muscle rigidity by blocking SR calcium release; (4) Bromocriptine (dopamine agonist) - counteracts D2 receptor blockade; (5) Monitor for renal failure (myoglobinuria from rhabdomyolysis). Differentiate from Serotonin Syndrome: NMS has lead-pipe rigidity vs SS has hyperreflexia+clonus.
highYield: NMS: stop antipsychotic + dantrolene + bromocriptine; tetrad = hyperthermia + rigidity + autonomic + AMS + elevated CK
pyqNote: NMS management is a must-know emergency topic in NEET PG psychiatry

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 38-year-old woman presents with persistent low mood for the past 6 weeks, inability to enjoy activities she previously loved, sleeping 12 hours a day, significant weight gain, difficulty concentrating at work, feelings of worthlessness, fatigue, and passive thoughts of wishing she were dead. She has no prior psychiatric history. What is the minimum number of SIG-E-CAPS criteria (including core mood/anhedonia criterion) required to diagnose Major Depressive Disorder?
options: 5 symptoms including depressed mood or anhedonia for >= 2 weeks
explanation: DSM-5 diagnostic criteria for Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) require >= 5 of the 9 symptoms from the SIG-E-CAPS mnemonic, present for >= 2 weeks, with at least one being either (1) depressed mood or (2) loss of interest/anhedonia. SIG-E-CAPS: Sleep (increased/decreased), Interest (anhedonia), Guilt (worthlessness), Energy (fatigue), Concentration (impaired), Appetite/weight change, Psychomotor changes (retardation/agitation), Suicidal ideation. The symptoms must represent a change from baseline, cause clinically significant distress/impairment, and not be due to substance/medical condition. This patient meets >5 criteria for >2 weeks = MDD.
highYield: MDD: >= 5 SIG-E-CAPS symptoms, >= 2 weeks duration, must include depressed mood OR anhedonia
pyqNote: Foundational NEET PG question: MDD diagnostic criteria number and duration

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 45-year-old woman is diagnosed with moderate MDD. She is started on an SSRI and initially responds but then has a suboptimal response at 8 weeks. Her psychiatrist adds lithium to augment the antidepressant effect. Which of the following is a recognized lithium augmentation strategy in MDD, and what is the monitoring required for lithium?
options: Lithium augments by blocking serotonin reuptake; monitor CBC monthly
Lithium augments via serotonin sensitization; monitor serum levels, TFT, RFT
Lithium augments by inhibiting norepinephrine release; monitor liver enzymes
Lithium has no role in SSRI augmentation; switch to SNRI instead
explanation: Lithium augmentation of antidepressants (especially in treatment-resistant depression) works via sensitization of postsynaptic serotonin receptors, enhancing serotonergic neurotransmission. It is a well-established augmentation strategy with strong evidence. Monitoring requirements for lithium include: (1) Serum lithium levels (therapeutic 0.6-1.2 mEq/L for maintenance; check 5-7 days after dose change and 12 hours post-last dose); (2) Thyroid function tests (TFT - lithium causes hypothyroidism); (3) Renal function tests (RFT - lithium is renally cleared, can cause nephrogenic DI and chronic nephropathy); (4) ECG (T-wave changes); (5) Weight. Signs of toxicity: tremor, ataxia, confusion, seizures (>1.5 mEq/L = danger).
highYield: Lithium augmentation in TRD + monitoring: levels + TFT + RFT; mechanism = serotonin sensitization
pyqNote: Combined topic: lithium augmentation in depression + lithium monitoring requirements

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 52-year-old woman with severe MDD has failed two adequate antidepressant trials. She has developed nihilistic delusions (believing she has no internal organs) and refuses to eat or drink. She is medically compromised and suicidal. Which of the following treatments offers the most rapid and effective clinical response?
explanation: Electroconvulsive therapy (ECT) is the treatment of choice for: (1) Severe/treatment-resistant depression (failed >= 2 antidepressants); (2) Psychotic depression (delusions/hallucinations with depression); (3) Severe suicidality with immediate risk; (4) Catatonia; (5) Depression with food refusal/medical compromise; (6) Pregnancy (when medications carry fetal risk). ECT produces the most rapid antidepressant response (2-4 weeks vs 6-8 weeks for medications), with response rates of 70-90%. TMS (transcranial magnetic stimulation) is used for moderate, non-psychotic, non-suicidal treatment-resistant depression and is slower than ECT. Clozapine is for treatment-resistant schizophrenia.
highYield: ECT indications: severe/refractory MDD, psychotic depression, acute suicidality, catatonia, pregnancy, food refusal
pyqNote: Scenario-based NEET PG: 'psychotic depression + suicidal + failed antidepressants -> fastest treatment = ECT'

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 22-year-old man reports hearing two voices that comment on his actions as he performs them (e.g., 'He is now sitting down,' 'Now he is eating') and voices that argue with each other about him in third person. He denies substance use. These hallucinations are best classified as which type of Schneider's first rank symptom?
options: Third-person auditory hallucinations with running commentary
explanation: Schneider's First Rank Symptoms include specific types of auditory hallucinations that are highly suggestive of schizophrenia: (1) Third-person auditory hallucinations - voices discussing or arguing about the patient in third person ('he/she'); (2) Running commentary - voices giving a continuous commentary on the patient's actions/thoughts. These are distinct from second-person hallucinations (voices speaking directly TO the patient, more common but less specific). Both types described in this vignette are classic FRS. They differ from pseudo-hallucinations (internal, in mind's eye/ear, patient has insight) which are not FRS. Somatic passivity is bodily sensations perceived as externally imposed.
highYield: FRS auditory hallucinations: 3rd person + running commentary = pathognomonic Schneider FRS types
pyqNote: Differentiation of hallucination types and their FRS classification is high-yield

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 55-year-old man presents with severe depression. On mental status examination, he sits slumped, moves extremely slowly, and takes several seconds to answer questions. His speech is slow, monotone, and has increased latency of response. On the other hand, another patient with MDD cannot sit still, wrings hands continuously, and paces the room. These two presentations represent which feature in the SIG-E-CAPS criteria?
options: Psychomotor retardation and psychomotor agitation respectively
Negative symptoms and positive symptoms respectively
explanation: The 'P' in SIG-E-CAPS stands for Psychomotor changes, which encompasses two opposite presentations in MDD: (1) Psychomotor retardation - slowed movement, speech, and thought; slumped posture; long response latency; this is more common and associated with melancholic/endogenous depression and is objectively observable by the clinician; (2) Psychomotor agitation - restlessness, hand wringing, inability to sit still, pacing; associated with anxious or mixed features. Both are observable signs (not just subjective complaints) and both count as the 'P' criterion in MDD diagnosis. Psychomotor changes are among the most objectively verifiable symptoms in depression.
highYield: SIG-E-CAPS 'P' = Psychomotor retardation (slowed) OR agitation (restless); both count as 1 criterion
pyqNote: Distinguishing psychomotor retardation from agitation in depression vignettes

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 30-year-old man with schizophrenia is started on an atypical antipsychotic known to have the highest risk of metabolic syndrome (weight gain, dyslipidemia, hyperglycemia) and also agranulocytosis, but is the most effective agent for treatment-resistant schizophrenia. Which receptor profile best explains the weight gain and metabolic effects of this drug?
options: D2 blockade in the nigrostriatal pathway
explanation: The drug described is clozapine. Metabolic side effects (weight gain, hyperlipidemia, diabetes risk) of clozapine and other atypical antipsychotics are primarily mediated by: (1) H1 (histamine) receptor blockade - stimulates appetite, sedation, promotes weight gain; (2) 5-HT2C receptor antagonism - disinhibits appetite regulation centers, further promoting weight gain and metabolic dysregulation. Clozapine has the highest affinity for H1 and the highest 5-HT2C blockade among antipsychotics, explaining its highest metabolic burden. D2 nigrostriatal blockade causes EPS (Parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia). Muscarinic M1 antagonism causes anticholinergic effects. Clozapine has LOW D2 affinity (explains minimal EPS) but HIGH H1/5-HT2C/muscarinic affinity.
highYield: Clozapine metabolic syndrome: H1 blockade (weight gain) + 5-HT2C antagonism; low D2 = minimal EPS
pyqNote: Receptor pharmacology explaining atypical antipsychotic side effects is increasingly tested

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 27-year-old woman states that she feels electricity entering her body through her chest, causing her heart to beat irregularly, and that this is being done to her by a machine in a distant city. She is distressed and feels helpless about this experience. Which Schneider first rank symptom does this best represent?
explanation: Somatic passivity is a Schneider First Rank Symptom in which bodily sensations, movements, or feelings are experienced by the patient as being imposed on them by an external agency. The patient feels that something external is controlling or producing physical sensations in their body. This is distinct from: Thought insertion (foreign thoughts inserted into mind), Thought withdrawal (thoughts removed), Made impulses (urges to do things feel alien/externally imposed), Made acts (actions themselves feel controlled), and Thought broadcasting (thoughts transmitting outward). The key feature of somatic passivity is that it involves BODILY/SOMATIC experiences that are perceived as alien/externally imposed.
highYield: Somatic passivity = bodily sensations/feelings perceived as externally imposed by alien force (FRS)
pyqNote: FRS matching from clinical vignette: somatic passivity vs made acts vs thought-related FRS

subject: Psychiatry
topic: Depression - SSRI Mechanism and First Line
stem: A 40-year-old woman is diagnosed with her first episode of moderate MDD without psychotic features. She has no medical comorbidities and is not pregnant. Which of the following represents the recommended first-line pharmacological treatment according to current guidelines?
options: Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors (e.g., sertraline, escitalopram)
explanation: SSRIs (Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitors) are the first-line pharmacological treatment for MDD due to their favorable efficacy-to-side-effect ratio. They inhibit the serotonin transporter (SERT), increasing synaptic serotonin availability. Commonly used SSRIs: sertraline, escitalopram (most selective), fluoxetine (longest half-life - safest in poor compliance), paroxetine (most anticholinergic, most discontinuation syndrome). TCAs are effective but have narrow therapeutic index (fatal in overdose - cardiac arrhythmia, QTc prolongation), anticholinergic burden, and orthostatic hypotension - used as third-line. MAOIs are used for atypical depression or TRD but have dietary tyramine restrictions (hypertensive crisis risk). Lithium monotherapy is used for bipolar disorder, not unipolar MDD.
highYield: SSRIs = first-line for MDD; escitalopram = most selective; fluoxetine = longest half-life
pyqNote: Bread-and-butter NEET PG: first-line antidepressant for uncomplicated MDD

subject: Psychiatry
topic: Schizophrenia vs NMS vs Serotonin Syndrome
stem: A 32-year-old man on haloperidol and sertraline is brought to the ER with fever (39.5 degrees C), agitation, tremor, myoclonus, hyperreflexia, clonus (ankle), and diarrhea. His CK is mildly elevated. He was recently started on tramadol for back pain. Which of the following best distinguishes this presentation from Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome?
options: Fever and autonomic instability are absent in serotonin syndrome
Hyperreflexia, clonus, and myoclonus suggest serotonin syndrome rather than NMS
NMS typically presents with rapid onset within hours while serotonin syndrome develops over weeks
Elevated CK is only seen in NMS, not serotonin syndrome
explanation: Serotonin Syndrome (SS) and NMS share hyperthermia, autonomic instability, and altered mental status, but differ critically in the TYPE of motor findings: (1) Serotonin Syndrome: hyperreflexia, clonus (especially ankle), myoclonus, tremor - neuromuscular hyperexcitability; onset rapid (hours); triggered by serotonergic drugs (SSRI + tramadol = serotonin excess via SSRI+mu opioid serotonin release). (2) NMS: lead-pipe rigidity (uniform, severe), bradyreflexia or normal reflexes, no clonus - dopamine blockade. Treatment differs: SS = cyproheptadine (5-HT2A antagonist) + benzodiazepines + stop serotonergic drug; NMS = stop antipsychotic + dantrolene + bromocriptine. Here, tramadol (serotonin releasing + reuptake inhibiting) + SSRI (sertraline) = SS.
highYield: SS vs NMS: clonus/hyperreflexia/myoclonus = SS; lead-pipe rigidity/bradyreflexia = NMS
pyqNote: High-stakes NEET PG differentiator: serotonin syndrome vs NMS in polypharmacy patient

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 44-year-old man with moderate MDD has failed two SSRI trials but refuses ECT due to fear of memory loss. He has no metallic implants. His psychiatrist recommends transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS). Which cortical region is the primary target for repetitive TMS (rTMS) in treatment-resistant depression?
options: Right dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (low frequency rTMS)
Left dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (high frequency rTMS)
explanation: Transcranial Magnetic Stimulation (TMS) uses magnetic fields to induce electrical currents in targeted cortical regions. For treatment-resistant depression: the standard target is the LEFT dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (L-DLPFC) using HIGH-FREQUENCY rTMS (10 Hz), which has an EXCITATORY effect on this region - the DLPFC is hypoactive in depression, so excitatory stimulation increases activity and improves mood. Alternatively, LOW-frequency rTMS (1 Hz) applied to the RIGHT DLPFC (which is hyperactive in depression) is also used (inhibitory effect). L-DLPFC HF-rTMS is FDA-approved and most commonly used. TMS is non-invasive, no anesthesia, no cognitive side effects (unlike ECT), and preferred in non-severe TRD.
highYield: rTMS: Left DLPFC + high frequency (excitatory) = standard for TRD; no anesthesia, no memory loss
pyqNote: Emerging NEET PG topic: TMS target and mechanism in treatment-resistant depression

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 32-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after a house fire with burns covering 40% of his body surface area. The burns occurred 36 hours ago. The anaesthesiologist plans rapid sequence induction for emergency debridement. Which of the following is the most appropriate muscle relaxant to use?
explanation: Succinylcholine is CONTRAINDICATED in burns older than 24 hours due to upregulation of extrajunctional acetylcholine receptors, which causes massive potassium efflux on depolarization, potentially leading to fatal hyperkalemia. Rocuronium at 1.2 mg/kg (high dose) provides conditions comparable to succinylcholine for RSI within 60 seconds and is the correct choice here. Its effects can be reversed with sugammadex if needed.
highYield: Burns >24 hours is a classic contraindication for succinylcholine due to hyperkalemia risk from extrajunctional ACh receptor upregulation.
pyqNote: This scenario (burn + timing) is a recurring NEET PG and USMLE-style question testing succinylcholine contraindications.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A patient is given succinylcholine during induction of anaesthesia. The anaesthesiologist observes brief involuntary muscle contractions throughout the body before complete relaxation occurs. What is the correct sequence of events following succinylcholine administration?
options: Fasciculations → flaccid paralysis (Phase I depolarizing block)
explanation: Succinylcholine is a depolarizing neuromuscular blocker. It mimics acetylcholine, causing persistent depolarization at the motor end plate. This initially produces fasciculations (due to asynchronous muscle fiber depolarization) followed by flaccid paralysis (Phase I block) as the end plate remains persistently depolarized and unable to repolarize for subsequent action potentials.
highYield: The hallmark sequence of succinylcholine: fasciculations → flaccid paralysis. This is a Phase I (depolarizing) block.
pyqNote: A fundamental pharmacology question frequently tested in NEET PG and PGI exams.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 45-year-old woman undergoing elective cholecystectomy receives succinylcholine for intubation. After 45 minutes, she remains apneic and unresponsive to nerve stimulator stimulation despite apparently adequate reversal attempts. Serum pseudocholinesterase activity is found to be markedly reduced. Which investigation will BEST characterize the enzyme variant responsible?
explanation: Prolonged apnea after succinylcholine suggests pseudocholinesterase deficiency. The dibucaine number test determines the percentage inhibition of pseudocholinesterase by dibucaine (a local anaesthetic). Normal enzyme: dibucaine number 80 (80% inhibition). Heterozygous variant: dibucaine number ~60. Homozygous atypical variant: dibucaine number ~20. A low dibucaine number confirms the presence of atypical pseudocholinesterase that cannot hydrolyze succinylcholine effectively, leading to prolonged paralysis.
highYield: Dibucaine number test is the gold standard for diagnosing pseudocholinesterase deficiency. Normal = 80, atypical homozygous = 20.
pyqNote: Dibucaine number is a high-yield NEET PG topic frequently paired with succinylcholine prolonged apnea scenarios.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 68-year-old man with end-stage renal disease on dialysis requires emergency laparotomy. The anaesthesiologist needs to choose a non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agent that can be safely used without dose adjustment. Which agent is MOST appropriate?
explanation: Atracurium undergoes Hofmann elimination - a spontaneous, non-enzymatic degradation that occurs at physiological pH and temperature in plasma, independent of hepatic or renal function. This makes it the ideal choice in patients with renal failure, hepatic failure, or combined organ dysfunction. Pancuronium and vecuronium are renally eliminated. Rocuronium is primarily hepatically eliminated but has some renal excretion. Atracurium's unique organ-independent metabolism ensures predictable duration of action.
highYield: Atracurium = Hofmann elimination = safe in renal AND hepatic failure. This is the most tested pharmacological property of atracurium.
pyqNote: Renal failure + muscle relaxant choice is a classic NEET PG exam question. Atracurium is the consistent correct answer.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: During a prolonged abdominal surgery, a patient received vecuronium for neuromuscular blockade. At the end of surgery, the anaesthesiologist plans to reverse the block. Which combination is MOST appropriate for reversal of vecuronium?
explanation: Neostigmine (anticholinesterase) is used to reverse non-depolarizing neuromuscular blockade. However, neostigmine also inhibits muscarinic acetylcholine receptors indirectly, causing bradycardia, increased secretions, and bronchospasm. An anticholinergic is co-administered to prevent these effects. Glycopyrrolate is preferred over atropine because it has a similar onset and duration to neostigmine, does not cross the blood-brain barrier, causes less tachycardia, and provides smoother hemodynamic profile. The combination neostigmine + glycopyrrolate is the current gold standard.
highYield: Neostigmine + glycopyrrolate is the preferred reversal combination. Glycopyrrolate is chosen over atropine for matched onset/offset kinetics.
pyqNote: Reversal agent combinations are repeatedly tested in NEET PG, particularly why glycopyrrolate is preferred over atropine with neostigmine.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A patient requires neuromuscular blockade with the fastest onset among non-depolarizing agents for modified rapid sequence induction. Which agent should be selected?
explanation: Rocuronium has the fastest onset among all non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agents. At a dose of 0.6 mg/kg, intubating conditions are achieved in approximately 60-90 seconds. At a higher dose of 1.2 mg/kg, onset is even faster (~45-60 seconds), making it suitable for RSI. Its effects can be completely reversed by sugammadex, a selective relaxant binding agent, which distinguishes it from other non-depolarizing agents.
highYield: Rocuronium = fastest onset non-depolarizing NMB. High-dose rocuronium (1.2 mg/kg) = RSI alternative to succinylcholine, reversed by sugammadex.
pyqNote: Onset times of neuromuscular blockers are a standard NEET PG question. Rocuronium fastest ND; succinylcholine fastest overall.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: Which of the following neuromuscular blocking agents undergoes primarily HEPATIC elimination and has an intermediate duration of action?
explanation: Vecuronium is a non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocker with primarily hepatic elimination (biliary excretion). It has an intermediate duration of action (25-40 minutes). Unlike atracurium (Hofmann elimination) or pancuronium (renal elimination), vecuronium depends on hepatic metabolism and biliary excretion, so its duration may be prolonged in patients with hepatic disease. Succinylcholine is metabolized by pseudocholinesterase in plasma.
highYield: Vecuronium = hepatic elimination. Atracurium = Hofmann. Pancuronium = renal. This elimination profile table is extremely high yield.
pyqNote: Elimination pathways of muscle relaxants are standard theory in NEET PG pharmacology and anaesthesia papers.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 22-year-old male is undergoing appendicectomy under general anaesthesia with sevoflurane and succinylcholine. Thirty minutes into surgery, the capnograph shows a sudden rise in end-tidal CO2 to 78 mmHg. His temperature is 39.8°C, HR is 148/min, and the surgeon notes that the muscles are extremely rigid. ABG reveals pH 7.18, K+ 6.8 mEq/L. Urine appears dark brown. What is the FIRST and MOST critical step in management?
options: Discontinue triggering agents and switch to total intravenous anaesthesia
Administer sodium bicarbonate for acidosis correction
explanation: In malignant hyperthermia (MH), the FIRST and most critical step is to STOP all triggering agents (volatile halogenated anaesthetics and succinylcholine) immediately and switch to a non-triggering anaesthetic technique (TIVA with propofol/opioids). Dantrolene is the specific treatment but discontinuing triggers is the immediate priority. Dantrolene (2.5 mg/kg IV, repeated every 5-10 minutes up to 10 mg/kg) blocks calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum via RYR1. Cooling, bicarbonate, and treating hyperkalemia are important but secondary measures.
highYield: MH management sequence: STOP triggers FIRST → dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV (may repeat up to 10 mg/kg) → cooling → correct acidosis/hyperkalemia. Never delay trigger removal.
pyqNote: MH management order is a classic NEET PG question. Stopping the trigger vs giving dantrolene - trigger removal is step 1.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A patient who survived a malignant hyperthermia crisis undergoes genetic counselling. Testing confirms an RYR1 mutation. His family members wish to know their susceptibility before elective surgery. Which investigation is the GOLD STANDARD diagnostic test for malignant hyperthermia susceptibility?
explanation: The Caffeine-Halothane Contracture Test (CHCT) is the gold standard for diagnosing malignant hyperthermia susceptibility. Fresh skeletal muscle biopsy samples are exposed to caffeine and halothane; MH-susceptible muscles contract abnormally at lower concentrations. While RYR1 genetic testing is important, not all mutations have been characterized, and CHCT detects functional susceptibility regardless of the specific mutation. Serum CK may be elevated in susceptible individuals at baseline but is not diagnostic.
highYield: CHCT (Caffeine-Halothane Contracture Test) = gold standard for MH susceptibility testing. Performed on fresh muscle biopsy.
pyqNote: Diagnosis of MH susceptibility is tested in NEET PG. CHCT vs genetic testing distinction is important.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: Which of the following agents is SAFE to use in a patient with known malignant hyperthermia susceptibility?
explanation: Propofol is a SAFE (non-triggering) agent in MH-susceptible patients. The triggers for MH are: all volatile halogenated inhalation anaesthetics (halothane, sevoflurane, desflurane, isoflurane, enflurane) and succinylcholine (the only depolarizing NMB that is a trigger). Safe agents include: propofol, ketamine, etomidate, barbiturates (thiopentone), nitrous oxide, all non-depolarizing NMBs, benzodiazepines, and opioids. Total intravenous anaesthesia (TIVA) with propofol is the preferred approach for MH-susceptible patients.
highYield: MH triggers: ALL volatile halogenated agents + succinylcholine. Safe: propofol, N2O, non-depolarizing NMBs, opioids, ketamine.
pyqNote: Identifying safe vs triggering agents in MH is a standard NEET PG question tested almost every year.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: During pre-anaesthetic assessment, a patient opens her mouth and only the soft palate is visible; the uvula, fauces, and pillars are completely obscured. Which Mallampati class does this represent, and what does it predict?
options: Mallampati Class I - easy intubation predicted
Mallampati Class II - moderate difficulty predicted
Mallampati Class III - difficult intubation likely
Mallampati Class IV - only hard palate visible, very difficult
explanation: Mallampati Classification: Class I = soft palate, uvula, fauces, pillars all visible (easy intubation). Class II = soft palate, uvula, fauces visible but not pillars. Class III = only soft palate and base of uvula visible (difficult intubation likely). Class IV = only hard palate visible (very difficult intubation). In this scenario, only the soft palate is visible with the uvula/fauces/pillars obscured = Class III. Classes III and IV predict difficult direct laryngoscopy and intubation.
highYield: Mallampati III = only soft palate base visible. Mallampati IV = only hard palate visible. Classes III/IV predict difficult intubation.
pyqNote: Mallampati classification is consistently tested in NEET PG. Know what is visible in each class precisely.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: The LEMON law is used to predict difficult intubation. A patient has a mouth opening of 2 finger breadths, a thyromental distance of 5 cm, a Mallampati Class III score, and has a large goitre causing tracheal deviation. Which components of LEMON are abnormal in this patient?
options: Mallampati, Obstruction, and Neck mobility only
All five components of LEMON are abnormal
explanation: LEMON law: L=Look externally (facial trauma, beard, obesity, receding mandible). E=Evaluate 3-3-2 rule: mouth opening ≥3 finger breadths (this patient has only 2 = ABNORMAL), hyomental distance ≥3 fingers, thyromental distance ≥2 finger breadths (5 cm = borderline). M=Mallampati (Class III = ABNORMAL). O=Obstruction (goitre causing tracheal deviation = ABNORMAL). N=Neck mobility. This patient has abnormal E (mouth opening <3 fingers), M (Class III), and O (goitre). The Evaluate 3-3-2, Mallampati, and Obstruction are all abnormal.
highYield: LEMON: L=Look, E=Evaluate 3-3-2 (mouth 3 fingers / hyomental 3 fingers / thyromental 2 fingers), M=Mallampati, O=Obstruction, N=Neck mobility.
pyqNote: LEMON law components and their thresholds are tested as a complete unit in NEET PG airway management questions.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 28-year-old woman develops a severe postural headache 24 hours after spinal anaesthesia for a caesarean section. The headache is worse on sitting up and relieved by lying flat. The spinal was performed with a 25G Quincke needle. What is the MOST effective treatment for Post-Dural Puncture Headache (PDPH)?
options: Oral paracetamol and bed rest for 48 hours
Epidural blood patch with 15-20 mL autologous blood
Oral caffeine and increased oral hydration
explanation: Epidural blood patch (EBP) is the MOST effective (definitive) treatment for PDPH with a success rate of approximately 90%. Autologous blood (15-20 mL) is injected epidurally at the same or one level below the puncture site, forming a clot that seals the dural hole and restores CSF pressure. Conservative management (bed rest, caffeine, hydration, analgesics) is tried first but EBP is the gold standard treatment. PDPH is more common with larger gauge cutting needles (Quincke) vs pencil-point needles (Whitacre, Sprotte), in young females, and during obstetric anaesthesia.
highYield: Epidural blood patch = gold standard/most effective treatment for PDPH. Success rate ~90%. Conservative measures tried first.
pyqNote: PDPH management including epidural blood patch is consistently tested in NEET PG obstetric anaesthesia questions.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: During spinal anaesthesia for a lower limb surgery, hyperbaric bupivacaine is injected at the L3-L4 interspace. The patient is then placed in the lithotomy position. Suddenly the patient becomes unconscious, apneic, and develops severe hypotension. What is the MOST likely diagnosis and the IMMEDIATE management?
options: Anaphylaxis to bupivacaine; administer epinephrine 0.5 mg IM
Total spinal anaesthesia; secure airway and support circulation immediately
Vasovagal syncope; place patient in Trendelenburg position
Local anaesthetic systemic toxicity; administer lipid emulsion 20%
explanation: The clinical picture of sudden loss of consciousness, apnea, and cardiovascular collapse following spinal anaesthesia represents Total Spinal (high spinal anaesthesia). Hyperbaric solutions in the lithotomy/Trendelenburg position can ascend to cervical/thoracic levels, blocking respiratory muscles (diaphragm at C3-5), and causing cardiovascular collapse. Immediate management: secure the airway (intubation), ventilate with 100% O2, support circulation (IV fluids, vasopressors - ephedrine/phenylephrine), and place in supine position. This is a true anaesthetic emergency.
highYield: Total spinal = sudden LOC + apnea + cardiovascular collapse. Airway management + circulatory support is the immediate priority.
pyqNote: High/total spinal anaesthesia complications are a favourite NEET PG question, especially in obstetric anaesthesia context.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: During establishment of epidural anaesthesia in labour, an anaesthesiologist administers a test dose through the epidural catheter. The test dose consists of 3 mL of 1.5% lignocaine with 1:200,000 epinephrine. Within 45 seconds, the patient's heart rate increases from 78 to 112 bpm. What does this indicate?
options: Intravascular (IV) catheter placement - positive test dose
Normal physiological response to epidural medication
explanation: The epidural test dose (3 mL of 1.5% lignocaine + 1:200,000 epinephrine = 15 mcg epinephrine) tests for two dangerous misplacements: 1) Intravascular injection: epinephrine causes tachycardia (>20 bpm increase within 45-60 seconds) - POSITIVE in this case (HR increased by 34 bpm), indicating IV placement. 2) Intrathecal injection: the lignocaine dose (45 mg) would cause rapid dense motor block and high spinal symptoms. A positive epinephrine response (tachycardia >20 bpm) indicates the catheter is intravascular, requiring immediate repositioning before further doses.
highYield: Epidural test dose: epinephrine 15 mcg - tachycardia >20 bpm = intravascular placement. Lignocaine 45 mg - motor block = intrathecal placement.
pyqNote: Epidural test dose interpretation is a NEET PG standard question. Both components (epinephrine for IV, lignocaine for intrathecal) should be known.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 55-year-old man with fever and productive cough has a chest X-ray showing loss of the right heart border with an area of increased opacity in the right mid-lung zone. The right costophrenic angle and the rest of the lung fields are clear. What is the MOST likely diagnosis based on the silhouette sign?
explanation: The silhouette sign occurs when two structures of similar radiodensity share a border, causing the border to disappear on X-ray. Loss of the RIGHT HEART BORDER = pathology in the RIGHT MIDDLE LOBE (RML), as the RML is in anatomical contact with the right heart border. Loss of left heart border = lingula pathology. Loss of right hemidiaphragm = right lower lobe pathology. Loss of aortic knuckle = left upper lobe/lingula pathology. The normal left heart border, right hemidiaphragm, and clear lung bases confirm RML involvement.
highYield: Silhouette sign: Right heart border lost = RML. Left heart border lost = Lingula. Right hemidiaphragm lost = RLL. Aortic knuckle lost = LUL.
pyqNote: Silhouette sign and lobe localization is one of the most tested radiology concepts in NEET PG and DNB exams.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A chest X-ray of a patient with acute pulmonary oedema shows thin horizontal lines approximately 1-2 cm long at the lung bases bilaterally, perpendicular to and reaching the pleural surface. These lines represent which pathological finding?
options: Kerley B lines indicating lymphatic congestion
Kerley A lines indicating deep lymphatic distension
Interlobular septal lines due to pneumothorax
explanation: Kerley B lines are short (1-2 cm), thin, horizontal lines at the lung periphery, perpendicular to and touching the pleural surface, best seen at the bases. They represent thickening of the interlobular septa due to lymphatic engorgement from elevated pulmonary capillary pressure (as in heart failure/pulmonary oedema) or lymphatic infiltration (lymphangitis carcinomatosa). Kerley A lines are longer, oblique lines extending from hila. Kerley B lines are the most clinically significant and commonly tested. Air bronchograms indicate airspace disease (consolidation), not interstitial pathology.
highYield: Kerley B lines = short horizontal peripheral lines at bases = lymphatic congestion = heart failure, lymphangitis carcinomatosa.
pyqNote: Kerley B lines are a standard NEET PG radiology question always appearing in the pulmonary oedema/CCF context.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 25-year-old man presents with sudden onset severe right-sided chest pain and dyspnoea after a diving accident. Chest X-ray shows absence of lung markings in the right hemithorax with a visible visceral pleural line and the trachea shifted to the LEFT. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
options: Massive right pleural effusion with ipsilateral shift
Right-sided tension pneumothorax with contralateral tracheal shift
Simple right pneumothorax without mediastinal shift
Right middle lobe collapse with volume loss
explanation: Tension pneumothorax is characterized by: absent lung markings + visible visceral pleural line (pneumothorax) + CONTRALATERAL mediastinal/tracheal shift (away from the side of pneumothorax). In simple pneumothorax, there is no mediastinal shift. In pleural effusion, the opacity is present and trachea shifts AWAY from the effusion (contralateral) in massive effusion, but the X-ray shows increased opacity, not absent markings. Tension pneumothorax is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate needle decompression (2nd intercostal space, midclavicular line) before formal chest drain.
highYield: Tension pneumothorax: absent lung markings + visceral pleural line + trachea shifts AWAY (contralateral). Immediate needle decompression needed.
pyqNote: Distinguishing simple vs tension pneumothorax on CXR is a high-yield NEET PG clinical radiology question.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A chest X-ray of a 70-year-old with chronic heart failure shows: bilateral basal opacities with a concave upper border (meniscus sign) and blunting of both costophrenic angles. On the left side, the entire hemithorax is opacified and the trachea is deviated to the RIGHT. What explains the tracheal deviation in this case?
options: Massive left pleural effusion pushing mediastinum to the right
Right-sided lung collapse causing traction to the right
Right upper lobe collapse with volume loss
explanation: In massive pleural effusion (causing complete hemithorax opacification), the large volume of fluid pushes the mediastinum and trachea to the CONTRALATERAL (opposite) side - in this case, to the right. This is an important distinguishing feature: collapse causes ipsilateral tracheal shift (towards the affected side), while massive effusion causes contralateral shift. The meniscus sign and costophrenic angle blunting confirm bilateral effusions with the left being massive.
highYield: Massive pleural effusion: trachea/mediastinum shifts AWAY (contralateral). Collapse: trachea shifts TOWARDS (ipsilateral). Critical distinction.
pyqNote: Tracheal deviation direction in effusion vs collapse is one of the most commonly tested CXR concepts in NEET PG.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 45-year-old immunocompromised patient has a high-resolution CT (HRCT) of the chest showing bilateral diffuse ground-glass opacities with superimposed interlobular septal thickening, creating a 'crazy paving' pattern. Which condition is MOST characteristically associated with this CT pattern?
explanation: Crazy paving pattern on HRCT = ground-glass opacities (GGO) + superimposed interlobular septal thickening, creating a pattern resembling irregularly shaped paving stones. The CLASSIC association is Pulmonary Alveolar Proteinosis (PAP), where lipid-proteinaceous material fills the alveoli. However, it can also be seen in COVID-19, Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, lipoid pneumonia, mucinous adenocarcinoma, and ARDS. Honeycombing = UIP/IPF. Tree-in-bud = endobronchial spread (TB/MAC/bronchopneumonia). GGO alone without septal lines = COVID/hypersensitivity pneumonitis.
highYield: Crazy paving = GGO + septal lines. Classic association = Pulmonary Alveolar Proteinosis (PAP). Also seen in COVID-19.
pyqNote: Crazy paving pattern and its classic association with PAP is a high-yield NEET PG HRCT question.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 62-year-old chronic smoker with progressive exertional dyspnoea has HRCT showing bilateral, subpleural, basal predominant honeycombing with peripheral traction bronchiectasis and minimal ground-glass opacity. Which pattern does this represent and what is the associated diagnosis?
options: Nonspecific Interstitial Pneumonia (NSIP) - associated with connective tissue disease
Usual Interstitial Pneumonia (UIP) pattern - associated with Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF)
Organizing Pneumonia (OP) - associated with post-infectious fibrosis
Desquamative Interstitial Pneumonia (DIP) - associated with smoking
explanation: The UIP (Usual Interstitial Pneumonia) pattern on HRCT is characterized by: bilateral, subpleural, basal-predominant distribution; honeycombing (clustered cystic airspaces 3-10mm); traction bronchiectasis; and minimal GGO. This is the radiological hallmark of Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF). A definitive UIP pattern on HRCT in the appropriate clinical context does not require surgical lung biopsy for diagnosis. NSIP shows more GGO than honeycombing and is symmetric. OP shows consolidation. DIP shows diffuse GGO bilaterally.
highYield: UIP pattern = subpleural + basal + honeycombing + traction bronchiectasis = Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF). Gold standard HRCT diagnosis.
pyqNote: UIP vs NSIP CT patterns and their clinical associations are frequently tested in NEET PG pulmonology/radiology questions.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 35-year-old HIV-positive patient has CT showing small centrilobular nodules with branching linear densities creating a 'tree-in-bud' pattern in the bilateral upper lobes. What is the MOST likely pathological process?
explanation: Tree-in-bud pattern represents filling of the centrilobular bronchioles and surrounding alveoli with material (mucus, pus, or fluid), with the terminal bronchiole as the 'trunk' and the filled alveolar ducts as 'buds'. It indicates endobronchial spread of disease. Classic causes: Tuberculosis (caseating granulomas spreading via airways), MAC (Mycobacterium avium complex), bacterial bronchopneumonia, and aspiration. In an HIV-positive patient with upper lobe predominance, TB endobronchial spread is the most likely etiology.
highYield: Tree-in-bud = endobronchial spread. TB most classic cause. Also: MAC, bacterial pneumonia, aspiration. NOT haematogenous spread (that = miliary/nodular).
pyqNote: Tree-in-bud pattern and its association with TB endobronchial spread is a standard NEET PG HRCT question.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 55-year-old woman post-total knee replacement presents with acute onset dyspnoea and pleuritic chest pain. CTPA (CT Pulmonary Angiography) shows a large filling defect straddling the bifurcation of the main pulmonary artery extending into both right and left pulmonary arteries. What is this finding called and what is the management implication?
options: Hampton's hump - wedge-shaped opacity; anticoagulation with LMWH
Saddle embolus at the pulmonary artery bifurcation; consider systemic thrombolysis or embolectomy
Westermark sign - oligemia; anticoagulation alone is sufficient
Fleischner sign - enlarged pulmonary artery; reassure and discharge
explanation: A saddle embolus is a large embolus straddling the main pulmonary artery bifurcation, occluding both main pulmonary arteries. It is a massive PE and a medical emergency associated with haemodynamic instability, right heart failure, and high mortality. Management: if haemodynamically unstable, systemic thrombolysis (alteplase 100 mg IV over 2 hours) or surgical embolectomy. If contraindications to thrombolysis, catheter-directed thrombolysis or ECMO. Hampton's hump = peripheral wedge-shaped consolidation (pulmonary infarction). Westermark sign = focal oligemia/decreased vascular markings distal to embolus on CXR.
highYield: Saddle embolus = filling defect at main PA bifurcation on CTPA = massive PE = haemodynamic instability = thrombolysis/embolectomy.
pyqNote: CTPA findings in PE including saddle embolus, Hampton's hump, and Westermark sign are classic NEET PG radiology questions.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 60-year-old hypertensive man presents with sudden tearing chest pain radiating to the back. CT aortography shows an intimal flap with true and false lumen extending from the aortic root, involving the ascending aorta, aortic arch, and descending thoracic aorta. According to DeBakey classification, what type is this dissection and what is the CORRECT management?
options: DeBakey Type II - involves only ascending aorta; medical management with beta-blockers
DeBakey Type I - involves ascending aorta + arch + descending; URGENT SURGICAL repair
DeBakey Type III - involves only descending aorta; medical management
Stanford Type B - does not involve arch; endovascular repair (TEVAR)
explanation: DeBakey Classification: Type I = ascending aorta + arch + descending aorta (entire aorta). Type II = ascending aorta ONLY. Type III = descending aorta only (below left subclavian artery). Stanford Classification: Type A = any dissection involving ascending aorta (DeBakey I + II) = SURGICAL EMERGENCY. Type B = dissection not involving ascending aorta (DeBakey III) = medical management (IV beta-blockers, target BP <120 mmHg). This patient has DeBakey Type I (Stanford Type A) - requires urgent surgical repair to prevent aortic rupture, pericardial tamponade, and coronary artery involvement.
highYield: DeBakey I = ascending + arch + descending = Stanford A = SURGERY. DeBakey III = descending only = Stanford B = MEDICAL management.
pyqNote: DeBakey vs Stanford classification and management (surgery vs medical) of aortic dissection is a key NEET PG vascular surgery/radiology question.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A chest X-ray shows air bronchograms within an area of lobar opacification. Which pathological process is MOST consistent with the presence of air bronchograms?
options: Lobar collapse with complete airway obstruction
Airspace consolidation with patent central airways
explanation: Air bronchograms are visible air-filled bronchi seen as dark tubular branching structures against a background of increased (white) opacity. They occur when alveolar airspaces are filled with fluid or cells (consolidation) while the central airways remain patent and air-filled. They are characteristic of: pneumonia (bacterial, viral), pulmonary oedema (alveolar), ARDS, and pulmonary haemorrhage. Importantly, air bronchograms are NOT seen in simple collapse (atelectasis with proximal airway obstruction, where airways also collapse) or pleural effusion (extrinsic compression). Their presence confirms airspace (alveolar) disease, not pure collapse.
highYield: Air bronchograms = alveolar/airspace disease with patent airways (consolidation). NOT seen in collapse. Distinguishes pneumonia from collapse.
pyqNote: Air bronchogram significance - distinguishing airspace disease from collapse - is a frequently tested CXR concept in NEET PG.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 50-year-old woman presents with exertional dyspnoea. HRCT shows bilateral, patchy ground-glass opacities with a perilymphatic distribution. Her BAL shows foamy macrophages. She is a non-smoker and works in a bird breeding facility. Which condition is MOST likely?
explanation: The combination of: bilateral ground-glass opacities on CT + bird-antigen exposure (avian antigens from bird breeding) + non-smoker + foamy macrophages on BAL is classic for Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (HP), also known as Extrinsic Allergic Alveolitis. Bird-fancier's lung is one of the most common forms. Subacute HP shows bilateral GGO with upper lobe predominance. Chronic HP may progress to fibrosis mimicking UIP/NSIP. PAP shows crazy-paving pattern. IPF shows honeycombing. Sarcoidosis shows perilymphatic nodules and lymphadenopathy.
highYield: GGO + bird/organic antigen exposure + BAL foamy macrophages = Hypersensitivity Pneumonitis (bird fancier's lung). Exposure history is the key clue.
pyqNote: Hypersensitivity pneumonitis with bird/organic antigen exposure and GGO on HRCT is a standard NEET PG clinical radiology scenario.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 40-year-old woman with left-sided pleuritic chest pain has a CXR showing a triangular (wedge-shaped) pleural-based opacity in the left lower zone with its apex pointing towards the hilum. There is also a small left pleural effusion. What is the MOST likely cause?
options: Pulmonary embolism with pulmonary infarction (Hampton's hump)
Left lower lobe pneumonia with reactive effusion
Left lower lobe collapse due to central bronchial obstruction
explanation: Hampton's hump is a wedge-shaped, pleural-based opacity with its apex pointing toward the hilum, typically in the lower lobe. It represents pulmonary infarction following pulmonary embolism (PE), where the occluded vessel leads to haemorrhagic infarction of the lung parenchyma. It occurs in approximately 30-40% of PE cases. The associated small effusion (often haemorrhagic) further supports this diagnosis. The combination of pleuritic chest pain + wedge opacity + effusion in a patient at risk for PE = Hampton's hump until proven otherwise. CT-PA is needed for confirmation.
highYield: Hampton's hump = pleural-based wedge opacity with apex to hilum = pulmonary infarction from PE. Classic CXR sign of PE.
pyqNote: Hampton's hump is a classic NEET PG question that tests knowledge of PE's radiological signs including Westermark sign and pleural effusion.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: On CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA), focal decreased vascularity distal to an occluded vessel, creating a relatively dark area compared to surrounding normal lung with normal pulmonary vascularity, corresponds to which sign seen on plain chest radiograph?
explanation: Westermark sign on plain CXR represents focal oligemia (decreased vascular markings) distal to a pulmonary embolus, caused by abrupt cessation of blood flow beyond the clot. On CT, this appears as a region of decreased attenuation and vascularity distal to the embolus. The Westermark sign is an indirect radiological sign of PE on CXR. Other PE signs on CXR: Hampton's hump (wedge infarction), Fleischner sign (enlarged central PA due to clot burden), Palla sign (enlarged right descending pulmonary artery). CTPA remains the gold standard for PE diagnosis.
highYield: Westermark sign = focal oligemia on CXR distal to PE = decreased perfusion. Know all PE CXR signs: Westermark + Hampton's hump + Fleischner.
pyqNote: PE radiological signs including Westermark sign, Hampton's hump, and Fleischner sign are all tested in NEET PG radiology questions.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: During a spinal anaesthesia procedure, the anaesthesiologist inserts the needle at the L4-L5 interspace in an adult patient. The needle enters the subarachnoid space safely. What is the MOST important anatomical reason this level is chosen over L1-L2?
options: The dura mater is thicker at L4-L5 providing better resistance to the needle
The spinal cord (conus medullaris) ends at approximately L1-L2 in adults, making L4-L5 safe
Hyperbaric bupivacaine spreads more predictably at L4-L5
CSF pressure is highest at L4-L5 facilitating drug spread
explanation: The conus medullaris (the terminal end of the spinal cord) is located at approximately L1-L2 in adults (can be as low as L2 in ~5% of adults). Spinal anaesthesia must be performed BELOW this level to avoid direct spinal cord injury. The L3-L4 or L4-L5 interspaces are the standard sites because: (1) well below conus medullaris, (2) only cauda equina nerve roots present (which can 'float away' from the needle), and (3) easily identified anatomically (Tuffier's line = line joining iliac crests = L4 or L4-L5 disc). In infants, the conus is lower (L2-L3), so L4-L5 must be used.
highYield: Conus medullaris ends at L1-L2 in adults. Spinal at L3-L4 or L4-L5 is safe (below conus). Tuffier's line = L4 level = landmark.
pyqNote: Conus medullaris level and safe spinal anaesthesia insertion site is a standard NEET PG and MD anaesthesia question.

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman attends a government health facility on the 9th of the month for antenatal care. Under the Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan (PMSMA), which of the following services is she entitled to receive FREE OF COST at this visit?
options: Blood grouping, urine culture, and obstetric ultrasound
Complete physical examination, haemoglobin estimation, urine examination, and ultrasound
Only ultrasound and iron-folic acid supplementation
explanation: PMSMA guarantees comprehensive ANC on the 9th of every month including physical examination, abdominal examination, haemoglobin, urine albumin/sugar, blood group, and ultrasound — all free of cost. It targets women at or after 4 months (2nd/3rd trimester). Option A is incomplete; options C and D miss the core clinical examination component.
highYield: PMSMA is held on the 9th of every month; provides comprehensive FREE ANC in 2nd and 3rd trimester including ultrasound.
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Mission Indradhanush was intensified (iMI) to cover children who had missed vaccination. Which of the following vaccines was ADDED to the Universal Immunisation Programme under Mission Indradhanush that was NOT previously in the schedule?
options: Rotavirus vaccine and Inactivated Polio Vaccine (IPV)
explanation: Mission Indradhanush (2014 onwards) aimed to achieve 90% full immunisation coverage. Key NEW additions under this initiative and the expanded UIP include Rotavirus vaccine (5 doses at 6, 10, 14 weeks and 9, 16-24 months initially, later revised) and IPV (introduced 2015). DPT, OPV, BCG, Hepatitis B, and Measles were already in the UIP before Mission Indradhanush.
highYield: Mission Indradhanush target = 90% full immunisation by 2020; new vaccines added include Rotavirus, IPV, PCV, and MR.
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Under Rashtriya Bal Swasthya Karyakram (RBSK), mobile health teams screen children for '4 Ds'. A 6-year-old child attending an Anganwadi is found to have a congenital heart defect. Which component of the 4 Ds does this fall under, and what is the age group targeted by RBSK?
explanation: RBSK targets children from 0 to 18 years of age and screens for 4 Ds: Defects at birth (e.g., congenital heart disease, cleft lip/palate), Deficiencies (anaemia, vitamin A, iodine), Diseases (skin conditions, dental, ear/vision), and Developmental delays. Congenital heart defect = Defect at birth. The age range 0-18 years covers both Anganwadi (0-6) and school-age children (6-18).
highYield: RBSK: 4 Ds = Defects, Deficiencies, Diseases, Developmental delays; targets 0-18 years; mobile health teams screen at Anganwadis and schools.
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A woman delivers at a government district hospital under Janani Shishu Suraksha Karyakram (JSSK). Which of the following benefits is she NOT entitled to under JSSK?
options: Free transport from home to facility and back
Free diet for 3 days after normal delivery and 7 days after caesarean
Cash incentive of Rs. 1400 for institutional delivery
explanation: JSSK (launched 2011) entitles pregnant women to free delivery (normal and C-section), free drugs and diagnostics, free diet (3 days normal, 7 days CS), free blood, and free transport. The cash incentive of Rs. 1400 for institutional delivery is part of JSSY (Janani Suraksha Yojana), NOT JSSK. JSSK is a cashless entitlement scheme, not a cash transfer scheme.
highYield: JSSK = cashless entitlements (free delivery, drugs, diet, transport); JSY = cash incentive (Rs. 1400 rural, Rs. 1000 urban for institutional delivery).
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A health worker at a PHC notices that the Vaccine Vial Monitor (VVM) on a BCG vial shows the inner square is DARKER than the outer circle. What action should the health worker take?
options: Use the vaccine immediately as it is still potent
Discard the vaccine as it has been exposed to excessive heat
Return it to the cold chain for 24 hours and reassess
Shake the vial and use it if the colour reverses
explanation: A VVM shows the vaccine has been heat-exposed when the inner square becomes DARKER than or matches the outer circle — this is Stage 3 (do not use) or Stage 4 (discard). When the inner square is darker than the outer circle, the cold chain has been irreversibly broken and the vaccine must be DISCARDED. The colour change is irreversible and cannot reverse on cooling. VVM monitors cumulative heat exposure using a chemical reaction.
highYield: VVM: discard when inner square = same colour or DARKER than outer circle; colour change is irreversible.
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: At the Primary Health Centre level, vaccines are stored in an Ice Lined Refrigerator (ILR). What is the correct storage temperature range for vaccines in an ILR, and which vaccine should NOT be stored in the freezer compartment of the ILR?
options: +2°C to +8°C; Hepatitis B vaccine should not be frozen
0°C to +4°C; OPV should not be stored here
-15°C to -25°C; BCG should not be frozen
+2°C to +8°C; OPV must always be stored in deep freeze
explanation: ILR maintains +2°C to +8°C for storage of most vaccines. Hepatitis B, DPT, DT, TT, and liquid formulations of Hib MUST NOT be frozen as freezing destroys their potency. OPV requires freezing (-15 to -25°C) and is stored in deep freezers. BCG is freeze-dried and can tolerate freezing. The freeze-sensitive vaccines are remembered by 'Hep B, DPT, TT, DT' — all inactivated adsorbed vaccines.
highYield: ILR = +2°C to +8°C; freeze-sensitive vaccines = Hepatitis B, DPT, TT, DT (freezing destroys aluminium-adsorbed vaccines); OPV needs deep freeze (-20°C).
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The Rotavirus vaccine has been included in India's Universal Immunisation Programme. A mother brings her 10-week-old infant for vaccination. According to the current UIP schedule, which doses of Rotavirus vaccine should the child receive today?
options: First dose at 6 weeks, second dose at 10 weeks — so give the second dose today
First dose at 10 weeks — give the first dose today
Rotavirus vaccine is not given before 14 weeks
Single dose at 9 months along with measles vaccine
explanation: In India's UIP, oral Rotavirus vaccine is given at 6, 10, and 14 weeks of age (3 doses) along with DPT and OPV. A 10-week-old infant would already have received the first dose at 6 weeks and is now due for the SECOND dose. The Rotavirus vaccine currently in UIP is the pentavalent RV5 or monovalent RV1 schedule. It is not given at 9 months.
highYield: Rotavirus vaccine in UIP India: 3 doses at 6, 10, 14 weeks (same schedule as DPT/OPV).
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A child receives MR vaccine at 9 months. According to the revised UIP schedule, when should the SECOND dose of MR vaccine be given, and at what age is Pneumococcal Conjugate Vaccine (PCV) primary series completed?
options: MR 2nd dose at 16-24 months; PCV primary series completed at 14 weeks
MR 2nd dose at 5 years; PCV primary series completed at 6 months
MR 2nd dose at 2 years; PCV given only once at 9 months
MR 2nd dose at 16-24 months; PCV primary series at 14 weeks with booster at 9 months
explanation: In the current UIP schedule: MR vaccine is given at 9-12 months (1st dose) and 16-24 months (2nd dose). PCV is given as primary series at 6 weeks, 10 weeks, and 14 weeks (3 doses), with a booster at 9 months. PCV was introduced in UIP India in select states. The 2nd MR dose at 16-24 months replaced the earlier measles booster schedule.
highYield: MR schedule: 9 months + 16-24 months; PCV: 6, 10, 14 weeks (primary) + 9 months (booster).
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A new screening test for tuberculosis is evaluated. Among 200 confirmed TB cases, the test is positive in 160. Among 300 non-TB controls, the test is negative in 270. Calculate the SENSITIVITY and SPECIFICITY of this test.
explanation: From the 2x2 table: TP = 160, FN = 40 (200-160), TN = 270, FP = 30 (300-270). Sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN) = 160/200 = 80%. Specificity = TN/(TN+FP) = 270/300 = 90%. Option B reverses the values. Sensitivity measures how well the test identifies true positives; specificity measures how well it identifies true negatives.
highYield: Sensitivity = TP/(TP+FN) = true positive rate; Specificity = TN/(TN+FP) = true negative rate.
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Using the same TB screening test data: 200 confirmed TB patients (160 test positive, 40 test negative) and 300 non-TB controls (30 test positive, 270 test negative). What is the Positive Predictive Value (PPV) of this test?
explanation: PPV = TP / (TP + FP) = 160 / (160 + 30) = 160/190 ≈ 84.2%. FP = 30 (non-TB patients who test positive). NPV = TN / (TN + FN) = 270 / (270 + 40) = 270/310 ≈ 87.1%. Option B is sensitivity, option C is specificity, option D is NPV. PPV answers: 'If positive, what is the probability of having disease?'
highYield: PPV = TP/(TP+FP); NPV = TN/(TN+FN); both depend on prevalence unlike sensitivity/specificity.
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A diagnostic test for a disease has a sensitivity of 85% and specificity of 75%. What is the Positive Likelihood Ratio (LR+) of this test?
explanation: LR+ = Sensitivity / (1 - Specificity) = 0.85 / (1 - 0.75) = 0.85 / 0.25 = 3.4. LR- = (1 - Sensitivity) / Specificity = 0.15 / 0.75 = 0.2. Option B is the LR-. A LR+ > 10 rules in disease; LR- < 0.1 rules out disease. LR+ of 3.4 provides moderate evidence for the disease.
highYield: LR+ = Sensitivity / (1-Specificity); LR- = (1-Sensitivity) / Specificity; LR+ >10 rules IN, LR- <0.1 rules OUT.
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: In a clinical trial, a new antihypertensive drug reduces the risk of stroke from 8% in the control group to 4% in the treatment group over 5 years. What is the Number Needed to Treat (NNT) to prevent one stroke?
explanation: Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR) = Risk in control - Risk in treatment = 8% - 4% = 4% = 0.04. NNT = 1/ARR = 1/0.04 = 25. So 25 patients must be treated for 5 years to prevent 1 stroke. Option C gives relative risk reduction (50%), not NNT. Option D (2) would be the NNT if ARR were 50%. NNT is the most clinically useful measure of treatment effect.
highYield: NNT = 1/ARR (Absolute Risk Reduction); lower NNT = more effective treatment; NNT = 1 means every patient benefits.
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: In a cohort study of smokers and non-smokers, the incidence of lung cancer is 250 per 100,000 per year in smokers and 10 per 100,000 per year in non-smokers. What is the Attributable Risk (AR) and Attributable Risk Percent (AR%) for smoking?
explanation: Attributable Risk (AR) = Incidence in exposed - Incidence in unexposed = 250 - 10 = 240 per 100,000 per year. AR% = (AR / Incidence in exposed) × 100 = (240/250) × 100 = 96%. This means 96% of lung cancer in smokers is attributable to smoking. Option B incorrectly adds rates. Option D is incorrect formula application. AR is used to estimate the public health burden of an exposure.
highYield: AR = Incidence(exposed) - Incidence(unexposed); AR% = AR/Incidence(exposed) × 100; measures excess risk due to exposure.
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A researcher wants to study the association between consumption of processed meat and colorectal cancer. Patients already diagnosed with colorectal cancer are identified, and their past dietary history is compared with matched controls. What type of study is this?
explanation: This is a CASE-CONTROL study: cases (colorectal cancer patients) and controls (without cancer) are identified, and PAST exposure (processed meat consumption) is compared retrospectively. Cohort studies follow exposure forward to outcome. Cross-sectional studies measure exposure and outcome simultaneously. RCTs randomly allocate to exposure. Case-control studies are ideal for rare diseases and generate Odds Ratio (OR), not Relative Risk.
highYield: Case-control study: start with outcome (disease), look BACK for exposure; measures ODDS RATIO; best for rare diseases.
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Multiple studies from different countries consistently show that smokers have a relative risk of 15-25 for developing lung cancer compared to non-smokers. Animal studies confirm the carcinogenic effects of tobacco smoke. Which Bradford Hill criterion is BEST demonstrated by the fact that the association is seen across different study designs, populations, and time periods?
explanation: CONSISTENCY refers to the association being replicated across different studies, populations, study designs, and time periods — exactly described here. Specificity means one exposure causes one disease (weak criterion). Coherence means the association is consistent with known biology/natural history. Biological gradient (dose-response) means increasing exposure leads to increasing disease — not what is described. Consistency is one of the strongest Bradford Hill criteria.
highYield: Bradford Hill: CONSISTENCY = same association found across different studies, populations, and time; strongest evidence for causality.
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A study finds that coffee drinking is associated with lung cancer (OR = 2.5). However, when the analysis is stratified by smoking status, the association between coffee and lung cancer disappears in both strata. What does this indicate?
options: Smoking is an effect modifier of the coffee-lung cancer association
Smoking is a confounder of the coffee-lung cancer association
Coffee and smoking have a synergistic interaction
explanation: When stratification removes the association (OR becomes ~1.0 in both strata), the third variable (smoking) is a CONFOUNDER, not an effect modifier. A confounder is associated with both exposure (coffee — smokers drink more coffee) and outcome (lung cancer) and is not on the causal pathway. Effect modification would show DIFFERENT ORs in each stratum (interaction). Confounding can be controlled by randomisation, restriction, matching, or statistical adjustment.
highYield: CONFOUNDER: association disappears on stratification (Mantel-Haenszel); EFFECT MODIFIER: different effects in different strata (interaction).
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A water sample from a municipal supply is tested using the Most Probable Number (MPN) method for coliform bacteria. The result is 4 coliforms per 100 mL. The residual chlorine is 0.1 mg/L. What is the correct interpretation?
options: Water is safe; both coliform count and chlorine are within acceptable limits
Water is unsafe; MPN should be zero and residual chlorine should be at least 0.2 mg/L
Water is safe for drinking but not for cooking
Coliform count is acceptable but chlorine level is adequate
explanation: WHO/Indian standards require: MPN coliform count = 0 per 100 mL for treated piped water supplies. Any coliform detection indicates faecal contamination. Minimum residual chlorine should be 0.2 mg/L (some guidelines 0.5 mg/L at source); 0.1 mg/L is INSUFFICIENT. Both values are abnormal — water is UNSAFE. Coliform count of 4/100 mL is clearly a violation of drinking water standards.
highYield: Safe drinking water: MPN coliforms = 0/100 mL; residual chlorine ≥ 0.2 mg/L; turbidity < 1 NTU (acceptable up to 5 NTU).
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A water sample from a rural hand pump shows turbidity of 8 NTU, MPN coliform count of 2/100 mL, and pH 7.5. Which parameter makes this water UNSAFE for consumption according to IS 10500 standards?
options: Both turbidity (>5 NTU) and coliform count (>0/100 mL)
Only the coliform count exceeds safe limits
Turbidity of 8 NTU is acceptable; only coliform is the concern
explanation: IS 10500 (Indian Drinking Water Standards): Turbidity acceptable limit = 1 NTU (permissible up to 5 NTU); 8 NTU EXCEEDS the permissible limit. Coliform count for treated supply = 0/100 mL; 2/100 mL exceeds this. pH 7.5 is within the acceptable range (6.5-8.5). BOTH turbidity and coliform count make this water unsafe. Option D incorrectly dismisses turbidity.
highYield: IS 10500: turbidity acceptable = 1 NTU, permissible = 5 NTU; coliform = 0/100 mL for treated water; pH acceptable = 6.5-8.5.
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A 5-year-old child presents with abdominal pain, constipation, developmental regression, and anaemia. Blood lead level is 45 mcg/dL. Which of the following is the MOST LIKELY source of lead exposure in an urban Indian household?
options: Contaminated drinking water from lead-free PVC pipes
Lead-based paints on walls and toys, and leaded petrol residue in soil
explanation: In India, major sources of lead poisoning in children include: lead-based paint (especially old paint in peeling condition), contaminated soil (from leaded petrol history), certain ayurvedic/herbal medicines, kohl (kajal), and artisanal crafts. PVC pipes are typically lead-free. Fluoride causes fluorosis, not lead poisoning. Iron deficiency increases lead absorption but is not a source. Blood lead level ≥ 10 mcg/dL requires intervention; ≥ 45 mcg/dL requires chelation therapy.
highYield: Lead poisoning in children: main sources = lead paint, contaminated soil, kohl (kajal), certain ayurvedic medicines; BLL ≥ 45 mcg/dL = chelation therapy.
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: NAAQS (National Ambient Air Quality Standards) of India sets the annual average standard for PM2.5. A city records an annual average PM2.5 of 52 mcg/m³. Which of the following correctly describes the situation?
options: Within limits; NAAQS annual standard for PM2.5 is 60 mcg/m³
Exceeds limits; NAAQS annual standard for PM2.5 is 40 mcg/m³
Within limits; PM2.5 standard is 100 mcg/m³ annually
Exceeds WHO limits only; Indian NAAQS allows 60 mcg/m³
explanation: NAAQS India (revised 2009): Annual average standard for PM2.5 = 40 mcg/m³; 24-hour standard = 60 mcg/m³. Annual average PM2.5 of 52 mcg/m³ EXCEEDS the annual standard of 40 mcg/m³. WHO guideline is even stricter (5 mcg/m³ annual, revised 2021). PM2.5 (fine particles <2.5 micrometres) penetrate deep into lungs and are associated with cardiovascular and respiratory disease.
highYield: NAAQS India PM2.5: annual = 40 mcg/m³, 24-hour = 60 mcg/m³; PM10: annual = 60 mcg/m³, 24-hour = 100 mcg/m³.
pyqNote: PSM — high frequency topic

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
