stem: A patient with known CSOM — unsafe type — develops acute lower motor neurone facial nerve palsy (House-Brackmann Grade IV). Which part of the facial nerve is most commonly affected by cholesteatoma, and what is the immediate management?
options: Second genu (horizontal/tympanic segment) — emergency mastoidectomy and facial nerve decompression
First genu (geniculate ganglion) — conservative management with steroids
explanation: In cholesteatoma, the most commonly eroded segment of the facial nerve is the horizontal (tympanic) segment, particularly at the second genu (where the nerve turns downward to become the mastoid segment). The tympanic segment runs in close proximity to the aditus and is the most vulnerable. Facial palsy in the context of CSOM is an emergency — the dehiscent nerve is directly compressed by the cholesteatoma. Treatment is urgent surgical mastoidectomy with removal of cholesteatoma, decompression, and if needed, nerve repair. Steroids alone are not adequate.
highYield: Cholesteatoma most commonly erodes the tympanic (horizontal) segment of facial nerve at second genu → emergency surgery.
pyqNote: Facial nerve segment most vulnerable in cholesteatoma is a recurring NEET PG question.

subject: ENT
stem: A 40-year-old woman who underwent superficial parotidectomy 6 months ago now complains of flushing, sweating, and warmth over the pre-auricular region whenever she eats. The most likely diagnosis and the nerve responsible are:
options: Frey's syndrome — auriculotemporal nerve (branch of V3)
Chorda tympani syndrome — chorda tympani nerve (branch of VII)
First bite syndrome — superior cervical sympathetic ganglion
Gustatory rhinorrhoea — greater petrosal nerve (branch of VII)
explanation: Frey's syndrome (auriculotemporal syndrome or gustatory sweating) occurs after parotid surgery when regenerating parasympathetic secretomotor fibres (which travelled with the auriculotemporal nerve to the parotid) aberrantly reinnervate the sweat glands of the overlying facial skin. Stimulation during eating causes sweating and flushing instead of salivation. The auriculotemporal nerve is a branch of V3 (mandibular division of trigeminal). Minor's starch-iodine test confirms the diagnosis. Treatment options include topical anticholinergic cream (glycopyrrolate) or botulinum toxin injection.
highYield: Frey's syndrome: post-parotidectomy gustatory sweating = aberrant reinnervation by auriculotemporal nerve. Treat with botulinum toxin.
pyqNote: Frey's syndrome nerve (auriculotemporal) is a classic NEET PG/AIIMS question; distinguish from chorda tympani.

subject: ENT
stem: A 35-year-old man presents with painful swelling under his chin that increases at mealtimes and subsides after eating. On bimanual palpation of the floor of the mouth, a hard nodule is felt along the course of Wharton's duct. X-ray of the floor of the mouth shows a radioopaque calculus. The most likely diagnosis and the most commonly affected gland are:
options: Submandibular duct calculus (Wharton's duct) — submandibular gland most commonly affected
Parotid duct calculus (Stensen's duct) — parotid gland most commonly affected
Sublingual gland calculus — sublingual gland most commonly affected
Minor salivary gland calculus — palatal glands most commonly affected
explanation: Salivary calculi (sialolithiasis) most commonly affect the submandibular gland (80–90% of cases) because: (1) submandibular saliva is more viscous and alkaline with higher calcium and mucin content; (2) Wharton's duct is longer (5 cm), tortuous, and runs uphill against gravity; (3) the duct calibre change at the hilum predisposes to stasis. The calculi are radioopaque (calcium phosphate/carbonate) in 80% of cases, visible on plain X-ray. Parotid calculi are less common and often radiolucent. Mealtime syndrome (pain and swelling with meals) is characteristic.
highYield: Submandibular calculi: most common (80%), radioopaque, Wharton's duct, mealtime syndrome — due to uphill duct and viscous saliva.
pyqNote: Why submandibular is most common is a classic NEET PG MCQ — know the four reasons.

subject: ENT
stem: A 65-year-old male smoker presents with a painless, soft, cystic swelling in the tail of the right parotid gland. It is bilateral in 10% of cases. Tc-99m pertechnetate scan shows a 'hot' spot in the region. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: Warthin's tumour (papillary cystadenoma lymphomatosum) is the second most common benign parotid tumour and is unique in that: (1) it occurs almost exclusively in the parotid (tail region); (2) it is bilateral in 10% of cases; (3) it is strongly associated with smoking; (4) it shows a 'hot' spot on Tc-99m pertechnetate scan (oncocytic cells concentrate technetium); (5) it shows a 'hot' lesion on 99mTc scan — virtually diagnostic. Treatment is superficial parotidectomy. Pleomorphic adenoma is the most common and shows a 'cold' spot on scan.
highYield: Warthin's tumour: parotid tail + bilateral 10% + smoking + hot on Tc-99m scan (only benign salivary tumour to do so).
pyqNote: Hot vs cold isotope scan for parotid tumours is a classic NEET PG differentiator.

subject: ENT
topic: Salivary Gland — Pleomorphic Adenoma and Facial Nerve
stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with a slow-growing, painless, firm, bosselated swelling in the right parotid region. Clinically benign. The surgeon plans surgical excision. Regarding the anatomical relationship of pleomorphic adenoma to the facial nerve in the parotid gland, which statement is most accurate?
options: The facial nerve divides the parotid into superficial and deep lobes; pleomorphic adenoma most commonly arises from the superficial lobe lateral to the nerve
The facial nerve lies medial (deep) to both lobes of the parotid; routine excision does not risk the nerve
Pleomorphic adenoma most commonly arises from the deep lobe, medial to the facial nerve
The facial nerve runs superficial to the parotid gland, making nerve injury rare during parotidectomy
explanation: The facial nerve exits the stylomastoid foramen and divides within the parotid into temporofacial and cervicofacial divisions, forming the pes anserinus. This plane divides the parotid into a superficial lobe (lateral to nerve) and deep lobe (medial to nerve). Pleomorphic adenoma most commonly arises from the superficial lobe (lateral to facial nerve), comprising 80% of cases. Surgical treatment is superficial parotidectomy with facial nerve preservation — the nerve must be identified (at the main trunk or via peripheral branches) and preserved. Enucleation alone has high recurrence rates due to pseudocapsule extensions.
highYield: Parotid: facial nerve divides it into superficial (lateral) and deep (medial) lobes; pleomorphic adenoma = superficial lobe (80%).
pyqNote: Facial nerve anatomy in parotid and its relation to pleomorphic adenoma is a standard NEET PG surgical anatomy question.

subject: ENT
stem: A 45-year-old presents with recurrent episodic vertigo lasting 20 minutes to 4 hours, associated with fluctuating low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness in the left ear. Audiogram shows a low-frequency rising pattern of sensorineural hearing loss. Electrocochleography shows an elevated summating potential to action potential (SP/AP) ratio >0.45. The diagnosis is:
explanation: Meniere's disease is caused by endolymphatic hydrops — distension of the endolymphatic compartment. The classic triad (Meniere's triad) is: episodic vertigo (20 min to 24 hrs) + fluctuating sensorineural hearing loss (initially low-frequency) + tinnitus, with aural fullness as the fourth symptom (Lermoyez variant). Electrocochleography showing elevated SP/AP ratio (>0.40–0.45) is the objective test supporting endolymphatic hydrops. Low-frequency SNHL early (distinguishing it from other SNHLs which are typically high-frequency). BPPV episodes last seconds; vestibular neuritis is a single prolonged attack; acoustic neuroma causes progressive unilateral SNHL.
highYield: Meniere's disease: low-frequency fluctuating SNHL + episodic vertigo (20 min-24 hr) + tinnitus + aural fullness. SP/AP ratio >0.45 on ECoG.
pyqNote: Meniere's triad, low-frequency SNHL, and ECoG findings are standard NEET PG topics.

subject: ENT
stem: A 50-year-old presents with progressive unilateral right-sided sensorineural hearing loss and high-frequency tinnitus for 2 years. Recently developed mild imbalance. Audiogram shows high-frequency downsloping SNHL on the right with very poor speech discrimination disproportionate to the pure-tone average. BAEP (BERA) shows prolonged I-III and I-V interwave latencies on the right. MRI with gadolinium shows an enhancing mass at the right cerebellopontine angle extending into the internal auditory canal. Diagnosis:
explanation: Vestibular schwannoma arises from the vestibular division of CN VIII (usually superior vestibular nerve) within the internal auditory canal. Features: progressive unilateral SNHL with disproportionately poor speech discrimination ('rollover' on PB-max), tinnitus, and late imbalance. BERA shows prolonged interwave latencies (I-III, I-V). MRI with gadolinium (gold standard) shows an enhancing CP angle mass with 'ice-cream cone' appearance extending into IAC. Associated with NF2 when bilateral. Management: observation, stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma knife), or surgery (translabyrinthine, retrosigmoid, or middle fossa approach depending on size and hearing status).
highYield: Acoustic neuroma: unilateral progressive SNHL + poor speech discrimination + prolonged BERA interwave latency + gadolinium MRI = gold standard.
pyqNote: BERA findings and investigation of choice in acoustic neuroma are repeatedly tested.

subject: ENT
stem: A 30-year-old woman with audiogram-confirmed otosclerosis (conductive hearing loss, As tympanogram, Carhart's notch at 2000 Hz) is currently pregnant with her second child. She declines surgery at this time. Which medical treatment can slow the progression of otosclerosis?
explanation: Sodium fluoride is the only medical treatment shown to slow the progression of otosclerosis. It inhibits the abnormal bone remodelling (spongiosis of otic capsule) by incorporating into the hydroxyapatite crystal and stabilising the lesion. Dose: 40–60 mg/day combined with calcium and vitamin D. It is contraindicated in renal failure and pregnancy. Bisphosphonates have also been studied but sodium fluoride remains the first-choice medical option. Definitive treatment remains surgical stapedectomy or stapedotomy. Pregnancy worsens otosclerosis (estrogen effect), hence early surgical counselling is important.
highYield: Sodium fluoride = only medical agent to slow otosclerosis progression; definitive treatment = stapedectomy/stapedotomy.
pyqNote: Medical treatment of otosclerosis (sodium fluoride) is a classic NEET PG question.

subject: ENT
topic: Tympanogram — Ad Type and Ossicular Discontinuity
stem: Audiogram of a patient with a history of head trauma shows a purely conductive hearing loss of 50-60 dB with a normal tympanic membrane on otoscopy. Tympanometry shows a very high-amplitude, sharply peaked compliance curve (Type Ad — high peak exceeding the upper limit of the scale). The most likely cause is:
explanation: Type Ad (deep/high compliance) tympanogram indicates a hyper-compliant middle ear system — the tympanic membrane moves excessively with pressure changes. This occurs in two situations: (1) ossicular chain discontinuity (incus long process or incudo-stapedial joint dislocation, as after trauma) — the TM flaps freely with no resistance; (2) a monomeric or thin, flaccid TM. With a large air-bone gap (50-60 dB) and a normal-appearing TM but Ad tympanogram, ossicular discontinuity is the diagnosis. Otosclerosis gives As (stiff/low compliance). CT temporal bone and exploratory tympanotomy confirm the diagnosis.
highYield: Ad tympanogram (high compliance) + large air-bone gap + normal TM = ossicular chain discontinuity.
pyqNote: Ad tympanogram and its causes is a subtle but tested topic in NEET PG and AIIMS.

subject: ENT
stem: A 45-year-old Southeast Asian male presents with unilateral epistaxis, nasal obstruction, unilateral otitis media with effusion, and a painless neck mass. On nasopharyngoscopy, an ulcerating mass is seen in the fossa of Rosenmuller (pharyngeal recess). Biopsy shows undifferentiated carcinoma. EBV serology is positive (elevated VCA IgA). Which nerve involvement explains the otitis media with effusion in this case?
options: Eustachian tube obstruction by the nasopharyngeal tumour (not a nerve — mechanical blockage)
Facial nerve involvement at the stylomastoid foramen
Greater petrosal nerve compression causing secretory dysfunction
Hypoglossal nerve invasion at the base of skull
explanation: Nasopharyngeal carcinoma (NPC) arises most commonly in the fossa of Rosenmuller and has three classic presentations: epistaxis, nasal obstruction, and cervical lymphadenopathy. Unilateral otitis media with effusion in an adult (especially with cervical nodes) is a red flag for NPC until proven otherwise — the Eustachian tube orifice is obstructed mechanically by the tumour, leading to middle ear effusion and conductive hearing loss. EBV association (elevated VCA IgA) is nearly universal in WHO Type III (undifferentiated). Trotter's triad = unilateral deafness + palatal paralysis + trigeminal neuralgia (from cavernous sinus invasion).
highYield: Adult unilateral OME + epistaxis + cervical node = NPC until proven otherwise. ET tube blockage causes the effusion.
pyqNote: NPC presentation, Trotter's triad, and EBV association are standard NEET PG ENT questions.

subject: ENT
stem: A 15-year-old boy presents with progressive unilateral nasal obstruction and recurrent severe episodes of spontaneous epistaxis. CT scan of the sinuses with contrast shows a highly vascular, intensely enhancing mass in the nasopharynx with extension into the pterygopalatine fossa and widening of the sphenopalatine foramen (Holman-Miller sign). The most appropriate next diagnostic step is:
options: MRI and digital subtraction angiography (DSA) with pre-operative embolisation — biopsy is contraindicated
Diagnostic nasal biopsy under local anaesthesia
Fine needle aspiration cytology of the mass
explanation: Juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma (JNA) is a benign but locally aggressive vascular tumour occurring exclusively in adolescent males. The Holman-Miller sign (anterior bowing of the posterior wall of the maxillary antrum on lateral X-ray or CT) is pathognomonic. BIOPSY IS ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED due to risk of catastrophic haemorrhage from this highly vascular tumour. Diagnosis is clinical + radiological (CT/MRI). Pre-operative embolisation (DSA) 24–48 hours before surgery reduces intra-operative blood loss. Treatment is surgical excision (endoscopic or open approach).
highYield: JNA = adolescent male + epistaxis + nasopharyngeal mass + Holman-Miller sign. Biopsy contraindicated. Pre-op embolisation then surgery.
pyqNote: JNA biopsy contraindication and Holman-Miller sign are high-yield and recurrently tested NEET PG questions.

subject: ENT
stem: A 5-year-old boy is brought for evaluation of inattentiveness at school and poor speech development. Audiogram shows bilateral flat conductive hearing loss of 20-30 dB. Tympanometry shows bilateral Type B (flat) tympanograms with no acoustic reflexes. Otoscopy shows dull, amber-coloured, retracted tympanic membranes. There is no history of ear pain. The most appropriate management after a 3-month observation period is:
options: Bilateral myringotomy with insertion of ventilation tubes (grommets)
explanation: Otitis media with effusion (OME, glue ear) in children is managed with watchful waiting for 3 months, as >50% resolve spontaneously. After 3 months of persistent bilateral OME with hearing loss causing developmental concerns, ventilation tube insertion (grommets) is the standard treatment — it restores hearing immediately. Adenoidectomy may be added in children >4 years with recurrent disease (adenoids contribute to ET dysfunction). Long-term antibiotics have no proven benefit for OME. The Type B tympanogram (flat, no peak) confirms fluid in the middle ear, and the amber colour of the drum confirms the mucoid effusion.
highYield: Bilateral OME with developmental delay after 3 months observation = grommets (ventilation tube insertion). Type B tympanogram confirms.
pyqNote: Management of persistent OME in children is a common NEET PG question — answer is grommets after 3 months.

subject: ENT
stem: A 10-year-old presents with recurrent anterior nasal bleeding from the right nostril, usually triggered by nose picking and dry weather. Examination under nasal light shows engorged blood vessels at the anteroinferior nasal septum. Digital pressure for 10 minutes failed to stop the current episode. The vascular area identified and the first-line treatment after failed compression are:
options: Little's area (Kiesselbach's plexus) — cauterisation with silver nitrate or chemical cauterisation
Woodruff's plexus (posterior septal) — posterior nasal packing
Anterior ethmoidal artery — ligation under endoscopy
Sphenopalatine artery — endoscopic sphenopalatine artery ligation
explanation: Little's area (Kiesselbach's plexus) is the anteroinferior part of the nasal septum where five arteries anastomose: anterior ethmoidal, posterior ethmoidal, sphenopalatine, greater palatine, and superior labial arteries. It is the most common site of epistaxis (90% of cases), especially in children. After failed digital pressure, chemical cauterisation with silver nitrate is the next step (active bleeding must be stopped first). Woodruff's plexus is at the posterior nasal cavity and bleeds in elderly hypertensives (posterior epistaxis requiring packing). Bipolar cautery or electrocautery is preferred over unilateral chemical cautery to avoid septal perforation.
highYield: Little's area (Kiesselbach's plexus) = anteroinferior septum = 90% of epistaxis. Silver nitrate cautery after failed compression.
pyqNote: Kiesselbach's plexus, its arterial supply, and management are standard NEET PG ENT topics.

subject: ENT
stem: Laryngoscopy of a 35-year-old professional singer shows bilateral, symmetrical, small, grey-white, sessile lesions at the junction of the anterior one-third and posterior two-thirds of both vocal cords. The patient's main symptom is hoarseness. The lesions appear at the point of maximum contact and vibration. The diagnosis and initial management are:
options: Vocal cord nodules (Singer's nodes) — voice rest and speech therapy first
Vocal cord polyp — microlaryngoscopy and stripping
Reinke's oedema — smoking cessation and voice therapy
Vocal cord granuloma — antireflux therapy and voice therapy
explanation: Vocal cord nodules (Singer's nodes, Screamers' nodes, Teachers' nodes) are bilateral, symmetrical, appear at the junction of anterior 1/3 and posterior 2/3 of the cords (the point of maximum vibration and contact), and result from vocal abuse. They start as soft/oedematous lesions and become firm/fibrotic. Initial treatment is conservative — voice rest and speech therapy. Surgery is reserved for persistent fibrotic nodules. Vocal cord polyp is typically unilateral, pedunculated, and requires microlaryngoscopy. Reinke's oedema is diffuse bilateral oedema of the superficial lamina propria associated with smoking.
highYield: Vocal cord nodules: bilateral + junction of ant-1/3 and post-2/3 + vocal abuse + treat with voice therapy first.
pyqNote: Singer's nodes vs polyp vs Reinke's oedema is a standard NEET PG laryngology differentiation question.

subject: ENT
stem: A 3-week-old infant presents with inspiratory stridor that is worse in the supine position and during feeding but improves when prone or crying. There is mild subcostal recession but no cyanosis. The stridor has been present since birth. Flexible nasopharyngoscopy shows omega-shaped epiglottis with supraglottic collapse on inspiration. The diagnosis is:
explanation: Laryngomalacia is the most common cause of stridor in neonates and infants (60% of congenital laryngeal anomalies). Features: inspiratory stridor from birth, worsening supine/feeding, improving prone/crying (crying provides positive pressure to maintain airway), omega-shaped floppy epiglottis, and collapse of aryepiglottic folds/arytenoids on inspiration. The stridor worsens until 6 months then gradually resolves by 18–24 months in 90% of cases. Severe cases (cyanosis, failure to thrive, apnoea) require supraglottoplasty. Subglottic stenosis causes biphasic stridor. Bilateral cord palsy causes weak cry with soft biphasic stridor.
highYield: Laryngomalacia: most common neonatal stridor — inspiratory, worse supine, improves prone/crying, floppy epiglottis, self-resolves by 2 years.
pyqNote: Most common cause of neonatal stridor and its characteristics is a standard NEET PG question.

subject: ENT
stem: A CT scan of the neck shows a well-defined mass in the right parapharyngeal space (PPS) that displaces the internal carotid artery posterolaterally and the internal jugular vein anterolaterally. The styloid process is intact. The mass is well-defined and homogeneous. Given this vascular displacement pattern, the most likely anatomical compartment and primary diagnosis are:
options: Pre-styloid compartment — deep lobe parotid tumour (displaces carotid posteriorly)
Post-styloid compartment — glomus jugulare tumour (displaces carotid anteriorly)
Pre-styloid compartment — schwannoma of the vagus nerve
Post-styloid compartment — pleomorphic adenoma of deep parotid
explanation: The parapharyngeal space is divided by the styloid process into pre-styloid (anterior) and post-styloid (posterior) compartments. Pre-styloid compartment contains fat, minor salivary glands, and the deep lobe of the parotid gland. Post-styloid contains the carotid artery, internal jugular vein, CN IX-XII, and sympathetic chain. A pre-styloid mass (deep lobe parotid tumour) displaces the carotid artery posterolaterally and IJV anterolaterally. A post-styloid mass (carotid body tumour, schwannoma, glomus jugulare) splays the carotid artery and IJV apart. In this question, both vessels displaced together = pre-styloid (parotid) mass.
highYield: Pre-styloid PPS mass = displaces carotid posteriorly (together with IJV) = parotid deep lobe. Post-styloid = splays carotid and IJV apart.
pyqNote: Parapharyngeal space compartments and vascular displacement patterns are high-yield for NEET PG and AIIMS MS.

subject: ENT
stem: A patient with suppurative otitis media develops a triad of: (1) persistent purulent otorrhoea, (2) deep-seated facial pain and headache in the distribution of the first and second divisions of the trigeminal nerve, and (3) ipsilateral lateral rectus palsy (diplopia on lateral gaze). The diagnosis and the anatomical basis of the CN VI palsy are:
options: Gradenigo's syndrome — petrous apicitis with involvement of Dorello's canal (CN VI runs through it)
Bezold's abscess — erosion of mastoid tip with tracking to the neck
Lateral sinus thrombosis — abducens nerve compression by raised ICP
Labyrinthitis — CN VI palsy from direct labyrinthine spread
explanation: Gradenigo's syndrome is a complication of otitis media with spread to the petrous apex (petrous apicitis). The classic triad is: (1) otorrhoea, (2) trigeminal neuralgia (first + second division pain, from involvement of Meckel's cave), and (3) ipsilateral abducens (CN VI) palsy causing lateral rectus paresis and diplopia on lateral gaze. The anatomical basis: CN VI runs through Dorello's canal (beneath the petrosphenoidal ligament/Gruber's ligament) at the petrous apex and is compressed/involved by the apical petrositis. Treatment: IV antibiotics and surgical drainage (petrous apicectomy).
highYield: Gradenigo's triad: otorrhoea + trigeminal neuralgia + abducens palsy = petrous apicitis. CN VI in Dorello's canal is key.
pyqNote: Gradenigo's syndrome and CN VI involvement via Dorello's canal is a classic AIIMS/NEET PG question.

subject: ENT
stem: A 4-year-old child presents with fever, ear pain, and pulling at the right ear. Otoscopy shows: loss of the light reflex, a bulging, erythematous tympanic membrane with loss of bony landmarks (handle of malleus and long process of incus not visible). The tympanic membrane appears to be under tension. The most likely diagnosis and the most appropriate next step are:
options: Acute otitis media — analgesics + oral amoxicillin (high dose) for 10 days in children under 2 years
Otitis media with effusion — watchful waiting for 3 months
Otitis externa — topical antibiotic and antifungal drops
Tympanic membrane perforation — ear toilet and topical antibiotic
explanation: Acute otitis media (AOM) presents with acute ear pain (otalgia), fever, and otoscopic findings of a bulging, erythematous TM with loss of normal landmarks and absent/distorted light reflex. The bulging indicates middle ear pus under pressure. In children under 2 years, immediate antibiotic therapy (amoxicillin 80–90 mg/kg/day for 10 days) is recommended. In children over 2 years with mild AOM, a 48–72 hour watchful waiting approach is acceptable. If the TM perforates spontaneously, it relieves pain and is generally favourable. Myringotomy provides drainage if there is no spontaneous perforation and pain is severe.
highYield: AOM otoscopy: bulging erythematous TM + loss of landmarks + absent light reflex. Under 2 years → immediate antibiotics.
pyqNote: AOM vs OME clinical and otoscopic differentiation is a standard NEET PG paediatric ENT question.

subject: Radiology
stem: A chest X-ray of a 5-year-old child with cyanosis since birth shows a boot-shaped cardiac silhouette. The cardiac apex is upturned, the pulmonary bay is concave, and pulmonary vascular markings are decreased. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: The 'boot-shaped heart' (coeur en sabot) on CXR is pathognomonic of Tetralogy of Fallot. Right ventricular hypertrophy causes the upturned apex, while pulmonary stenosis causes a concave pulmonary bay and decreased pulmonary vascular markings. TAPVC shows a 'snowman' or 'figure-of-8' heart. TGA shows an 'egg-on-string' appearance. Ebstein's shows a massively enlarged right atrium (box-shaped heart).
highYield: Boot-shaped heart with decreased pulmonary vascularity = ToF
pyqNote: Repeated in NEET PG and AIIMS PG almost every year; highest-yield cardiac X-ray question

subject: Radiology
stem: A chest X-ray of a neonate with cyanosis shows an egg-shaped cardiac shadow with a narrow superior mediastinum and increased pulmonary vascular markings. The right heart border is abnormally prominent. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: The 'egg-on-string' (or egg-on-side) appearance in TGA results from the parallel arrangement of the aorta and pulmonary artery, which narrows the superior mediastinum and gives the cardiac shadow an ovoid appearance. Pulmonary vascularity is increased because the pulmonary circuit receives mixed blood under high flow. The narrow pedicle ('string') is due to the AP relationship of great vessels lying on top of each other rather than side-by-side.
highYield: Egg-on-string heart with narrow pedicle and increased vascularity = TGA
pyqNote: High-frequency question; often paired with ToF boot-shaped heart in single-best-answer format

subject: Radiology
stem: A chest X-ray of a 6-week-old neonate with respiratory distress shows a 'snowman' or 'figure-of-8' cardiac silhouette. The upper mediastinum is widened bilaterally by a vertical vein shadow. Pulmonary vascular markings are markedly increased. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: Supracardiac TAPVC is the only congenital anomaly producing the classic 'snowman' or 'figure-of-8' cardiac silhouette. The upper part of the snowman is formed by the left vertical vein + left SVC + innominate vein + right SVC creating a dense shadow in the superior mediastinum. The lower part is the enlarged cardiac shadow. Pulmonary vascularity is markedly increased as all pulmonary venous blood drains anomalously into the systemic venous system. It does not occur in other cyanotic lesions.
highYield: Snowman/figure-of-8 heart = supracardiac TAPVC
pyqNote: Distinguishing TAPVC from TGA and ToF on CXR is a classic NEET PG trap

subject: Radiology
stem: A chest X-ray of a 55-year-old patient in the ICU with acute onset dyspnea shows bilateral perihilar haziness in a 'bat-wing' or 'butterfly' pattern. Cardiomegaly is present, Kerley B lines are visible at the costophrenic angles, and there is blunting of both costophrenic angles. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: The bat-wing/butterfly pattern of bilateral perihilar haziness with sparing of the lung periphery is a hallmark of acute cardiogenic pulmonary edema. Cardiomegaly, Kerley B lines (horizontal lines at the lung periphery representing distended lymphatics), and bilateral pleural effusions all confirm increased pulmonary venous pressure. Miliary TB shows uniformly distributed 1-2mm nodules. Bilateral pneumonia shows patchy consolidation without the central predominance or Kerley lines. Sarcoidosis typically shows hilar lymphadenopathy with upper-lobe predominant changes.
highYield: Bat-wing opacity + Kerley B lines + cardiomegaly = cardiogenic pulmonary edema
pyqNote: Bat-wing pattern and Kerley B lines appear frequently in pulmonary edema questions

subject: Radiology
stem: A chest X-ray of a 40-year-old asymptomatic man shows a well-defined 2 cm rounded opacity in the right middle zone with a 'target lesion' appearance — concentric rings of calcification ('bull's eye' calcification). There is no hilar lymphadenopathy. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: Concentric rings (target or bull's eye) calcification within a pulmonary coin lesion is pathognomonic of a healed histoplasmoma (Histoplasma capsulatum granuloma). Hamartoma classically shows 'popcorn' calcification (multiple punctate foci resembling exploded popcorn) and is the most common benign pulmonary coin lesion. Primary lung carcinoma rarely calcifies and when it does shows an eccentric pattern. Carcinoid tumors are endobronchial, enhance vividly on CT, and are rarely calcified. Target calcification = histoplasmoma is the key differentiator.
highYield: Target/bull's eye calcification in coin lesion = histoplasmoma
pyqNote: Calcification patterns in pulmonary coin lesions are a repeated NEET PG one-liner

subject: Radiology
stem: A chest X-ray of a 50-year-old non-smoker shows a well-defined 1.5 cm rounded opacity in the left lower zone. CT shows 'popcorn' calcification — multiple punctate calcific foci scattered throughout the lesion. The lesion has been stable in size for 3 years. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: Popcorn calcification scattered throughout the lesion is the hallmark of a pulmonary hamartoma — the most common benign lung tumor. It is composed of mesenchymal elements (cartilage, fat, connective tissue, smooth muscle), and the CT finding of fat attenuation within the lesion is also diagnostic. Stability over 3 years further supports a benign etiology. Histoplasmoma shows target (concentric ring) calcification. Adenocarcinoma rarely calcifies and grows over time. Carcinoid is typically endobronchial with homogeneous enhancement.
highYield: Popcorn calcification in stable pulmonary nodule = hamartoma
pyqNote: Most common benign coin lesion in adults — frequently tested in differentiation questions

subject: Radiology
stem: A chest X-ray of a 28-year-old immunocompromised HIV-positive patient shows uniformly distributed, well-defined 1–2 mm nodules scattered throughout both lung fields from apex to base with equal density. There is no hilar lymphadenopathy visible. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: Miliary TB classically shows uniform 1–2 mm ('millet seed') nodules distributed randomly throughout both lungs from apex to base, including behind the heart and diaphragm. The absence of hilar lymphadenopathy in this HIV patient suggests a compromised immune response (anergic state). Sarcoidosis nodules are larger (3–5 mm), have an upper-lobe predominance and lymphangitic distribution, and virtually always have bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy. Metastases are typically larger and randomly distributed. Silicosis has upper-lobe predominance and eggshell calcification of lymph nodes.
highYield: Uniform 1-2 mm nodules apex to base = miliary TB; random distribution is key
pyqNote: Miliary pattern differential appears in nearly every NEET PG radiology section

subject: Radiology
stem: A chest X-ray of a healthy 3-year-old child taken for a mild fever shows a triangular opacity projecting from the right mediastinal border, obliterating the right cardiac border partially. On a lateral view, it appears anterior and superior. The child has no respiratory symptoms and the trachea is central. The most likely explanation is:
explanation: The sail sign (or thymic sail) is a normal variant in children under 3–4 years of age where the thymus produces a triangular opacity that projects from the superior mediastinum, typically on the right side, resembling a sailing boat. It has a wavy right border (thymic wave sign) and is continuous with the cardiac silhouette. Unlike pathological masses, the thymus does not displace the trachea, is not associated with respiratory symptoms, and shrinks with stress or steroid treatment. On lateral view it occupies the anterior mediastinum. It must not be mistaken for pneumonia or a mediastinal mass.
highYield: Triangular right mediastinal opacity in a child under 3 = thymic sail sign (normal)
pyqNote: Classic pediatric CXR question — examiners frequently ask whether this is normal or pathological

subject: Radiology
stem: HRCT chest of a 65-year-old male smoker with progressive dyspnea over 3 years shows bilateral basal and subpleural predominant reticulation, honeycombing with a peripheral distribution, and traction bronchiectasis. Ground glass opacities are minimal. There is no hilar lymphadenopathy. The pattern is most consistent with:
options: Usual Interstitial Pneumonia (UIP) — Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis
explanation: The HRCT pattern of bilateral basal subpleural reticulation with honeycombing and traction bronchiectasis with minimal GGO is the hallmark UIP (Usual Interstitial Pneumonia) pattern, which is the pathological and radiological substrate of Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (IPF). Honeycombing (clustered air-filled cystic spaces with thick walls) is the key distinguishing feature of UIP over NSIP. NSIP shows bilateral basal GGO-predominant changes with subpleural sparing and no honeycombing. DIP shows diffuse bilateral GGO. COP shows peripheral consolidation with peribronchial distribution.
highYield: Basal subpleural honeycombing + traction bronchiectasis = UIP/IPF on HRCT
pyqNote: UIP vs NSIP differentiation on HRCT is a core NEET PG radiology question

subject: Radiology
stem: HRCT chest of a 45-year-old woman with systemic sclerosis shows bilateral basal ground glass opacities with a subpleural sparing pattern. There is fine reticulation but no honeycombing. Traction bronchiectasis is present. The pattern is most consistent with:
explanation: NSIP is the most common ILD associated with connective tissue diseases, especially systemic sclerosis (scleroderma). The hallmark HRCT features are bilateral basal ground glass opacities with subpleural sparing (a feature not seen in UIP), fine reticulation, and traction bronchiectasis. The absence of honeycombing differentiates NSIP from UIP. Subpleural sparing is the key CT finding that distinguishes NSIP from UIP. Hypersensitivity pneumonitis shows upper/mid-lobe predominance and centrilobular nodules. LIP shows diffuse GGO with thin-walled cysts.
highYield: Bilateral basal GGO with subpleural sparing and no honeycombing = NSIP
pyqNote: Subpleural sparing is the single most important distinguishing CT sign of NSIP vs UIP

subject: Radiology
stem: CT abdomen of a 22-year-old male with right iliac fossa pain shows a tubular blind-ending structure in the right iliac fossa with a wall thickness of 8 mm. Periappendiceal fat stranding is seen. A 5 mm hyperdense focus is seen at the base of the appendix in the lumen. No free air is noted. The most likely diagnosis is:
options: Crohn's disease of the terminal ileum
explanation: CT is the gold standard for diagnosing acute appendicitis. The diagnostic criteria include an appendiceal wall thickness >6 mm (normal is <6 mm), periappendiceal fat stranding indicating inflammation, a non-compressible blind-ending tubular structure, and a fecalith (calcified appendicolith visible as a hyperdense focus in the lumen). Free air or fluid would suggest perforation. Crohn's disease affects the terminal ileum with skip lesions and does not typically show a fecalith. Ovarian cysts would be lateral to the midline. Mesenteric lymphadenitis shows enlarged lymph nodes with a normal appendix.
highYield: Appendix wall >6mm + fat stranding + fecalith on CT = acute appendicitis
pyqNote: CT criteria for appendicitis, especially the 6 mm wall thickness cutoff, is frequently examined

subject: Radiology
stem: CECT abdomen of a 45-year-old male with severe epigastric pain and elevated serum amylase shows a markedly edematous pancreas with heterogeneous enhancement. There are two peripancreatic fluid collections — one in the lesser sac and one along the left paracolic gutter. No gas is seen within these collections. The pancreatic parenchyma enhances throughout. What is the Balthazar CT Severity Index (CTSI) grade?
explanation: Balthazar grading: A = normal pancreas (0 pts), B = pancreatic enlargement only (1 pt), C = pancreatic inflammation + peripancreatic fat changes (2 pts), D = single peripancreatic fluid collection (3 pts), E = two or more fluid collections or gas in/adjacent to pancreas (4 pts). In this case, 2 peripancreatic fluid collections = Grade E (4 pts). However, since pancreatic necrosis is absent (enhancing throughout), the necrosis score is 0. Therefore CTSI = 4 + 0 = 4, which corresponds to Grade D scoring. Note: The grade is E but no necrosis gives CTSI 4. Grades D/E with necrosis carry the highest morbidity and mortality.
highYield: Two or more peripancreatic fluid collections = Balthazar Grade E
pyqNote: Balthazar grading A through E and CTSI calculation appear regularly in surgery/radiology combined questions

subject: Radiology
stem: CT angiography of the aorta in a 60-year-old hypertensive male with sudden-onset tearing chest pain radiating to the back shows a linear low-density intimal flap within the aortic lumen, dividing it into two channels. The anterior (true) lumen appears smaller. The posterior (false) lumen is larger and shows slower contrast flow. The false lumen shows a 'beak sign'. The dissection begins just distal to the left subclavian artery. This is classified as:
explanation: Stanford classification: Type A involves the ascending aorta (regardless of origin); Type B does not involve the ascending aorta. DeBakey: Type I = ascending + descending; Type II = ascending only; Type III = descending only. This dissection begins distal to the left subclavian artery (descending aorta only) — therefore it is Stanford Type B = DeBakey Type III. The 'beak sign' is a CT sign where the acute angle of the false lumen at its periphery forms a beak-like projection, helping identify the false lumen. The true lumen is typically smaller, more anterior, and has faster contrast flow.
highYield: Dissection distal to left subclavian = Stanford Type B; no ascending involvement
pyqNote: Aortic dissection classification (Stanford vs DeBakey) is a must-know NEET PG question

subject: Radiology
stem: MRI brain of a 30-year-old patient shows a well-defined lesion in the right temporal lobe that is hyperintense (bright) on T1-weighted images and isointense to slightly hyperintense on T2-weighted images. The lesion does not suppress on fat suppression sequences. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: T1 bright (hyperintense) lesions on MRI brain have a classic mnemonic: Fat, Blood (methemoglobin), Melanin, Protein (highly proteinaceous), Gadolinium, Manganese (FaBMPGM). Subacute hemorrhage contains methemoglobin (both intracellular and extracellular forms) which is T1 bright due to dipole-dipole interaction. Fat is also T1 bright but suppresses on fat-saturation sequences (this lesion does not). Acute infarct is T1 dark (hypointense) and T2 bright. Epidermoid cyst follows CSF on T1/T2 but is DWI-restricted. Simple arachnoid cyst follows CSF (T1 dark, T2 bright).
highYield: T1 bright lesions: Fat, Blood (methemoglobin), Melanin, Protein, Gadolinium
pyqNote: T1 and T2 signal characteristics appear in almost every NEET PG MRI-based question

subject: Radiology
stem: MRI brain of a 68-year-old hypertensive patient with sudden onset left-sided weakness shows a wedge-shaped area in the right MCA territory that is bright on DWI (diffusion-weighted imaging) and dark on ADC (apparent diffusion coefficient) map. On FLAIR, the area is not yet bright. The time since onset is most likely:
explanation: DWI bright + ADC dark = restricted diffusion = acute/hyperacute ischemic infarct. In acute infarction, cytotoxic edema leads to water moving from extracellular to intracellular space, restricting Brownian motion of water molecules. DWI detects this within 30 minutes of onset. FLAIR becomes positive at 6–12 hours, so a DWI-FLAIR mismatch (DWI bright, FLAIR dark) indicates the stroke is less than 6 hours old — the 'DWI-FLAIR mismatch' sign. This is used to identify patients who may benefit from thrombolysis when the exact onset time is unknown. Subacute infarct (days–weeks) eventually becomes T2/FLAIR bright. Chronic infarct shows encephalomalacia.
highYield: DWI bright + ADC dark + FLAIR negative = hyperacute stroke (<6 hours)
pyqNote: DWI-FLAIR mismatch is a modern NEET PG favorite; restricted diffusion concept is core

subject: Radiology
stem: An erect chest X-ray of a 55-year-old male with sudden severe abdominal pain shows free air under both domes of the diaphragm as crescentic lucencies. A plain X-ray of the abdomen in the supine position shows gas outlining both sides of the bowel wall simultaneously — the inner (mucosal) and outer (serosal) surfaces of the bowel are both visible as distinct lines. This sign is called:
explanation: Rigler's sign (also called the 'double wall sign') is the visibility of both the inner (luminal gas) and outer (peritoneal free gas) surfaces of the bowel wall simultaneously on a supine abdominal X-ray. Normally only the inner bowel wall is visible. This sign requires a significant amount of free intraperitoneal air (at least 800–1000 mL) and indicates pneumoperitoneum. The erect CXR showing free air under the diaphragm is the most sensitive X-ray sign. Football sign refers to a large oval lucency over the abdomen in neonates with pneumoperitoneum. String of beads = small bowel obstruction. Double bubble = duodenal atresia.
highYield: Rigler's sign = double wall sign = both bowel wall surfaces visible = pneumoperitoneum
pyqNote: Signs of pneumoperitoneum on plain X-ray are frequently tested; Rigler's vs football sign distinction matters

subject: Radiology
stem: A plain X-ray abdomen (supine) of a 35-year-old male with colicky abdominal pain and vomiting shows multiple centrally placed loops of small bowel that are distended and show multiple small air-fluid levels arranged in an inverted U or stepladder pattern. On erect film, multiple small rounded gas bubbles are seen strung together in a row in the right iliac fossa. The diagnosis is:
options: Small bowel obstruction with 'string of beads' sign
explanation: The 'string of beads' (or 'string of pearls') sign on plain X-ray represents tiny gas bubbles trapped between the valvulae conniventes in fluid-filled, obstructed small bowel loops. It is seen on an erect or decubitus film and is pathognomonic of small bowel obstruction. The central location, plicated mucosal folds (valvulae conniventes visible across the full width of the bowel), and multiple air-fluid levels at different heights all point to small bowel obstruction. In contrast, large bowel obstruction shows peripheral haustra that do not cross the full diameter of the bowel. Paralytic ileus involves the entire bowel with no transition point.
highYield: String of beads sign on erect X-ray = small bowel obstruction
pyqNote: Small vs large bowel obstruction differentiation on plain X-ray is a classic surgical radiology question

subject: Radiology
stem: A plain X-ray abdomen of a 1-day-old neonate with bilious vomiting and failure to pass meconium shows two large gas-filled structures in the upper abdomen — a large air-filled stomach on the left and a smaller gas-filled structure on the right (the first part of duodenum). The rest of the abdomen is gasless. The diagnosis is:
explanation: The 'double bubble' sign on plain X-ray of a neonate is pathognomonic of duodenal atresia. The two bubbles represent air in the stomach (large left bubble) and the dilated first part of the duodenum proximal to the atresia (smaller right bubble), with a gasless rest of abdomen. Bilious vomiting indicates the obstruction is distal to the ampulla of Vater (most cases of duodenal atresia). Duodenal atresia is strongly associated with Down syndrome (Trisomy 21) in 30% of cases. Pyloric stenosis shows a single large gas bubble (stomach only) with a 'caterpillar sign'. Jejunal atresia shows three or more bubbles (triple bubble).
highYield: Double bubble sign in neonate = duodenal atresia; associated with Down syndrome
pyqNote: Double bubble vs triple bubble vs single bubble neonatal signs appear every 2-3 years in NEET PG

subject: Radiology
stem: Barium swallow of a 48-year-old man with progressive dysphagia to solids and liquids for 2 years shows a smooth, tapering 'rat-tail' or 'bird-beak' narrowing at the gastroesophageal junction. The esophagus above is massively dilated and tortuous. Peristalsis is absent on fluoroscopy. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: Achalasia cardia is characterized on barium swallow by a smooth, symmetric, tapering narrowing at the lower esophageal sphincter producing the 'rat-tail' or 'bird-beak' (beak sign) appearance. The proximal esophagus is markedly dilated (megaesophagus). Fluoroscopy shows absent peristalsis (aperistalsis) and failure of the LES to relax. Importantly, the narrowing in achalasia is smooth and regular — in contrast to carcinoma of the esophagus, which produces an irregular 'rat-bite' narrowing with shouldering (abrupt transition, mucosal destruction). Peptic stricture shows a short smooth stricture above the Z-line with reflux. Diffuse esophageal spasm shows a 'corkscrew' esophagus.
highYield: Smooth bird-beak narrowing at GEJ + massively dilated esophagus + aperistalsis = achalasia
pyqNote: Rat-tail (achalasia) vs rat-bite (carcinoma) barium esophagus distinction is the most tested barium question in NEET PG

subject: Radiology
stem: Barium swallow of a 60-year-old male smoker with rapidly progressive dysphagia to solids over 3 months shows an irregular, asymmetric narrowing in the mid-esophagus with mucosal destruction. There is abrupt shouldering at the upper and lower margins of the stricture (shelf-like projections). The mucosa is completely destroyed within the lesion. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: Carcinoma of the esophagus on barium swallow produces an irregular, asymmetric, 'rat-bite' appearance with shouldering — abrupt shelf-like projections at the transition between normal and abnormal esophagus (apple core lesion in the esophagus). Mucosal destruction is a hallmark of malignancy. The progression is rapid (weeks to months) and dysphagia is initially to solids only (later both). This contrasts with achalasia's smooth rat-tail narrowing, absent peristalsis, and dysphagia to both solids and liquids from the start. Peptic stricture is short, smooth, and located at the GEJ. Esophageal web is a thin, shelf-like projection in the upper esophagus.
highYield: Irregular rat-bite narrowing with shouldering = esophageal carcinoma; contrast with smooth rat-tail of achalasia
pyqNote: This distinction from achalasia is the single most frequently asked barium esophagus question

subject: Radiology
stem: A barium enema of a 3-week-old neonate with failure to pass meconium shows a narrow, contracted rectosigmoid segment with an abrupt transition to a massively dilated, distended proximal colon (transition zone). The narrow segment shows no haustral markings. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: Hirschsprung's disease (congenital aganglionic megacolon) is diagnosed on barium enema by the classic 'transition zone' — a narrowed aganglionic distal segment (usually rectosigmoid) transitioning abruptly to a massively dilated proximal colon containing normal ganglion cells. The narrowed segment lacks haustral markings and has an irregular saw-tooth appearance. The transition zone is at the recto-sigmoid junction in 75% of cases (short segment). Delayed films at 24 hours showing retained barium supports the diagnosis. Definitive diagnosis is by rectal suction biopsy showing absence of ganglion cells. Meconium ileus shows microcolon with pellets of meconium in the terminal ileum.
highYield: Transition zone with narrow rectosigmoid + dilated proximal colon on barium enema = Hirschsprung's
pyqNote: Hirschsprung's barium enema transition zone is a high-frequency NEET PG question

subject: Radiology
stem: A plain X-ray of the distal femur of a 16-year-old male with a painful swelling for 3 months shows a destructive metaphyseal lesion with an ill-defined zone of transition. There is periosteal elevation at the periphery of the lesion producing a triangular periosteal shadow at the cortical edge (Codman's triangle). Radiating spicules of new bone formation perpendicular to the cortex give a 'sunburst' appearance. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: Osteosarcoma is the most common primary malignant bone tumor in adolescents, with a predilection for the distal femur and proximal tibia metaphysis. The classic X-ray findings are: (1) Codman's triangle — periosteal elevation at the margin of the tumor creating a triangular reactive periosteal bone, (2) sunburst pattern — radiating spicules of new bone perpendicular to the cortex (perpendicular periosteal reaction), and (3) ill-defined zone of transition indicating aggressive lesion. Ewing's sarcoma shows an onion-peel (lamellated) periosteal reaction in the diaphysis of long bones. Giant cell tumor shows soap-bubble epiphyseal lesion. Chondrosarcoma shows calcified chondroid matrix (rings and arcs).
highYield: Codman triangle + sunburst pattern in metaphysis of adolescent = osteosarcoma
pyqNote: Osteosarcoma vs Ewing's sarcoma X-ray distinction is the single most repeated bone tumor question in NEET PG

subject: Radiology
stem: A plain X-ray of the mid-shaft of the femur of a 12-year-old child with fever, pain, and swelling shows a permeative destructive lesion in the diaphysis with multiple layers of periosteal new bone formation parallel to the cortex, resembling the layers of an onion. There is a large soft tissue mass. ESR is elevated. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: Ewing's sarcoma characteristically involves the diaphysis (shaft) of long bones — in contrast to osteosarcoma which involves the metaphysis. The hallmark X-ray finding is an 'onion-peel' (lamellated) periosteal reaction with multiple parallel layers of new bone formation. A large soft tissue mass disproportionate to the bone destruction and systemic symptoms (fever, elevated ESR, leukocytosis) may mimic osteomyelitis ('great mimicker'). The age group (10–15 years), diaphyseal location, and onion-peel reaction clinch the diagnosis. Osteomyelitis can show similar periosteal reaction but lacks the soft tissue mass and has sequestrum/involucrum. LCH shows a 'hole-within-a-hole' or vertebra plana.
highYield: Onion-peel periosteal reaction in diaphysis of child = Ewing's sarcoma
pyqNote: Ewing's sarcoma is tested almost every year — diaphyseal location and onion-peel are the key distinguishers from osteosarcoma

subject: Radiology
stem: A plain X-ray of the distal femur of a 30-year-old male with a painful bony swelling shows an eccentric, expansile, lytic lesion in the epiphysis extending to the articular surface. The lesion has a 'soap bubble' appearance with multiple internal trabeculations and a thin expanded cortical shell. There is no periosteal reaction and no calcification. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: Giant cell tumor (GCT) occurs in skeletally mature adults (epiphyseal fusion complete) and has a classic epiphyseal location extending to the articular surface — the 'open-end-of-bone' lesion. The 'soap bubble' appearance reflects the internal trabeculations. The lesion is eccentric, expansile, lytic, and has NO matrix calcification and NO periosteal reaction (unless fractured). It is locally aggressive but can rarely metastasize. Aneurysmal bone cyst also shows soap bubble but is metaphyseal in children. Chondroblastoma is epiphyseal but occurs BEFORE epiphyseal fusion (adolescents) and often shows calcification. Osteosarcoma is metaphyseal with Codman triangle and sunburst.
highYield: Soap bubble epiphyseal lytic lesion touching articular surface in an adult = GCT
pyqNote: GCT location (epiphysis, subarticular) and soap bubble appearance are high-yield distinguishing features

subject: Radiology
stem: A plain X-ray of the proximal femur of a 20-year-old female shows an expansile intramedullary lesion in the diaphysis and metaphysis with a 'ground glass' (hazy, smudgy) matrix opacity resembling frosted glass. The cortex is thinned but intact. There is no periosteal reaction. The proximal femur shows bowing deformity resembling a 'shepherd's crook' deformity. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: Fibrous dysplasia classically shows a 'ground glass' matrix density on plain X-ray — a hazy, homogeneous opacity caused by the fibrous tissue replacing normal trabecular bone. The shepherd's crook deformity of the proximal femur results from repeated stress fractures and bowing in the polyostotic form. It can be monostotic or polyostotic (McCune-Albright syndrome = polyostotic FD + café-au-lait spots + precocious puberty in females). The cortex is thinned but intact and there is no periosteal reaction. Paget's disease shows cortical thickening, coarse trabecular pattern, and 'blade of grass' lesion. Non-ossifying fibroma is metaphyseal with a sclerotic rim. Enchondroma shows stippled chondroid calcification.
highYield: Ground glass matrix + shepherd's crook deformity of femur = fibrous dysplasia
pyqNote: Fibrous dysplasia ground glass appearance and shepherd's crook deformity appear in bone radiology and orthopedics sections

subject: Radiology
stem: A plain X-ray of the knee of a 65-year-old obese woman with pain on movement and morning stiffness lasting less than 30 minutes shows narrowing of the medial joint space, subchondral sclerosis (increased bone density beneath the cartilage), marginal osteophytes (bone spurs) at the joint margins, and subchondral cysts (small lucent areas in the bone). The diagnosis is:
explanation: The four classic X-ray features of osteoarthritis can be remembered as LOSS: L = Loss of joint space (asymmetric, medial compartment predominantly in the knee), O = Osteophytes (marginal bony spurs), S = Subchondral sclerosis (eburnation), S = Subchondral cysts (geodes). OA is a non-inflammatory arthritis with brief morning stiffness (<30 min). Rheumatoid arthritis shows symmetrical joint space loss, periarticular osteoporosis, marginal erosions, and NO osteophytes. Gout shows rat-bite erosions with overhanging edges and soft tissue tophi. Pseudogout shows chondrocalcinosis (calcification in the cartilage).
highYield: LOSS mnemonic: Loss of space + Osteophytes + Subchondral sclerosis + Subchondral cysts = OA
pyqNote: OA vs RA differentiation on X-ray is a perennial NEET PG question across radiology, orthopedics, and medicine

subject: Radiology
stem: A PA chest X-ray of a 40-year-old male shows complete opacification of the right hemithorax with shift of the trachea and mediastinum to the LEFT (away from the opacity). The right intercostal spaces are widened. There is no air bronchogram within the opacity. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: A massive pleural effusion causes complete opacification of the hemithorax with mediastinal shift AWAY from the opacity (to the contralateral side) due to the mass effect of the large fluid collection. This distinguishes it from collapse/atelectasis, which pulls the mediastinum TOWARD the opacity (ipsilateral shift). The widened intercostal spaces further support effusion (fluid pushes ribs apart). The absence of air bronchograms suggests the opacity is due to fluid rather than airspace consolidation. Right lung collapse would show trachea deviated to the right with crowded ribs and an opaque hemithorax.
highYield: Opaque hemithorax with contralateral mediastinal shift = massive pleural effusion
pyqNote: Tracheal shift direction is the key discriminator between effusion (away) and collapse (toward) — NEET PG favorite

subject: Radiology
stem: A PA chest X-ray of a 25-year-old tall thin male with sudden onset dyspnea and chest pain shows a hyperlucent right lung field with no lung markings visible in the right upper zone. A sharp linear edge (visceral pleural line) is visible running parallel to the chest wall, separating collapsed lung from free air. The trachea is central. The diagnosis is:
explanation: Simple spontaneous pneumothorax presents with a hyperlucent hemithorax devoid of lung markings with a visible visceral pleural line (the edge of the partially collapsed lung). In a simple pneumothorax, the trachea is CENTRAL because there is no tension. Tension pneumothorax would show tracheal deviation to the CONTRALATERAL side (away from the pneumothorax), along with hypotension, and is a medical emergency (needle decompression required immediately). Bullous emphysema can mimic pneumothorax — the distinction is that bullae have concave walls (curving away from the chest wall) while pneumothorax has a convex edge. Spontaneous pneumothorax affects tall thin males (Marfan body habitus) due to apical blebs.
highYield: Visceral pleural line + absent lung markings + central trachea = simple spontaneous pneumothorax
pyqNote: Simple vs tension pneumothorax differentiation based on tracheal position is essential NEET PG knowledge

subject: Radiology
stem: A non-contrast CT brain of a 70-year-old hypertensive patient presenting with acute-onset headache and progressive right hemiplegia shows a well-defined, homogeneous hyperdense (white) lens-shaped (biconvex) lesion in the left temporal region. It does not cross the midline. There is no crossing of suture lines. Mass effect with left-to-right midline shift is seen. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: An acute epidural hematoma on CT appears as a hyperdense (bright white — acute blood) biconvex (lens-shaped) extra-axial collection that does not cross suture lines (because the dura is firmly attached to sutures, limiting the hematoma's spread). It is most commonly due to rupture of the middle meningeal artery following temporal bone fracture. Classic clinical presentation is lucid interval followed by sudden deterioration. Acute subdural hematoma is crescent-shaped (concave inner margin) and DOES cross suture lines but not the falx. SAH fills the cisterns and sulci. Hypertensive hemorrhage is intracerebral (basal ganglia — putamen most common).
highYield: Biconvex hyperdense extra-axial collection not crossing suture lines = epidural hematoma
pyqNote: Biconvex (EDH) vs crescent (SDH) CT distinction is a fundamental NEET PG question

subject: Radiology
stem: A non-contrast CT brain of an 80-year-old patient on anticoagulants shows a crescentic (concave inner margin) hyperdense extra-axial collection overlying the right cerebral convexity. It crosses the coronal suture but does not cross the midline (falx). Mild effacement of right-sided sulci is noted. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: Acute subdural hematoma appears as a hyperdense crescent-shaped extra-axial collection that conforms to the cerebral convexity and crosses suture lines (unlike EDH) but does NOT cross the midline (falx) or tentorium. It is typically due to tearing of bridging veins between the cortex and the dural sinuses, and is common in elderly and anticoagulated patients. Over days-weeks it becomes isodense, then hypodense (chronic SDH). The crescentic shape and crossing of sutures differentiates it from the biconvex EDH. Subarachnoid hemorrhage fills the basal cisterns and sulci. Chronic SDH may present as a 'mixed density' collection.
highYield: Crescent-shaped hyperdense collection crossing suture lines = acute subdural hematoma
pyqNote: SDH vs EDH CT morphology distinction is examined repeatedly; acute vs chronic SDH density change is equally important

subject: Radiology
stem: A chest X-ray of a 35-year-old African-American female with breathlessness and erythema nodosum shows bilateral symmetrical hilar enlargement with multiple lobulated paratracheal shadows on the right. Lung fields appear clear with no pulmonary infiltrates. The most likely diagnosis is:
options: Bilateral primary tuberculosis with hilar adenopathy
explanation: The Scadding classification of sarcoidosis on CXR: Stage 0 = normal; Stage I = bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy (BHL) ONLY (no parenchymal changes); Stage II = BHL + parenchymal changes; Stage III = parenchymal changes alone (no BHL); Stage IV = pulmonary fibrosis. This patient shows clear lung fields with only bilateral hilar lymphadenopathy = Stage I. The 'potato nodes' (bilaterally symmetric lobulated hilar adenopathy) are characteristic of sarcoidosis. Erythema nodosum with BHL is Löfgren syndrome (good prognosis). TB usually shows asymmetric adenopathy and is not perfectly symmetric. Lymphoma often has anterior mediastinal involvement.
highYield: Bilateral symmetric hilar lymphadenopathy alone (no parenchymal changes) = Sarcoidosis Stage I
pyqNote: Sarcoidosis staging on CXR (Scadding) is a regularly tested question; Löfgren syndrome association is a bonus

subject: Radiology
stem: Ultrasound of the abdomen of a 45-year-old female with right upper quadrant pain shows a well-defined echogenic lesion measuring 3 cm in the right lobe of the liver. On posterior acoustic enhancement, there is increased through-transmission (posterior acoustic enhancement). The lesion shows no internal vascularity on Doppler. A thin echogenic capsule is seen. The lesion is unchanged on a scan done 3 years earlier. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: Hepatic cavernous hemangioma is the most common benign liver lesion. Classic ultrasound features include: (1) well-defined homogeneous hyperechoic (echogenic) lesion, (2) posterior acoustic enhancement (increased through-transmission due to vascular spaces filled with slow-moving blood), (3) no internal vascularity on Doppler (paradoxically, due to very slow blood flow in large venous sinusoids). Stability over years is characteristic. MRI confirmation shows a classic 'peripheral nodular enhancement with centripetal fill-in' on dynamic contrast study. HCC is usually heterogeneous with internal vascularity. Hepatic abscess shows internal debris and posterior enhancement but with clinical signs of infection. Metastases are usually hypoechoic with a 'halo' sign.
highYield: Well-defined hyperechoic liver lesion with posterior acoustic enhancement = hemangioma
pyqNote: Hepatic hemangioma ultrasound features appear in both radiology and surgery NEET PG questions

subject: Radiology
stem: Ultrasound of the thyroid of a 42-year-old female with a palpable neck mass shows a 1.5 cm hypoechoic solid nodule in the right lobe with irregular margins and microcalcifications (punctate echogenic foci <1 mm). There is no posterior acoustic shadowing. Color Doppler shows intranodular vascularity. The ACR TIRADS category most appropriate for this nodule is:
explanation: ACR TIRADS scoring assigns points based on five categories: composition, echogenicity, shape, margin, and echogenic foci. Microcalcifications score 3 points (highest echogenic foci score), irregular margin scores 2 points, solid composition scores 2 points, hypoechoic scores 2 points. Total score ≥7 = TIRADS 5 (highly suspicious, fine needle aspiration recommended if ≥1 cm). This nodule has multiple high-risk features: microcalcifications (most specific for papillary thyroid carcinoma), irregular margins, solid, hypoechoic, with intranodular vascularity. These features together make it highly suspicious for papillary thyroid carcinoma, the most common thyroid malignancy (≥1 cm warrants FNA).
highYield: Hypoechoic solid nodule + microcalcifications + irregular margins = TIRADS 5 (highly suspicious for PTC)
pyqNote: Microcalcifications in thyroid nodules as a predictor of papillary carcinoma is an important NEET PG concept

subject: Radiology
stem: MRI lumbar spine (sagittal T2W) of a 40-year-old male with low back pain radiating to the right leg shows the L4-L5 intervertebral disc is dark (hypointense) on T2W sequences compared to adjacent discs which are bright. There is a posterior disc herniation at L4-L5 compressing the traversing right L5 nerve root in the lateral recess. The T2 hypointensity of the disc most likely represents:
options: Disc dessication (loss of water content — degenerative disc disease)
explanation: On T2-weighted MRI, a normal intervertebral disc is hyperintense (bright) due to its high water content (nucleus pulposus is 88% water in adults). Disc degeneration causes loss of proteoglycan content and desiccation (dehydration), resulting in T2 hypointensity (dark disc). This is the hallmark of degenerative disc disease. The posterior herniation then compresses the traversing nerve root (L4-L5 disc herniates to compress L5 root which traverses at that level — not the exiting root). Discitis would show T2 hyperintensity and end-plate destruction with contrast enhancement. Hemorrhage would be T1 bright.
highYield: Dark disc on T2 MRI = disc desiccation = degenerative disc disease
pyqNote: MRI disc signal interpretation and nerve root anatomy at each level are high-frequency spine questions in NEET PG

subject: Radiology
stem: A Tc-99m MDP bone scan of a 65-year-old male with known carcinoma of the prostate shows multiple areas of increased radiotracer uptake ('hot spots') throughout the axial skeleton — including the spine, pelvis, ribs, and skull — in a random scattered pattern. One vertebra shows markedly reduced uptake ('cold spot'). The most likely explanation for the cold spot in one vertebra is:
options: Normal variant — some vertebrae always appear cold
A vertebral metastasis with complete replacement of bone marrow causing no osteoblastic activity ('superscan' component or photopenic metastasis)
explanation: Bone scan detects osteoblastic activity (new bone formation in response to tumor). Most prostate metastases are osteoblastic (hot spots). However, purely osteolytic metastases (common in myeloma, renal cell carcinoma, thyroid cancer) may show cold spots because there is no reactive new bone formation to take up the tracer. In this case, a single cold spot among multiple hot spots in a known prostate cancer patient most likely represents a lytic metastasis with complete marrow replacement (photopenic/cold metastasis). A 'superscan' refers to diffusely increased bone uptake with absent renal/soft tissue activity due to massive metastatic disease. Paget's disease shows characteristic 'expanding' hot lesion. Bone scan cannot diagnose osteoporosis.
highYield: Cold spot on bone scan = lytic lesion (no osteoblastic response); myeloma is the classic cold-scan tumor
pyqNote: Hot vs cold spots on bone scan and which tumors cause which pattern is a recurring NEET PG nuclear medicine question

subject: Radiology
stem: An intravenous pyelogram (IVP) of a 50-year-old male with painless gross hematuria shows a filling defect in the right renal pelvis. The filling defect has an irregular surface and is non-opaque. On retrograde pyelography, the defect persists and shows a 'stippled' pattern of contrast within the lesion. Urine cytology shows transitional cells. The most likely diagnosis is:
options: Transitional cell carcinoma (urothelial carcinoma) of the renal pelvis
Blood clot in the renal pelvis
explanation: A non-opaque (radiolucent) filling defect in the renal pelvis on IVP has the following differential: (1) TCC — irregular surface, persists on retrograde pyelography, stippled contrast within the tumor matrix, positive urine cytology; (2) Blood clot — changes shape and position over time, resolves spontaneously, no urine cytology evidence; (3) Sloughed papilla — triangular defect, history of analgesic nephropathy or diabetes; (4) Radiolucent calculus (uric acid) — smooth well-defined defect, dissolves on urine alkalinization. The positive urine cytology for transitional cells clinches TCC. Stippled contrast pattern (contrast tracking into tumor interstices) is characteristic of TCC. Renal calculi are usually radio-opaque (80%).
highYield: Non-opaque filling defect in renal pelvis + positive urine cytology = TCC (urothelial carcinoma)
pyqNote: Filling defect differential on IVP is a classic NEET PG radiology question; non-opaque vs radiolucent stone distinction matters

subject: Radiology
stem: A screening mammogram of a 52-year-old asymptomatic woman shows an irregular spiculated mass in the upper outer quadrant of the right breast with associated clustered pleomorphic microcalcifications. The mass has ill-defined margins and there is skin thickening overlying the lesion. Axillary lymph nodes appear enlarged and dense. This mammographic appearance is classified as:
options: BIRADS 5 — Highly suggestive of malignancy (biopsy essential)
explanation: The ACR BIRADS (Breast Imaging-Reporting and Data System) classification: BIRADS 1 = negative, BIRADS 2 = benign, BIRADS 3 = probably benign (<2% malignancy risk, 6-month follow-up), BIRADS 4 = suspicious (2-95% risk, tissue sampling), BIRADS 5 = highly suggestive of malignancy (>95% risk, biopsy). This case has multiple BIRADS 5 features: (1) spiculated mass — the single most suspicious mammographic finding, (2) clustered pleomorphic microcalcifications (irregular sizes/shapes = malignant calcification pattern), (3) ill-defined margins, (4) skin thickening (lymphatic obstruction), (5) suspicious axillary nodes. All together strongly indicate invasive breast carcinoma (most likely invasive ductal carcinoma, NOS).
highYield: Spiculated mass + pleomorphic microcalcifications = BIRADS 5; spiculation is the single most suspicious mammographic sign
pyqNote: BIRADS classification and mammographic signs of malignancy appear in NEET PG radiology and surgery sections

subject: Radiology
stem: Ultrasound abdomen of a 9-month-old infant with colicky abdominal pain and passage of red currant jelly stools shows a rounded echogenic mass in the right upper quadrant. In transverse section, the mass shows alternating hyperechoic and hypoechoic concentric rings producing a 'target sign' or 'doughnut sign'. In longitudinal section, it produces a 'pseudokidney sign'. The diagnosis is:
explanation: Intussusception is the most common cause of intestinal obstruction in infants aged 6–18 months. Ultrasound is diagnostic and non-invasive: the 'target sign' or 'doughnut sign' on transverse section represents the intussuscipiens (outer receiving loop) surrounding the intussusceptum (inner invaginated loop) with interposed mesenteric fat (echogenic center). The 'pseudokidney sign' on longitudinal section shows the intussusception resembling a kidney cross-section due to the alternating layers. 'Red currant jelly stool' (blood mixed with mucus) represents mucosal ischemia. Air or hydrostatic (water) enema is both diagnostic and therapeutic (fluoroscopic or ultrasound-guided reduction).
highYield: Target sign/doughnut sign on US in a child with currant jelly stool = intussusception
pyqNote: Intussusception ultrasound signs (target/doughnut/pseudokidney) are a high-yield pediatric radiology question

subject: Pathology
stem: Electron microscopy of a renal biopsy from a 42-year-old male with nephrotic syndrome shows subepithelial electron-dense deposits with intervening projections of GBM material ('spike and dome' pattern). Immunofluorescence reveals granular IgG and C3 deposits along the glomerular capillary walls. Light microscopy shows diffuse thickening of the GBM without cellular proliferation. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: Membranous nephropathy (MGN) is characterized by subepithelial immune complex deposits forming the classic 'spike and dome' pattern on EM, where spikes are GBM projections between deposits. IF shows granular IgG/C3 along capillary walls ('full-house' pattern absent). It is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in adults. PLA2R antibody is positive in ~70% of primary cases.
highYield: Spike and dome on EM + granular IgG on IF = Membranous nephropathy. Stage I: subepithelial deposits; Stage II: GBM spikes between deposits; Stage III: deposits incorporated into GBM.
pyqNote: Frequently asked in NEET PG; spike and dome EM pattern and IF pattern are classic exam favorites.

subject: Pathology
stem: Electron microscopy of a renal biopsy from a 5-year-old boy presenting with sudden onset of massive proteinuria (5g/day) and periorbital edema shows diffuse effacement (fusion) of podocyte foot processes. Immunofluorescence is completely negative. Light microscopy with H&E and PAS stains shows normal-appearing glomeruli. The diagnosis is:
explanation: Minimal change disease (lipoid nephrosis) is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in children. The hallmark is diffuse podocyte foot process effacement on EM with completely negative IF (no immune deposits). LM appears normal or may show lipid droplets in tubular cells (hence 'lipoid nephrosis'). It responds dramatically to corticosteroids.
highYield: Negative IF + podocyte foot process effacement on EM + normal LM = Minimal change disease. Most common nephrotic syndrome in children aged 2-6.
pyqNote: High-yield topic; EM finding of foot process effacement with negative IF is the definitive diagnostic triad.

subject: Pathology
stem: Renal biopsy from an 8-year-old boy presenting with hematuria, hypertension, and oliguria 3 weeks after a sore throat shows on H&E: diffuse endocapillary hypercellularity with neutrophilic infiltration and enlarged glomeruli ('lumpy bumpy' appearance). Electron microscopy reveals large 'hump-shaped' subepithelial deposits. Immunofluorescence shows coarse granular ('lumpy bumpy') deposits of IgG and C3 along capillary walls. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: PSGN classically follows Group A beta-hemolytic streptococcal pharyngitis (3 weeks) or skin infection (6 weeks). Pathognomonic feature is 'hump-shaped' subepithelial deposits on EM (corresponds to the lumpy bumpy IF pattern). LM shows exudative/proliferative glomerulonephritis with neutrophil infiltration. ASO titer is elevated, complement C3 is decreased.
highYield: Hump-shaped subepithelial deposits on EM = PSGN. The IF pattern is 'lumpy bumpy' (starry night). Associated with low C3 and elevated ASO titer.
pyqNote: PSGN EM findings and latency period after infection are repeatedly tested in NEET PG.

subject: Pathology
stem: Renal biopsy from a 25-year-old female with hematuria and proteinuria shows on H&E: hypercellular glomeruli with lobular accentuation and GBM thickening. PAS stain reveals a double-contour or 'tram-track' appearance of the GBM. Immunofluorescence shows granular C3 and IgG deposits in a mesangial and subendothelial pattern. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: MPGN type I (immune complex-mediated) is characterized by lobular accentuation, mesangial and subendothelial deposits, and GBM duplication ('tram-track' appearance) due to mesangial cell interposition into the GBM. IF shows IgG + C3 in a granular pattern. It is associated with chronic infections, cryoglobulinemia, and autoimmune diseases. C3 and C4 levels are decreased (classical pathway activation).
highYield: Tram-track GBM on PAS/silver stain = MPGN type I (subendothelial deposits + mesangial interposition). Dense deposit disease (type II) shows ribbon-like intramembranous deposits on EM.
pyqNote: Tram-track appearance is the most tested histological finding for MPGN in NEET PG and PYQs.

subject: Pathology
stem: A 22-year-old male presents with recurrent episodes of gross hematuria occurring within 1-2 days of upper respiratory tract infections (synpharyngitic hematuria). Renal biopsy shows mesangial hypercellularity on H&E. Immunofluorescence reveals dominant mesangial IgA deposits with co-deposits of C3. Electron microscopy confirms mesangial electron-dense deposits. The diagnosis is:
explanation: IgA nephropathy (Berger disease) is the most common GN worldwide. Key feature is synpharyngitic hematuria (hematuria concurrent with or within 1-2 days of URTI, unlike PSGN which has a 3-week latency). IF shows dominant mesangial IgA deposits. LM typically shows mesangial proliferation. Galactose-deficient IgA1 is implicated in pathogenesis. Serum IgA is elevated in ~50% of cases.
highYield: Mesangial IgA on IF = IgA nephropathy. Synpharyngitic hematuria vs PSGN (3-week latency) is a high-yield distinction. Most common primary GN globally.
pyqNote: IgA nephropathy IF pattern and clinical distinction from PSGN latency is frequently tested.

subject: Pathology
stem: Congo red stain of a kidney biopsy from a 60-year-old male with rheumatoid arthritis and nephrotic syndrome shows pink amorphous deposits in the mesangium and along the glomerular basement membrane. Under polarized light microscopy, these deposits exhibit apple-green birefringence. The amyloid protein most likely responsible for this finding is:
explanation: Secondary (reactive) amyloidosis is associated with chronic inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis, osteomyelitis, bronchiectasis, and leprosy. The deposited protein is AA (derived from serum amyloid A, an acute-phase reactant). Kidneys are the most commonly and severely affected organ. Congo red stain + apple-green birefringence under polarized light is the definitive test for all types of amyloid.
highYield: AA amyloid = secondary/reactive amyloidosis (chronic inflammation, RA, TB, osteomyelitis). AL = primary/myeloma. Congo red + apple-green birefringence is pathognomonic.
pyqNote: Congo red stain and type of amyloid associated with each disease is a perennial NEET PG topic.

subject: Pathology
stem: Liver biopsy from a 45-year-old chronic alcoholic shows H&E section with hepatocyte ballooning degeneration and cytoplasmic eosinophilic hyaline inclusions within the hepatocytes, particularly in zone 3 (centrilobular). There is surrounding neutrophilic infiltration. These intracytoplasmic inclusions are composed of aggregated cytokeratins 8 and 18. These are called:
explanation: Mallory-Denk bodies (Mallory hyaline or alcoholic hyaline) are eosinophilic cytoplasmic inclusions composed of aggregated intermediate filaments (cytokeratin 8 and 18) within ballooned hepatocytes. They are the hallmark of alcoholic hepatitis but can also be seen in NASH, Wilson disease, primary biliary cholangitis, and hepatocellular carcinoma. Zone 3 (centrilobular) is classically affected first in alcoholic liver disease.
