highYield: Mallory-Denk bodies = eosinophilic cytokeratin aggregates in ballooned hepatocytes, Zone 3 predominance, neutrophilic satellitosis. Not specific to alcohol - also in NASH and other liver diseases.
pyqNote: Mallory-Denk bodies are tested in virtually every NEET PG exam - composition and conditions they appear in.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of a liver biopsy from a patient with acute viral hepatitis shows scattered eosinophilic, round, shrunken hepatocytes with pyknotic or absent nuclei, undergoing apoptosis. These cells are phagocytosed by neighboring Kupffer cells and macrophages. Some are present within sinusoids. These apoptotic bodies in viral hepatitis are called:
explanation: Councilman bodies (acidophil bodies) are eosinophilic apoptotic hepatocytes, the hallmark of viral hepatitis (originally described in yellow fever). They represent individual hepatocyte apoptosis/acidophilic necrosis. They are shrunken, deeply eosinophilic, and may contain pyknotic nuclear remnants. In contrast, ground-glass hepatocytes (HBsAg inclusions) are seen in chronic HBV infection, and Mallory-Denk bodies are seen in alcoholic hepatitis.
highYield: Councilman bodies = eosinophilic apoptotic hepatocytes in viral hepatitis (yellow fever classically). Ground-glass hepatocytes = HBsAg in chronic HBV. These are different entities.
pyqNote: Distinction between Councilman bodies (viral hepatitis) and Mallory-Denk bodies (alcoholic) is a classic MCQ stem.

subject: Pathology
stem: Gross specimen of the liver from a patient with long-standing right heart failure shows a cut surface with an alternating pattern of dark red (congested) and yellow-tan (fatty change) areas in a distinctive pattern resembling the cut surface of a plant. The hepatic sinusoids in zone 3 are markedly dilated and congested. This gross appearance is described as:
explanation: Nutmeg liver (or 'nutmeg pattern') occurs in chronic passive venous congestion due to right heart failure. Zone 3 (centrilobular) hepatocytes are compressed and undergo necrosis due to hypoxia, while zone 1 (periportal) hepatocytes undergo fatty change (appearing yellow). This creates the characteristic alternating dark red (congested zone 3) and yellow-tan (fatty zone 1) pattern resembling a nutmeg's cut surface.
highYield: Nutmeg liver = CCF → centrilobular congestion (zone 3, dark red) + periportal fatty change (zone 1, yellow). Chronic cases progress to cardiac cirrhosis.
pyqNote: Nutmeg liver gross appearance and zone 3 predominance is a frequent pathology and pathophysiology question.

subject: Pathology
stem: Liver biopsy from a 16-year-old presenting with liver cirrhosis, Kayser-Fleischer rings, and low serum ceruloplasmin shows brownish granular deposits in periportal hepatocytes on H&E. A special stain performed on the biopsy section stains the deposits ruby-red/brick-red in color. The special stain used and the deposited material are:
options: Perl's Prussian blue stain - iron deposits
explanation: Wilson disease (hepatolenticular degeneration) is an AR disorder of copper transport (ATP7B mutation) causing copper accumulation in liver, brain, and cornea (KF rings). Rhodanine stain (or orcein stain for copper-associated protein) stains copper deposits ruby-red in hepatocytes. Perl's Prussian blue is for iron (hemochromatosis). The liver progresses from fatty change to chronic hepatitis to macronodular cirrhosis.
highYield: Wilson disease histology: Rhodanine stain for copper (ruby-red). Serum copper high, ceruloplasmin low. Perl's Prussian blue = iron (hemochromatosis). Do not confuse the two stains.
pyqNote: Special stain for Wilson disease (rhodanine) vs hemochromatosis (Perl's Prussian blue) is a tested pairing.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of a lymph node biopsy from a 28-year-old male with B-symptoms and mediastinal mass shows a mixed cellular infiltrate of lymphocytes, eosinophils, neutrophils, and plasma cells. Within this background, there are large binucleate cells with prominent eosinophilic 'owl-eye' nucleoli and abundant pale cytoplasm. Immunohistochemistry shows these cells are CD30+ and CD15+, but CD20- and CD45-. These cells are:
explanation: Classic Reed-Sternberg (RS) cells are pathognomonic of classical Hodgkin lymphoma. They are large, binucleate (or multilobed) with prominent eosinophilic owl-eye nucleoli and abundant pale cytoplasm. The immunophenotype is CD30+ (strong), CD15+ (variable), CD20-, CD45- (LCA negative). The background inflammatory infiltrate in mixed cellularity subtype contains eosinophils, neutrophils, plasma cells, and lymphocytes.
highYield: Reed-Sternberg cells: CD30+ CD15+ CD20- CD45-. Owl-eye nucleoli. Background inflammatory cells are non-neoplastic. Lacunar cells are RS variant in nodular sclerosis subtype.
pyqNote: RS cell morphology and CD30/CD15 immunophenotype is tested in almost every NEET PG exam.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of a lymph node biopsy from a 22-year-old female with a large anterior mediastinal mass shows the lymph node divided into cellular nodules by broad bands of collagen. Within these nodules, there are large cells with abundant pale-staining cytoplasm that appears retracted from surrounding tissue (giving a lacunar appearance), and some cells show lobulated nuclei. The most likely diagnosis and the RS cell variant seen is:
options: Mixed cellularity Hodgkin - classic RS cells
explanation: Nodular sclerosis is the most common subtype of Hodgkin lymphoma in developed countries, with a bimodal peak at 15-34 years and >50 years, with female predominance. The hallmarks are broad collagen bands dividing the lymph node into nodules and lacunar cells (RS cell variants with retracted cytoplasm due to formalin fixation, creating a 'lacune' or space around them). It has the best prognosis among mixed cellularity and lymphocyte-depleted subtypes.
highYield: Nodular sclerosis HL: broad collagenous bands + lacunar cells (RS variant). Most common HL subtype. Female > male. Anterior mediastinum involvement is classic.
pyqNote: Nodular sclerosis is the most common HL subtype - lacunar cells and collagen bands are high-yield distinguishing features.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of a lymph node biopsy from a 10-year-old African boy with a jaw mass shows sheets of intermediate-sized lymphoid cells with round nuclei, multiple small nucleoli, and basophilic cytoplasm with lipid vacuoles. The background shows numerous macrophages with ingested apoptotic debris scattered throughout the tumor cells, giving a characteristic appearance. This pattern is called:
explanation: Burkitt lymphoma (endemic form = EBV-associated, jaw in African children) shows the classic 'starry-sky' pattern on H&E: numerous scattered pale-staining macrophages (the 'stars') containing apoptotic debris against a dark background of densely packed lymphoma cells (the 'sky'). This reflects the extremely high proliferation rate (Ki-67 nearly 100%) with frequent apoptosis. Translocation t(8;14) juxtaposes MYC with IgH promoter.
highYield: Starry-sky pattern = Burkitt lymphoma. t(8;14) = MYC/IgH. Ki-67 ~100%. Lipid vacuoles on touch imprints. Three forms: endemic (EBV, jaw), sporadic (ileocecal), immunodeficiency-associated.
pyqNote: Starry-sky pattern and t(8;14) translocation in Burkitt lymphoma is a high-frequency NEET PG question.

subject: Pathology
stem: Lymph node biopsy from a 58-year-old male with painless generalized lymphadenopathy for 2 years shows H&E section with effacement of normal lymph node architecture by closely packed follicular structures throughout the node, including the medulla. The follicles lack mantle zones and are composed of small cleaved (centrocytes) and large non-cleaved (centroblasts) cells. The follicles are positive for BCL-2 protein by IHC. The genetic abnormality most likely present is:
explanation: Follicular lymphoma is the most common indolent (low-grade) B-cell NHL in Western countries. The hallmark translocation is t(14;18) causing overexpression of BCL-2 (anti-apoptotic protein), which prevents normal follicle center B-cell apoptosis. The neoplastic follicles lack the normal mantle zone and tingible body macrophages, and BCL-2+ follicular cells distinguish them from reactive follicles (which are BCL-2 negative in germinal centers).
highYield: Follicular lymphoma: t(14;18) = BCL2 overexpression. BCL-2 positive neoplastic follicles (reactive germinal centers are BCL-2 negative). Small cleaved cells = centrocytes predominate.
pyqNote: t(14;18) and BCL-2 overexpression in follicular lymphoma is a classic NEET PG genetics question.

subject: Pathology
stem: Bone marrow biopsy from a 4-year-old child with leukocytosis (WBC 80,000/µL), lymphadenopathy, and hepatosplenomegaly shows sheets of small to medium-sized lymphoid cells with scant cytoplasm, irregular nuclear contours, and fine chromatin. Special stain (TdT immunostain) shows nuclear positivity in the malignant cells. The malignant cells also express CD10, CD19, and CD34. The diagnosis is:
explanation: B-ALL (B-lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma) is the most common childhood malignancy. Lymphoblasts express TdT (terminal deoxynucleotidyl transferase) - a nuclear enzyme present only in immature lymphoid precursors - along with B-cell markers CD19, CD10 (CALLA), CD34, and PAX5. TdT positivity distinguishes ALL/LBL from mature B-cell lymphomas (Burkitt, DLBCL). Favorable prognosis in children, especially with hyperdiploidy or ETV6-RUNX1 fusion.
highYield: TdT+ = immature lymphoblast (ALL/LBL). B-ALL: CD10, CD19, CD34, TdT+. T-ALL: CD1a, CD2, CD3, CD7, TdT+. TdT distinguishes ALL from mature lymphomas (Burkitt, DLBCL).
pyqNote: TdT positivity as a marker of immaturity in ALL is one of the most tested IHC markers in NEET PG pathology.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of a biopsy from a distal femur mass in a 16-year-old male shows highly pleomorphic spindle-shaped cells with hyperchromatic nuclei, numerous atypical mitoses, and production of pink-staining osteoid matrix directly by the tumor cells. Areas of cartilage and fibrous tissue are also present. X-ray shows a lytic lesion with periosteal reaction elevating the periosteum (Codman triangle) and a 'sunburst' pattern. The diagnosis is:
explanation: Osteosarcoma is the most common primary malignant bone tumor (excluding myeloma). Key diagnostic feature is direct production of osteoid/immature bone by malignant cells (neoplastic osteoid). It arises in the metaphysis of long bones (distal femur > proximal tibia > proximal humerus). X-ray shows Codman triangle (periosteal elevation) and sunburst pattern (perpendicular periosteal spicules). Associated with RB1 and TP53 mutations, Paget disease, prior radiation.
highYield: Osteosarcoma = malignant cells producing osteoid + Codman triangle + sunburst on X-ray. Metaphysis of long bones. Most common in 2nd decade. RB gene mutation association.
pyqNote: Osteosarcoma histology (malignant osteoid), location (metaphysis), and X-ray findings are perennially tested.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of a diaphyseal femur biopsy from a 13-year-old shows sheets of small, round, blue cells with scant cytoplasm, uniform oval nuclei, and finely dispersed chromatin arranged in solid sheets. Occasional rosette-like structures (Homer Wright rosettes) are present. PAS stain reveals abundant intracytoplasmic glycogen. FISH analysis detects t(11;22) translocation. The diagnosis is:
explanation: Ewing sarcoma is the second most common primary malignant bone tumor in children, arising in the diaphysis (unlike osteosarcoma in metaphysis). Histology shows small round blue cells (SRBC tumor). PAS stain is positive due to cytoplasmic glycogen. Homer Wright rosettes indicate neural differentiation (PNET = Primitive Neuroectodermal Tumor, same family). Translocation t(11;22)(q24;q12) fuses EWS-FLI1 genes. X-ray shows 'onion-skin' periosteal reaction.
highYield: Ewing sarcoma: diaphysis, small round blue cells, PAS+, t(11;22) EWS-FLI1, onion-skin periosteal reaction on X-ray. Homer Wright rosettes = neural differentiation (PNET spectrum).
pyqNote: Ewing sarcoma small round blue cells + PAS positivity + t(11;22) + onion skin X-ray = complete NEET PG answer.

subject: Pathology
stem: Gross specimen of a distal femur mass in a 32-year-old female shows a lytic, expansile lesion involving the epiphysis extending to the subchondral plate (epiphyseal location). H&E section shows two cell populations: numerous uniformly distributed osteoclast-like giant cells (with 20-30 nuclei resembling those of surrounding stromal cells) and mononuclear oval to spindle stromal cells. The lesion shows no osteoid production. The diagnosis is:
explanation: Giant cell tumor of bone (osteoclastoma) characteristically occurs in the epiphysis of long bones (distal femur, proximal tibia) in adults aged 20-40 years, after growth plate closure. Histology shows benign-appearing osteoclast-like giant cells with nuclei resembling those of surrounding mononuclear stromal cells (this nuclear similarity is key). No osteoid production. X-ray shows 'soap bubble' lytic appearance without periosteal reaction. RANKL overexpression by stromal cells drives osteoclast activation.
highYield: GCTB = epiphysis + post-growth plate + giant cell nuclei similar to stromal cells + soap bubble on X-ray. Benign but locally aggressive. Denosumab (anti-RANKL) is targeted therapy.
pyqNote: GCT epiphyseal location and 'soap bubble' X-ray appearance are the most tested radiological-pathological correlations.

subject: Pathology
stem: Autopsy of a previously healthy 20-year-old who died of pulmonary tuberculosis shows a small (1 cm) area of caseating granulomatous inflammation in the lower part of the upper lobe (just below the first rib anteriorly) of the right lung, with associated hilar lymph node enlargement showing similar caseating necrosis. This combination of the subpleural parenchymal lesion and the draining lymph node involvement is called:
explanation: Ghon complex (primary/Ranke complex) consists of: (1) Ghon focus - a subpleural caseating granuloma at the lower part of upper lobe or upper part of lower lobe (zone of preferential ventilation in children), plus (2) affected ipsilateral hilar/tracheobronchial lymph nodes. Together these form the primary complex. Simon foci are apical pleural foci of secondary (reactivation) TB. Assmann focus is the early infiltrate of secondary TB.
highYield: Ghon focus location: lower part of upper lobe or upper part of lower lobe (NOT apex). Ghon complex = Ghon focus + hilar LN. Secondary TB = apical (Simon focus, reactivation in high O2 zone).
pyqNote: Ghon focus location (NOT apex - this is secondary TB) is one of the most commonly incorrectly answered questions - tested repeatedly.

subject: Pathology
stem: Electron microscopy of a lung biopsy from a 35-year-old heavy smoker with diffuse bilateral cystic lung disease and lytic bone lesions shows characteristic intracytoplasmic organelles within the proliferating histiocytic cells. These organelles have a pentalaminar membrane with a linear central density and a tennis-racket-shaped morphology. These are:
explanation: Birbeck granules (Langerhans cell granules) are the pathognomonic ultrastructural feature of Langerhans cells on EM - tennis racket-shaped pentalaminar organelles containing Langerin (CD207). Langerhans cell histiocytosis (formerly histiocytosis X) can involve lung (unifocal = eosinophilic granuloma), bone, skin (Letterer-Siwe in infants), or be multisystem (Hand-Schuller-Christian triad: lytic skull lesions + diabetes insipidus + exophthalmos). LCH cells are CD1a+ and S100+.
highYield: Birbeck granules on EM = Langerhans cell histiocytosis. Tennis-racket shaped. LCH markers: CD1a+, CD207 (Langerin)+, S100+. Smoking-related pulmonary LCH is most common adult form.
pyqNote: Birbeck granule morphology (tennis-racket shaped) and LCH IHC markers (CD1a, S100) are high-frequency EM/IHC questions.

subject: Pathology
stem: Lung biopsy from a 60-year-old shipyard worker with progressive exertional dyspnea and bilateral basal crackles shows H&E section with interstitial fibrosis predominantly in the lower lobes. Iron stain (Perl's Prussian blue) highlights elongated structures with a translucent asbestos fiber core coated with iron-protein material and beaded golden-brown appearance, with a 'dumbbell' or 'drumstick' shape. These are called:
explanation: Ferruginous bodies (asbestos bodies) are asbestos fibers coated with iron-containing protein, giving them a golden-brown beaded or dumbbell shape. They are highlighted by Perl's Prussian blue stain (iron stain). Asbestosis causes lower-lobe-predominant pulmonary fibrosis with bilateral pleural plaques (parietal pleura, diaphragm). Associated malignancies: mesothelioma (most specific) and bronchogenic carcinoma (most common). Amphibole fibers (crocidolite) are most carcinogenic.
highYield: Asbestos bodies = ferruginous bodies = golden dumbbell-shaped structures on iron stain. Lower lobe fibrosis + pleural plaques. Mesothelioma is most specific association; lung cancer most common.
pyqNote: Asbestos body morphology (dumbbell/ferruginous body), stain, and disease associations are tested repeatedly in NEET PG.

subject: Pathology
stem: Gross specimen of a kidney from a 55-year-old male smoker shows a large (8 cm) upper pole tumor with a pseudocapsule. The cut surface shows a variegated appearance with yellow-tan areas (lipid-rich), areas of hemorrhage (dark red), and areas of necrosis (gray-white). The tumor is confined to the kidney. H&E section shows cells with clear cytoplasm, distinct cell membranes, and round uniform nuclei arranged in nests separated by delicate blood vessels. The diagnosis is:
options: Urothelial carcinoma of the renal pelvis
explanation: Clear cell RCC (conventional RCC) is the most common renal malignancy in adults (70-80% of RCCs), associated with VHL gene mutation (3p25-26 deletion). Gross features: upper pole, yellow-tan (cholesterol/lipid-rich), variegated with hemorrhage and necrosis. Histology: clear cytoplasm (lipid and glycogen washed out during processing), thin-walled 'chicken-wire' vasculature. Classic paraneoplastic triad: hematuria, flank pain, palpable mass (occurs in only 10% of cases).
highYield: Clear cell RCC: yellow variegated upper pole tumor, clear cells in alveolar pattern, VHL mutation (3p deletion). Most common adult kidney tumor. Lipid and glycogen cause clear cytoplasm on H&E.
pyqNote: Clear cell RCC gross appearance (yellow-tan variegated), histology (clear cells), and VHL association are tested in NEET PG.

subject: Pathology
stem: Pap smear from a 28-year-old female shows superficial squamous cells with large perinuclear halos (cytoplasmic clearing with sharp outer borders) and nuclear abnormalities including nuclear enlargement, hyperchromasia, irregular nuclear membranes, and bi-nucleation. These cytopathic changes in squamous cells are called:
explanation: Koilocytes are the hallmark of productive HPV infection in squamous cells. They show characteristic perinuclear cytoplasmic halos (due to viral E4 protein disrupting cytokeratin) with nuclear abnormalities (enlargement, hyperchromasia, irregular nuclear membrane = nuclear wrinkling or raisinoid). They are found in LSIL (CIN 1) Pap smears. Low-risk HPV types 6 and 11 cause condylomas; high-risk types 16 and 18 cause CIN 2/3 and invasive cancer.
highYield: Koilocyte = perinuclear halo + nuclear hyperchromasia/irregularity = HPV cytopathic effect. Seen in LSIL/CIN1. HPV 16/18 = high risk (cancer). HPV 6/11 = low risk (condyloma).
pyqNote: Koilocyte definition and HPV cytopathic effect is a standard, high-frequency Pap smear question in NEET PG.

subject: Pathology
stem: Cervical biopsy from a 35-year-old female with an abnormal Pap smear showing HSIL shows H&E section with full-thickness replacement of the squamous epithelium by dysplastic cells with high nuclear-cytoplasmic ratio, hyperchromatic irregular nuclei, loss of normal maturation, and atypical mitoses extending to the upper one-third of the epithelium. The basement membrane is intact. This histological appearance is classified as:
explanation: CIN grading is based on the extent of dysplastic cell involvement in the squamous epithelial thickness: CIN 1 (lower 1/3 with koilocytes in superficial layers = LSIL), CIN 2 (lower 2/3 = HSIL), CIN 3/CIS (full thickness replacement, no surface maturation = HSIL). Full-thickness dysplasia with intact basement membrane = CIN 3/carcinoma in situ. Invasion through the basement membrane = invasive SCC. CIN 3 has ~30-35% risk of progression to invasion if untreated.
highYield: CIN grading: CIN1 = lower 1/3 (LSIL); CIN2 = lower 2/3 (HSIL); CIN3/CIS = full thickness (HSIL). Intact BM throughout CIN spectrum. BM breach = invasion = SCC.
pyqNote: CIN grading criteria (by thickness of epithelial involvement) and LSIL/HSIL correlation is a standard NEET PG question.

subject: Pathology
stem: Liver biopsy from a 50-year-old male with portal hypertension and jaundice shows H&E section with loss of normal lobular architecture, formation of spherical nodules of hepatocytes surrounded by fibrous septa. The nodules vary from <3 mm to 1-3 cm in diameter. Trichrome stain highlights the fibrous bands in blue. This end-stage liver pathology is classified as:
explanation: Macronodular cirrhosis has regenerative nodules >3 mm (most >1 cm) and is caused by viral hepatitis (HBV, HCV) and Wilson disease. Micronodular cirrhosis has uniform nodules <3 mm and is caused by alcoholic liver disease and hemochromatosis (the insult is diffuse and uniform). Key features of cirrhosis: regenerative nodules + fibrous septa + distorted architecture. Trichrome stain (Masson's) highlights collagen/fibrous septa in blue.
highYield: Macronodular (>3mm) = viral hepatitis/Wilson. Micronodular (<3mm) = alcohol/hemochromatosis. Cirrhosis = regenerative nodules + fibrous septa on Masson trichrome. Irreversible end-stage.
pyqNote: Distinction between micro vs macronodular cirrhosis and their causes is a high-yield paired question in NEET PG.

subject: Pathology
stem: Renal biopsy from a 35-year-old HIV-positive male with heavy proteinuria (8g/day) shows H&E section with segmental sclerosis (obliteration of capillary lumina by hyaline deposits) affecting only some glomeruli, with collapse of the glomerular tuft particularly in the perihilar region. Electron microscopy shows diffuse foot process effacement similar to minimal change disease. Immunofluorescence shows IgM and C3 in areas of sclerosis. The most likely diagnosis is:
explanation: FSGS (focal = some glomeruli, segmental = part of glomerulus) is a morphological pattern of glomerular injury presenting as nephrotic syndrome. HIV-associated nephropathy (HIVAN) causes a collapsing variant of FSGS with severe podocyte injury. EM shows podocyte foot process effacement (similar to MCD but more extensive injury). The key distinguishing features from MCD are: IF positive (IgM/C3 in sclerotic segments), clinical context, and LM showing sclerosis. FSGS is the most common cause of nephrotic syndrome in African Americans.
highYield: FSGS: focal (some glomeruli) + segmental (part of tuft) sclerosis. HIV-associated = collapsing FSGS. More common in Black patients. Foot process effacement on EM (like MCD but with LM sclerosis).
pyqNote: FSGS vs MCD distinction (FSGS has LM changes + IgM/C3 on IF; MCD has normal LM and negative IF) is frequently tested.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of a thyroid nodule biopsy from a 32-year-old female shows papillary structures lined by cells with characteristic nuclear features: ground-glass or 'Orphan Annie eye' nuclei (large, pale, optically clear nuclei), nuclear grooves, and nuclear pseudoinclusions (intranuclear cytoplasmic invaginations). The tumor cells have overlapping nuclei. A psammoma body (laminated calcification) is also present. The diagnosis is:
explanation: Papillary thyroid carcinoma (PTC) is the most common thyroid malignancy (85%). Its diagnosis rests entirely on nuclear features: Orphan Annie eye nuclei (ground-glass/optically clear), nuclear grooves, and pseudoinclusions. Psammoma bodies (concentrically laminated calcifications) are seen in 50% of cases. RET/PTC translocations and BRAF V600E mutation are the major molecular alterations. PTC spreads via lymphatics to cervical nodes. Prognosis is excellent (>95% survival).
highYield: PTC nuclear triad: Orphan Annie eye nuclei + nuclear grooves + pseudoinclusions. Psammoma bodies + lymph node metastasis. BRAF V600E most common mutation. Excellent prognosis.
pyqNote: PTC nuclear features and psammoma bodies are perennially tested; BRAF mutation association is increasingly common in recent exams.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of a thyroid mass from a 45-year-old male with a family history of thyroid cancer and pheochromocytoma shows nests and sheets of polygonal to spindle-shaped cells with granular cytoplasm separated by stroma. Congo red stain of the stroma shows apple-green birefringence under polarized light. Calcitonin immunostain is strongly positive in the tumor cells. The tumor arises from which cell type?
explanation: Medullary thyroid carcinoma (MTC) arises from parafollicular C cells (neural crest-derived), which produce calcitonin. Amyloid deposits in the stroma are composed of calcitonin fibrils (Congo red positive, apple-green birefringent). MTC is part of MEN 2A (RET mutation, with parathyroid hyperplasia + pheochromocytoma) and MEN 2B. 25% are familial (RET proto-oncogene germline mutation). Serum calcitonin is the tumor marker. Not TSH-dependent.
highYield: MTC = C cells + calcitonin-producing + amyloid stroma (calcitonin amyloid, Congo red+). MEN 2A/2B = RET mutation. Calcitonin is tumor marker. Familial = germline RET testing for family members.
pyqNote: MTC cell of origin (C cells), amyloid stroma, calcitonin marker, and RET/MEN2A association are all tested in NEET PG.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of a breast biopsy shows ducts filled and distended by large pleomorphic cells with high nuclear-cytoplasmic ratio and frequent mitoses. The center of the duct shows central necrosis with dystrophic calcification (forming a cheesy/paste-like plug). The basement membrane of the ducts is intact with no stromal invasion. This pattern of ductal carcinoma in situ is called:
explanation: Comedo DCIS (comedocarcinoma) is the highest-grade pattern of DCIS characterized by high nuclear grade cells (pleomorphic, frequent mitoses) with central necrosis and dystrophic calcification. The name 'comedo' refers to the cheesy plug-like necrotic material that can be expressed from the duct (like a comedo in acne). It is associated with HER2/neu overexpression and has higher risk of progression to invasive carcinoma. Basement membrane remains intact (in situ lesion).
highYield: Comedo DCIS = high-grade DCIS + central necrosis + dystrophic calcification (visible on mammogram) + HER2 overexpression. Highest risk of progression among DCIS subtypes.
pyqNote: Comedo DCIS morphology and its distinction from invasive carcinoma (intact basement membrane) is a tested pathology concept.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of a skin biopsy from a 45-year-old shows a malignant melanocytic proliferation with pagetoid spread (scatter of atypical melanocytes singly and in clusters throughout all layers of the epidermis, including the granular layer). The tumor has invaded the dermis to a depth of 2.5 mm (Clark level IV, Breslow thickness). The cells contain brown pigment (melanin). Immunostain is positive for S100, HMB-45, and Melan-A. The diagnosis is:
options: Basal cell carcinoma with melanin pigment
explanation: Superficial spreading melanoma is the most common type of cutaneous melanoma. Pagetoid spread (atypical melanocytes scattered singly and in nests throughout the epidermis, like Paget cells) is the characteristic pattern. Breslow thickness (measured from granular layer to deepest tumor cell in mm) is the single most important prognostic factor. Melanoma markers: S100 (most sensitive), HMB-45 (most specific), Melan-A, SOX10. BRAF V600E mutation in ~50%.
highYield: Pagetoid spread = melanocytes at all epidermal levels = superficial spreading melanoma. Breslow thickness = most important prognostic factor. S100 (sensitive) and HMB-45 (specific) are key IHC markers.
pyqNote: Melanoma staging by Breslow thickness and Clark levels, plus IHC markers, are tested in NEET PG dermpath.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of a prostate needle biopsy from a 65-year-old male with PSA 18 ng/mL shows glands of variable size, many of which are small with an infiltrative pattern among the benign glands. The malignant glands lack a basal cell layer (confirmed by absence of CK5/14 or p63 staining), show prominent nucleoli, and have luminal blue mucin. The Gleason pattern shows predominantly pattern 4 (fused glands) with some pattern 3. The Gleason score would be:
explanation: Gleason grading assigns a score from 1-5 to the two most predominant patterns, adding them. Pattern 3 = discrete well-formed glands; Pattern 4 = fused glands, cribriform, ill-defined glands; Pattern 5 = solid sheets, single cells, comedonecrosis. Score = primary pattern + secondary pattern. Predominantly pattern 4 with some pattern 3 = 4 (primary) + 3 (secondary) = Gleason score 7, Grade Group 3. Absence of basal cells confirmed by absent p63/CK5 is diagnostic of carcinoma (benign glands have basal cell layer).
highYield: Prostate adenocarcinoma lacks basal cells (p63-, CK5-). Gleason score = primary + secondary pattern. Score 4+3=7 (Grade Group 3) is more aggressive than 3+4=7 (Grade Group 2). PSA is tumor marker.
pyqNote: Gleason grading system and absence of basal cells in prostate adenocarcinoma are tested with increasing frequency in NEET PG.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of an orchiectomy specimen from a 30-year-old male with a painless testicular mass shows sheets of large uniform polygonal cells with clear cytoplasm (due to glycogen), distinct cell membranes, central round nuclei with prominent nucleoli, and intervening fibrous septa with lymphocytic infiltration. Granulomatous reaction is also noted. Tumor cells are PAS positive and show membranous PLAP staining. The diagnosis is:
explanation: Seminoma is the most common testicular germ cell tumor and the most common GCT in cryptorchid testes. Classic histology: large uniform clear cells (glycogen-rich, PAS+) in sheets/lobules separated by fibrous septa infiltrated by lymphocytes, with granulomatous inflammation. PLAP (placental alkaline phosphatase) is the characteristic tumor marker. CD117 (c-kit) and OCT3/4 are also positive. Isochromosome 12p [i(12p)] is the signature chromosomal abnormality.
highYield: Seminoma: PAS+ clear cells + lymphocytic septa + granulomas + PLAP+. Most common testicular GCT. Isochromosome i(12p). Radiosensitive! AFP is NOT raised (AFP elevation = non-seminomatous component).
pyqNote: Seminoma histology, PLAP marker, radiosensitivity, and AFP-negative status are all critical NEET PG distinctions from non-seminomatous GCTs.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of a brain tumor from a 60-year-old male shows a highly cellular glial tumor with marked nuclear pleomorphism, frequent mitoses, areas of geographic (palisading) necrosis with tumor cells arranged around the necrotic foci in a palisading pattern, and areas of endothelial (microvascular) proliferation with glomeruloid vascular tufts. GFAP immunostain is positive. The diagnosis is:
explanation: Glioblastoma (GBM, WHO grade 4) is the most common primary malignant brain tumor in adults. The two cardinal histological features that distinguish GBM from anaplastic astrocytoma (grade 3) are: (1) microvascular/endothelial proliferation (glomeruloid tufts) and (2) necrosis with pseudopalisading (tumor cells arrayed around necrotic foci). GFAP+ confirms glial lineage. IDH wild-type primary GBM has worst prognosis (median survival ~15 months). MGMT promoter methylation predicts response to temozolomide.
highYield: GBM diagnostic features: pseudopalisading necrosis + microvascular proliferation + marked pleomorphism. WHO grade 4. IDH wildtype in primary GBM. MGMT methylation = better TMZ response.
pyqNote: Pseudopalisading necrosis and microvascular proliferation distinguishing GBM from anaplastic astrocytoma is a core NEET PG distinction.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of a dural-based brain tumor from a 55-year-old female shows syncytial whorls of meningothelial cells with uniform oval nuclei, inconspicuous nucleoli, and intranuclear pseudoinclusions. Laminated calcified structures (psammoma bodies) are scattered throughout. The tumor cells are positive for EMA (epithelial membrane antigen) and negative for GFAP. The diagnosis is:
explanation: Meningioma arises from arachnoid cap cells (meningothelial cells) and is the most common primary intracranial tumor overall (but usually benign). Key histological features: syncytial whorls, intranuclear pseudoinclusions, and psammoma bodies (concentric calcifications). IHC: EMA+ and PR (progesterone receptor) positive, GFAP negative. Most are benign WHO grade 1. NF2 mutation is common. Females > Males (2:1). Dural attachment (dural tail sign on MRI).
highYield: Meningioma: syncytial whorls + psammoma bodies + EMA+/GFAP-. Arises from arachnoid cap cells. Dural based. Most common intracranial tumor overall. NF2 mutation common.
pyqNote: Meningioma histology (whorls + psammoma bodies) and EMA positivity with GFAP negativity are standard IHC questions.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of a kidney mass resected from a 4-year-old child shows a characteristic triphasic histology consisting of: (1) blastemal cells (small, round, blue cells with high nuclear-cytoplasmic ratio), (2) stromal component (spindle cells, loose mesenchymal tissue), and (3) epithelial component (tubular and glomeruloid structures). The tumor is well-demarcated from the normal kidney. The diagnosis is:
options: Clear cell sarcoma of the kidney
explanation: Wilms tumor (nephroblastoma) is the most common renal malignancy of childhood (peak age 3-4 years). Classic triphasic histology: blastemal (small blue cells), stromal (spindle/mesenchymal), and epithelial (tubules, glomeruloid structures) components. Associated with WT1 gene mutation, WAGR syndrome (Wilms/Aniridia/GU abnormalities/intellectual Retardation), Beckwith-Wiedemann syndrome (WT2/IGF2), and Denys-Drash syndrome. Anaplasia (nuclear enlargement + atypical mitoses) is an adverse feature.
highYield: Wilms tumor = triphasic (blastemal + stromal + epithelial) + WT1 mutation + WAGR/Beckwith-Wiedemann associations. Most common pediatric renal tumor. Peak age 3-4 years. 85-90% survival with treatment.
pyqNote: Wilms tumor triphasic histology and genetic/syndrome associations are tested in pediatric pathology sections of NEET PG.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of an abdominal mass from a 2-year-old child shows sheets of small round blue cells with dark hyperchromatic nuclei, scant cytoplasm, and areas of neuropil (pink fibrillar background). Some cells form rosette-like structures with central eosinophilic neuropil fibers in the center (Homer Wright rosettes, without a lumen). The tumor cells are positive for NSE, synaptophysin, and chromogranin A. The diagnosis is:
explanation: Neuroblastoma arises from neural crest-derived cells of the adrenal medulla or sympathetic chain. Histology: small round blue cells + neuropil (fibrillar background, pathognomonic) + Homer Wright rosettes (pseudorosettes with central neuropil, no lumen). Neuroendocrine markers: NSE, synaptophysin, chromogranin A. MYCN amplification (>10 copies) is the worst prognostic factor. VMA and HVA are elevated in urine. Most common solid extracranial tumor in children under 2 years.
highYield: Neuroblastoma: Homer Wright rosettes + neuropil + NSE/synaptophysin/chromogranin+. MYCN amplification = poor prognosis. Urine VMA/HVA elevated. Most common extra-cranial solid tumor in children <2 yrs.
pyqNote: Neuroblastoma Homer Wright rosettes (with neuropil center) vs Ewing (Homer Wright with no neuropil) distinction is frequently tested.

subject: Pathology
stem: Bone marrow biopsy from a 65-year-old male with back pain, hypercalcemia, renal failure, anemia, and serum M-protein (IgG kappa) shows H&E section with sheets of plasma cells comprising >30% of the marrow cellularity. The plasma cells show 'clock-face' (cartwheel) chromatin pattern, eccentric nuclei, and a prominent perinuclear hof (Golgi zone). A subset shows large inclusion bodies called Russell bodies (condensed immunoglobulin). Bone marrow biopsy Congo red stain may show amyloid deposition. The diagnosis is:
explanation: Multiple myeloma is a clonal plasma cell neoplasm producing monoclonal immunoglobulin (M-protein). Plasma cell morphology: clock-face chromatin, eccentric nucleus, perinuclear hof (pale Golgi zone), Russell bodies (eosinophilic Ig inclusions). Diagnostic criteria include >10% clonal BM plasma cells + CRAB criteria (hyperCalcemia, Renal failure, Anemia, Bone lesions) or myeloma-defining events. AL amyloidosis (light-chain amyloid) is a complication (Congo red+, apple-green birefringence).
highYield: Myeloma plasma cell: clock-face chromatin + perinuclear hof + Russell bodies. CRAB criteria for diagnosis. AL amyloid is a complication (light-chain deposits). M-protein = diagnostic tool.
pyqNote: Multiple myeloma plasma cell morphology and CRAB criteria are repeatedly tested. AL amyloid in myeloma vs AA in chronic inflammation is a paired question.

subject: Pathology
stem: H&E section of a cystoscopic biopsy from a 60-year-old male smoker with painless hematuria shows papillary fronds lined by urothelium that is 8-10 cell layers thick (normally 3-7 layers) with moderate nuclear pleomorphism, loss of nuclear polarity, and mitoses in the mid-upper layers. The basement membrane is intact. This lesion is classified as:
explanation: High-grade papillary urothelial carcinoma shows papillary architecture with urothelial lining showing marked nuclear pleomorphism, disorganized architecture, loss of polarity, and mitoses at any level. Low-grade tumors have orderly architecture with minimal atypia. Flat CIS (carcinoma in situ) shows full-thickness atypia without papillary architecture. Bladder cancer risk factors: smoking (most common), aromatic amines (aniline dyes), cyclophosphamide, schistosomiasis (squamous cell carcinoma). The intact BM indicates in situ/non-invasive disease.
highYield: High-grade urothelial carcinoma: papillary + marked pleomorphism + disorganized architecture + mitoses at any level. Flat CIS = no papillary growth. Smoking = most common risk factor for TCC.
pyqNote: Grading of urothelial carcinoma and distinction between papillary TCC and flat CIS are tested in NEET PG pathology sections.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 38-year-old woman presents with 8 weeks of symmetric swelling of both wrists and the 2nd and 3rd MCP joints bilaterally. Morning stiffness lasts 90 minutes. ESR is 65 mm/hr, CRP is 18 mg/L. Rheumatoid factor is negative. Anti-CCP antibody is positive at 45 U/mL (normal <20). X-ray shows periarticular osteopenia but no erosions. What is her 2010 ACR/EULAR score and classification status?
options: Score 6 — classified as RA (4 small joints = 3 pts, positive anti-CCP high titer = 3 pts, abnormal ESR = 1 pt, duration >6 weeks = 1 pt; total exceeds threshold of 6)
Score 4 — not classified as RA; requires at least one erosion on X-ray to fulfill criteria
Score 5 — not classified as RA; negative RF reduces score below threshold despite anti-CCP positivity
Score 8 — classified as RA; large joint involvement provides additional points under the 2010 criteria
explanation: The 2010 ACR/EULAR RA criteria score: joint involvement (4 small joints = 3 pts), serology (high-positive anti-CCP ≥3× ULN = 3 pts), acute phase reactants (abnormal CRP or ESR = 1 pt), symptom duration ≥6 weeks (1 pt) = total 8 pts. A score ≥6 classifies as definite RA. Erosions are NOT required for classification. Anti-CCP positivity alone carries 2 pts (low positive) or 3 pts (high positive, >3× ULN). RF negativity is irrelevant when anti-CCP is positive.
highYield: 2010 ACR/EULAR: score ≥6 = definite RA. High-positive anti-CCP (>3× ULN) = 3 points alone. Erosions NOT required.
pyqNote: RA classification criteria tested frequently; 2010 criteria replaced 1987 criteria — know the scoring domains.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 45-year-old man is being evaluated for possible rheumatoid arthritis. His physician orders both rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide (anti-CCP) antibody. Which of the following best describes the comparative diagnostic utility of these two tests?
options: Anti-CCP has higher specificity (~95%) than RF (~85%) for RA, while RF has modestly higher sensitivity; anti-CCP also predicts erosive disease and appears years before symptoms
RF has higher specificity (~95%) than anti-CCP (~70%) and is the preferred single test for early RA diagnosis
Both tests have equivalent sensitivity and specificity of approximately 70% and 85% respectively and are interchangeable
Anti-CCP is more sensitive but less specific than RF; RF is preferred when confirming established RA rather than screening
explanation: Anti-CCP antibody has specificity of approximately 95-98% for RA versus RF at approximately 85%. RF sensitivity is slightly higher (~70-80%) compared to anti-CCP (~60-70%). Anti-CCP is particularly valuable because it can precede clinical RA by up to 10 years and is a strong predictor of erosive, destructive disease. RF can be positive in many other conditions (Sjogren's, SBE, chronic liver disease, sarcoidosis) reducing its specificity. Double positivity (RF+ and anti-CCP+) carries the worst prognosis.
highYield: Anti-CCP: specificity ~95% (higher than RF ~85%). Anti-CCP appears years before RA onset and predicts erosive disease.
pyqNote: Anti-CCP vs RF comparison is a recurring single-best-answer topic in NEET PG Medicine.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 24-year-old woman presents with malar rash, photosensitivity, painless oral ulcers, and left-sided pleuritic chest pain. Labs: ANA 1:320 (homogeneous), anti-dsDNA positive, complement C3 and C4 low, urine shows 2+ protein and RBC casts. Platelet count is 88,000/µL. Using SLICC 2012 criteria, how many criteria does she fulfill and does she meet classification for SLE?
options: She fulfills 8 SLICC criteria (malar rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, pleuritis, nephritis with RBC casts, thrombocytopenia, positive ANA, positive anti-dsDNA) — meets classification requiring ≥4 criteria (at least 1 clinical + 1 immunological)
She fulfills 6 SLICC criteria but requires biopsy-proven lupus nephritis plus ANA or anti-dsDNA positivity alone to classify as SLE
She fulfills 5 ACR 1997 criteria (malar rash, photosensitivity, serositis, renal disorder, thrombocytopenia) and SLICC 2012 requires the same threshold of 4 criteria from 11 domains
She does not meet SLICC 2012 classification because she lacks anti-Sm antibody, which is required as part of the immunological criteria
explanation: SLICC 2012 requires ≥4 of 11 criteria (combining clinical and immunological), with at least 1 clinical AND 1 immunological criterion; OR biopsy-proven lupus nephritis with ANA or anti-dsDNA. Clinical criteria include: malar rash, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, non-scarring alopecia, synovitis, serositis (pleuritis), acute cutaneous lupus, chronic cutaneous lupus, renal (proteinuria or RBC casts), neurological, hemolytic anemia, leukopenia, thrombocytopenia. Immunological: ANA, anti-dsDNA, anti-Sm, antiphospholipid Ab, low complement, direct Coombs. This patient fulfills at least 8 criteria. Anti-Sm is not required.
highYield: SLICC 2012: ≥4 criteria (≥1 clinical + ≥1 immunological) OR biopsy-proven LN + ANA/anti-dsDNA. SLICC replaced ACR 1997 (which needed 4 of 11).
pyqNote: SLE classification criteria — know SLICC 2012 vs ACR 1997 differences; SLICC has 11 clinical + 6 immunological domains.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 32-year-old woman with SLE has had two episodes of deep vein thrombosis and one unexplained miscarriage at 14 weeks gestation. Labs show: lupus anticoagulant positive on two occasions 12 weeks apart, anti-cardiolipin IgG 85 GPL units (high positive), and anti-beta2 glycoprotein-I IgG strongly positive. She is on hydroxychloroquine. Which of the following best describes her APS status and its clinical significance?
options: She has triple-positive APS (LA + aCL + anti-β2GPI) — this carries the highest thrombotic risk and risk of recurrence; long-term anticoagulation with warfarin (target INR 2-3) is indicated
She has double-positive APS (LA + aCL); anti-β2GPI does not contribute to APS classification and its positivity is incidental
She meets laboratory criteria but only one clinical criterion (DVT) is required for APS — the miscarriage at 14 weeks is not a qualifying obstetric criterion
Triple positivity reduces the pre-test probability of true APS as false positivity increases with the number of positive tests; single confirmatory test suffices for classification
explanation: APS (Sapporo/revised Miyakis criteria) requires ≥1 clinical criterion (vascular thrombosis OR obstetric morbidity) AND ≥1 lab criterion (LA, aCL IgG/IgM ≥40 GPL/MPL, or anti-β2GPI IgG/IgM ≥99th percentile) on 2 occasions ≥12 weeks apart. Obstetric criteria include ≥1 unexplained fetal loss at ≥10 weeks gestation — this patient qualifies. Triple positivity (LA + aCL + anti-β2GPI) confers the highest risk of thrombotic recurrence. Standard anticoagulation target is INR 2-3; some guidelines suggest 3-4 for arterial events.
highYield: Triple positivity (LA + aCL + anti-β2GPI) = highest APS risk. Obstetric APS includes fetal loss ≥10 weeks. Lab criteria must be confirmed ≥12 weeks apart.
pyqNote: APS classification and triple positivity significance frequently tested; distinguish obstetric vs thrombotic APS.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 55-year-old man presents with persistent sinusitis for 6 months, saddle-nose deformity, hemoptysis, and hematuria. Chest CT shows cavitating nodules bilaterally. Serology shows c-ANCA positive with anti-PR3 antibody strongly reactive. Renal biopsy reveals pauci-immune crescentic glomerulonephritis. What is the diagnosis and the characteristic ANCA pattern?
options: Granulomatosis with polyangiitis (GPA, formerly Wegener's) — PR3-ANCA (c-ANCA pattern) is characteristic; classic triad is upper airway + lung + kidney involvement with necrotizing granulomas
Microscopic polyangiitis (MPA) — MPO-ANCA (p-ANCA pattern) is characteristic; cavitating pulmonary nodules are the hallmark distinguishing feature from GPA
Eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA, Churg-Strauss) — PR3-ANCA is seen in 40% of cases; sinusitis, asthma, and eosinophilia form the classic triad
Polyarteritis nodosa (PAN) — ANCA positivity with PR3 pattern confirms this diagnosis; renal involvement with pauci-immune GN is the typical renal lesion
explanation: GPA (Wegener's) is associated with PR3-ANCA (c-ANCA pattern) in ~90% of cases. The classic clinical triad is upper airway disease (sinusitis, nasal septum perforation, saddle-nose), lower respiratory tract (pulmonary nodules, often cavitating), and renal involvement (pauci-immune crescentic GN). MPA is associated with MPO-ANCA (p-ANCA) and lacks granulomas/upper airway involvement. EGPA has MPO-ANCA in 40%, requires asthma and eosinophilia. PAN is ANCA-negative and does not cause GN.
highYield: GPA = PR3-ANCA (c-ANCA). MPA = MPO-ANCA (p-ANCA). EGPA = MPO-ANCA (40%). PAN is ANCA-negative. All three AAV show pauci-immune GN.
pyqNote: PR3 vs MPO ANCA association with specific vasculitides is high-yield for NEET PG.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 28-year-old man presents with hemoptysis and rapidly worsening renal function. Serum creatinine rose from 1.2 to 4.8 mg/dL over 3 weeks. Urine shows RBC casts and 3+ proteinuria. Anti-GBM antibody is strongly positive. ANCA is negative. Renal biopsy shows crescentic GN with linear IgG deposits along the GBM on immunofluorescence. What is the RPGN type and appropriate treatment?
options: Type I RPGN (anti-GBM disease / Goodpasture syndrome) — linear IgG on IF is pathognomonic; treatment is plasma exchange + cyclophosphamide + corticosteroids to remove circulating anti-GBM antibodies
Type II RPGN (immune complex) — granular IgG deposition confirms immune complex–mediated disease; treatment is corticosteroids alone
Type III RPGN (pauci-immune) — anti-GBM positivity is a non-specific finding; linear staining on IF is seen in all types of RPGN and does not distinguish subtypes
Type I RPGN — treatment is corticosteroids only; plasma exchange is reserved for dialysis-dependent patients with >50% crescents on biopsy
explanation: RPGN Type I (anti-GBM disease) is characterized by linear IgG deposition along the GBM on immunofluorescence, circulating anti-GBM antibodies, and clinical presentation of pulmonary-renal syndrome (Goodpasture syndrome). Type II shows granular IgG deposits (immune complex — lupus, IgA nephropathy, post-strep). Type III shows scanty/absent immunoglobulin (pauci-immune, ANCA-associated). Treatment for anti-GBM disease is urgent plasma exchange (to remove antibodies) combined with immunosuppression (cyclophosphamide + steroids). Delayed treatment leads to irreversible renal loss.
highYield: RPGN Type I = linear IgG (anti-GBM). Type II = granular (immune complex). Type III = pauci-immune (ANCA). Treatment of Type I: plasma exchange + CYC + steroids.
pyqNote: IF pattern on renal biopsy distinguishing RPGN types is a classic NEET PG question format.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 58-year-old diabetic woman has an eGFR of 28 mL/min/1.73m² on two measurements taken 3 months apart. Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR) is 220 mg/g. According to KDIGO 2012 CKD staging, what is her CKD G and A stage, and what does this imply for her management?
options: G3b A3 — eGFR 15-29 corresponds to G4; wait — eGFR 28 is G3b (eGFR 15-29 is G4, eGFR 30-44 is G3b); the patient has eGFR 28, which falls in G4 (15-29), with UACR 220 mg/g placing her in A3 (>300 mg/g) — actually A2 (30-300 mg/g)
G4 A2 — eGFR 28 mL/min/1.73m² falls in G4 (eGFR 15-29); UACR 220 mg/g falls in A2 (30-300 mg/g); G4A2 = high-risk CKD with nephrology referral indicated
G3a A2 — eGFR 28 falls in G3a (45-59); UACR 220 mg/g is A2; this is moderate-risk CKD managed entirely in primary care
G3b A3 — eGFR 28 is G3b (30-44); UACR 220 mg/g (>200) qualifies as severely increased albuminuria (A3)
explanation: KDIGO 2012 eGFR categories: G1 ≥90, G2 60-89, G3a 45-59, G3b 30-44, G4 15-29, G5 <15 mL/min/1.73m². eGFR of 28 falls in G4. Albuminuria categories: A1 <30 mg/g (normal to mildly increased), A2 30-300 mg/g (moderately increased), A3 >300 mg/g (severely increased). UACR of 220 mg/g = A2. G4 A2 = high risk (orange in KDIGO heat map); nephrology referral, preparation for renal replacement therapy, and aggressive cardiovascular risk factor management are indicated.
highYield: KDIGO G stages: G4 = eGFR 15-29. Albuminuria A2 = UACR 30-300 mg/g. G4 triggers nephrology referral and RRT preparation.
pyqNote: CKD staging eGFR cutoffs and albuminuria categories are directly tested in NEET PG.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 65-year-old man undergoes elective hip replacement. Pre-operative creatinine was 0.9 mg/dL. On post-operative day 2, his creatinine is 1.8 mg/dL. Urine output over the last 12 hours was 0.3 mL/kg/hr. He is hemodynamically stable. What is his AKI stage according to KDIGO 2012?
options: AKI Stage 2 — creatinine has risen to ≥2× baseline (1.8/0.9 = 2-fold); urine output of 0.3 mL/kg/hr for 12 hours meets Stage 2 urine output criterion (<0.5 mL/kg/hr for ≥12 hours); use the higher stage
AKI Stage 1 — creatinine rise of 0.9 mg/dL within 48 hours meets Stage 1 criterion (rise ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours); urine output does not yet meet criteria
AKI Stage 3 — post-surgical creatinine elevation always qualifies as Stage 3 regardless of degree; surgical AKI carries the highest mortality
No AKI — the creatinine rise is expected after surgery and does not meet KDIGO minimum threshold; baseline creatinine must be established over 7 days not 48 hours for classification
explanation: KDIGO AKI definition: rise in creatinine ≥0.3 mg/dL within 48 hours, OR rise to ≥1.5× baseline within 7 days, OR urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr for ≥6 hours. Staging — Stage 1: Cr ≥1.5-1.9× baseline OR ≥0.3 mg/dL rise (UO <0.5 mL/kg/hr ≥6h); Stage 2: Cr 2.0-2.9× baseline (UO <0.5 mL/kg/hr ≥12h); Stage 3: Cr ≥3× baseline or ≥4.0 mg/dL or RRT needed. Here, creatinine doubled (2×) = Stage 2 by creatinine; UO 0.3 mL/kg/hr for 12 hours = Stage 2 by UO. Highest stage applies = Stage 2.
highYield: KDIGO AKI Stage 2: creatinine 2-2.9× baseline OR UO <0.5 mL/kg/hr for ≥12 hours. Always use the highest stage from either criterion.
pyqNote: AKI KDIGO staging criteria with creatinine and urine output thresholds are tested numerically in NEET PG.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 50-year-old man with CKD stage 5 presents to the emergency department. His serum potassium is 6.8 mEq/L with peaked T waves on ECG. He has severe metabolic acidosis (pH 7.10, bicarbonate 8 mEq/L) refractory to bicarbonate therapy. He is confused and has bilateral leg edema with pulmonary crackles. BUN is 110 mg/dL. He denies taking any nephrotoxins. Which mnemonic best captures ALL the dialysis indications present in this patient?
options: AEIOU — Acidosis (pH 7.10, refractory), Electrolytes (K+ 6.8 with ECG changes), Ingestion/Intoxication (not present), Overload (pulmonary edema), Uremia (encephalopathy + BUN 110); he meets A, E, O, U — 4 of 5 indications
MUDPILES — metabolic acidosis is the only dialysis indication; the other findings are managed with conservative therapy before initiating dialysis
AEIOU with I present — the CKD itself constitutes chronic ingestion/intoxication with uremic toxins, making all 5 AEIOU criteria met
RIFLE criteria — the patient meets the F (Failure) stage of RIFLE and dialysis is automatically indicated at RIFLE-F regardless of other parameters
explanation: AEIOU mnemonic for emergency dialysis indications: A = Acidosis (pH <7.1 or refractory metabolic acidosis); E = Electrolytes (hyperkalemia refractory to medical treatment, especially with ECG changes); I = Ingestion/Intoxication (toxins — methanol, ethylene glycol, lithium, salicylates); O = Overload (volume overload/pulmonary edema refractory to diuretics); U = Uremia (encephalopathy, pericarditis, bleeding). This patient has A (pH 7.10 refractory), E (K+ 6.8 with ECG changes), O (pulmonary edema), and U (confusion = uremic encephalopathy). Meeting even one indication warrants urgent dialysis.
highYield: AEIOU dialysis indications: Acidosis (pH<7.1), Electrolytes (K+>6.5 with ECG changes), Intoxication, Overload, Uremia. Any one indication is sufficient.
pyqNote: AEIOU mnemonic for dialysis indications is directly tested; know each component and the exact thresholds.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with sudden palpitations, mild dizziness, and anxiety. ECG shows: regular narrow complex tachycardia at 155 bpm, P waves are visible AFTER each QRS complex with a short RP interval (RP < PR), P waves appear inverted in leads II, III, aVF with apparent pseudo-S wave pattern in inferior leads and pseudo-R' in V1. BP is 110/70 mmHg. She has had 3 similar episodes in the past that resolved spontaneously. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Typical AVNRT (slow-fast) — the short RP interval with P waves immediately after QRS (RP <70 ms) and pseudo-S/pseudo-R' pattern is pathognomonic; the reentry circuit is entirely within the AV node
AVRT (orthodromic) using a concealed accessory pathway — short RP with retrograde P waves; distinguishable from AVNRT by RP interval >70 ms and P waves visible in the ST segment
Atrial flutter with 2:1 block — regular narrow complex tachycardia at 150 bpm with visible flutter waves that can mimic P waves in inferior leads
Sinus tachycardia with first-degree AV block — the prolonged PR interval causes P waves to appear after the preceding QRS, creating a pseudo-short RP pattern
explanation: Typical AVNRT (slow-fast) is the most common SVT in young women. The defining ECG feature is RP interval <70 ms (P wave nearly buried in QRS), producing pseudo-S waves in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) and pseudo-R' in V1 due to retrograde atrial activation. In orthodromic AVRT, the P wave is visible in the ST segment (RP 70-120 ms) because retrograde conduction through the accessory pathway is slower. AVNRT rate is typically 150-250 bpm. Vagal maneuvers or adenosine terminate it. Distinction from AVRT: AVNRT RP <70 ms; AVRT RP >70 ms.
highYield: AVNRT: RP <70 ms, pseudo-S in II/III/aVF, pseudo-R' in V1. AVRT: RP 70-120 ms, P visible in ST segment. Both respond to adenosine.
pyqNote: ECG distinction between AVNRT and AVRT using RP interval is a classic NEET PG cardiology question.

subject: Medicine
stem: During an electrophysiology study for recurrent SVT, a 30-year-old man is found to have a concealed accessory pathway. During induced tachycardia, the VA interval (ventricle to atrium conduction time) is 120 ms and P waves are clearly visible in the ST segment at a rate of 165 bpm with a narrow QRS complex. The tachycardia is terminated by adenosine. What is the mechanism?
options: Orthodromic AVRT — anterograde conduction via the AV node (narrow QRS), retrograde conduction via the accessory pathway; VA interval >70 ms with visible ST-segment P waves distinguishes this from AVNRT
Antidromic AVRT — anterograde conduction via the accessory pathway produces a wide QRS with delta waves; narrow QRS rules out antidromic AVRT
Typical AVNRT — VA interval of 120 ms is within the AVNRT range; the slow retrograde limb causes delayed atrial activation with visible P waves
Atrial tachycardia — the regular rate, visible P waves, and adenosine termination confirm an ectopic atrial focus as the mechanism
explanation: Orthodromic AVRT involves anterograde conduction through the AV node (producing narrow QRS) and retrograde conduction through the accessory pathway (causing delayed atrial activation). VA interval >70 ms (here 120 ms) and P waves visible in the ST segment are characteristic. In typical AVNRT, retrograde conduction via the slow pathway is extremely fast, making VA interval <70 ms (P buried in QRS). Antidromic AVRT uses the accessory pathway anterogradely, producing wide complex (delta wave) tachycardia. Adenosine terminates both AVNRT and AVRT by blocking AV nodal conduction.
highYield: Orthodromic AVRT: narrow QRS + VA >70 ms + P in ST segment. AVNRT: narrow QRS + VA <70 ms + P buried in QRS. Antidromic AVRT: wide QRS.
pyqNote: AVRT vs AVNRT electrophysiology distinction via VA interval is tested in advanced NEET PG cardiology scenarios.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with an episode of right optic neuritis (loss of vision with pain on eye movement) 6 months ago, and now has bilateral lower limb weakness and bladder dysfunction for 3 weeks. MRI brain shows: periventricular and juxtacortical T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesions, one with incomplete ring enhancement on gadolinium. MRI spine shows a T2 lesion at C5-C6 spanning <2 vertebral segments. CSF shows 8 oligoclonal bands not present in serum. AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG are negative. What is the diagnosis and the criteria fulfilled?
options: Multiple sclerosis (McDonald 2017 criteria) — dissemination in space (DIS: periventricular + juxtacortical + spinal cord lesions), dissemination in time (DIT: enhancing + non-enhancing lesions simultaneously OR two clinical attacks), CSF oligoclonal bands support DIT; AQP4/MOG negativity excludes NMOSD and MOGAD
NMOSD (neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder) — optic neuritis + spinal cord involvement is the classic NMOSD pattern; AQP4-IgG negativity does not exclude seronegative NMOSD
MOGAD (MOG antibody disease) — MOG-IgG negativity at presentation does not exclude MOGAD; the incomplete ring enhancement and short cord lesion are characteristic of MOGAD

Clinically isolated syndrome (CIS) — only one clinical attack has occurred (the second episode has not been confirmed as MS-defining); McDonald criteria require at least 2 separate clinical attacks for diagnosis
explanation: McDonald 2017 criteria for MS require DIS and DIT. DIS can be demonstrated by MRI lesions in ≥2 of 4 CNS regions (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord) OR two clinical attacks in different locations. DIT can be demonstrated by simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions OR new T2/enhancing lesion on follow-up OR two clinical attacks. CSF oligoclonal bands can replace DIT evidence when DIS is met. Short spinal cord lesions (<3 vertebral segments) and periventricular lesions favor MS over NMOSD. AQP4/MOG negativity further supports MS over NMOSD/MOGAD.
highYield: MS (McDonald 2017): DIS in ≥2 of 4 regions + DIT. CSF OCBs can fulfill DIT criterion. Short cord lesions (<3 segments) = MS. Long cord lesions (≥3 segments) = NMOSD.
pyqNote: MS vs NMOSD distinction using lesion length, AQP4-IgG, and McDonald criteria is high-yield and frequently tested.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 35-year-old woman presents with bilateral visual loss and a severe longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis involving T4-T10 (6 vertebral segments on MRI). She also has intractable hiccups and nausea. AQP4-IgG is strongly positive at 1:512. MRI brain shows no periventricular lesions. CSF has no oligoclonal bands. Which diagnosis is correct and what distinguishes it from MS?
options: NMOSD (AQP4-IgG seropositive) — longitudinally extensive cord lesion (≥3 segments), area postrema syndrome (hiccups/nausea), bilateral optic neuritis, and AQP4-IgG positivity are hallmarks; periventricular lesions and OCBs are absent (contrast with MS)
MS — bilateral optic neuritis and longitudinal myelitis are atypical for MS but do occur; AQP4-IgG may be false-positive at low titers; McDonald criteria should be applied
MOGAD — MOG-IgG should be tested before AQP4-IgG in this demographic; area postrema syndrome occurs only in MOGAD
Neurosarcoidosis — hiccups and bilateral optic involvement with longitudinal myelitis constitute the classic sarcoid CNS presentation; AQP4-IgG cross-reacts with sarcoid granulomas
explanation: NMOSD is defined by ≥1 core clinical characteristic plus AQP4-IgG seropositivity (or ≥2 core features without seropositivity). Core features: optic neuritis, acute myelitis, area postrema syndrome (hiccups/nausea/vomiting), acute brainstem syndrome, symptomatic narcolepsy/diencephalic syndrome, symptomatic cerebral syndrome. Longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (LETM, ≥3 vertebral segments) is characteristic. MS features periventricular lesions, OCBs, and short cord lesions. Area postrema involvement (intractable hiccups/nausea) is almost pathognomonic for NMOSD. High-titer AQP4-IgG confirms the diagnosis.
highYield: NMOSD hallmarks: LETM (≥3 segments), area postrema syndrome (hiccups), bilateral ON, AQP4-IgG positive, no OCBs, no periventricular lesions.
pyqNote: Area postrema syndrome (hiccups + nausea) as a distinguishing NMOSD feature is directly tested in NEET PG.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 23-year-old woman develops high fever, severe headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgias, and a maculopapular rash on day 3 of illness. She lives in a dengue-endemic area. Which serological/antigen test is most appropriate NOW to confirm the diagnosis, and what is the timing of dengue markers?
options: NS1 antigen test — NS1 is detectable from day 1 to day 5 of fever (early viraemic phase); IgM antibody appears from day 3-5 and peaks at day 2 weeks; on day 3, NS1 + IgM may both be positive but NS1 is the highest-yield early test; IgG appears later and persists for life
IgM antibody only — NS1 antigen is only reliable after day 7 of fever; performing NS1 early will give false-negative results
IgG antibody — high IgG titer on day 3 of a second dengue infection (secondary dengue) is the most sensitive early marker and should be ordered first
PCR only — dengue serology is unreliable before day 5; RT-PCR is the only validated early diagnostic test but requires specialized laboratory infrastructure
explanation: Dengue diagnostic timeline: NS1 antigen is detectable from day 1 of fever and remains positive up to day 5-6 (during viraemia). IgM antibody appears from day 3-5 and is most useful after day 5. IgG appears later in primary infection but in secondary dengue, a 4-fold rise in IgG (or high IgG on the first sample) is characteristic. On day 3, NS1 is the most reliable test. A combined NS1 + IgM/IgG rapid test has sensitivity >90% when used from day 1-7. PCR is the gold standard but not routine. NS1 sensitivity decreases after day 5.
highYield: Dengue: NS1 antigen detectable day 1-5 (early). IgM appears day 3-5. IgG persists lifelong. Day 1-5 = NS1; Day 5+ = IgM. Secondary dengue = early IgG rise.
pyqNote: Dengue NS1 vs IgM timing is a high-frequency NEET PG question; know the day-by-day serology profile.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 30-year-old man returns from Assam with fever, chills, and rigors every 48 hours. Blood smear shows Plasmodium vivax ring forms and gametocytes. He is treated with chloroquine 25 mg/kg over 3 days with resolution of fever. However, 6 weeks later, symptoms recur and repeat smear shows P. vivax. He denies re-exposure. G6PD level is normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Primaquine 0.25 mg/kg/day for 14 days (standard radical cure) — the relapse is due to dormant hypnozoites; chloroquine has no activity against hypnozoites; primaquine is the only drug effective for radical cure; confirm G6PD before use
Repeat chloroquine course with higher dose (total 50 mg/kg) — this is chloroquine-resistant P. vivax; higher-dose chloroquine is effective for resistant strains from Northeast India
Artemether-lumefantrine for 3 days — ACT is now recommended for all P. vivax in India regardless of relapse to avoid resistance
Tafenoquine single dose 300 mg — this is the preferred anti-relapse agent and has replaced primaquine in Indian national guidelines as first-line for hypnozoite clearance
explanation: P. vivax and P. ovale form hypnozoites in the liver that cause relapses weeks to months after the primary infection. Chloroquine treats the blood-stage (erythrocytic) infection only. Primaquine (8-aminoquinoline) is required for radical cure — eliminating hypnozoites. Standard dose: 0.25 mg/kg/day × 14 days. G6PD testing is mandatory before primaquine (causes hemolysis in G6PD deficiency). In chloroquine-resistant P. vivax (common in Papua New Guinea, emerging in India), ACT is used for blood stages; primaquine dose may be doubled to 0.5 mg/kg/day × 14 days per WHO. Tafenoquine is not in current Indian National Vector Borne Disease Control Programme guidelines.
highYield: P. vivax relapse = hypnozoites not killed by chloroquine. Primaquine 0.25 mg/kg/day × 14 days = radical cure. Always check G6PD before primaquine.
pyqNote: Primaquine for P. vivax radical cure and G6PD prerequisite is a standard NEET PG infectious disease question.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 28-year-old farmer presents after working in flooded rice fields. He has high fever, severe myalgia (especially calf muscles), conjunctival suffusion, jaundice, oliguria, and thrombocytopenia. Serum bilirubin is 18 mg/dL (predominantly direct), creatinine is 4.2 mg/dL, and CK is elevated. Which diagnosis does this clinical picture represent, and what is the name of its severe form?
options: Leptospirosis — Weil's disease is the severe icteric form characterized by the triad of jaundice + AKI + bleeding tendency; conjunctival suffusion and calf muscle tenderness are classic early features; treatment is IV penicillin G or ceftriaxone
Scrub typhus — eschar and regional lymphadenopathy are absent but the fever, myalgia, and thrombocytopenia in a field worker are classic; severe form is Sims-Hogg triad
Malaria with cerebral involvement — jaundice and AKI define severe malaria by WHO criteria; calf muscle tenderness is a non-specific feature commonly seen in falciparum malaria
Hantavirus infection — the clinical picture of AKI, thrombocytopenia, and rodent/floodwater exposure defines hantavirus hemorrhagic fever with renal syndrome
explanation: Leptospirosis is transmitted through contact with water or soil contaminated by urine of infected animals (rats). Classic early features: fever, severe myalgia (especially calf muscles), conjunctival suffusion (redness without discharge — pathognomonic), and headache. Weil's disease is the severe icteric form: jaundice (hepatocellular + cholestatic, predominantly direct bilirubin), AKI (non-oliguric or oliguric), and hemorrhagic tendency (thrombocytopenia, bleeding). Elevated CK reflects rhabdomyolysis. Treatment: IV benzylpenicillin 1.5 MU q6h or ceftriaxone 1g/day. Doxycycline 200 mg/week is used for prophylaxis.
highYield: Weil's disease (severe leptospirosis): jaundice + AKI + hemorrhage triad. Conjunctival suffusion = pathognomonic early sign. Treatment: IV penicillin G or ceftriaxone.
pyqNote: Weil's disease features and conjunctival suffusion as pathognomonic sign are directly tested in NEET PG.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 25-year-old man presents from a rural hilly area with 10 days of high fever, severe headache, diffuse maculopapular rash starting on the trunk, and painful axillary lymphadenopathy. On examination, a painless black eschar is found in the groin. Weil-Felix test (OX-K) is positive. What is the diagnosis, the causative organism, and treatment of choice?
options: Scrub typhus — caused by Orientia tsutsugamushi (formerly Rickettsia tsutsugamushi), transmitted by larval trombiculid mites (chiggers); classic tetrad: fever + rash + eschar + lymphadenopathy; Weil-Felix OX-K positive; treatment is doxycycline 100 mg BD for 7-14 days
Murine typhus — caused by Rickettsia typhi, transmitted by rat fleas; eschar does not occur; Weil-Felix OX-19 and OX-2 are positive but OX-K is negative
Rocky Mountain spotted fever — caused by Rickettsia rickettsii; eschar is characteristic; Weil-Felix OX-19 and OX-2 reactive; predominantly seen in the Americas not India
Epidemic typhus — Rickettsia prowazekii transmitted by body louse; eschar is not a feature; Weil-Felix OX-19 strongly positive; occurs in overcrowded conditions
explanation: Scrub typhus is caused by Orientia tsutsugamushi transmitted by larval trombiculid mites. The classic tetrad is: fever, rash (maculopapular, centrifugal spread), eschar (painless black necrotic lesion at bite site — found in groin, axilla, behind ears), and lymphadenopathy (regional or generalized). Weil-Felix OX-K agglutination is positive (specific to scrub typhus; OX-19 and OX-2 are for epidemic and murine typhus). Treatment: doxycycline 100 mg twice daily for 7-14 days; alternative: azithromycin (preferred in pregnancy/children). India (especially Northeast, Himalayas) is highly endemic.
highYield: Scrub typhus tetrad: fever + rash + eschar + lymphadenopathy. OX-K positive. Treatment: doxycycline. Organism: Orientia tsutsugamushi.
pyqNote: Scrub typhus eschar location, Weil-Felix OX-K pattern, and doxycycline treatment are classic NEET PG questions.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 35-year-old woman with central obesity, purple striae, and proximal myopathy has elevated 24-hour urinary free cortisol (3× upper normal). Low-dose dexamethasone suppression test (1 mg overnight): cortisol 8.5 µg/dL (non-suppressed, confirms Cushing's). High-dose DST (8 mg overnight) is performed: cortisol suppresses from 32 µg/dL to 13 µg/dL (a 59% reduction). ACTH is 48 pg/mL (mildly elevated). What is the most likely source of Cushing's syndrome?
options: Pituitary Cushing's disease (Cushing's disease) — high-dose DST showing >50% suppression of cortisol indicates the pituitary corticotroph adenoma retains some feedback sensitivity; ectopic ACTH sources and adrenal causes do NOT suppress on high-dose DST; MRI pituitary is the next step
Ectopic ACTH syndrome — ACTH of 48 pg/mL with partial suppression (59%) on high-dose DST is consistent with bronchial carcinoid secreting ACTH ectopically; CT chest is the next step
Adrenal adenoma — adrenal sources of Cushing's are ACTH-independent; ACTH would be suppressed (<10 pg/mL) in adrenal Cushing's; high-dose DST result does not apply to adrenal causes
Pseudo-Cushing's syndrome — high-dose DST suppression >50% indicates physiological stress response rather than true autonomous cortisol hypersecretion; further workup is not warranted
explanation: High-dose DST (8 mg) interpretation: >50% suppression of baseline cortisol indicates Cushing's disease (pituitary origin) in ~80-90% of cases. Ectopic ACTH tumors (small cell lung cancer, bronchial carcinoid) typically do NOT suppress, even with high-dose DST. Adrenal causes show suppressed ACTH (<10 pg/mL) and are ACTH-independent. This patient has ACTH-dependent Cushing's (ACTH 48, mildly elevated) with >50% suppression = pituitary source. Next step: MRI pituitary. If MRI is negative, bilateral inferior petrosal sinus sampling (IPSS) is required. Cortisol threshold: suppression to <50% of baseline on HDDST = does NOT suppress.
highYield: High-dose DST: >50% suppression = pituitary Cushing's disease. No suppression = ectopic ACTH or adrenal. ACTH helps: elevated = ACTH-dependent; suppressed = ACTH-independent (adrenal).
pyqNote: High-dose DST interpretation and differentiating pituitary vs ectopic Cushing's is a classic NEET PG endocrinology question.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 28-year-old man with a history of kidney stones presents with hypercalcemia (serum Ca 11.8 mg/dL) and elevated PTH. Gastrin levels are markedly elevated with recurrent peptic ulcers despite proton pump inhibitor therapy. MRI shows a 0.5 cm lesion in the pancreatic head. His father died of 'endocrine cancer.' What syndrome does this patient most likely have and what are the three components of its classic triad?
options: MEN1 (Wermer syndrome) — classic triad of 3Ps: Parathyroid hyperplasia/adenoma (most common, causing hypercalcemia), Pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma most common), Pancreatic islet cell tumors (gastrinoma causing Zollinger-Ellison syndrome most dangerous); autosomal dominant mutation in MEN1 (menin) gene on chromosome 11q13
MEN2A (Sipple syndrome) — triad of Medullary thyroid carcinoma + Pheochromocytoma + Hyperparathyroidism; pancreatic tumors are not a feature of MEN2A; RET proto-oncogene mutation on chromosome 10
MEN2B — triad of Medullary thyroid carcinoma + Pheochromocytoma + Mucosal neuromas (marfanoid habitus); hyperparathyroidism and pancreatic tumors are absent; also caused by RET mutation

Sporadic Zollinger-Ellison syndrome — the combination of gastrinoma + hyperparathyroidism is coincidental; familial endocrine tumors in this pattern are seen in hereditary hyperparathyroidism-jaw tumor syndrome
explanation: MEN1 (Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia Type 1) is caused by germline mutations in the MEN1 gene (chromosome 11q13, tumor suppressor encoding menin). Classic triad: Parathyroid (95-100%, most common — hyperparathyroidism from hyperplasia/adenoma), Pituitary (30-40%, usually prolactinoma), Pancreas (30-75%, gastrinoma most common and most deadly). Gastrinoma causes Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (recurrent ulcers, diarrhea, high gastrin). MEN2A has MTC + Pheo + HyperPTH (no pancreatic tumors). MEN2B has MTC + Pheo + mucosal neuromas + marfanoid habitus (no HyperPTH).
highYield: MEN1 = 3Ps (Parathyroid + Pituitary + Pancreas), chromosome 11q13, menin. MEN2A = MTC + Pheo + HyperPTH, RET. MEN2B = MTC + Pheo + neuromas, RET.
pyqNote: MEN type differentiation (1 vs 2A vs 2B) and their specific components are very high-frequency NEET PG questions.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 54-year-old woman undergoes CT abdomen for renal stone evaluation. An incidental right adrenal mass is found measuring 2.8 cm, with Hounsfield units of 6 HU on unenhanced CT. She has no signs of Cushing's syndrome, no hypertension, and is not diabetic. Which is the most appropriate initial workup for this adrenal incidentaloma?
options: 1 mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (for subclinical hypercortisolism) + 24-hour urine metanephrines or plasma free metanephrines (for pheochromocytoma) + serum aldosterone/renin ratio if hypertensive; lipid-rich adenoma is suggested by HU ≤10 on unenhanced CT
Immediate surgical resection — any incidental adrenal mass >2 cm requires adrenalectomy regardless of functional status due to risk of malignancy
FDG-PET scan — this is the first-line imaging modality to distinguish benign adenoma from malignancy in all incidentalomas regardless of CT characteristics
Serum DHEA-S and androgens only — adrenal incidentalomas in women are most commonly androgen-secreting; cortisol and catecholamine testing are only indicated if symptoms are present
explanation: Adrenal incidentaloma workup (European Society of Endocrinology/Endocrine Society guidelines): ALL incidentalomas need functional assessment: (1) Subclinical Cushing's: 1 mg overnight DST (cortisol >1.8 µg/dL = abnormal); (2) Pheochromocytoma: 24-hr urine or plasma metanephrines — must rule out before any procedure; (3) Hyperaldosteronism: aldosterone/renin ratio if hypertensive. HU ≤10 on unenhanced CT = lipid-rich = almost certainly benign adenoma. HU >10 requires washout CT or chemical shift MRI. Surgery indicated if: >4 cm, growing lesion, or functional. This 2.8 cm, HU 6 lesion is likely a benign lipid-rich adenoma but functional workup is mandatory.
highYield: Adrenal incidentaloma workup: overnight 1 mg DST + metanephrines + aldosterone/renin (if HTN). HU ≤10 = likely benign adenoma. Surgery if >4 cm or functional.
pyqNote: Adrenal incidentaloma stepwise evaluation is a frequently tested NEET PG endocrinology question — the HU threshold is specific.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 32-year-old man presents with episodic dark brown morning urine, severe fatigue, and recurrent abdominal pain. Labs show: Hb 7.2 g/dL, reticulocyte count 8%, LDH 1200 IU/L, indirect bilirubin elevated, direct Coombs test NEGATIVE, peripheral smear shows no schistocytes. Flow cytometry shows absence of CD55 and CD59 on red blood cells and granulocytes in a significant clone. Ham test is positive. What is the diagnosis and the pathophysiological basis?
options: Paroxysmal nocturnal hemoglobinuria (PNH) — caused by a somatic mutation in the PIGA gene on the X chromosome leading to deficiency of GPI-anchor proteins (CD55/DAF and CD59/protectin); complement-mediated intravascular hemolysis causes hemoglobinuria; flow cytometry is the gold standard diagnostic test
Autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AIHA) — warm-type AIHA causes Coombs-positive hemolysis; the negative Coombs test here makes this unlikely but paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria (PCH) is Coombs-negative
G6PD deficiency — episodic hemolysis with hemoglobinuria in a young male, triggered by oxidative stress; LDH elevation and Heinz bodies on peripheral smear are expected; Ham test is negative
TTP — thrombotic microangiopathy with microangiopathic hemolytic anemia causes hemoglobinuria; schistocytes on smear and ADAMTS13 deficiency are expected; flow cytometry is normal in TTP
explanation: PNH results from acquired somatic mutation in PIGA gene, which encodes an enzyme critical for GPI-anchor biosynthesis. Loss of GPI-anchored proteins CD55 (decay accelerating factor) and CD59 (protectin) allows uncontrolled complement activation on the cell surface, causing intravascular hemolysis (high LDH, indirect bilirubin, hemoglobinuria).
