Coombs test is NEGATIVE (complement-mediated, not antibody-mediated). Ham test (acid hemolysis test) was the classic diagnostic test. Flow cytometry demonstrating absence of CD55 and CD59 on a clone >1% is now the gold standard. PNH also causes venous thrombosis (especially Budd-Chiari) and aplastic anemia association.
highYield: PNH = PIGA mutation → GPI-anchor deficiency → loss of CD55/CD59 → complement hemolysis. Coombs negative. Ham test positive. Flow cytometry = gold standard.
pyqNote: PNH pathophysiology (GPI anchor, CD55/CD59) and Ham test vs flow cytometry are frequently tested in NEET PG hematology.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with confusion, fever, and petechiae. Labs: Hb 7.8 g/dL, platelet count 18,000/µL, peripheral smear shows numerous schistocytes, LDH 2800 IU/L, bilirubin elevated, Coombs test negative, creatinine 1.4 mg/dL, ADAMTS13 activity is <5% with inhibitor present. Which diagnosis is correct and what is the immediate management?
options: TTP (thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura) — ADAMTS13 activity <10% with inhibitor confirms acquired TTP; neurological predominance (confusion) over renal involvement; immediate treatment is plasma exchange (plasmapheresis) + corticosteroids; platelet transfusion is CONTRAINDICATED
HUS (hemolytic uremic syndrome) — STEC-associated HUS presents with severe AKI predominance; ADAMTS13 levels are irrelevant in HUS diagnosis; treatment is supportive dialysis if needed
TTP — ADAMTS13 <5% confirms TTP; treatment of choice is platelet transfusion to raise count above 50,000/µL before starting plasma exchange to reduce bleeding risk
Atypical HUS (aHUS) — complement dysregulation causes microangiopathic hemolysis; ADAMTS13 activity <5% is the hallmark of aHUS; eculizumab is first-line treatment
explanation: TTP vs HUS distinction: TTP is characterized by ADAMTS13 activity <10% (often <5%) with or without inhibitor — caused by autoantibody against ADAMTS13, the metalloprotease that cleaves ultra-large von Willebrand factor multimers. Clinical pentad: microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia, neurological symptoms (headache, confusion, seizures), fever, renal dysfunction (mild in TTP). HUS (STEC-associated): severe AKI predominates, ADAMTS13 is normal, associated with diarrhea prodrome, Shiga toxin (E. coli O157:H7). Platelet transfusion is CONTRAINDICATED in TTP (worsens thrombosis). Treatment: plasma exchange 1.5 plasma volumes daily + steroids + rituximab for refractory cases.
highYield: TTP: ADAMTS13 <10% + neurological predominance. HUS: ADAMTS13 normal + renal predominance + diarrhea prodrome. Platelet transfusion CONTRAINDICATED in TTP.
pyqNote: TTP vs HUS distinction using ADAMTS13, clinical features, and platelet transfusion contraindication are very high-yield NEET PG topics.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 25-year-old woman presents with spontaneous gum bleeding, petechiae, and menorrhagia for 2 weeks. Platelet count is 8,000/µL. Peripheral smear shows large platelets but no schistocytes. WBC and Hb are normal. Bone marrow biopsy (done to exclude other causes) shows increased megakaryocytes. She has no splenomegaly. Which statement best describes initial treatment thresholds and first-line management for ITP?
options: Treatment is indicated for platelets <30,000/µL with bleeding, or <20,000/µL with no bleeding but high-risk features (wet purpura, mucosal bleeding); first-line is oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day OR IV dexamethasone 40 mg/day × 4 days; IVIG is added for severe/life-threatening bleeding
Treatment threshold is platelet count <50,000/µL in all patients regardless of symptoms; splenectomy is the recommended first-line intervention for newly diagnosed ITP
No treatment is needed until platelets <5,000/µL as ITP is almost always self-limiting in young women; platelet transfusions are the preferred initial treatment
Platelet count <10,000/µL is the sole criterion for treatment; corticosteroids are contraindicated in women of childbearing age due to teratogenic risk
explanation: ITP treatment thresholds (ASH 2019 guidelines): treat if platelets <30,000/µL WITH bleeding symptoms, OR <20,000/µL without bleeding but with high-risk features (wet purpura, active mucosal bleeding, high-risk occupation). Platelet count <10,000/µL is typically always treated. First-line: corticosteroids — prednisone 1-1.5 mg/kg/day × 2-4 weeks with taper, OR dexamethasone 40 mg/day × 4 days (preferred for rapid response). IVIG 1 g/kg (raises platelets within 24-48 hours) for urgent bleeding or pre-procedure. Platelet transfusion: used ONLY for life-threatening bleeding (not routine — destroyed immediately by autoantibodies). Splenectomy is second-line after 3-6 months.
highYield: ITP treatment threshold: platelets <30,000 with bleeding OR <20,000 with high-risk features. First-line: steroids. IVIG for urgent situations. Splenectomy = second-line.
pyqNote: ITP platelet thresholds for treatment and first-line management sequence are directly tested in NEET PG.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 10-year-old boy presents 3 weeks after a throat infection with periorbital edema, hematuria (cola-colored urine), and hypertension. Serum C3 is markedly reduced; C4 is normal. Renal biopsy immunofluorescence shows granular (lumpy-bumpy) deposits of IgG and C3 along the glomerular basement membrane and mesangium. Electron microscopy shows subepithelial humps. Which type of RPGN/GN is this?
options: Type II RPGN (immune complex–mediated) — post-streptococcal GN; granular IgG + C3 deposits on IF with subepithelial humps on EM is pathognomonic; low C3 with normal C4 reflects alternative complement pathway activation; the prognosis in children is excellent with supportive care
Type I RPGN (anti-GBM) — linear IgG along GBM is the hallmark; granular deposits indicate Type I with superimposed infection
Type III RPGN (pauci-immune) — ANCA-positive GN shows scanty immunoglobulin; low C3 is not a feature of pauci-immune GN
IgA nephropathy — post-infection GN with IgA deposits occurs with concurrent infections (synpharyngitic hematuria), not 2-3 weeks after; C3 is not consumed in IgA nephropathy
explanation: Post-streptococcal GN (PSGN) is the prototype of Type II immune complex GN. The latent period of 1-3 weeks (after pharyngitis) or 3-6 weeks (after skin infection) is characteristic. Granular (lumpy-bumpy) IgG and C3 deposits on IF reflect immune complex deposition. Subepithelial humps on EM are pathognomonic for PSGN. Low C3 with normal C4 = alternative pathway activation. IgA nephropathy has normal complement and synpharyngitic pattern (hematuria concurrent with infection). Anti-GBM disease shows linear IF. Pauci-immune GN has negative IF.
highYield: PSGN (Type II RPGN): granular IF, subepithelial humps (EM), low C3 normal C4, latent period 1-3 weeks. Linear IF = Type I. Absent IF = Type III.
pyqNote: Granular vs linear vs absent IF in the 3 types of RPGN is a classic NEET PG renal pathology question.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 22-year-old woman with known SLE presents with worsening proteinuria (3.2 g/24h), peripheral edema, and hematuria. Serum creatinine rises from 0.8 to 1.6 mg/dL over 4 weeks. Complement levels (C3 and C4) are low. Anti-dsDNA titers are rising. Renal biopsy shows diffuse proliferative glomerulonephritis involving >50% of glomeruli with wire-loop lesions and full-house immunofluorescence (IgG, IgA, IgM, C1q, C3). What is the ISN/RPS classification and treatment?
options: Lupus nephritis Class IV (diffuse proliferative LN) — the most severe and most common form requiring aggressive treatment; treatment is mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) or IV cyclophosphamide (NIH or Euro-Lupus regimen) + high-dose steroids (pulse methylprednisolone × 3 days then oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day); full-house IF is characteristic of LN
Lupus nephritis Class III (focal proliferative) — less than 50% glomeruli involved is the definition of Class III; treatment is the same as Class IV
Lupus nephritis Class V (membranous LN) — nephrotic syndrome with wire-loop lesions and subepithelial deposits is the hallmark; treatment is ACE inhibitors alone unless creatinine is rising
Lupus nephritis Class II (mesangial LN) — mild disease with mesangial deposits only; proteinuria <3g/day with minimal crescents; hydroxychloroquine alone is sufficient treatment
explanation: ISN/RPS LN classes: Class I = minimal mesangial, Class II = mesangial proliferative, Class III = focal (< 50% glomeruli), Class IV = diffuse (≥50% glomeruli), Class V = membranous, Class VI = sclerosing. Class IV is the most severe, with diffuse endocapillary proliferation, wire-loop lesions (subendothelial immune deposits), and full-house IF (IgG, IgA, IgM, C1q, C3). Treatment: induction with MMF 2-3 g/day (preferred in Black/Hispanic) OR low-dose CYC (Euro-Lupus) + pulse methylprednisolone × 3 days then oral steroids. Maintenance: MMF or azathioprine. Hydroxychloroquine continued in all LN patients.
highYield: LN Class IV = diffuse (≥50% glomeruli), wire-loops, full-house IF. Treatment: MMF or cyclophosphamide + steroids. Most common class requiring aggressive therapy.
pyqNote: ISN/RPS LN classification and treatment of Class IV is a high-frequency NEET PG nephrology question.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 68-year-old man with known aortic stenosis presents with exertional syncope and angina. ECG shows: regular sinus rhythm at 78 bpm, left axis deviation, broad notched P waves in lead II (duration 120 ms), tall R waves in V5-V6 (>35 mm total with deep S in V1), inverted T waves with ST depression in I, aVL, V5-V6 (strain pattern). Echocardiography shows LV ejection fraction of 55% with peak aortic gradient of 72 mmHg. What is the ECG diagnosis and its significance?
options: Left ventricular hypertrophy (LVH) with strain pattern + left atrial enlargement (P mitrale) — LVH is the expected response to pressure overload from severe AS (gradient >40 mmHg); ST depression/T-wave inversion in lateral leads = strain = chronic pressure overload; surgery indicated (severe AS with symptoms)
Right ventricular hypertrophy (RVH) — right axis deviation and dominant R in V1 would be expected in RVH; the findings described are opposite and indicate left-sided pathology
LBBB (left bundle branch block) — broad QRS >120 ms with notched R in V5-V6 and ST changes; LBBB may coexist with LVH but the description does not specifically mention broad QRS complex
Anterolateral STEMI — ST depression in V5-V6 and lateral leads in a patient with angina represents ischemia/STEMI equivalent requiring urgent catheterization before any other evaluation
explanation: LVH with strain pattern is the hallmark ECG finding in chronic severe aortic stenosis (pressure overload). Cornell voltage criteria: S in V3 + R in aVL >28 mm (men) or >20 mm (women). Sokolow-Lyon: S in V1 + R in V5/V6 >35 mm. Strain pattern = asymmetric ST depression and T-wave inversion in lateral leads (I, aVL, V5, V6) WITHOUT acute ischemia. P mitrale (broad, notched, bifid P wave in II, duration >120 ms) indicates left atrial enlargement from elevated LV filling pressures. Severe AS = peak gradient >40 mmHg or mean gradient >20-25 mmHg with reduced AVA. Symptomatic severe AS (syncope, angina, heart failure) = AVR indicated.
highYield: AS ECG: LVH with strain (lateral ST depression/T-wave inversion) + P mitrale. Strain pattern = chronic pressure overload, not ischemia. Symptomatic severe AS = surgical/TAVR indication.
pyqNote: LVH criteria and strain pattern vs ischemic changes on ECG in the context of AS are tested in NEET PG medicine.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 30-year-old man develops progressive ascending weakness of both legs over 5 days, 2 weeks after a diarrheal illness. He now has bilateral facial weakness and absent deep tendon reflexes throughout. Nerve conduction studies show prolonged distal motor latencies, reduced motor conduction velocities, and conduction block. CSF shows protein 280 mg/dL with only 3 cells/µL (albumin-cytological dissociation). He is ambulatory but deteriorating. What is the diagnosis and treatment?
options: Guillain-Barre syndrome (GBS) — AIDP variant (acute inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy); classic NCS findings of demyelination; albumin-cytological dissociation in CSF; treat with IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days OR plasma exchange; monitor respiratory function (FVC); do NOT use steroids (no benefit)
GBS — AMAN variant (axonal) — conduction block on NCS indicates demyelination making AMAN unlikely; axonal GBS shows reduced amplitudes without conduction block
GBS — treat with oral prednisolone 60 mg/day × 4 weeks; steroids are the preferred first-line treatment to reduce the autoimmune inflammatory attack on peripheral myelin
Transverse myelitis — ascending weakness with CSF pleocytosis would favor TM; albumin-cytological dissociation does not occur in TM; NCS abnormalities are not expected in spinal cord disease
explanation: GBS (AIDP) is the most common cause of acute flaccid paralysis in adults. Classic features: ascending weakness, areflexia, preceding infection (Campylobacter jejuni is most common bacterial trigger). NCS findings of AIDP: prolonged distal latencies, reduced conduction velocities, conduction block, prolonged F-waves (demyelinating pattern). CSF: albuminocytological dissociation (elevated protein, normal cells) appears after 1 week. Treatment: IVIG (0.4 g/kg × 5 days) OR plasma exchange (5 exchanges over 10 days) — equivalent efficacy; combination NOT better. Corticosteroids are explicitly NOT effective in GBS. Monitor FVC — intubate if FVC <20 mL/kg or dropping rapidly (20-30-40 rule: intubate if FVC <20, <30 in 6h, <40 trending down).
highYield: GBS: ascending weakness + areflexia + CSF albuminocytological dissociation. Treatment: IVIG or plasma exchange (NOT steroids). Monitor FVC for respiratory failure.
pyqNote: GBS treatment (IVIG/PE but NOT steroids) and CSF findings are directly tested in NEET PG neurology.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 35-year-old HIV-positive man has a CD4 count of 165 cells/µL. He presents with painless creamy white plaques on the buccal mucosa that scrape off, leaving a raw erythematous surface. KOH preparation of the scrapings shows pseudohyphae and budding yeast. He has no fever or dysphagia. Which condition does this represent and what does his CD4 count indicate about his HIV stage?
options: Oropharyngeal candidiasis (thrush) in WHO Stage 3 / CDC Category B HIV disease — CD4 165 cells/µL indicates advanced immunosuppression (AIDS threshold <200); thrush is an AIDS-defining condition that occurs with CD4 <300; treatment is fluconazole 150-200 mg/day × 7-14 days
Oral hairy leukoplakia (OHL) — caused by EBV; white patches on lateral tongue borders that do NOT scrape off; KOH is negative; OHL is a marker of advancing HIV but is not AIDS-defining
Oral candidiasis in CDC Category C (AIDS) — CD4 <200 defines AIDS; oral candidiasis alone is an AIDS-defining illness regardless of CD4 count when HIV-positive
Vincent's angina (necrotizing ulcerative gingivitis) — pseudomembranous plaques with bleeding gingiva in HIV-positive patients; KOH showing pseudohyphae is a false-positive result
explanation: Oropharyngeal candidiasis (thrush) presents as white plaques that scrape off (unlike OHL) with pseudohyphae on KOH. In HIV, thrush is common when CD4 <300 and is classified as WHO Stage 3 / CDC Category B (symptomatic, not AIDS-defining). Esophageal candidiasis (with dysphagia) is AIDS-defining (CDC Category C). CD4 <200 = AIDS (CDC Category C threshold). This patient has CD4 165 (below 200) = AIDS stage, but oral thrush alone without esophageal involvement is WHO Stage 3. Treatment: fluconazole 100-200 mg/day for 7-14 days. Prophylaxis generally not recommended due to resistance risk.
highYield: Oral candidiasis = WHO Stage 3 / CDC Category B. Esophageal candidiasis = AIDS-defining (CDC Category C). CD4 <200 = AIDS. OHL doesn't scrape off, KOH negative.
pyqNote: WHO HIV staging and AIDS-defining illnesses vs non-AIDS-defining conditions are high-frequency NEET PG questions.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 42-year-old woman presents with weight gain of 8 kg over 1 year, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, periorbital puffiness, bradycardia (58 bpm), and delayed relaxation of deep tendon reflexes. TSH is 68 mIU/L, free T4 is 0.4 ng/dL (low). Anti-TPO antibodies are 850 IU/mL. Which is the most likely diagnosis and initial treatment?
options: Hashimoto's thyroiditis causing primary hypothyroidism — anti-TPO antibodies are the most sensitive marker for autoimmune thyroid disease; treatment is levothyroxine (LT4) starting at 1.6 µg/kg/day titrated to achieve TSH 0.5-2.5 mIU/L; start lower (25-50 µg) in elderly or cardiac disease
Secondary hypothyroidism (pituitary cause) — elevated TSH rules out central hypothyroidism; secondary hypothyroidism always presents with low TSH with low T4
Subclinical hypothyroidism — TSH 68 mIU/L with low free T4 and overt symptoms constitutes subclinical disease; treatment is not indicated until TSH exceeds 100 mIU/L
Sick euthyroid syndrome — any systemic illness suppresses T4 and raises TSH artifactually; anti-TPO positivity is non-specific in the sick euthyroid state; no treatment needed
explanation: Primary hypothyroidism presents with elevated TSH and low free T4. Hashimoto's thyroiditis (chronic autoimmune thyroiditis) is the most common cause in iodine-sufficient regions — positive anti-TPO antibodies confirm autoimmune etiology. Classic symptoms: weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, fatigue, bradycardia, myxedema (periorbital puffiness), delayed DTR relaxation (Woltman sign). Secondary hypothyroidism: low TSH + low T4 (hypothalamic-pituitary failure). Subclinical hypothyroidism: elevated TSH with normal free T4. Sick euthyroid: low T3 and T4 with inappropriately low/normal TSH (TSH 68 rules this out). LT4 is the treatment of choice.
highYield: Primary hypothyroidism: high TSH + low free T4. Hashimoto's = anti-TPO positive. Delayed DTR relaxation = Woltman sign. Treatment: LT4 1.6 µg/kg/day.
pyqNote: Hashimoto's thyroiditis clinical features and LT4 dosing are standard NEET PG endocrinology questions.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 58-year-old man presents with headache, facial flushing, pruritus after hot showers (aquagenic pruritus), splenomegaly, and thrombophlebitis. Labs: Hb 19.2 g/dL, Hct 58%, WBC 13,800/µL, platelet count 560,000/µL, serum erythropoietin is markedly LOW. JAK2 V617F mutation is detected. Bone marrow biopsy shows hypercellularity with panmyelosis. What is the diagnosis and the WHO 2016 major criteria fulfilled?
options: Polycythemia vera (PV) — WHO 2016 major criteria: (1) Hb >16.5 g/dL in men/>16 g/dL in women (or Hct >49/>48%) [met], (2) BM biopsy showing panmyelosis [met], (3) JAK2 V617F or exon 12 mutation [met]; all 3 major criteria met = diagnosis confirmed; low EPO is a minor criterion

Secondary polycythemia — elevated EPO level would be expected in secondary causes (hypoxia, EPO-secreting tumor); low EPO here indicates a primary bone marrow disorder but JAK2 mutation is not always found
Essential thrombocythemia (ET) — platelet count >450,000 with JAK2 mutation is the hallmark of ET; Hb elevation is not a diagnostic criterion for ET
Myelofibrosis — splenomegaly and panmyelosis indicate primary myelofibrosis; JAK2 mutation occurs in ~50% of myelofibrosis cases; Hb of 19.2 g/dL is atypically high for MF which causes anemia
explanation: WHO 2016 PV criteria: Major criteria: (1) Hb >16.5 g/dL (men) or >16 g/dL (women) OR Hct >49%/48%, (2) BM biopsy showing age-adjusted hypercellularity with panmyelosis (trilineage), (3) JAK2 V617F or JAK2 exon 12 mutation. Minor criterion: subnormal serum EPO. Diagnosis = all 3 major criteria OR first 2 major + minor. Aquagenic pruritus (from histamine release by mast cells/basophils) is characteristic of PV. Splenomegaly reflects extramedullary hematopoiesis. Treatment: low-risk <60y = phlebotomy + aspirin; high-risk or >60y = hydroxyurea.
highYield: PV diagnosis requires all 3 major WHO criteria: Hb/Hct threshold + BM panmyelosis + JAK2 mutation. Aquagenic pruritus + low EPO are characteristic features.
pyqNote: WHO 2016 PV diagnostic criteria and JAK2 mutation significance are directly tested in NEET PG hematology.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 45-year-old IV drug user presents with 3 weeks of fever, night sweats, and right-sided pleuritic chest pain. Examination reveals conjunctival petechiae (Roth spots), Janeway lesions on the palms, and Osler nodes on the finger pulps. Blood cultures grow Staphylococcus aureus in 3 of 4 bottles. Echocardiography shows a 1.2 cm mobile vegetation on the tricuspid valve with moderate tricuspid regurgitation. What criteria and what is the classification?
options: Definite infective endocarditis by modified Duke criteria — 2 major criteria met: (1) positive blood cultures (S. aureus in ≥2 separate cultures = typical organism), (2) new valvular regurgitation with vegetation on echo; minor criteria (Roth spots, Janeway lesions, Osler nodes, IVDU) also support; definite IE requires 2 major OR 1 major + 3 minor OR 5 minor criteria
Possible infective endocarditis — only 1 major criterion (positive blood culture) is met; echocardiographic vegetation alone does not qualify as a major Duke criterion
Definite IE by Duke criteria — 1 major criterion (echo vegetation) + 4 minor criteria (fever, IVDU, Janeway lesions, Osler nodes) meets the threshold of 1 major + 3 minor

Rejected Duke criteria — S. aureus bacteremia in an IVDU is more likely primary bacteremia than endocarditis; vegetations on echocardiography are non-specific and can represent non-bacterial thrombotic endocarditis
explanation: Modified Duke criteria for definite IE: 2 major criteria, OR 1 major + 3 minor, OR 5 minor. Major criteria: (1) positive blood cultures (typical organisms like S. aureus, viridans streptococci, HACEK in ≥2 cultures OR persistently positive), (2) evidence of endocardial involvement (echo showing vegetation/abscess OR new valvular regurgitation). Minor criteria: predisposing condition (IVDU, valve disease), fever >38°C, vascular phenomena (Janeway lesions, septic emboli), immunological phenomena (Osler nodes, Roth spots, positive RF), microbiological evidence not meeting major. This patient has 2 major criteria (S. aureus bacteremia + echo vegetation + TR) = definite IE. Treatment: IV nafcillin or vancomycin × 4-6 weeks.
highYield: Duke criteria: 2 major = definite IE. Major criteria: typical organism in ≥2 cultures + echo vegetation/new regurgitation. Janeway lesions = septic emboli (painless). Osler nodes = immune complexes (painful).
pyqNote: Duke criteria for IE and distinguishing Janeway lesions (painless) vs Osler nodes (painful) are classic NEET PG questions.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 62-year-old man with a history of anterior MI 2 years ago presents with dyspnea on minimal exertion (climbing a few steps), orthopnea, and bilateral ankle edema. He can perform light household activities but not climb stairs without stopping. Echocardiography shows EF of 32%. Which NYHA functional class does he belong to and what is the ACC/AHA heart failure stage?
options: NYHA Class III (marked limitation of physical activity; comfortable at rest; less than ordinary activity causes symptoms) + ACC/AHA Stage C (structural heart disease with prior or current symptoms of HF); EF 32% = HFrEF; treatment: ARNI/ACEi + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i (quadruple therapy)
NYHA Class II (slight limitation; comfortable at rest; ordinary activity causes symptoms) — the description of dyspnea on minimal exertion and stopping on stairs fits Class II not III
NYHA Class IV (unable to carry on any physical activity; symptoms at rest) + ACC/AHA Stage D (refractory HF); EF 32% with any exertional limitation automatically qualifies for Class IV
ACC/AHA Stage B (structural heart disease without symptoms of HF) — orthopnea and edema are not HF symptoms per ACC/AHA classification; Stage B requires asymptomatic LV dysfunction
explanation: NYHA classification: Class I = no limitation; Class II = slight limitation, ordinary activity causes symptoms; Class III = marked limitation, less than ordinary activity causes symptoms, comfortable at rest; Class IV = symptoms at rest, unable to carry on any activity without discomfort. Unable to climb stairs without stopping = marked limitation = Class III. ACC/AHA stages: Stage A = risk factors only (no structural disease); Stage B = structural disease, no symptoms; Stage C = structural disease with current/prior symptoms; Stage D = refractory. This patient has structural heart disease (post-MI, EF 32%) with active symptoms = Stage C. HFrEF (EF <40%) guideline-directed therapy: ARNI (sacubitril-valsartan) or ACEi + beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2i.
highYield: NYHA III = dyspnea with less-than-ordinary activity, comfortable at rest. ACC/AHA Stage C = structural heart disease + HF symptoms. HFrEF quadruple therapy: ARNI + BB + MRA + SGLT2i.
pyqNote: NYHA class differentiation and ACC/AHA HF staging are standard NEET PG cardiology topics.
tags: heart failure with reduced ejection fraction

subject: Medicine
stem: A 68-year-old man presents with 2 years of progressive resting tremor (pill-rolling) in his right hand, bradykinesia, and cogwheel rigidity. He also reports constipation, hyposmia, and REM sleep behavior disorder (acting out dreams). On examination, his posture is stooped, gait is shuffling with reduced arm swing, and he has hypomimia (masked facies). Levodopa trial results in significant symptom improvement. Which of the following is NOT a feature of idiopathic Parkinson's disease?
options: Early prominent dementia or cognitive decline within 1 year of motor symptoms — this is a red flag suggesting dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB) not PD; in PD, dementia occurs late (>1 year after motor onset = Parkinson's disease dementia PDD)
Resting tremor — 3-6 Hz pill-rolling tremor that disappears with voluntary movement is the classic tremor of PD and a core feature
Constipation and hyposmia as non-motor prodromal symptoms — these pre-date motor symptoms by years in PD and are part of Braak staging (early Lewy body pathology in enteric nerves and olfactory bulb)
Levodopa responsiveness — a clear and dramatic response to levodopa is a supportive criterion for idiopathic PD and helps distinguish it from atypical Parkinsonism syndromes
explanation: UK Parkinson's Disease Society Brain Bank criteria for PD: Bradykinesia + at least one of rigidity, resting tremor, postural instability, with levodopa responsiveness. Red flags against PD diagnosis: early falls (PSP), early dementia within 1 year (DLB), rapid progression, cerebellar signs, autonomic failure (MSA), downward gaze palsy (PSP), and symmetric onset. Dementia with Lewy bodies (DLB): dementia precedes or occurs within 1 year of motor features (1-year rule). Parkinson's disease dementia (PDD): dementia occurs >1 year after well-established motor disease. Non-motor features (hyposmia, constipation, REM sleep behavior disorder) are part of prodromal PD — they support the diagnosis.
highYield: Red flag against PD: dementia within 1 year of motor onset = DLB. PDD = dementia >1 year after motor onset. REM sleep behavior disorder + hyposmia = prodromal PD markers.
pyqNote: PD red flags, DLB 1-year rule, and prodromal non-motor symptoms are tested in NEET PG neurology.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 22-year-old man with sickle cell disease presents with severe right hip pain for 2 days, fever of 38.8°C, and right hip tenderness. X-ray of the hip shows flattening of the femoral head. WBC is 18,000/µL. Hb is 7.4 g/dL (baseline 7.8 g/dL). CRP is 85 mg/L. He is on hydroxyurea. What is the most likely complication and the most common causative organism if infection is suspected?
options: Avascular necrosis (AVN) of the femoral head — the most common large joint complication of SCD; caused by vaso-occlusion in the femoral head microcirculation; flattening on X-ray represents late AVN; if septic arthritis is also suspected, the most common organism in SCD is Salmonella species (not Staphylococcus as in general population)
Acute vaso-occlusive (painful) crisis — hip pain is the most common presentation; flattening of the femoral head is a normal X-ray finding and does not indicate AVN; MRI is required to diagnose AVN
Septic arthritis in SCD — Staphylococcus aureus is the most common organism; empiric antibiotics should cover MSSA; Salmonella infection in SCD is a myth propagated in textbooks
Osteomyelitis due to Streptococcus pneumoniae — functional asplenia in SCD makes encapsulated organisms (Pneumococcus, H. influenzae) the most feared pathogens in ALL SCD complications including bone
explanation: AVN (aseptic necrosis) of the femoral head is the most common large joint complication of SCD, occurring due to ischemia from vaso-occlusion. X-ray findings progress: early = normal, then increased density, then flattening (subchondral collapse = crescent sign). Fever and elevated WBC/CRP can occur in painful crisis or secondary infection. Distinguishing AVN from osteomyelitis/septic arthritis requires MRI and joint aspiration. Regarding infection: in SCD, functional asplenia predisposes to Salmonella osteomyelitis (the classic teaching) — Salmonella is uniquely common in SCD bone infections compared to general population where S. aureus predominates. Pneumococcal vaccine and penicillin prophylaxis are standard SCD care.
highYield: AVN = most common large joint complication of SCD. Salmonella = most common organism in SCD osteomyelitis (unlike general population = Staphylococcus). Hydroxyurea reduces vaso-occlusive crises.
pyqNote: Salmonella as the most common cause of osteomyelitis in SCD is a classic NEET PG distinction question.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 52-year-old obese man with hypertension on hydrochlorothiazide presents with the 3rd acute attack of severe pain, redness, and swelling of the first metatarsophalangeal joint (podagra). Serum uric acid is 9.8 mg/dL. Joint aspirate from a previous attack showed negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals under polarized light. He takes aspirin 81 mg/day and has a creatinine of 1.0 mg/dL. What is the treatment plan for chronic gout management?
options: Urate-lowering therapy (ULT) with allopurinol (xanthine oxidase inhibitor) targeting serum uric acid <6 mg/dL; continue prophylactic colchicine 0.5-0.6 mg once or twice daily for ≥3-6 months during initiation of ULT to prevent acute flares; discontinue hydrochlorothiazide if possible (it raises uric acid); avoid aspirin at low doses (paradoxically raises uric acid)

Acute attack only management with colchicine; no urate-lowering therapy is indicated until the patient has ≥5 attacks per year; serum uric acid of 9.8 mg/dL does not require treatment in the absence of tophi
Allopurinol alone without colchicine prophylaxis; starting allopurinol does not trigger acute flares and colchicine is only needed during the acute attack

Febuxostat is preferred over allopurinol in all patients with normal renal function; allopurinol is contraindicated with any diuretic use due to allopurinol-diuretic drug interaction causing fatal reactions
explanation: Indications for ULT in gout: ≥2 attacks/year, tophi, uric acid nephrolithiasis, CKD stage ≥2, or serum UA >9 mg/dL (some guidelines). This patient has ≥2 attacks + UA 9.8 mg/dL. Allopurinol is first-line ULT; start at 100 mg/day and titrate to target UA <6 mg/dL (<5 mg/dL if tophi). Key: initiation of ULT can precipitate acute flares due to urate crystal mobilization; colchicine prophylaxis 0.5-0.6 mg daily for ≥3-6 months is mandatory during initiation. Hydrochlorothiazide raises uric acid (substitute with losartan which is uricosuric). Low-dose aspirin raises uric acid by reducing renal clearance; avoid or discuss risk-benefit. Febuxostat: use if allopurinol intolerant; has FDA black box warning for cardiovascular mortality.
highYield: Gout ULT: target UA <6 mg/dL with allopurinol. Start colchicine prophylaxis for ≥3-6 months at ULT initiation. HCTZ raises UA; low-dose aspirin raises UA.
pyqNote: Colchicine prophylaxis with ULT initiation and target uric acid levels are tested in NEET PG rheumatology/medicine.

subject: Medicine
stem: A 19-year-old man with Type 1 DM presents with vomiting and polyuria for 2 days after missing his insulin. Vitals: BP 90/60 mmHg, HR 124 bpm, RR 28 breaths/min (Kussmaul), temperature 37.2°C. Labs: glucose 485 mg/dL, sodium 128 mEq/L, potassium 5.8 mEq/L, bicarbonate 8 mEq/L, pH 7.12, chloride 92 mEq/L, ketones 3+ (urine and serum). Anion gap = 128 - (92+8) = 28 mEq/L. What is the most critical immediate intervention before starting insulin?
options: IV fluid resuscitation with normal saline (0.9% NaCl) 1-2 L over the first hour to restore intravascular volume; do NOT give insulin before checking potassium — serum K+ 5.8 mEq/L appears high but will drop rapidly with insulin; wait until K+ >3.5 mEq/L before starting insulin; monitor K+ hourly
Immediate insulin bolus IV 0.1 units/kg followed by infusion — insulin must be given first to stop ketogenesis; fluid resuscitation can be deferred until glucose is below 250 mg/dL
IV sodium bicarbonate infusion to correct pH 7.12 — bicarbonate is routinely given when pH <7.2 to prevent cardiac arrhythmias before any other intervention
Potassium replacement 40 mEq/hr immediately — serum K+ 5.8 mEq/L is falsely elevated in DKA due to acidosis; the true total body K+ is depleted and must be replaced before any other treatment
explanation: DKA management priorities: (1) IV fluids FIRST — normal saline 1-2 L bolus for hemodynamic stabilization; corrects the hypertonicity and pre-renal state. (2) Potassium: DKA patients are total-body K+ depleted despite elevated serum K+ (due to acidosis-driven extracellular shift). Insulin drives K+ intracellularly, causing life-threatening hypokalemia. Rule: start insulin ONLY when K+ >3.5 mEq/L; replace K+ to keep 3.5-5 mEq/L. (3) Insulin: 0.1 units/kg/hr continuous infusion after fluids and K+ correction. (4) Bicarbonate: NOT routinely given; only considered if pH <6.9. Corrected sodium = measured Na + 1.6 × (glucose-100)/100 = 128 + 6 = 134 (mildly depleted).
highYield: DKA: Fluids FIRST (NS 1-2 L/hr), then check K+ — do NOT start insulin if K+ <3.5. Bicarbonate only if pH <6.9. Total body K+ depleted despite high serum K+ in acidosis.
pyqNote: DKA management sequence and potassium threshold before insulin are among the highest-yield NEET PG emergency medicine topics.

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman (16 weeks gestation) is diagnosed with urinary tract infection caused by Escherichia coli. Urine culture shows sensitivity to multiple antibiotics. Which of the following is the most appropriate antibiotic for this patient?
explanation: Nitrofurantoin is safe and effective for uncomplicated UTI in pregnancy during the second trimester. Ciprofloxacin (fluoroquinolone) is contraindicated in pregnancy due to fetal cartilage damage. Doxycycline is contraindicated due to teeth and bone effects. TMP-SMX is avoided in first trimester (neural tube defects) and third trimester (neonatal jaundice).
highYield: Nitrofurantoin is the drug of choice for UTI in pregnancy (2nd trimester); avoid in first trimester and near term.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 40-year-old male farmer from Rajasthan presents with fever, headache, myalgia, and rash after tick exposure. Weil-Felix test shows agglutination with OX-19. Blood culture is negative. What is the drug of choice for this condition?
explanation: The clinical picture with Weil-Felix positivity (OX-19) is consistent with Rickettsial disease (likely scrub typhus or spotted fever group). Doxycycline is the drug of choice for all rickettsial infections. Chloramphenicol is an alternative but is second-line. Amoxicillin and erythromycin are not effective against intracellular organisms like Rickettsia.
highYield: Doxycycline is the drug of choice for all rickettsial diseases; Weil-Felix OX-19 positive suggests spotted fever group.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Medicine
stem: A 55-year-old male with Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus and CKD Stage 3b (eGFR 32 mL/min/1.73m2) presents for glycemic management. His HbA1c is 8.4%. Cardiovascular risk is high. Which antidiabetic drug is the most appropriate first-line addition to metformin in this patient?
explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) have demonstrated cardioprotective and nephroprotective benefits in patients with T2DM and CKD per 2022 ADA guidelines. Empagliflozin (EMPA-REG OUTCOME) reduces cardiovascular mortality and slows CKD progression. Glibenclamide causes hypoglycemia and is avoided in CKD. Pioglitazone causes fluid retention worsening renal outcomes. DPP4i (sitagliptin) is safe but lacks proven nephroprotective advantage.
highYield: SGLT2 inhibitors are now first-line add-on for T2DM with CKD and high cardiovascular risk per 2022 ADA guidelines.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Pharmacology
stem: A 3-year-old child presents with fever, cough, and inspiratory stridor. Lateral neck X-ray shows a 'steeple sign'. Oxygen saturation is 96%. The child is not drooling. What is the drug of choice for this condition?
explanation: The clinical presentation with steeple sign on X-ray is classic for viral croup (laryngotracheobronchitis), most commonly caused by Parainfluenza virus. Management includes nebulised racemic epinephrine (for immediate relief of stridor) and systemic/nebulised corticosteroids (dexamethasone reduces severity and duration). Antibiotics are not indicated for viral croup. Budesonide alone is insufficient when epinephrine is indicated.
highYield: Steeple sign = croup (Parainfluenza virus); treatment = nebulised epinephrine + dexamethasone.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: A 30-year-old primigravida at 38 weeks presents with severe pre-eclampsia (BP 160/110 mmHg, proteinuria 2+, headache). She has no history of epilepsy. What is the drug of choice for seizure prophylaxis in this patient?
explanation: Magnesium sulfate (MgSO4) is the drug of choice for seizure prophylaxis in severe pre-eclampsia and for treatment of eclampsia, as per WHO and ACOG guidelines. It acts by blocking NMDA receptors and causing cerebral vasodilation. Phenytoin and diazepam are used in general epilepsy but are inferior to MgSO4 in eclampsia. The Pritchard regimen (4g IV + 10g IM) or modified Zuspan regimen may be used.
highYield: MgSO4 is the drug of choice for eclampsia prophylaxis and treatment; antidote is calcium gluconate.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Medicine
stem: A 65-year-old male with known COPD (FEV1/FVC < 0.7, FEV1 45% predicted) presents with acute worsening of dyspnea and increased sputum production. ABG shows pH 7.28, PaO2 55 mmHg, PaCO2 62 mmHg on room air. He is alert and cooperative. What is the best next step in management?
options: High-flow oxygen via non-rebreather mask at 15 L/min
explanation: This patient has hypercapnic respiratory failure with acidosis (pH 7.28) - a classic indication for NIV (BiPAP) in COPD exacerbation. NIV reduces the need for intubation, ICU stay, and mortality in AECOPD. High-flow O2 at 15 L/min is dangerous in COPD as it can worsen hypercapnia (Haldane effect, loss of hypoxic drive). Intubation is reserved for NIV failure. Aminophylline has limited role in acute exacerbation.
highYield: NIV (BiPAP) is first-line for AECOPD with hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH 7.25-7.35); avoid high-flow O2.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Surgery
stem: A 45-year-old female presents with right upper quadrant pain, fever (38.8°C), and jaundice. USG abdomen shows common bile duct dilation (12 mm) with multiple gallstones in the CBD. Her vitals are now stable after IV fluids and antibiotics. What is the best next step?
options: Conservative management and repeat USG after 6 weeks
explanation: This patient has Charcot's triad (pain, fever, jaundice) indicating ascending cholangitis due to choledocholithiasis. Once stabilized, ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone extraction is the best next step as it relieves biliary obstruction endoscopically with low morbidity. Emergency cholecystectomy does not address CBD stones and is high-risk. PTC is reserved for failed ERCP. Conservative management alone is inadequate.
highYield: Charcot's triad (pain, fever, jaundice) = ascending cholangitis; best next step = ERCP after stabilization.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Medicine
stem: A 25-year-old male presents with palpitations, weight loss, heat intolerance, and tremors for 3 months. On examination, lid lag, exophthalmos, and a diffusely enlarged thyroid are noted. TSH is 0.01 mIU/L and Free T4 is markedly elevated. What is the best next investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Fine needle aspiration cytology of thyroid
explanation: The clinical picture is consistent with Graves' disease (autoimmune hyperthyroidism with ophthalmopathy). TSH receptor antibodies (TRAb/TSHR-Ab) are the most specific confirmatory test for Graves' disease, present in >95% of cases. RAIU scan shows diffusely increased uptake but is not needed when TRAb is positive. CT orbit is for orbital decompression planning, not diagnosis. FNAC is for nodular disease.
highYield: TSH receptor antibodies (TRAb) are the most specific marker for Graves' disease.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: A 32-year-old G2P1 at 34 weeks gestation presents with sudden painless vaginal bleeding (soaking 2 pads in 1 hour). Her previous delivery was by cesarean section. Vitals: BP 100/70 mmHg, HR 110 bpm. USG shows placenta previa (central). What is the best next step?
options: Digital vaginal examination to assess cervix
IV fluids, blood transfusion, corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity, close monitoring
explanation: This patient has hemodynamically significant antepartum hemorrhage due to placenta previa at 34 weeks. Since fetal viability is at stake and she is not in extremis, immediate resuscitation (IV fluids, blood transfusion), corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity (betamethasone), and close monitoring to assess whether delivery is needed are the best next steps. Digital vaginal examination is absolutely contraindicated in placenta previa. Emergency CS is indicated if bleeding is uncontrolled or mother/fetus is compromised.
highYield: Placenta previa: NEVER do digital vaginal exam; give steroids at 34 weeks; emergency CS if unstable.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 6-month-old infant is brought with fever for 5 days, bilateral non-purulent conjunctival injection, cracked red lips, strawberry tongue, polymorphous rash over the trunk, and cervical lymphadenopathy (>1.5 cm). Echo shows coronary artery Z-score of 2.8. What is the best next step?
options: Observation and repeat echo in 2 weeks
explanation: This child meets all 5 criteria for Kawasaki disease (fever >5 days + bilateral conjunctival injection + lip/oral changes + rash + cervical lymphadenopathy). The coronary artery Z-score >2.5 indicates early aneurysm formation. IVIG 2 g/kg as a single infusion along with high-dose aspirin (80-100 mg/kg/day in acute phase) is the treatment of choice and reduces coronary artery aneurysm risk from 25% to <5%. Steroids are added in IVIG-resistant cases.
highYield: Kawasaki disease: IVIG 2 g/kg single dose + aspirin; reduces coronary aneurysm risk from 25% to <5%.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Pharmacology
stem: Regarding the mechanism of action and pharmacokinetics of Heparin, which of the following is NOT true?
options: It acts by activating antithrombin III
It can cross the placenta and cause fetal bleeding
It does not cross the blood-brain barrier
explanation: Heparin does NOT cross the placenta due to its large molecular weight (high molecular weight heparin) and negative charge. This makes it the anticoagulant of choice in pregnancy. It acts by potentiating antithrombin III (inhibiting thrombin and factor Xa). It does not cross the blood-brain barrier. Protamine sulfate (1 mg per 100 units heparin) reverses its action. Hence, statement B is NOT true.
highYield: Heparin does NOT cross the placenta (drug of choice in pregnancy); Warfarin crosses placenta and is teratogenic.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Physiology
stem: All of the following are true regarding the diving reflex (mammalian diving response) EXCEPT:
options: Bradycardia is the most prominent feature
Peripheral vasoconstriction occurs to conserve oxygen
Cerebral and cardiac blood flow is decreased to conserve oxygen
explanation: During the diving reflex, selective vasoconstriction occurs in peripheral organs (limbs, splanchnic) while blood flow is preferentially maintained to vital organs - the BRAIN and HEART. This is the key survival mechanism. Bradycardia is the most prominent feature (vagally mediated). Peripheral vasoconstriction is correct. Splenic contraction does release stored RBCs increasing oxygen-carrying capacity. Hence D is NOT true - cerebral and cardiac flow is PRESERVED, not decreased.
highYield: Diving reflex: bradycardia + peripheral vasoconstriction + blood redistributed TO brain and heart (not away from).
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Biochemistry
stem: Regarding Gaucher's disease (Type 1), all of the following are true EXCEPT:
options: It is caused by deficiency of glucocerebrosidase (beta-glucosidase)
Gaucher cells show 'wrinkled tissue paper' appearance on PAS stain
It is the most common lysosomal storage disorder
It characteristically involves the central nervous system in Type 1
explanation: Type 1 Gaucher's disease is the NON-neuronopathic form - CNS involvement is absent in Type 1. CNS involvement occurs in Type 2 (acute neuronopathic) and Type 3 (chronic neuronopathic). Type 1 is the most common lysosomal storage disorder overall. It is caused by glucocerebrosidase deficiency leading to glucocerebroside accumulation. Gaucher cells show characteristic 'crumpled tissue paper' or 'wrinkled tissue paper' appearance on PAS stain. Hence D is NOT true.
highYield: Gaucher Type 1 = NO CNS involvement; most common lysosomal storage disorder; enzyme deficiency = glucocerebrosidase.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Physiology
stem: Regarding the juxtaglomerular apparatus (JGA), which of the following is NOT correct?
options: Macula densa cells are located in the thick ascending limb of loop of Henle
JG cells are modified smooth muscle cells that secrete renin
Lacis cells (extraglomerular mesangial cells) are located between afferent arteriole, efferent arteriole, and macula densa
Increased sodium delivery to macula densa stimulates renin secretion
explanation: Increased sodium (NaCl) delivery to the macula densa INHIBITS renin secretion (negative feedback). Renin is secreted when NaCl delivery to macula densa DECREASES (as occurs in hypovolemia/low BP). Macula densa cells are indeed in the thick ascending limb (early distal tubule at the juxtaglomerular area). JG cells are modified smooth muscle cells of afferent arteriole. Lacis cells (Goormaghtigh cells) are correctly located in the angle between afferent and efferent arterioles and macula densa.
highYield: Macula densa: increased NaCl delivery INHIBITS renin; decreased NaCl delivery STIMULATES renin secretion.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Pharmacology
stem: All of the following statements about Warfarin are true EXCEPT:
options: It inhibits Vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKORC1)
Its anticoagulant effect can be monitored by INR (PT ratio)
It can be safely used in the first trimester of pregnancy
explanation: Warfarin is absolutely contraindicated in the FIRST trimester of pregnancy (6-12 weeks) as it crosses the placenta and causes Warfarin embryopathy (nasal hypoplasia, stippled epiphyses, optic atrophy). It is also avoided near term. LMWH is used instead. Warfarin inhibits VKORC1 (Vitamin K epoxide reductase). INR (PT-based) is the correct monitoring parameter. Vitamin K1 reverses its action (takes 12-24 hours; FFP for immediate reversal).
highYield: Warfarin is CONTRAINDICATED in 1st trimester (embryopathy: nasal hypoplasia, stippled epiphyses); use LMWH in pregnancy.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Medicine
stem: A 14-year-old boy presents with fever (40°C), severe headache, retro-orbital pain, myalgia, and a maculopapular rash appearing on day 3. Platelet count is 65,000/µL, NS1 antigen positive. No warning signs are present. BP is 100/70 mmHg. As per the revised WHO 2009 dengue classification, this patient should be categorized as:
explanation: Per WHO 2009 classification (which replaced the 1997 DHF classification), dengue is classified as: (1) Dengue without warning signs, (2) Dengue with warning signs (abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, fluid accumulation, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, liver >2cm, rising HCT with rapid platelet fall), and (3) Severe dengue (severe plasma leakage, severe bleeding, severe organ impairment). This patient has dengue features with thrombocytopenia but NO warning signs, hence Category 1. DHF grading is obsolete per 2009 WHO guidelines.
highYield: WHO 2009 dengue classification replaced 1997 DHF grading: 3 categories - without warning signs / with warning signs / severe dengue.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Medicine
stem: A 55-year-old patient with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) has joint pain, oral ulcers, photosensitivity, and lymphopenia (800/µL). Which classification criteria is currently recommended to diagnose SLE, and what is the minimum score required?
options: SLICC 2012 criteria: 4 out of 17 criteria including 1 clinical and 1 immunological
EULAR/ACR 2019 criteria: score ≥10 with ANA as entry criterion
Modified ACR 1997 criteria: 4 out of 11 with updated renal criteria
explanation: The current gold standard for SLE classification is the EULAR/ACR 2019 criteria. It requires ANA positivity (HEp-2 cells, titer ≥1:80) as an entry criterion, followed by weighted scoring across 7 clinical domains and 3 immunological domains. A total score ≥10 classifies as SLE. This replaced SLICC 2012 and ACR 1982/1997 criteria. The 2019 criteria have better sensitivity and specificity than the older criteria.
highYield: EULAR/ACR 2019 SLE criteria: ANA as mandatory entry criterion + weighted score ≥10; replaced SLICC 2012.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Medicine
stem: A 62-year-old male with hypertension, diabetes, and atrial fibrillation (non-valvular) has a CHA2DS2-VASc score of 3. As per the latest ESC 2020 guidelines on atrial fibrillation, what is the recommended antithrombotic therapy?
options: Direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) e.g., Rivaroxaban or Apixaban
No antithrombotic therapy as DOACs are only for score ≥4
explanation: Per ESC 2020 AF guidelines, DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) are the PREFERRED anticoagulants over VKAs (warfarin) for non-valvular AF in patients with CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 in males and ≥3 in females. Aspirin alone is NOT recommended for stroke prevention in AF. DOACs have similar or superior efficacy to warfarin with lower bleeding risk. Anticoagulation should be offered when CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 in men and ≥3 in women.
highYield: ESC 2020: DOACs preferred over warfarin for non-valvular AF; anticoagulate when CHA2DS2-VASc ≥2 (men) or ≥3 (women).
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Medicine
stem: A 48-year-old male with chronic kidney disease is found to have eGFR of 28 mL/min/1.73m2 with urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR) of 320 mg/g for more than 3 months. As per KDIGO 2024 CKD classification, what is the correct staging of this patient's CKD?
explanation: KDIGO 2024 CKD classification uses GFR categories (G1-G5) and Albuminuria categories (A1-A3). eGFR 28 mL/min = G3b (eGFR 29-15 range; G3b = 15-29 mL/min). Wait - eGFR 28 falls in G3b (15-29). UACR 320 mg/g = A3 (>300 mg/g = severely increased albuminuria). Hence G3b A3. Note: G1 ≥90, G2 60-89, G3a 45-59, G3b 30-44, G4 15-29, G5 <15. eGFR 28 = G4 (15-29 mL/min). UACR 320 = A3. Correct answer is G4 A3.
highYield: KDIGO CKD staging: G4 = eGFR 15-29; A3 = UACR >300 mg/g; combined staging guides prognosis and management.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: As per the revised National Tuberculosis Elimination Program (NTEP) guidelines 2022, which of the following statements about treatment of drug-sensitive pulmonary TB is CORRECT?
options: Treatment is given as thrice-weekly directly observed therapy (DOT)
Daily treatment regimen (2HRZE/4HR) under Ni-kshay app-based monitoring is now recommended
Category II retreatment includes adding Kanamycin for 3 months
explanation: NTEP 2022 updated guidelines mandate DAILY (not thrice-weekly) fixed-dose combination treatment for all new and retreatment TB cases under Ni-kshay digital monitoring. The regimen is 2HRZE/4HR (Intensive phase 2 months HRZE + Continuation phase 4 months HR). Thrice-weekly DOT was the old approach, now abandoned for daily treatment. Category II regimen with Kanamycin (injectable) is no longer recommended; replaced by shorter BPaL regimen for MDR-TB.
highYield: NTEP 2022: DAILY fixed-dose combination therapy (2HRZE/4HR) replaced thrice-weekly DOT for all drug-sensitive TB.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Radiology
stem: A 55-year-old male smoker presents with progressive dyspnea and non-productive cough. Chest X-ray shows bilateral hilar enlargement with 'eggshell calcification' of hilar lymph nodes and small bilateral upper lobe reticulonodular opacities. He works in a granite quarry. What is the most likely diagnosis?
explanation: Eggshell calcification of hilar lymph nodes is pathognomonic of silicosis (and occasionally sarcoidosis, but combined with quarry worker history and upper lobe predominant nodules, silicosis is the diagnosis). Silicosis occurs due to inhalation of free crystalline silica (SiO2). Chest X-ray shows upper lobe predominant small round nodules and hilar lymph node calcification with 'eggshell' pattern. It predisposes to tuberculosis (silicotuberculosis). Coal worker's pneumo shows lower lobe opacities without eggshell calcification.
highYield: Eggshell calcification of hilar lymph nodes = pathognomonic of SILICOSIS; occupational history in quarry/mining workers.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Pathology
stem: Microscopy of a thyroid mass shows follicular structures of varying sizes lined by cells with enlarged nuclei showing fine powdery 'Orphan Annie eye' chromatin, nuclear grooves, and intranuclear cytoplasmic inclusions. Psammoma bodies are also seen. What is the diagnosis?
explanation: The nuclear features described - Orphan Annie eye nuclei (ground glass/empty-appearing nuclei), nuclear grooves, and intranuclear cytoplasmic pseudoinclusions - are pathognomonic of Papillary Thyroid Carcinoma (PTC), the most common thyroid malignancy. Psammoma bodies (laminated calcifications) are also characteristic. Follicular carcinoma shows capsular/vascular invasion without these nuclear features. Medullary carcinoma shows amyloid stroma (Congo red positive) and C-cell origin.
highYield: Papillary thyroid carcinoma: Orphan Annie eye nuclei + nuclear grooves + intranuclear inclusions + psammoma bodies.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Anatomy
stem: A 35-year-old male is brought to the ER after a road traffic accident. On examination, he cannot extend his wrist (wrist drop), cannot extend the MCP joints of fingers, and cannot extend the thumb. Sensation over the dorsal aspect of the first web space is lost. However, triceps reflex is intact and elbow extension is normal. Where is the most likely site of nerve injury?
options: Radial nerve in the radial groove (spiral groove) of humerus
explanation: Wrist drop + loss of finger and thumb extension + sensory loss over first dorsal web space with INTACT triceps (elbow extension preserved) indicates radial nerve injury at the spiral groove (mid-humerus level). At this level, the branch to triceps has already been given off. In axillary radial nerve injury, triceps is also paralyzed. PIN injury (below elbow) causes finger drop without wrist drop (extensor carpi radialis is spared). Posterior cord injury affects multiple nerves.
highYield: Wrist drop + intact triceps = radial nerve injury at spiral groove of humerus; axillary injury = triceps also weak.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Ophthalmology
stem: Fundus examination of a 60-year-old hypertensive male shows flame-shaped hemorrhages, cotton wool spots, AV nicking, copper wiring of arterioles, and disc edema with blurring of margins. His BP is 210/130 mmHg. What grade of hypertensive retinopathy is this according to Keith-Wagener-Barker (KWB) classification?
explanation: Keith-Wagener-Barker (KWB) classification: Grade I = mild arteriolar narrowing; Grade II = AV nicking + copper/silver wiring; Grade III = flame hemorrhages + cotton wool spots + hard exudates (retinal changes without disc changes); Grade IV = Grade III features + PAPILLEDEMA (disc edema). This patient has all Grade III features PLUS disc edema/blurred margins = Grade IV hypertensive retinopathy, indicating hypertensive encephalopathy/malignant hypertension.
highYield: KWB Grade IV = Grade III features + papilledema; indicates malignant/accelerated hypertension with encephalopathy.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Medicine
stem: A 45-year-old male with no prior cardiac history presents with sudden onset crushing chest pain radiating to the left arm for 2 hours. ECG shows ST elevation in leads II, III, and aVF with reciprocal ST depression in leads I, aVL, V1-V3. Which artery is most likely occluded?
explanation: ST elevation in inferior leads (II, III, aVF) indicates inferior wall STEMI. The inferior wall is supplied predominantly by the Right Coronary Artery (RCA) in 85% of right-dominant individuals. Reciprocal changes in leads I, aVL (lateral) and anterior leads (V1-V3) further support RCA occlusion. LAD supplies anterior wall (V1-V4 ST elevation). LCx supplies lateral wall (I, aVL, V5-V6). Left main occlusion is catastrophic with widespread changes.
highYield: Inferior STEMI (ST elevation in II, III, aVF) = Right Coronary Artery (RCA) occlusion in 85% cases.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: A 38-week primigravida is assessed for induction of labor. Per vaginal examination reveals: cervical dilation 2 cm, effacement 60%, consistency soft, position central, and presenting part at -1 station. What is her Bishop score and what does it indicate?
options: Bishop score 4 — cervix unfavorable, induction likely to fail
Bishop score 7 — cervix favorable, induction likely to succeed
Bishop score 9 — cervix very favorable, spontaneous labor imminent
Bishop score 5 — cervix borderline, cervical ripening needed before induction
explanation: Bishop score components: Dilation 2cm = 1, Effacement 60% = 2, Consistency soft = 2, Position central = 1, Station -1 = 1. Total = 7. Bishop score ≥8 (some say ≥6) indicates favorable cervix with high likelihood of successful induction. Score ≥8 = spontaneous labor likely and induction likely to succeed. Score <6 = unfavorable, cervical ripening with prostaglandins or Foley catheter recommended. Score 7 is borderline favorable.
highYield: Bishop score ≥8 = favorable cervix; components = Dilation + Effacement + Consistency + Position + Station.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: A neonate is delivered at term. At 1 minute, assessment shows: heart rate 90 bpm, weak cry with good respiratory effort, active movement of limbs, grimace on stimulation, and blue extremities with pink trunk. What is the APGAR score and appropriate management?
options: APGAR 7 — no intervention needed, reassess at 5 minutes
APGAR 6 — supplemental oxygen via face mask and stimulation
explanation: APGAR scoring at 1 minute: Heart rate 90 (<100 bpm) = 1; Respiratory effort (good) = 2; Muscle tone (active) = 2; Reflex (grimace) = 1; Color (peripheral cyanosis, trunk pink = acrocyanosis) = 1. Total = 7. Wait - Heart rate 90 <100 = score 1. So: 1+2+2+1+1 = 7. APGAR 7-10 = normal, reassess at 5 minutes and provide warmth. APGAR 4-6 = moderate depression, supplemental O2 + stimulation. APGAR <4 = severe, requires immediate resuscitation. Score 6 requires O2 + stimulation.
highYield: APGAR: 0-3 severe, 4-6 moderate (O2 + stimulation), 7-10 normal; assessed at 1 and 5 minutes.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: A multigravida is in active labor. She was 4 cm dilated at 8:00 AM. At 12:00 PM (4 hours later), she is 6 cm dilated. Membranes are intact. Fetal heart rate is normal. As per the WHO partograph, the cervical progress is plotted on the alert line. What is the significance and the next step?
options: Progress is normal; continue routine monitoring
Progress has crossed the action line; immediate cesarean section
Progress is on the alert line; transfer to higher facility / increase monitoring and reassess in 2 hours
Progress is on the alert line; immediate ARM (artificial rupture of membranes) and oxytocin augmentation
explanation: WHO partograph has an Alert line (represents rate of 1 cm/hour cervical dilation from 4 cm) and an Action line (4 hours to the right of alert line). Expected cervical progress at 1 cm/hour from 4 cm: at 4 hours = 8 cm. Patient achieved only 6 cm in 4 hours, placing her ON the alert line. Alert line crossing = transfer to higher center, increased monitoring, reassess. Action line crossing (4 hours later with inadequate progress) = active intervention (augmentation or cesarean).
highYield: Partograph: alert line = 1 cm/hour progress; action line = 4 hours to the right; crossing action line requires intervention.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: During vaginal delivery of a macrosomic baby (estimated fetal weight 4.2 kg), after delivery of the head, the anterior shoulder fails to deliver with gentle downward traction. 'Turtle sign' is noted. What is the FIRST maneuver to be performed?
options: Rubin II maneuver (posterior displacement of anterior shoulder)
explanation: Shoulder dystocia is an obstetric emergency. The FIRST and most effective maneuver is McRoberts maneuver (hyperflexion of maternal thighs onto the abdomen) combined with suprapubic pressure (not fundal). This flattens the lumbar lordosis, rotates the pubic symphysis superiorly, and frees the impacted anterior shoulder. Success rate ~50% with McRoberts alone. If unsuccessful, proceed to: Rubin II/Woods screw, delivery of posterior arm, all-fours position. Zavanelli is the last resort.
highYield: Shoulder dystocia: FIRST maneuver = McRoberts + suprapubic pressure; NEVER apply fundal pressure.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: During the second stage of labor, which maneuver involves the delivery of the fetal head by placing a draped hand beneath the perineum to maintain flexion of the fetal head as it passes through the introitus, while the other hand applies pressure over the occiput to control the rate of delivery?
explanation: The Ritgen maneuver (modified Ritgen) involves placing a towel-covered hand under the perineum, applying upward pressure on the fetal chin through the perineum to extend the head, while the other hand on the occiput controls delivery speed and maintains deflexion. This maneuver protects the perineum and allows controlled delivery of the head. The modified Ritgen is performed between contractions. Kristeller = fundal pressure (not recommended). Prague = for delivery of after-coming head in breech.
highYield: Ritgen maneuver: hand under perineum applies pressure on fetal chin to control head delivery; protects perineum.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 9-month-old child is brought for routine immunization. As per the National Immunization Schedule (NIS) of India 2023, which vaccines are due at this visit?
options: MMR, Vitamin A (1st dose), JE (1st dose)
explanation: As per India NIS 2023, at 9 months: MMR (Measles-Mumps-Rubella) 1st dose, Vitamin A 1st dose (1 lakh IU), and JE 1st dose (in JE-endemic districts). OPV+DPT+HepB+Hib are given at 6, 10, and 14 weeks. The 9-month visit is a key milestone for MMR and Vitamin A supplementation. Second MMR dose is given at 15-18 months. JE schedule: 1st dose at 9 months, 2nd at 16-24 months in endemic areas.
highYield: NIS 2023 at 9 months: MMR (1st dose) + Vitamin A (1st dose, 1 lakh IU) + JE 1st dose (endemic areas).
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A mother brings her 10-month-old infant for a developmental checkup. Which of the following developmental milestones is EXPECTED to be present at this age?
explanation: Pincer grasp (tip of thumb to tip of index finger) is a fine motor milestone typically achieved at 9-10 months. Walking independently is achieved at 12-15 months. Building a tower of 3 cubes is an 18-month milestone. Two-three word sentences are expected at 24 months. Other 9-10 month milestones include: pulling to stand, cruising along furniture, 'mama/dada' with meaning, separation anxiety, stranger anxiety.
highYield: Pincer grasp = 9-10 months; palmar grasp = 4-5 months; walks alone = 12-15 months.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 2-year-old child is brought with fever for 4 days and cough. On examination, the child is lethargic, unable to drink, has fast breathing (56 breaths/min), and lower chest indrawing. As per IMCI (Integrated Management of Childhood Illness) guidelines, this child should be classified as:
options: Pneumonia — give oral amoxicillin and follow-up in 2 days
Severe pneumonia — give first dose of appropriate antibiotic and refer urgently
Cough or cold — reassure and follow-up if not improving
Very severe disease — admit, IV antibiotics, oxygen
explanation: IMCI classifies respiratory illness based on: Fast breathing alone (without danger signs) = Pneumonia (outpatient amoxicillin). Fast breathing + lower chest indrawing = Severe pneumonia (give antibiotics, refer urgently). General danger signs (unable to drink/breastfeed, persistent vomiting, convulsions, lethargic/unconscious, stridor, severe malnutrition) = Very severe disease. This child has fast breathing + lower chest indrawing = Severe pneumonia requiring urgent referral with first antibiotic dose.
highYield: IMCI: fast breathing + lower chest indrawing = Severe Pneumonia; give antibiotics and REFER URGENTLY.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A community health worker screens children aged 6-59 months in a rural village. A 2-year-old child has a mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) of 10.8 cm, bilateral pitting edema of the feet, and a weight-for-height Z-score (WHZ) of -3.2. How should this child be classified as per WHO/UNICEF SAM criteria?
options: Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM) — MUAC <11.5 cm only
Severe Acute Malnutrition (SAM) with complicated SAM requiring inpatient NRC admission
explanation: WHO/UNICEF 2013 SAM criteria: MUAC <11.5 cm OR WHZ <-3 OR bilateral pitting edema (kwashiorkor). This child has MUAC 10.8 cm (<11.5 cm) + WHZ -3.2 (<-3) + bilateral edema (kwashiorkor) = SAM. The presence of bilateral pitting edema with SAM = complicated SAM requiring inpatient management at Nutrition Rehabilitation Center (NRC). Uncomplicated SAM (no edema, no medical complications) can be treated with RUTF (Ready-to-Use Therapeutic Food) in community.
highYield: SAM criteria: MUAC <11.5 cm OR WHZ <-3 OR bilateral pitting edema; edema = complicated SAM needing NRC admission.
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 15-month-old child missed some vaccines. As per India NIS 2023, which combination of vaccines is due at the 15-month visit?
options: DPT booster 1 + OPV booster 1 + MMR 2nd dose + Vitamin A 2nd dose
Hepatitis B booster + PCV booster + Hib booster
DPT 3rd dose + OPV 3rd dose + HepB 3rd dose
explanation: As per India NIS 2023, at 16-18 months: DPT Booster 1, OPV Booster 1, MMR 2nd dose, and Vitamin A 2nd dose (2 lakh IU). This is also when PCV Booster is given in states where PCV is included. The primary series (3 doses each of OPV, DPT, HepB, Hib) is completed by 14 weeks. The 15-16 month visit is critical for boosters and MMR 2nd dose. Vitamin A is continued 6-monthly until 5 years (total 9 doses).
highYield: NIS at 16-18 months: DPT Booster 1 + OPV Booster 1 + MMR 2nd dose + Vitamin A 2nd dose (2 lakh IU).
pyqNote: NEET PG 2025 pattern

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A surgeon performs a routine appendicectomy but inadvertently ligates the ureter, leading to the patient's death. Under the CURRENT LAW (BNS 2024), which section governs criminal liability for causing death by medical negligence?
explanation: BNS Section 106 replaced IPC Section 304A and governs causing death by rash or negligent act. For medical professionals, it carries a punishment of up to 5 years imprisonment and/or fine. BNS 103 is murder, BNS 101 is culpable homicide not amounting to murder, and BNS 125 is rash/negligent act endangering life without causing death.
highYield: BNS Section 106 (formerly IPC 304A) — medical negligence causing death; max 5 years + fine
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Under BNS 2024, which section defines rape and specifies the 7 circumstances under which sexual intercourse constitutes rape, with the age of consent remaining at 18 years?
explanation: BNS Section 63 defines rape (formerly IPC Section 375), enumerating 7 circumstances of non-consent including without will, against will, with fraud, with intoxication, and with a minor under 18 years. BNS 64 prescribes punishment for rape, BNS 70 covers gang rape, and BNS 75 covers sexual harassment.
highYield: BNS Section 63 (formerly IPC 375) — definition of rape; 7 circumstances; age of consent = 18 years
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 20-year-old woman is assaulted by a group of four men. Under BNS 2024, which section covers gang rape and what is the minimum sentence?
explanation: BNS Section 70 (formerly IPC 376D) specifically addresses gang rape, defined as rape committed by one or more persons constituting a group. The minimum punishment is 20 years rigorous imprisonment, extendable to life imprisonment. BNS 64 covers individual rape with a minimum of 10 years. BNS 75 governs sexual harassment, not gang rape.
highYield: BNS Section 70 (formerly IPC 376D) — gang rape; minimum 20 years to life imprisonment
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: Under BNS 2024, which section lists the 8 types of grievous hurt, previously enumerated under IPC Section 320?
explanation: BNS Section 114 (formerly IPC 320) enumerates the 8 types of grievous hurt: emasculation, permanent loss of sight, permanent loss of hearing, loss of any member or joint, destruction of any member or joint, permanent disfiguration of head/face, fracture or dislocation of a bone or tooth, and any hurt endangering life or causing severe bodily pain lasting 20 days. BNS 115 is voluntarily causing hurt and BNS 117 is voluntarily causing grievous hurt.
highYield: BNS Section 114 (formerly IPC 320) — 8 types of grievous hurt; same list retained under BNS
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A man with severe schizophrenia kills his neighbour, genuinely believing the neighbour was a demon sent to destroy the world. Under BNS 2024, which section provides the defence of unsoundness of mind (McNaughton rules)?
explanation: BNS Section 22 (formerly IPC Section 84) incorporates the McNaughton rules, providing complete defence for acts committed by a person of unsound mind who, at the time of the act, did not know the nature of the act or did not know it was wrong. BNS 19 covers absolute immunity for children under 7 years, BNS 20 covers children 7-12 years, and BNS 24 covers intoxication defences.
highYield: BNS Section 22 (formerly IPC 84) — unsoundness of mind; McNaughton rules; complete defence
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 5-year-old child pushes a classmate off a balcony, causing death. Under BNS 2024, which section provides absolute criminal immunity to this child?
explanation: BNS Section 19 (formerly IPC Section 82) provides absolute immunity to children under 7 years of age — nothing is an offence done by a child under 7 years. This is doli incapax — incapable of crime by age. BNS 20 covers children 7-12 years who may be held responsible if they have reached sufficient maturity of understanding. BNS 22 covers unsoundness of mind.
highYield: BNS Section 19 (formerly IPC 82) — absolute immunity for children under 7 years; doli incapax
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A burn victim in the emergency department is in critical condition. A police constable records her statement as she describes her attacker. Under BNSS 2024, which section governs the recording of dying declaration?
explanation: BNSS Section 183 (formerly CrPC Section 164) governs the recording of statements and confessions, including dying declarations. A magistrate is preferred for recording a dying declaration, but any witness is legally acceptable if a magistrate is unavailable. BNSS Section 194 covers the inquest procedure. CrPC 164 and CrPC 174 are the old provisions now replaced.
highYield: BNSS Section 183 (formerly CrPC 164) — dying declaration; magistrate preferred; any witness acceptable
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A body is found under suspicious circumstances in a railway yard. Under BNSS 2024, which section mandates the police inquest procedure to determine the cause and manner of death?
explanation: BNSS Section 194 (formerly CrPC Section 174) governs the police inquest procedure for unnatural or suspicious deaths. It mandates the officer-in-charge to investigate the apparent cause of death, prepare an inquest report, and send the body for postmortem if necessary. BNSS 183 governs dying declarations and statements. CrPC 174 is the old provision now superseded.
highYield: BNSS Section 194 (formerly CrPC 174) — police inquest; unnatural/suspicious deaths
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A man plans and executes the killing of his business rival with a firearm. Under BNS 2024, which section defines and punishes murder?
explanation: BNS Section 103 (formerly IPC Section 302) defines and punishes murder with death penalty or life imprisonment, along with fine. Murder requires intention to kill or knowledge that the act is imminently dangerous and likely to cause death. BNS 101 is culpable homicide not amounting to murder, BNS 106 is causing death by negligence, and BNS 115 is voluntarily causing hurt.
highYield: BNS Section 103 (formerly IPC 302) — murder; punishment is death or life imprisonment
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A man in a sudden quarrel strikes his opponent with a wooden plank intending to cause grievous hurt; the opponent dies unexpectedly. Under BNS 2024, which section most appropriately governs this act of culpable homicide not amounting to murder?
explanation: BNS Section 101 (formerly IPC Sections 299/304) covers culpable homicide not amounting to murder. When the act is done with intention to cause such bodily injury as is likely to cause death but without intention to cause death, and without knowledge that it is imminently dangerous, it falls under BNS 101. This is distinct from murder (BNS 103) which requires intention to kill.
highYield: BNS Section 101 (formerly IPC 299/304) — culpable homicide not amounting to murder
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
topic: Medical Negligence — BNS vs IPC trap
stem: A patient dies following a doctor's failure to administer prescribed anticoagulants post-cardiac surgery. A NEET PG student in 2025 is asked which section of CURRENT LAW applies to this medical negligence causing death. The correct answer is:
options: IPC Section 304A — as it is the established provision for negligent death
BNS Section 106 — as BNS replaced IPC from July 1, 2024
BNS Section 103 — as the death was foreseeable
BNS Section 101 — as there was some degree of intent
explanation: Since July 1, 2024, the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita (BNS) 2024 replaced the Indian Penal Code. IPC Section 304A is now replaced by BNS Section 106, which governs causing death by rash or negligent act. Students who memorised IPC 304A will be caught by this trap in 2025 NEET PG. The current operative law mandates citing BNS 106, not IPC 304A.
highYield: BNS Section 106 is the CURRENT law for medical negligence causing death — IPC 304A is repealed as of July 1, 2024
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A hospital staff member installs a hidden camera in the changing room used by female patients. Under BNS 2024, which section applies to this act of voyeurism?
explanation: BNS Section 77 (formerly IPC Section 354C) defines and punishes voyeurism — watching or capturing images of a woman engaging in a private act without her consent. In a healthcare setting this has severe professional and criminal consequences. BNS 75 covers sexual harassment, BNS 63 defines rape, and BNS 64 punishes rape.
highYield: BNS Section 77 (formerly IPC 354C) — voyeurism; hidden cameras/watching private acts without consent
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A male colleague repeatedly makes unwanted sexual comments to a female colleague at a medical college and also shows her pornographic content on his phone. Under BNS 2024, which section covers sexual harassment?
explanation: BNS Section 75 (formerly IPC Section 354A) covers sexual harassment, including unwelcome physical contact, demands for sexual favours, sexually coloured remarks, and showing pornography without consent. The POSH Act (Prevention of Sexual Harassment at Workplace, 2013) may also apply in a workplace context but BNS 75 is the criminal provision.
highYield: BNS Section 75 (formerly IPC 354A) — sexual harassment; 4 defined acts including showing pornography
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A doctor in a government hospital examines a 14-year-old girl who presents with evidence of sexual assault. Regarding mandatory reporting, which of the following is correct under current law?
options: The doctor must report under BNS Section 63 to the nearest police station
The doctor must report under POCSO Act Section 19 to the Special Juvenile Police Unit or local police
Reporting is optional if parents are present and decline to report
The doctor must report to the Child Welfare Committee within 48 hours under BNSS
explanation: POCSO Act 2012 (Protection of Children from Sexual Offences) is a separate legislation from IPC/BNS and its sections are UNCHANGED. Under POCSO Section 19, any person including a doctor who has knowledge of a sexual offence committed against a child (under 18 years) MUST report it to the Special Juvenile Police Unit or local police. Non-reporting is itself a criminal offence. BNS does not override POCSO obligations.
highYield: POCSO Act Section 19 — mandatory reporting by any person including doctors; POCSO sections UNCHANGED by BNS
