pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 10-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department with evidence of anal penetration. Under the POCSO Act 2012, which section covers penetrative sexual assault and what is the minimum punishment?
options: POCSO Section 4; minimum 10 years rigorous imprisonment
POCSO Section 6; minimum 20 years rigorous imprisonment
explanation: POCSO Section 4 covers penetrative sexual assault on a child (under 18 years) and prescribes a minimum of 10 years rigorous imprisonment extendable to life, plus fine. POCSO Section 6 covers aggravated penetrative sexual assault (minimum 20 years to life). POCSO sections are completely separate from BNS and remain unchanged. BNS sections do not apply when POCSO is applicable.
highYield: POCSO Section 4 — penetrative sexual assault; minimum 10 years; POCSO is separate from IPC/BNS
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 7-year-old girl is brought to the paediatric ward. Examination reveals evidence of sexual assault committed by her father. Under POCSO Act 2012, the applicable section for aggravated penetrative sexual assault by a relative is:
options: POCSO Section 6 — aggravated penetrative sexual assault
explanation: POCSO Section 6 covers aggravated penetrative sexual assault, which includes assault by a relative, guardian, teacher, police officer, or public servant. The minimum punishment is 20 years rigorous imprisonment extendable to life, or death in the most heinous cases. This is distinct from Section 4 (basic penetrative assault, 10 years minimum). BNS does not apply when POCSO covers the case.
highYield: POCSO Section 6 — aggravated penetrative sexual assault by relative/authority figure; minimum 20 years
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 12-year-old girl reports that a neighbour touched her private parts without penetration. The doctor examining her should be aware that under POCSO Act 2012, which sections cover sexual assault (non-penetrative) and the related offence?
options: POCSO Section 7 and Section 8 — sexual assault and its punishment
BNS Section 63 and Section 64 — rape definition and punishment
explanation: POCSO Section 7 defines sexual assault (non-penetrative) as touching the sexual organ, anus, or breast of a child with sexual intent. Section 8 prescribes the punishment: minimum 3 years, maximum 5 years rigorous imprisonment with fine. These are separate from penetrative assault sections (4 and 6). The POCSO Act is entirely independent of BNS and IPC.
highYield: POCSO Section 7 — sexual assault (non-penetrative); Section 8 — punishment; 3-5 years RI
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A junior resident drives at high speed in a hospital campus and injures a pedestrian but the pedestrian survives. Under BNS 2024, the rash or negligent act endangering life but NOT causing death is governed by:
explanation: BNS Section 125 (formerly IPC Sections 336/337/338) covers rash or negligent act endangering life or personal safety of others. This section applies when injury occurs but the victim does not die. When death occurs due to negligence, BNS Section 106 applies. BNS 101 covers culpable homicide and BNS 115 covers voluntarily causing hurt.
highYield: BNS Section 125 (formerly IPC 336/337/338) — rash/negligent act endangering life without causing death
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A psychiatrist locks a patient in a room against the patient's will and without legal authorisation, preventing all escape. Under BNS 2024, this act constitutes:
options: BNS Section 22 — unsoundness of mind defence
explanation: BNS Section 127 (formerly IPC Section 340) covers wrongful confinement — wrongfully restraining a person in such a manner as to prevent them from proceeding beyond certain circumscribing limits. Locking someone in a room constitutes confinement. BNS Section 126 (formerly IPC 339) covers wrongful restraint, which is a lesser degree — preventing movement in a particular direction without complete enclosure.
highYield: BNS Section 127 (formerly IPC 340) — wrongful confinement; complete prevention from leaving a defined space
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A person consumes alcohol voluntarily and in an intoxicated state commits murder. He pleads that he did not know the nature of his act due to intoxication. Under BNS 2024, the intoxication defence is governed by which section, and is it a valid complete defence here?
options: BNS Section 23; valid complete defence if intoxication was voluntary
BNS Section 23/24; NOT a complete defence if intoxication was voluntary and self-induced
BNS Section 22; valid — same as unsoundness of mind
BNS Section 19; valid — absolute immunity applies
explanation: BNS Sections 23 and 24 (formerly IPC 85/86) govern the intoxication defence. When intoxication is involuntary (administered without knowledge/consent), it can provide a complete defence. However, when intoxication is VOLUNTARY and self-induced, it is NOT a complete defence. The person is presumed to have the same intention as if they were sober. This differs from insanity (BNS 22) which can provide a complete defence.
highYield: BNS Section 23/24 (formerly IPC 85/86) — voluntary intoxication is NOT a complete defence; involuntary intoxication may be
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
topic: BNS vs IPC — Rape Punishment Comparison
stem: A medical student is comparing the old IPC provisions with the current BNS 2024. Regarding punishment for rape committed by a man on a woman above 18 years, which statement CORRECTLY reflects the change from IPC to BNS?
options: IPC 376 prescribed minimum 7 years; BNS 64 now prescribes minimum 5 years — sentence reduced
IPC 376 prescribed minimum 7 years; BNS 64 now prescribes minimum 10 years — sentence increased
Both IPC 376 and BNS 64 prescribe the same minimum of 7 years — no change
BNS 64 applies only to gang rape; individual rape is now under BNS 63
explanation: Under the old IPC Section 376, the minimum punishment for rape was 7 years rigorous imprisonment. Under BNS Section 64 (replacing IPC 376), the minimum sentence has been INCREASED to 10 years rigorous imprisonment, extendable to life imprisonment. This is a significant change and a common exam trap. BNS 63 defines rape (does not prescribe punishment), and BNS 70 covers gang rape.
highYield: BNS Section 64 — punishment for rape increased to minimum 10 years (was 7 years under IPC 376)
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A forensic expert is asked to advise a court under CURRENT LAW (2025). A person convicted of murder under BNS 2024 would be charged under BNS Section 103 (formerly IPC 302). The punishment prescribed is:
options: Death penalty or life imprisonment with fine — same as IPC 302
Life imprisonment only; death penalty abolished under BNS
explanation: BNS Section 103 retains the same punishment as the former IPC Section 302 — death penalty or life imprisonment, along with fine. The BNS did not abolish capital punishment for murder. The section number changed (302 to 103) but the substance of punishment remained the same. Life imprisonment without possibility of death penalty applies only to specific BNS subsections.
highYield: BNS Section 103 (formerly IPC 302) — murder; punishment unchanged: death or life imprisonment + fine
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: In a rape case, the forensic pathologist collects DNA evidence from the victim. Under the current legal framework (2025), the admissibility of DNA evidence in Indian courts is governed by:
options: Indian Evidence Act, Section 45 — expert opinion
explanation: The Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam (BSA) 2023 replaced the Indian Evidence Act 1872 and governs admissibility of all forms of evidence in Indian courts, including DNA evidence. BSA explicitly recognises electronic/digital evidence and forensic evidence. The old Indian Evidence Act Section 45 (expert opinion) is now subsumed under BSA provisions. BNSS 183 covers dying declarations and BNS 63 defines rape.
highYield: Bharatiya Sakshya Adhiniyam (BSA) 2023 — replaced Indian Evidence Act; governs DNA evidence admissibility
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 9-year-old boy sets fire to a neighbour's house after being teased. He claims he did not understand that people inside could be harmed. Under BNS 2024, his criminal liability depends on:
options: Whether he had reached sufficient maturity of understanding at the time — BNS Section 20
BNS Section 22 — whether he was of unsound mind
Automatic liability as he is above 7 years — BNS Section 103
explanation: BNS Section 20 (formerly IPC Section 83) applies to children between 7 and 12 years. Unlike children under 7 who have absolute immunity (BNS 19), a child aged 7-12 may be held criminally responsible IF it is proved that at the time of the act, they had attained sufficient maturity of understanding to judge the nature and consequences of the act. The burden is on the prosecution to prove this maturity.
highYield: BNS Section 20 (formerly IPC 83) — children 7-12 years; liability depends on maturity of understanding
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A paediatrician examines a 16-year-old boy who discloses sexual abuse by a teacher. The parents beg the doctor not to report it to avoid social stigma. Under current law, which statement is CORRECT?
options: The doctor may withhold reporting if parents give written consent to not report
Reporting is mandatory under POCSO Section 19 regardless of parental consent; failure to report is a criminal offence
The doctor must report under BNS Section 75 as it replaces POCSO reporting obligations
Reporting is required only if the victim is female, as POCSO applies only to girls
explanation: POCSO Section 19 mandates that any person who has apprehension or knowledge of a sexual offence against a child MUST report it to the Special Juvenile Police Unit (SJPU) or local police. This obligation is absolute and cannot be waived by parental consent. Failure to report is a criminal offence punishable under POCSO Section 21. POCSO applies to children of all genders (below 18 years). BNS does NOT replace POCSO reporting obligations.
highYield: POCSO Section 19 — mandatory reporting by ANY person including doctors; parental consent irrelevant; failure = criminal offence (Section 21)
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A senior resident slaps a junior resident during rounds, causing a minor bruise. Under BNS 2024, this act of voluntarily causing hurt is covered under which section?
explanation: BNS Section 115 (formerly IPC Sections 321/323) covers the act of voluntarily causing hurt — bodily pain, disease, or infirmity caused intentionally. A minor bruise from a slap constitutes 'hurt' but not 'grievous hurt.' BNS Section 117 is voluntarily causing grievous hurt (for the 8 types listed in BNS 114). BNS 114 lists the types of grievous hurt. BNS 127 is wrongful confinement.
highYield: BNS Section 115 (formerly IPC 321/323) — voluntarily causing hurt; not grievous; minor bodily pain/injury
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A man deliberately throws acid on a colleague's face, causing permanent disfigurement. Under BNS 2024, which section covers the act of voluntarily causing grievous hurt?
explanation: BNS Section 117 (formerly IPC Sections 322/325) covers voluntarily causing grievous hurt. Permanent disfigurement of the face is one of the 8 types of grievous hurt enumerated in BNS Section 114. When grievous hurt is caused voluntarily, BNS 117 applies. For acid attacks specifically, there are also enhanced punishment provisions. BNS 115 covers ordinary hurt and BNS 114 only defines the types.
highYield: BNS Section 117 (formerly IPC 322/325) — voluntarily causing grievous hurt; permanent facial disfigurement qualifies
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
topic: BNS vs IPC — Rape Definition Trap
stem: A medical student studying for NEET PG 2025 is asked: 'The definition of rape and the 7 circumstances of non-consent, previously under IPC Section 375, is now found under which section of current law?' The correct answer is:
options: IPC Section 375 — still the operative provision as it has not been repealed
BNS Section 64 — punishment section which also contains the definition
BNS Section 63 — the definition of rape under current law
BNSS Section 183 — as rape must be reported under the procedural code
explanation: Since July 1, 2024, IPC Section 375 has been REPEALED and replaced by BNS Section 63, which contains the complete definition of rape including all 7 circumstances. BNS Section 64 contains only the punishment provisions for rape (minimum 10 years). Students who cite IPC 375 in 2025 NEET PG will lose marks. BNSS 183 is a procedural provision for statements/declarations.
highYield: BNS Section 63 (NOT IPC 375 — repealed July 1, 2024) — definition of rape; 7 circumstances of non-consent
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
topic: Medical Negligence — Rash vs Negligent Act
stem: An anaesthesiologist fails to monitor a patient's oxygen saturation during surgery; the patient develops hypoxic brain injury but survives. The hospital administration wants to know which section of BNS 2024 applies to this medical negligence where there is NO death:
options: BNS Section 106 — causing death by negligence
BNS Section 125 — rash or negligent act endangering life
BNS Section 101 — culpable homicide not amounting to murder
explanation: When a negligent act endangers life or causes bodily injury but does NOT result in death, BNS Section 125 (formerly IPC 336/337/338) applies. BNS 106 (formerly IPC 304A) requires actual death as a consequence. Since the patient survived, BNS 125 is the applicable provision. This is an important distinction in medical negligence cases — the outcome (death vs. injury) determines which section applies.
highYield: BNS 125 — negligent act causing injury without death; BNS 106 — only when death results; outcome determines section
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 35-year-old road accident victim in the ICU shows absence of all brainstem reflexes, positive apnoea test, and two confirmatory assessments 6 hours apart by independent physicians. Brain death is certified. Organ harvesting proceeds. The legal framework governing brain death certification and organ transplantation in India is:
options: BNS Section 106 — medical negligence if transplant fails
Transplantation of Human Organs and Tissues Act (THOTA) 1994, amended 2023
BNSS Section 194 — inquest must be conducted before organ retrieval
POCSO Act if the victim is under 18 years
explanation: Brain death certification and organ transplantation in India are governed by the Transplantation of Human Organs and Tissues Act (THOTA) 1994, most recently amended in 2023. The 2023 amendment recognized Donation after Cardiac Death (DCD). Brain death definition under THOTA requires absence of all brainstem reflexes, positive apnoea test, and two physician certifications 6 hours apart (Harvard 1968 criteria/AIIMS protocol). BNS 106 is for negligence causing death, not transplantation.
highYield: THOTA 1994 (amended 2023) — brain death certification; 2023 amendment added DCD recognition; 2 physicians 6 hours apart
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Forensic Medicine
topic: Wrongful Restraint vs Wrongful Confinement — BNS 2024
stem: A doctor refuses to allow a patient to leave the hospital, posting security guards at every exit. The patient is free to move within the ward but cannot leave. Under BNS 2024, this scenario BEST fits:
options: BNS Section 126 — wrongful restraint, as the patient can still move within the ward
BNS Section 127 — wrongful confinement, as circumscribing limits are imposed
explanation: BNS Section 127 (formerly IPC 340) covers wrongful confinement — restraining a person within circumscribing limits (such as a ward or hospital). The patient cannot leave the hospital, which constitutes confinement within defined boundaries. BNS Section 126 (wrongful restraint, formerly IPC 339) prevents movement in a particular direction but does not involve circumscribing limits. The key distinction: confinement = enclosed area; restraint = directional prevention.
highYield: BNS 127 = wrongful confinement (circumscribing limits); BNS 126 = wrongful restraint (directional); critical distinction
pyqNote: BNS 2024 — replaces IPC, critical for 2025 NEET PG

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) releases the National Air Quality Index (AQI) for Indian cities. A city records the following sub-index values on a given day: PM2.5 = 185, PM10 = 142, NO2 = 78, SO2 = 60. What is the AQI for that city on that day?
explanation: India's National AQI (CPCB) is calculated as the HIGHEST sub-index among all measured pollutants, not an average. In this scenario, PM2.5 sub-index of 185 is the highest; therefore the city AQI = 185, placing it in the 'Moderate' category (101-200, yellow). This single highest-pollutant-wins rule ensures the worst hazard governs the reported index.
highYield: AQI = highest sub-index among all pollutants tested; PM2.5 sub-index 185 falls in Moderate category (101-200).
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: According to India's National Air Quality Index (CPCB), which color band corresponds to the AQI category 'Very Poor'?
explanation: The CPCB National AQI color coding is: Good (0-50, green), Satisfactory (51-100, light green), Moderate (101-200, yellow), Poor (201-300, orange), Very Poor (301-400, red), and Severe (401-500, dark red/maroon). Very Poor corresponds to red, while Severe corresponds to dark red/maroon — a commonly confused pair in MCQs.
highYield: Very Poor AQI = 301-400 = Red; Severe AQI = 401-500 = Dark red/maroon.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A public health officer notes that the AQI of an industrial city reads 260. Which of the following statements correctly describes this AQI category and the associated health advisory?
options: Moderate (101-200); breathing discomfort to sensitive people
Poor (201-300); breathing discomfort to most people on prolonged exposure
Very Poor (301-400); respiratory illness on prolonged exposure
Severe (401-500); affects healthy people; serious impact on sensitive people
explanation: An AQI of 260 falls in the 'Poor' category (201-300, orange band). In this category, breathing discomfort occurs in most people on prolonged exposure. Very Poor (301-400) leads to respiratory illness on prolonged exposure, and Severe (401-500) affects even healthy individuals. The eight pollutants measured in India's National AQI include PM2.5, PM10, NO2, SO2, O3, CO, NH3, and Pb.
highYield: AQI 201-300 = Poor (orange); breathing discomfort to most people on prolonged exposure.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: As per the Directorate General Factory Advice Service & Labour Institutes (DGFASLI) of India, what is the permissible noise exposure limit for an 8-hour working day?
explanation: Both DGFASLI (India) and OSHA (USA) set the permissible noise exposure limit at 90 dB(A) for an 8-hour working day. Noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL) is the most common occupational disease worldwide. The exchange rate used is 5 dB(A) doubling: for every 5 dB increase, the permissible exposure time halves (e.g., 95 dB(A) = 4 hours, 100 dB(A) = 2 hours).
highYield: Permissible occupational noise = 90 dB(A) for 8 hours (DGFASLI India / OSHA).
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A factory safety officer is assessing workplace lighting. He measures the illuminance in the general office area as 350 lux. Which of the following statements is CORRECT regarding this measurement?
options: 350 lux is inadequate; office work requires at least 500 lux
350 lux is within the recommended range of 200-500 lux for office environments
The SI unit for illuminance is candela, not lux
350 lux exceeds safe limits and may cause glare injury
explanation: The SI unit of illuminance is LUX (lm/m²). Recommended illuminance for office work is 200-500 lux; for passageways and corridors, 50 lux is sufficient. A reading of 350 lux is therefore within the acceptable range for office environments. Candela is the SI unit of luminous intensity, not illuminance — a common distractor in NEET PG questions.
highYield: Illuminance unit = LUX; office work 200-500 lux; passageways 50 lux.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A chemical plant worker is exposed to a solvent for 15 minutes at a concentration exceeding TLV-TWA but within a defined short-term limit. Which threshold limit value concept applies specifically to a 15-minute maximum exposure?
options: IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health)
explanation: TLV-STEL (Short Term Exposure Limit) is the maximum concentration to which workers can be exposed continuously for up to 15 minutes without suffering irritation, chronic tissue damage, or narcosis. TLV-TWA is the 8-hour time-weighted average. TLV-C (Ceiling) must not be exceeded even instantaneously. IDLH is the concentration immediately dangerous to life or health, used for selecting respiratory protection. All TLVs are set by ACGIH (American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists).
highYield: TLV-STEL = 15-minute maximum short-term exposure limit; set by ACGIH.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Several office employees in a centrally air-conditioned building report headaches, eye irritation, and fatigue during working hours. All symptoms resolve completely on leaving the building. Indoor CO2 monitoring shows levels of 1,100 ppm. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis and the key diagnostic criterion?
options: Multiple Chemical Sensitivity; criterion is >500 ppm CO2
Legionnaire's disease; criterion is positive urinary antigen test
Sick Building Syndrome; criterion is symptom resolution on leaving + CO2 >1000 ppm
Building-Related Illness; criterion is symptoms persisting after leaving the building
explanation: Sick Building Syndrome (SBS) is diagnosed when occupants experience non-specific symptoms (headache, eye/nose/throat irritation, fatigue, dizziness) that resolve upon leaving the building. A CO2 level >1000 ppm indicates inadequate ventilation and is a key SBS marker. In contrast, Building-Related Illness (BRI) — such as Legionnaire's disease or Humidifier fever — has identifiable causative agents and symptoms that persist after leaving the building.
highYield: SBS criteria: symptoms resolve on leaving + indoor CO2 >1000 ppm indicating poor ventilation.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Under India's National STI/RTI Control Programme, a 25-year-old male presents with urethral discharge. Which color-coded syndromic management kit should be dispensed?
explanation: The Red kit under India's National STI/RTI Control Programme is for urethral discharge in males, covering gonorrhoea and chlamydia with syndromic management (tab ciprofloxacin + tab doxycycline). Blue kit = vaginal discharge in females; Green kit = genital ulcer disease; Yellow kit = lower abdominal pain/PID in females.
highYield: Red kit = urethral discharge in males (ciprofloxacin + doxycycline).
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A 30-year-old woman presents to a PHC with vaginal discharge. The ANM follows India's National STI/RTI Control Programme guidelines. Which color kit should be given, and what is the correct drug regimen?
explanation: The Blue kit is designated for vaginal discharge in females under India's syndromic management approach for STI/RTI. The regimen includes metronidazole (for bacterial vaginosis/trichomoniasis) and fluconazole (for candidiasis). Yellow kit is for lower abdominal pain/PID and contains cefixime + doxycycline + metronidazole. Green kit covers genital ulcer disease with benzathine penicillin + ciprofloxacin.
highYield: Blue kit = vaginal discharge in females; drugs = metronidazole + fluconazole.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with lower abdominal pain, fever, and mucopurulent cervical discharge at a Community Health Centre. The medical officer decides to use syndromic management under the National STI/RTI Control Programme. Which kit and regimen is MOST appropriate?
explanation: Lower abdominal pain with cervicitis in a female represents the PID/lower abdominal pain syndrome, managed with the Yellow kit under India's National STI/RTI Control Programme. The regimen (cefixime + doxycycline + metronidazole) covers Neisseria gonorrhoeae, Chlamydia trachomatis, and anaerobes — the typical polymicrobial aetiology of PID. Red kit is for males with urethral discharge; Green kit is for genital ulcer disease.
highYield: Yellow kit = PID/lower abdominal pain in females; cefixime + doxycycline + metronidazole.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Which international agency is responsible for the International Health Regulations (IHR 2005) and is headquartered in Geneva?
explanation: The World Health Organization (WHO), headquartered in Geneva, Switzerland, is responsible for the International Health Regulations (IHR 2005) — the legal framework requiring countries to report public health events of international concern (PHEIC). UNICEF and UNFPA are headquartered in New York; the World Bank is in Washington DC. WHO was established on 7 April 1948 (now celebrated as World Health Day).
highYield: WHO = Geneva; custodian of IHR 2005 and declaration of PHEIC.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The Codex Alimentarius Commission, which sets international food safety standards, is a joint body of WHO and which other UN agency headquartered in Rome?
explanation: The Codex Alimentarius Commission was established in 1963 as a joint FAO/WHO body to develop international food standards, guidelines, and codes of practice. FAO (Food and Agriculture Organization) is headquartered in Rome, Italy. The Codex standards protect consumer health and facilitate fair international trade in food. UNICEF (child health, New York) and UNFPA (reproductive health, New York) are different agencies.
highYield: Codex Alimentarius = joint FAO/WHO body; FAO headquartered in Rome.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The Colombo Plan, established in 1951, primarily focuses on which of the following?
options: Regulation of pharmaceutical patent rights in Asia
Technical cooperation for socioeconomic and health development in Asia-Pacific
International disease surveillance and outbreak response
Maternal and child nutrition supplementation in South Asia
explanation: The Colombo Plan (1951) is an intergovernmental regional organization established to promote technical cooperation for socioeconomic development, including health, in the Asia-Pacific region. It was inaugurated at the Commonwealth Conference in Colombo, Sri Lanka. It is distinct from WHO (disease surveillance), UNICEF (child nutrition), and TRIPS/WTO (pharmaceutical patents). Membership now includes 27 countries spanning Asia-Pacific.
highYield: Colombo Plan 1951 = technical cooperation for Asia-Pacific socioeconomic and health development.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: What is the minimum free residual chlorine required at the consumer's tap in a water distribution system, as per standard water quality guidelines?
explanation: A minimum free residual chlorine of 0.2 mg/L must be maintained at the consumer's tap (farthest point in the distribution system) to ensure microbiological safety of drinking water. At the treatment plant, a higher residual is maintained to allow for decay in the distribution network. Residual below 0.2 mg/L at the tap indicates inadequate chlorination or re-contamination in pipes.
highYield: Minimum free residual chlorine at consumer's tap = 0.2 mg/L.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: An infant in a rural village develops cyanosis and methaemoglobinaemia after feeding on formula prepared with well water. Testing reveals a nitrate level of 60 mg/L. Which of the following statements is CORRECT?
options: Nitrate level is within WHO safe limits of <100 mg/L
Nitrate level exceeds the WHO guideline of <45 mg/L and is the likely cause of blue baby syndrome
Methaemoglobinaemia at this nitrate level affects adults more than infants
The safe nitrate level for drinking water is <10 mg/L as per WHO
explanation: The WHO guideline for nitrate in drinking water is <45 mg/L (as nitrate). Levels >45 mg/L cause blue baby syndrome (infantile methaemoglobinaemia) because gut bacteria in infants convert nitrate to nitrite, which oxidises haemoglobin to methaemoglobin. Adults are less susceptible due to higher gastric acidity inhibiting gut bacterial conversion and higher methaemoglobin reductase activity. A level of 60 mg/L clearly exceeds the safe limit.
highYield: Nitrate safe limit = <45 mg/L (WHO); >45 mg/L causes blue baby syndrome in infants.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A community in an endemic fluorosis zone uses drinking water with a fluoride level of 2.5 mg/L. What is the MOST LIKELY fluorosis manifestation at this concentration?
options: No effect; fluoride up to 3 mg/L is safe
explanation: Fluoride levels: 0.5-0.8 mg/L is the optimal/acceptable range; >1.5 mg/L causes dental fluorosis (mottled enamel); >3-6 mg/L causes skeletal fluorosis (osteosclerosis, crippling). At 2.5 mg/L, dental fluorosis is the expected and most common manifestation. Skeletal fluorosis typically requires prolonged exposure to >3 mg/L. Thyroid disruption is associated with excess iodine or goitrogens, not fluoride at this level.
highYield: Fluoride >1.5 mg/L = dental fluorosis; >3-6 mg/L = skeletal fluorosis.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: In bacteriological examination of drinking water, what is the acceptable MPN (Most Probable Number) of coliform organisms per 100 mL?
explanation: The MPN of coliform organisms in drinking water must be ZERO per 100 mL to be considered microbiologically safe. Any detection of coliforms (total or faecal) indicates faecal contamination and is unacceptable. The WHO also recommends 0 E. coli per 100 mL in treated piped water. WHO acceptable turbidity is <1 NTU (preferred) and <4 NTU (not objectionable) — a separate but related standard.
highYield: Acceptable MPN of coliforms in drinking water = 0 per 100 mL.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: A 22-year-old male labourer from Bihar presents with progressive spastic paraplegia of the lower limbs. He has no sensory loss, no bladder involvement, and reports consuming a local grain-based diet for months during drought. Which of the following is the MOST LIKELY causative toxin?
explanation: Lathyrism is caused by BOAA (beta-oxalyl-amino-alanine), a neurotoxic amino acid found in the grass pea Lathyrus sativus (khesari dal). It primarily affects young adult males, causing irreversible spastic paraplegia without sensory loss or sphincter dysfunction. It is prevalent during droughts when khesari dal is consumed in excess as a food substitute. Sanguinarine causes epidemic dropsy; aflatoxin B1 causes hepatocellular carcinoma; ergotamine causes gangrenous/convulsive ergotism.
highYield: Lathyrism = BOAA from Lathyrus sativus; spastic paraplegia in young adult males.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: During a festival, several people in a village develop bilateral pitting oedema of the legs, erythema, and diarrhoea after consuming adulterated mustard oil. Skin biopsy shows capillary dilatation. Which toxin and its source is responsible?
options: Sanguinarine from Argemone mexicana oil contaminating mustard oil
explanation: Epidemic dropsy is caused by contamination of mustard oil with Argemone mexicana (Mexican prickly poppy) oil, which contains the toxic glucoside sanguinarine. Sanguinarine causes capillary leak syndrome leading to oedema, erythema of skin (especially legs), diarrhoea, and in severe cases, glaucoma and cardiac failure. Outbreaks have occurred repeatedly in Delhi and other Indian states. The disease is NOT caused by ergot, lathyrus, or aflatoxin.
highYield: Epidemic dropsy = argemone oil (sanguinarine) contaminates mustard oil; capillary leak syndrome.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: Which of the following statements about aflatoxin is CORRECT?
options: Aflatoxin is produced by Claviceps purpurea on wheat
Aflatoxin B1 is the most potent and is associated with hepatocellular carcinoma; it resists autoclaving
Aflatoxin is destroyed by boiling at 100°C for 30 minutes
Aflatoxin G1 is more potent than B1
explanation: Aflatoxin is produced by Aspergillus flavus and Aspergillus parasiticus, primarily contaminating groundnuts, maize, and grains. Aflatoxin B1 is the most potent naturally occurring hepatocarcinogen (IARC Group 1). A critical property: aflatoxins are HEAT STABLE and resist autoclaving (121°C, 15 psi), making processing ineffective once contamination occurs. Aflatoxin B1 > B2 > G1 > G2 in order of potency. Claviceps purpurea produces ergot alkaloids, not aflatoxin.
highYield: Aflatoxin B1 = most potent; from Aspergillus flavus on groundnuts; heat stable — resists autoclaving.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: An outbreak occurs in a community where rye bread was the staple food. Affected individuals develop intense burning sensation of the extremities followed by dry gangrene. Some others develop convulsions. The causative organism is:
explanation: Ergotism is caused by Claviceps purpurea, a fungus that parasitises rye, wheat, and other grains. It produces ergot alkaloids with powerful vasoconstrictive and neurotoxic properties. There are two clinical forms: (1) Gangrenous ergotism — vasospasm causing burning sensations ('St Anthony's Fire') followed by dry gangrene of extremities; (2) Convulsive ergotism — CNS effects with epileptiform seizures. Aspergillus flavus causes aflatoxicosis; Fusarium causes fumonisins; Penicillium citrinum produces citrinin.
highYield: Ergotism = Claviceps purpurea on rye; two types: gangrenous (vasospasm) and convulsive.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The WHO STEPwise approach to Surveillance (STEPS) for non-communicable diseases is conducted in three steps. Which step involves BIOCHEMICAL measurements such as blood glucose and cholesterol?
explanation: The WHO STEPS framework for NCD surveillance has three core steps: Step 1 = Questionnaire (self-reported information on tobacco, alcohol, diet, physical activity, history of raised BP/glucose); Step 2 = Physical measurements (height, weight, waist circumference, blood pressure, heart rate); Step 3 = Biochemical measurements (fasting blood glucose, total cholesterol, urinary sodium). This stepwise approach allows countries to build surveillance capacity progressively.
highYield: STEPS: Step 1 = questionnaire; Step 2 = physical measurements; Step 3 = biochemical measurements.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: The National Programme for Prevention and Control of Cancer, Diabetes, CVD and Stroke (NPCDCS) in India primarily operates at which level of the health system for opportunistic screening?
options: Primary Health Centre and above (PHC, CHC, District Hospital)
Only at tertiary care AIIMS centres
explanation: NPCDCS (launched 2010, now merged under National NCD Mission) operates through the primary healthcare system at PHC, CHC, and district hospital levels for opportunistic screening of common NCDs — cancers (oral, breast, cervical), diabetes, hypertension, CVD, and stroke. Health and Wellness Centres (CPHC model) now serve as the frontline for NCD screening, with district NCD clinics for management and cardiac care units at district level.
highYield: NPCDCS operates at PHC and above for opportunistic NCD screening; now part of National NCD Mission.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: NPHCE, a national programme under India's Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, specifically targets which population group?
explanation: NPHCE stands for the National Programme for Health Care of the Elderly, targeting persons aged 60 years and above. It provides geriatric services at all levels: dedicated geriatric OPDs at district hospitals, geriatric wards at regional/state medical colleges, and domiciliary visits at the community level. It was launched in 2010 under the 11th Five-Year Plan to address the growing burden of age-related morbidity in India.
highYield: NPHCE = National Programme for Health Care of the Elderly (60+ years); launched 2010.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Social and Preventive Medicine
stem: During a fire in a chemical storage facility, rescue workers enter without respiratory protection. The concept used to decide the threshold concentration above which entry requires full self-contained breathing apparatus is:
options: TLV-C — ceiling value not to be exceeded at any time
IDLH — Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health
explanation: IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health) is defined by NIOSH as any atmosphere that poses an immediate threat to life, causes irreversible adverse health effects, or impairs an individual's ability to escape. It is the criterion for mandating full self-contained breathing apparatus (SCBA) during emergency rescue. TLV-TWA governs routine 8-hour exposures; TLV-STEL is for 15-minute peaks; TLV-C is for instantaneous ceiling — none of these specifically govern emergency SCBA selection.
highYield: IDLH = immediately dangerous to life/health; threshold for mandatory SCBA in emergency entry.
pyqNote: SPM fringe — confirmed NEET PG miss

subject: Biochemistry
stem: A molecular biology researcher studying gene regulation in C. elegans finds a small non-coding RNA (~22 nucleotides) that binds to the 3' untranslated region of a target mRNA and recruits a multi-protein complex, ultimately suppressing protein expression WITHOUT cleaving the mRNA. Which of the following best describes the molecular complex mediating this silencing?
options: Spliceosome complex containing U1 and U2 snRNPs
Signal Recognition Particle (SRP) blocking ribosomal transit
Proteasome complex targeting polyubiquitinated ribosomal subunits
explanation: The Nobel Prize 2024 (Ambros and Ruvkun) recognized the discovery of microRNA (miRNA). miRNAs are ~22-nucleotide endogenous non-coding RNAs that bind with PARTIAL complementarity to the 3' UTR of target mRNAs. They function via the RISC (RNA-Induced Silencing Complex), with AGO2 (Argonaute 2) as the catalytic core protein. Unlike siRNA (which causes mRNA cleavage via perfect complementarity), miRNA primarily causes translational repression or mRNA deadenylation without cleavage. The first miRNA discovered was lin-4 in C. elegans by Victor Ambros.
highYield: miRNA mechanism: RISC + AGO2 + partial complementarity to 3' UTR = translational repression (NOT mRNA cleavage); contrast with siRNA = perfect complementarity = mRNA cleavage
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Biochemistry
stem: A cancer biologist compares two types of small RNA molecules in tumor cells. RNA-A (23 nucleotides) is endogenously transcribed, forms hairpin precursors processed by Dicer, shows partial complementarity to its target, and inhibits translation. RNA-B (21 nucleotides) is introduced exogenously, shows perfect complementarity to its target, and causes endonucleolytic cleavage of the target transcript. Which statement correctly distinguishes RNA-A from RNA-B?
options: RNA-A is siRNA; RNA-B is miRNA
RNA-A is miRNA; RNA-B is siRNA
Both are miRNA but differ in origin — endogenous vs viral
Both are siRNA but RNA-A uses the Drosha/Dicer pathway while RNA-B uses only Dicer
explanation: RNA-A describes miRNA (endogenous, processed by Drosha in nucleus then Dicer in cytoplasm, partial complementarity, translational repression). RNA-B describes siRNA (exogenous/synthetic, perfect complementarity, AGO2-mediated mRNA cleavage/slicing). Both use the RISC complex and AGO2, but the degree of complementarity and the outcome differ. The Nobel 2024 discovery by Ambros and Ruvkun specifically covers endogenous miRNAs as universal post-transcriptional gene regulators across all multicellular eukaryotes.
highYield: miRNA vs siRNA distinction: endogenous vs exogenous origin, partial vs perfect complementarity, translational repression vs mRNA cleavage — all via RISC/AGO2
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Biochemistry
stem: An oncologist notes that miR-21 is markedly overexpressed in breast cancer tissue compared to normal breast epithelium. This miRNA targets PTEN mRNA and suppresses its translation, leading to uncontrolled PI3K/Akt signalling. Based on Nobel Prize 2024-recognized miRNA biology, miR-21 is best classified as:
options: A tumour suppressor miRNA that reduces oncogene expression
An onco-miRNA (oncomiR) that promotes tumour progression by silencing a tumour suppressor
A siRNA therapeutically introduced to silence PI3K
A long non-coding RNA regulating chromatin remodelling at the PTEN locus
explanation: miR-21 is the prototypical oncomiR (onco-miRNA) — it is overexpressed in multiple cancers and silences tumour suppressor genes including PTEN, PDCD4, and RECK. By suppressing PTEN, it disinhibits PI3K/Akt signalling, promoting proliferation and survival. In contrast, tumour suppressor miRNAs (e.g., miR-34a, which is a p53 transcriptional target and silences CDK6 and BCL-2) are downregulated in cancers. Circulating miRNAs (including miR-21) in plasma are being investigated as liquid biopsy biomarkers — a direct clinical translation of the Nobel 2024 discovery.
highYield: miR-21 = oncomiR (overexpressed in cancer, silences PTEN); miR-34a = tumour suppressor miRNA (p53 target); circulating miRNAs = cancer biomarkers
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Biochemistry
stem: The 2024 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine was awarded for the discovery of microRNA and its role in post-transcriptional gene regulation. The FIRST miRNA ever discovered, identified in C. elegans in 1993, was named:
explanation: lin-4 was the first miRNA discovered, identified by Victor Ambros in 1993 in Caenorhabditis elegans. It was found to regulate the lin-14 gene post-transcriptionally by binding its 3' UTR. The second miRNA, let-7, was discovered by Gary Ruvkun in 2000 and was notable for being conserved across species including humans — demonstrating that miRNA regulation is a universal mechanism of gene regulation. Both discoveries formed the basis of the 2024 Nobel Prize.
highYield: First miRNA = lin-4 (Ambros, 1993, C. elegans); second = let-7 (Ruvkun, 2000) — first shown to be conserved across species including humans
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Biochemistry
stem: Katalin Karikó and Drew Weissman (Nobel 2023) made a critical modification to in-vitro transcribed mRNA that allowed it to be used therapeutically without triggering harmful innate immune responses. A pharmaceutical scientist studying this modification notes it reduces activation of TLR7 and TLR8 while simultaneously INCREASING translational efficiency. This key nucleoside modification involves replacing uridine with:
explanation: The Nobel 2023-winning discovery by Karikó and Weissman was that substituting uridine with pseudouridine (Ψ) — and more specifically N1-methyl-pseudouridine (m1Ψ) — in synthetic mRNA prevents recognition by innate immune pattern recognition receptors TLR7 and TLR8 (endosomal toll-like receptors that detect single-stranded RNA). Crucially, this modification also increases ribosomal translational efficiency, producing more protein per mRNA molecule. Both the Pfizer-BioNTech (BNT162b2) and Moderna (mRNA-1273) COVID-19 vaccines use m1Ψ-modified mRNA.
highYield: N1-methyl-pseudouridine (m1Ψ) substitution: evades TLR7/TLR8 innate immunity + increases translation efficiency — the core Nobel 2023 discovery enabling COVID-19 mRNA vaccines
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Biochemistry
stem: A medical student studying COVID-19 vaccines reads that mRNA-based vaccines cannot integrate into the host genome and do not carry risk of insertional mutagenesis. Which of the following correctly explains the intracellular fate of the mRNA vaccine molecule after entry into host cells?
options: mRNA is reverse-transcribed into cDNA by host reverse transcriptase and forms an episome
mRNA is directly translated in the cytoplasm by ribosomes, producing spike protein, and is then degraded within days
mRNA is transported to the nucleus where it undergoes splicing before cytoplasmic translation
mRNA integrates into mitochondrial DNA and is expressed only in energy-generating tissues
explanation: mRNA vaccines (BNT162b2, mRNA-1273) deliver modified mRNA enclosed in lipid nanoparticles (LNPs). After endocytosis, the LNP disassembles in the endosome, releasing mRNA into the cytoplasm. Ribosomes directly translate the mRNA into SARS-CoV-2 spike protein in the cytoplasm. The mRNA never enters the nucleus (human cells lack the cellular machinery to reverse-transcribe mRNA into DNA under normal circumstances) and is degraded within days by normal mRNA turnover mechanisms. The expressed spike protein triggers adaptive immune responses (B and T cells). This mechanism was enabled by the Nobel 2023 discovery of m1Ψ modification.
highYield: mRNA vaccine fate: cytoplasmic translation only → spike protein production → immune response → mRNA degraded within days; NEVER enters nucleus, NEVER integrates into genome
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Biochemistry
topic: mRNA vaccines — LNP delivery — Nobel Prize 2023
stem: The mRNA vaccines for COVID-19 use lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) as delivery vehicles. The key functional lipid component of these LNPs is an ionisable cationic lipid that is positively charged at low pH (facilitating mRNA loading) but neutral at physiological pH (reducing toxicity). For the Moderna mRNA-1273 vaccine, this ionisable lipid is:
explanation: Both Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna vaccines use ionisable cationic lipids as the key functional LNP component. SM-102 (heptadecan-9-yl 8-((2-hydroxyethyl)(6-oxo-6-(undecyloxy)hexyl)amino)octanoate) is used in Moderna's mRNA-1273. ALC-0315 is used in Pfizer-BioNTech's BNT162b2. Ionisable lipids are positively charged at the low pH of manufacturing (facilitating mRNA encapsulation via electrostatic interaction) but become neutral at physiological pH 7.4 (reducing systemic toxicity). After cellular uptake, acidification in endosomes re-protonates the lipid, disrupting the endosomal membrane and releasing mRNA into the cytoplasm.
highYield: SM-102 = Moderna mRNA-1273 ionisable lipid; ALC-0315 = Pfizer-BioNTech BNT162b2 ionisable lipid; ionisable lipids = positively charged at low pH (manufacturing) → neutral at pH 7.4 (physiological)
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Biochemistry
topic: mRNA vaccines vs other platforms — Nobel Prize 2023
stem: A vaccinologist compares mRNA vaccines with adenoviral vector vaccines (such as ChAdOx1, used in AstraZeneca COVID-19 vaccine). Which of the following is a CORRECT distinction between mRNA vaccines and adenoviral vector vaccines?
options: mRNA vaccines require the antigen gene to enter the nucleus for transcription; adenoviral vaccines do not
mRNA vaccines use m1Ψ-modified RNA translated in the cytoplasm and cannot integrate into the genome; adenoviral vector DNA enters the nucleus and carries a theoretical integration risk
mRNA vaccines stimulate only humoral immunity; adenoviral vaccines stimulate both humoral and cellular immunity
mRNA vaccines must be administered intravenously; adenoviral vaccines can be given intramuscularly
explanation: The critical Nobel 2023-recognized distinction: mRNA vaccines deliver m1Ψ-modified mRNA that is translated directly in the cytoplasm, never entering the nucleus, making genomic integration biologically impossible. Adenoviral vector vaccines (ChAdOx1, Sputnik V, Ad26.COV2.S) deliver a DNA insert that DOES enter the nucleus for transcription into mRNA, carrying a theoretical (though extremely rare) risk of genomic integration. Both vaccine types stimulate humoral (antibody) and cellular (T-cell) immunity. Both Pfizer-BioNTech and Moderna mRNA vaccines are given intramuscularly (deltoid).
highYield: mRNA vaccine: cytoplasmic translation, NO nuclear entry, NO integration risk; Adenoviral vector: DNA enters nucleus for transcription, theoretical integration risk
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Physiology
stem: A 28-year-old man accidentally bites into a very hot chilli pepper. He experiences an intense burning sensation in his mouth. A research physiologist explains that this sensation is mediated by the same receptor that also responds to temperatures above 42°C and to acidic pH. The receptor responsible is:
options: TRPM8 — activated by capsaicin, cold, and menthol
TRPA1 — activated by pungent chemicals and noxious cold
TRPV1 — activated by capsaicin, heat >42°C, and low pH
TRPV4 — activated by warm temperatures (27-35°C) and mechanical stimuli
explanation: TRPV1 (Transient Receptor Potential Vanilloid 1) was identified by David Julius (Nobel 2021) using capsaicin as a molecular probe. TRPV1 is a polymodal nociceptor activated by: (1) capsaicin (the pungent compound in chilli peppers), (2) temperatures above 42°C (noxious heat threshold), and (3) low pH (acidosis during inflammation). This explains why the same receptor mediates the 'burning' sensation from chilli and from actual heat. TRPM8 responds to menthol and cold <25°C; TRPA1 to mustard oil/allicin; TRPV4 to warm (27-35°C) and mechanical stimuli.
highYield: TRPV1 = triple polymodal nociceptor: capsaicin + heat >42°C + low pH; this Nobel 2021 discovery explained why chilli and heat both 'burn'
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Physiology
topic: TRP channels and thermal allodynia — Nobel Prize 2021
stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with severe sunburn on her back. She reports that even lukewarm water (35°C) on her back causes intense burning pain, whereas 35°C is normally a non-painful warm stimulus. This phenomenon is called thermal allodynia. Which of the following best explains the molecular mechanism underlying this observation?
options: Sunburn destroys TRPM8 cold receptors, causing unmasking of TRPV1-mediated heat sensation
Inflammatory mediators (PGE2, bradykinin, NGF) sensitize TRPV1, lowering its activation threshold from >42°C to ~35°C, making normally innocuous warmth painful
Sunburn causes central sensitization via NMDA receptor activation in the thalamus, making all sensory input perceived as painful
UV radiation directly opens Piezo2 channels in skin keratinocytes, which then release substance P causing pain
explanation: Thermal allodynia after sunburn is the classic demonstration of peripheral TRPV1 sensitization. Inflammatory mediators released at the sunburn site (PGE2 via EP receptors, bradykinin via B2 receptors, NGF via TrkA) activate intracellular signalling (PKA, PKC) that phosphorylates TRPV1, lowering its heat-activation threshold from >42°C to approximately 35°C. Consequently, water that normally feels comfortably warm (35°C) now activates TRPV1, generating a pain signal. This sensitization mechanism — discovered in Julius's laboratory before the Nobel 2021 award — explains peripheral sensitization in inflammation.
highYield: Thermal allodynia mechanism: inflammatory mediators → PKA/PKC phosphorylation of TRPV1 → threshold shifts from >42°C to ~35°C → normally warm stimulus activates TRPV1 → burning pain
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Physiology
topic: TRP channels — cold and menthol — Nobel Prize 2021
stem: A toothpaste manufacturer uses menthol to create a 'cooling' sensation. A physiologist explains that menthol activates the same receptor that is activated by temperatures below 25°C, producing the sensation of cold. The receptor is also activated by TRPA1 agonists at lower temperatures. Which receptor mediates the cooling effect of menthol?
explanation: TRPM8 (Transient Receptor Potential Melastatin 8) is the molecular receptor for 'cold' sensation identified by David Julius (Nobel 2021). It is activated by: (1) temperatures below 25°C (cool to cold range) and (2) menthol and other cooling compounds (icilin). This explains why menthol creates a 'cooling' sensation without actually lowering tissue temperature. TRPA1 (activated by allyl isothiocyanate in mustard, allicin in garlic, and noxious cold <10°C) is distinct. TRPV1 responds to heat and capsaicin; TRPV4 to warm temperatures (27-35°C).
highYield: TRPM8 = cold receptor (<25°C) + menthol/icilin; TRPA1 = pungent chemicals (mustard oil, garlic/allicin) + noxious cold (<10°C); TRPV1 = heat (>42°C) + capsaicin + low pH
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Physiology
stem: A haematologist evaluates a 30-year-old man with chronic haemolytic anaemia. Red blood cell (RBC) osmotic fragility is decreased (cells resist hypotonic lysis), but RBCs appear shrunken (stomatocytes), are dehydrated, and have elevated MCHC. Genetic testing reveals a gain-of-function mutation in a mechanosensitive ion channel constitutively expressed in RBCs. The mutation causes excessive Ca²⁺ influx → K⁺ and water efflux → RBC dehydration. The mutated channel is:
options: Piezo1 — the RBC mechanosensitive channel linked to hereditary xerocytosis
KCNN4 (Gardos channel) — the Ca²⁺-activated K⁺ channel upstream of RBC dehydration
explanation: Piezo1 (discovered by Ardem Patapoutian, Nobel 2021) is a mechanosensitive cation channel expressed in red blood cells. Gain-of-function mutations in PIEZO1 cause hereditary xerocytosis (dehydrated stomatocytosis): constitutive Piezo1 activation → excessive Ca²⁺ influx → activation of the Gardos channel (KCNN4, a Ca²⁺-activated K⁺ channel) → K⁺ and water efflux → RBC dehydration and shrinkage. The result is haemolytic anaemia with dehydrated RBCs (elevated MCHC, decreased osmotic fragility). Piezo2 is expressed in sensory neurons and mediates proprioception and light touch, not RBC function.
highYield: Piezo1 gain-of-function = hereditary xerocytosis: Piezo1 → Ca²⁺ influx → Gardos channel activation → K⁺/water efflux → dehydrated RBCs (elevated MCHC, decreased osmotic fragility)
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Physiology
stem: A neurophysiologist wants to study the behaviour of a SINGLE voltage-gated Na⁺ channel in its native cell membrane environment without disrupting intracellular contents. She positions a polished glass pipette on the cell membrane and applies gentle suction to form a gigaohm seal. She then records channel currents WITHOUT rupturing the membrane patch. Which patch clamp configuration is she using?
explanation: In the cell-attached (on-cell) configuration, the patch pipette forms a tight gigaohm seal (gigaseal) on the intact cell membrane without rupturing it. This allows recording of single-channel currents through the membrane patch while the cell interior remains intact and undisturbed. This is the fundamental starting configuration for all patch clamp experiments (Nobel 1991, Neher and Sakmann). Whole-cell involves rupturing the patch to access cytoplasm; inside-out exposes the cytoplasmic face by pulling the pipette away; outside-out exposes the extracellular face by pulling after going whole-cell.
highYield: Cell-attached (on-cell) patch clamp: gigaohm seal + intact cell + single-channel recording; cytoplasm undisturbed — the starting configuration for all patch clamp variants
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Physiology
stem: A researcher studying ligand-gated ion channels wants to apply intracellular second messengers (such as cAMP and Ca²⁺) directly to the cytoplasmic face of a channel while recording single-channel currents. To do this, she needs the cytoplasmic face of the membrane patch to face the bath solution. Which patch clamp configuration allows this?
explanation: In the inside-out configuration, the pipette forms a gigaseal in cell-attached mode, and then the pipette is rapidly pulled away from the cell — the patch remains attached to the pipette tip but the cytoplasmic (intracellular) face is now exposed to the bath solution. This allows precise manipulation of intracellular ligands, ions, and second messengers (cAMP, Ca²⁺, ATP) while recording single-channel currents. Outside-out configuration (achieved by going whole-cell then pulling the pipette) exposes the extracellular face to the bath — useful for studying neurotransmitter receptors. Whole-cell allows access to total cellular currents but not single-channel resolution.
highYield: Inside-out patch: cytoplasmic face exposed to bath → manipulate intracellular ligands; Outside-out patch: extracellular face exposed → test neurotransmitters; Whole-cell: total cell current, not single-channel
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Physiology
stem: A type 2 diabetes patient with severe insulin resistance starts a supervised exercise programme. Despite absent insulin signalling (due to defective IRS-1 phosphorylation), his skeletal muscle glucose uptake increases significantly during exercise. The mechanism responsible for this insulin-independent GLUT4 translocation during exercise involves activation of:
options: mTORC2 via Akt phosphorylation at Ser473
AMPK (AMP-activated protein kinase) via increased AMP:ATP ratio during muscle contraction
PKC-ζ (atypical protein kinase C) downstream of PI3K activation
explanation: Exercise-induced GLUT4 translocation is insulin-INDEPENDENT. During muscle contraction, ATP consumption raises the AMP:ATP ratio, activating AMPK (AMP-activated protein kinase). AMPK phosphorylates TBC1D1 (a Rab-GAP protein), which releases its inhibition of Rab GTPases, allowing GLUT4-containing vesicles to fuse with the plasma membrane. This pathway is entirely separate from the insulin pathway (IRS-1 → PI3K → Akt → AS160 phosphorylation → GLUT4 fusion). This is why exercise is particularly beneficial in type 2 diabetes: it bypasses the defective insulin signalling pathway entirely.
highYield: Exercise → AMPK (via high AMP:ATP) → TBC1D1 phosphorylation → GLUT4 translocation (insulin-INDEPENDENT); Insulin → IRS-1 → PI3K → Akt → AS160 → GLUT4 (separate pathway)
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Physiology
stem: A physiologist compares two pathways that both cause GLUT4 translocation to the plasma membrane in skeletal muscle cells. Pathway A is activated by insulin binding to its receptor and involves sequential activation of IRS-1, PI3K, PDK1, and Akt, ultimately phosphorylating AS160 (TBC1D4). Pathway B is activated by muscle contraction and involves a sensor of cellular energy status. Which of the following correctly pairs Pathway B's proximal sensor with its direct GLUT4-regulatory substrate?
options: AMPK (energy sensor, activated by high AMP:ATP) → TBC1D1 phosphorylation
CAMKII (Ca²⁺/calmodulin-dependent kinase) → AS160 (TBC1D4) phosphorylation — same substrate as insulin
PKA (activated by cAMP from adrenergic stimulation) → HSL phosphorylation
explanation: During exercise, the AMP:ATP ratio rises as ATP is hydrolysed during contraction. AMPK (AMP-activated protein kinase) senses this and is activated by AMP binding to its gamma subunit (and by LKB1-mediated phosphorylation at Thr172). AMPK phosphorylates TBC1D1 (a paralog of AS160/TBC1D4 used by the insulin pathway) — though both TBC1D1 and AS160 can be phosphorylated by AMPK in muscle. Phosphorylation of TBC1D1/AS160 inhibits their Rab-GAP activity, allowing GTP-bound Rab proteins to facilitate GLUT4 vesicle docking and fusion. Note: CaMKII also contributes to exercise-induced GLUT4 but is not the primary proximal energy sensor.
highYield: Pathway B (exercise): AMPK → TBC1D1 phosphorylation → GLUT4; Pathway A (insulin): Akt → AS160 (TBC1D4) phosphorylation → GLUT4; both converge on Rab-GAP inhibition but use distinct upstream kinases
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Physiology
stem: A neurologist reviews a patient who underwent bilateral temporal lobectomy for refractory epilepsy. Post-operatively, the patient exhibits placidity, absence of fear responses, inability to recognise threatening stimuli, hypersexuality, hyperorality, and visual agnosia. This constellation of findings — named Klüver-Bucy syndrome — is primarily attributed to bilateral damage to which structure?
options: Hippocampus — bilateral damage causes anterograde amnesia without affective changes
Cingulate gyrus — bilateral damage impairs attention and pain processing
Amygdala — bilateral damage abolishes fear recognition, emotional valence assignment, and threat response
Nucleus accumbens — bilateral damage impairs reward-driven behaviour and motivation
explanation: Klüver-Bucy syndrome results from bilateral amygdala (plus anterior temporal cortex) damage. The amygdala is the critical limbic structure for emotional processing, particularly fear conditioning and threat recognition. The basolateral nucleus mediates fear conditioning and associative emotional learning; the central nucleus coordinates autonomic (via hypothalamus) and neuroendocrine fear responses (via periaqueductal grey). Bilateral amygdala destruction produces the classic Klüver-Bucy triad: (1) psychic blindness/visual agnosia, (2) placidity and absence of fear (loss of threat processing), (3) hyperorality and hypersexuality (disinhibited oral/sexual exploratory behaviour). The hippocampus (HM case) produces amnesia without these affective features.
highYield: Klüver-Bucy syndrome = bilateral amygdala damage: placidity + hypersexuality + hyperorality + visual agnosia + psychic blindness; Basolateral amygdala = fear conditioning; Central amygdala = autonomic/neuroendocrine fear response
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Physiology
stem: A neuroscientist maps the amygdala's efferent projections in a rat fear conditioning experiment. After a tone is paired with a footshock, the rat freezes upon hearing the tone alone. Inactivating a specific amygdala sub-nucleus abolishes the freezing response and the associated tachycardia and corticotropin release, while sparing the ability to form new tone-shock associations. The sub-nucleus inactivated, and the downstream projection responsible for freezing behaviour, are respectively:
options: Basolateral nucleus inactivated; projection to hippocampus responsible for contextual freezing
Central nucleus inactivated; projection to periaqueductal grey (PAG) responsible for freezing behaviour
Lateral nucleus inactivated; projection to nucleus accumbens responsible for appetitive responses
Medial nucleus inactivated; projection to olfactory bulb responsible for predator odour detection
explanation: The amygdala fear circuit is functionally divided: the BASOLATERAL complex (lateral + basal nuclei) receives sensory inputs from thalamus and cortex and is the site of fear ACQUISITION and associative learning (tone-shock pairing). The CENTRAL nucleus (CeA) is the principal OUTPUT hub — it projects to the periaqueductal grey (PAG, mediating freezing/immobility), to the lateral hypothalamus (autonomic responses: tachycardia, blood pressure increase), and to the paraventricular nucleus/HPA axis (corticotropin/cortisol release). Inactivating CeA abolishes the expression of fear responses (freezing, autonomic, neuroendocrine) while sparing acquisition, because the basolateral complex remains intact to form the association.
highYield: Basolateral amygdala = fear ACQUISITION (sensory input); Central amygdala = fear EXPRESSION (outputs to PAG → freezing; lateral hypothalamus → autonomic; PVN → HPA axis); damage to central nucleus abolishes fear expression
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Physiology
stem: A 19-year-old girl presents with progressive gait ataxia since childhood. Examination reveals absent proprioception (impaired joint position sense), absent vibration sense, and absent light touch, but INTACT pain and temperature sensation. Remarkably, she shows no scoliosis correction despite normal muscle strength. Genetic testing reveals a loss-of-function mutation in a mechanosensitive ion channel expressed in proprioceptive neurons (large-diameter Aβ fibres) and Merkel cells. The mutated gene encodes:
options: TRPV1 — the heat and capsaicin receptor
Piezo1 — the RBC and vascular endothelial mechanosensitive channel
Piezo2 — the mechanosensitive channel mediating proprioception, light touch, and baroreceptor function
TRPA1 — the pungent chemical and noxious cold receptor
explanation: Piezo2 (discovered by Ardem Patapoutian, Nobel 2021) is the primary mechanosensitive ion channel mediating: (1) proprioception (joint position sense, muscle spindle afference), (2) discriminative light touch (via Merkel cells and Meissner corpuscles), and (3) baroreceptor reflex (arterial pressure sensing). Loss-of-function mutations in PIEZO2 cause a syndrome of absent proprioception and absent discriminative touch with INTACT pain and temperature sensation (because pain/temperature use small C and Aδ fibres via TRP channels, not Piezo2). The characteristic stance/gait ataxia from absent proprioception, combined with progressive scoliosis (due to absent proprioceptive feedback for postural correction), are hallmarks. Piezo1 is expressed in RBCs and vascular endothelium.
highYield: Piezo2 loss-of-function: absent proprioception + absent light touch + scoliosis, but INTACT pain/temperature (TRP channel-mediated); Piezo2 = mechanosensory ion channel for proprioception, light touch, baroreception
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Biochemistry
topic: mRNA vaccine vs inactivated vaccine platforms — Nobel Prize 2023
stem: A public health officer compares mRNA vaccines (Nobel 2023) with traditional inactivated virus vaccines (e.g., Covaxin, BBV152) for COVID-19. She notes that inactivated vaccines present the entire viral proteome to the immune system, whereas mRNA vaccines present only the spike protein. Which of the following is an ADVANTAGE of the mRNA vaccine platform that is directly attributable to the Nobel 2023 discovery?
options: mRNA vaccines require only a single dose and do not need cold chain storage
mRNA vaccines can be rapidly redesigned within weeks when a new variant emerges, and the m1Ψ modification ensures efficient translation without immune suppression of the mRNA itself
mRNA vaccines stimulate only T-cell immunity, providing longer-lasting protection than inactivated vaccines
mRNA vaccines use live attenuated virus, making them more immunogenic than inactivated preparations
explanation: The Nobel 2023 discovery — m1Ψ modification of mRNA — directly enables two platform advantages: (1) speed of redesign: only the mRNA sequence encoding the antigen needs to change (no virus culture, attenuation, or inactivation required), enabling variant-adapted boosters within weeks; and (2) the m1Ψ modification prevents TLR7/TLR8 recognition while increasing translational efficiency, ensuring robust antigen production without the mRNA being degraded by innate immune responses before it can be translated. Both mRNA vaccines require two doses (primary series) and ultra-cold storage (Pfizer: -70°C, Moderna: -20°C), which are disadvantages, not advantages. They stimulate both humoral and cellular immunity.
highYield: mRNA vaccine advantage: rapid redesign (weeks for new variant) + m1Ψ modification enables efficient translation without innate immune degradation — directly enabled by Nobel 2023 discovery
pyqNote: Nobel Prize research — confirmed 2024/2025 NEET PG prediction

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 34-year-old agricultural worker presents with painful tingling and burning sensation in both lower limbs for 3 weeks, followed by progressive hair loss all over the scalp. Examination reveals transverse white bands on all fingernails. Neurological exam shows diminished deep tendon reflexes and loss of vibration sense distally. Which of the following BEST distinguishes this poisoning from arsenic toxicity?
options: Presence of transverse white nail bands
Gastrointestinal symptoms in the acute phase
explanation: Both thallium and arsenic can cause Mees lines (transverse white nail bands) and peripheral neuropathy. However, ALOPECIA (hair loss) occurring 2-3 weeks after exposure is the hallmark that distinguishes thallium poisoning. Arsenic causes rain-drop pigmentation, hyperkeratosis, and Aldrich-Mees lines but does NOT characteristically cause alopecia. The painful neuropathy plus alopecia triad is pathognomonic for thallium. Lead causes Burton line on gums; mercury causes neuropsychiatric symptoms and salivation.
highYield: Alopecia + painful peripheral neuropathy + Mees lines = Thallium poisoning triad
pyqNote: Rare but recurring — confirmed NEET PG miss 2022

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 28-year-old man is brought to the emergency department with acute confusion, severe alopecia, and painful burning feet. Nail examination reveals transverse white lines across all nails. Blood and urine thallium levels are markedly elevated. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate antidote?
explanation: Prussian blue (ferric hexacyanoferrate) is the specific antidote for thallium poisoning. It acts in the gut by ion exchange — thallium ions substitute for potassium in the crystal lattice of Prussian blue, preventing enterohepatic recirculation and increasing fecal excretion. BAL (dimercaprol) is used for arsenic and mercury poisoning. EDTA/DMSA is used for lead poisoning. Desferrioxamine chelates iron. Remember: Prussian blue = Thallium; BAL = Arsenic/Mercury; EDTA = Lead.
highYield: Prussian blue is the specific antidote for thallium poisoning
pyqNote: Rare but recurring — confirmed NEET PG miss 2022

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 45-year-old pesticide factory worker presents with progressive hair loss, painful peripheral neuropathy, and white transverse lines on the nails appearing 3 weeks after a workplace exposure incident. The MOST characteristic finding that separates thallium poisoning from lead poisoning is:
explanation: Burton line (blue-black line on the gingival margin) is the hallmark of LEAD poisoning, not thallium. Lead poisoning features include basophilic stippling on peripheral blood smear, ALA dehydratase inhibition, lead colic, and the Burton line on gums. Thallium poisoning does NOT cause a gum line. The distinguishing features of thallium are alopecia + painful neuropathy + Mees lines. Mees lines can also occur in arsenic and occasionally mercury but NOT in lead poisoning.
highYield: Burton line = Lead; Alopecia + Mees lines = Thallium; No Burton line or gum line in thallium
pyqNote: Rare but recurring — confirmed NEET PG miss 2022

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A 38-year-old woman presents with sudden onset total scalp hair loss over 3 weeks, associated with shooting pains in both feet and patchy numbness. Examination reveals all nails showing equidistant white transverse bands. The husband works in a factory manufacturing rat poison. The antidote chosen for this patient works by which mechanism?
options: Chelation of thallium in the bloodstream and urinary excretion
Ion exchange in the gut interrupting enterohepatic recirculation
Oxidation of thallium to a non-toxic form
Competitive inhibition of thallium at cellular receptors
explanation: Prussian blue (ferric hexacyanoferrate) acts by ion exchange in the intestinal lumen — thallium ions (Tl+) substitute for K+ ions within the crystal lattice of Prussian blue, trapping thallium and preventing its reabsorption during enterohepatic circulation. This increases fecal excretion. It does NOT work by chelation in blood (unlike EDTA for lead) or by oxidation. Thallium mimics potassium due to similar ionic radius, which is why it is so toxic to K+-dependent processes in the body.
highYield: Prussian blue mechanism = ion exchange in gut, interrupts enterohepatic recirculation of thallium
pyqNote: Rare but recurring — confirmed NEET PG miss 2022

subject: Forensic Medicine
stem: A toxicology case report describes a patient with subacute onset of painful lower limb neuropathy, complete loss of scalp and body hair at 3 weeks, and white transverse lines on all nails. Compared to arsenic poisoning, which of the following features is UNIQUE to thallium poisoning?
explanation: Alopecia (hair loss) 2-3 weeks after exposure is the UNIQUE feature of thallium poisoning that does not occur in arsenic toxicity. Both thallium and arsenic cause Mees lines (Aldrich-Mees lines) and peripheral neuropathy. Arsenic causes rain-drop pigmentation, hyperkeratosis of palms and soles, and GI symptoms but NOT alopecia. Thallium causes no rain-drop pigmentation and no GI melanosis. The mnemonic: Thallium = 'Thinning hair' (alopecia) while Arsenic = 'Rain-drop' pigmentation.
highYield: Alopecia is UNIQUE to thallium; both thallium and arsenic cause Mees lines and neuropathy
pyqNote: Rare but recurring — confirmed NEET PG miss 2022

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 52-year-old farmer from rural Andhra Pradesh presents with a slowly progressive verrucous, warty, crusted lesion on the dorsum of the right foot over 5 years. He gives history of a thorn prick injury at the same site years ago. Skin biopsy with H&E staining shows thick-walled, brown, muriform cells with internal septation in two planes within the dermis. What is the MOST likely diagnosis?
explanation: The description of thick-walled, brown, muriform cells (sclerotic bodies/Medlar bodies/copper pennies) with internal septation in two planes is pathognomonic for chromoblastomycosis. The clinical picture — a farmer, traumatic inoculation, verrucous lesion on foot — strongly supports this. Sporotrichosis shows cigar-shaped yeast in macrophages. Lobomycosis shows chain-like lemon-shaped yeast. Eumycetoma shows fungal grains (not muriform cells). The brown color is due to melanin in the cell wall of dematiaceous fungi.
highYield: Medlar bodies (muriform cells/copper pennies/sclerotic bodies) = pathognomonic for chromoblastomycosis
pyqNote: Rare but recurring — confirmed NEET PG miss 2022

subject: Microbiology
stem: A 44-year-old agricultural worker presents with a warty plaque on the lower limb. KOH preparation of scrapings shows brown, thick-walled, round cells with cross-wall septa. Which of the following organisms is the MOST common cause of this condition?
explanation: Fonsecaea pedrosoi is the most common causative organism of chromoblastomycosis worldwide (accounting for >90% of cases in tropical regions). Other agents include Cladophialophora carrionii, Phialophora verrucosa, Fonsecaea monophora, and Rhinocladiella aquaspersa. All are dematiaceous (melanized) fungi. The KOH preparation revealing brown muriform cells (Medlar bodies/sclerotic bodies/copper pennies) is diagnostic. The melanin pigment in cell walls is what gives them the characteristic brown color visible even without special stains.
highYield: Fonsecaea pedrosoi = most common cause of chromoblastomycosis
pyqNote: Rare but recurring — confirmed NEET PG miss 2022

subject: Microbiology
stem: A patient with chromoblastomycosis confirmed by biopsy showing sclerotic bodies is referred for treatment. The lesion is localized to a 3 cm area on the dorsum of the foot. What is the FIRST-LINE medical treatment?
explanation: Itraconazole is the first-line treatment for chromoblastomycosis, often combined with terbinafine for synergistic effect. Terbinafine alone is also effective. Flucytosine can be used as an adjunct. Fluconazole has minimal activity against chromoblastomycosis. Amphotericin B is used for systemic infections like mucormycosis and cryptococcosis, not chromoblastomycosis. Griseofulvin is for dermatophytes only. For localized disease, wide surgical excision combined with antifungal therapy is curative.
highYield: Itraconazole (+/- terbinafine) = first-line for chromoblastomycosis; surgery for localized disease
pyqNote: Rare but recurring — confirmed NEET PG miss 2022

subject: Microbiology
stem: A pathology slide from a skin biopsy of a foot lesion in a farmer shows pseudoepitheliomatous hyperplasia of the epidermis with transepidermal elimination. In the dermis, giant cells are seen engulfing brown, round, thick-walled bodies with internal septation in horizontal and vertical planes. These structures are BEST described as:
explanation: Medlar bodies (also called sclerotic bodies, copper pennies, or muriform cells) are the pathognomonic finding of chromoblastomycosis. They are thick-walled, brown, round to oval structures with internal septation in two perpendicular planes, resembling a copper penny or a pea. Asteroid bodies are seen in sporotrichosis (Splendore-Hoeppli material around yeast). Schaumann bodies are seen in sarcoidosis (calcified concentric laminae in giant cells). Michaelis-Gutmann bodies are the hallmark of malakoplakia (targetoid calcified inclusions).
highYield: Medlar bodies = copper pennies = sclerotic bodies = muriform cells; all synonyms for chromoblastomycosis hallmark
pyqNote: Rare but recurring — confirmed NEET PG miss 2022

subject: Pathology
stem: A 56-year-old diabetic woman with recurrent UTIs undergoes cystoscopy, which reveals multiple soft, yellow-brown plaques on the bladder mucosa.
