explanation: Prolonged Grief Disorder (PGD) is the only brand-new disorder added in DSM-5-TR. It is characterized by intense yearning or preoccupation with the deceased persisting >12 months after the loss (>6 months for children), causing significant functional impairment. It is placed in the Trauma and Stressor-Related Disorders chapter. DSM-5-TR is a Text Revision that revised text for >70 disorders but added only one new disorder. Complex PTSD exists in ICD-11 but was not added to DSM-5-TR. Persistent Depressive Disorder and DMDD were in the original DSM-5 (2013).
highYield: DSM-5-TR: only ONE new disorder added = Prolonged Grief Disorder (>12 months yearning/preoccupation with deceased)

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A patient's mother died 14 months ago. He has persistent intense yearning for her, finds it difficult to accept her death, feels emotionally numb, and cannot engage in meaningful activities. He scores high on the Prolonged Grief Disorder scale. According to DSM-5-TR, what duration criterion must be met for diagnosing Prolonged Grief Disorder in adults?
explanation: In DSM-5-TR, the duration criterion for Prolonged Grief Disorder (PGD) in adults is >12 months since the death of the person. The intense yearning/preoccupation must persist beyond this period and cause significant functional impairment. For children, the threshold is >6 months. PGD is distinct from normal bereavement and from major depressive disorder. It was placed in the Trauma and Stressor-Related Disorders chapter. The >3 month criterion does not apply to PGD.
highYield: Prolonged Grief Disorder DSM-5-TR: >12 months in adults (>6 months in children); yearning/preoccupation with deceased + functional impairment

subject: Psychiatry
stem: DSM-5-TR made a significant change regarding schizophrenia diagnostic classification. Which major diagnostic system had already made a similar change before DSM-5-TR regarding schizophrenia subtypes?
options: ICD-10, which retained subtypes until the DSM-5-TR revision
ICD-11, which removed schizophrenia subtypes before DSM-5-TR; DSM-5 had already done so in 2013
DSM-IV-TR, which was the last to use subtypes before DSM-5
explanation: DSM-5 (2013) removed schizophrenia subtypes (paranoid, disorganized/hebephrenic, catatonic, undifferentiated, residual) in favor of dimensional specifiers. ICD-11 subsequently also removed these subtypes, aligning with DSM-5. ICD-10 retained the subtypes. DSM-5-TR (2022) maintained the DSM-5 position (no subtypes). Both DSM-5 and ICD-11 now use dimensional approaches to characterize schizophrenia presentations. This is a frequently tested classification distinction.
highYield: Schizophrenia subtypes removed: DSM-5 (2013) and ICD-11 both removed them; ICD-10 still had them

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 45-year-old woman with Treatment-Resistant Depression (TRD) has failed three adequate antidepressant trials. She is referred for a novel intranasal treatment administered in a certified healthcare setting under the REMS program due to risk of dissociation and sedation. What is this drug and its mechanism?
options: Brexanolone; GABA-A positive allosteric modulator; 60-hour IV infusion
Esketamine (Spravato); NMDA receptor antagonist; intranasal route
Dextromethorphan + Bupropion; NMDA antagonist + DNRI; oral tablet
explanation: Esketamine (Spravato) is the S-enantiomer of ketamine that works as an NMDA receptor antagonist/modulator. It is administered intranasally in certified healthcare settings under the REMS (Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy) program due to risks of dissociation, sedation, and misuse. It is FDA-approved for TRD and MDD with acute suicidal ideation. In 2025, monotherapy approval for TRD was granted. Brexanolone is IV (60-hour infusion) for postpartum depression. Zuranolone is an oral 14-day course neuroactive steroid. DXM+bupropion (Auvelity) is an oral combination for MDD.
highYield: Esketamine (Spravato): S-enantiomer of ketamine, NMDA antagonist, intranasal, REMS program, approved for TRD and acute suicidal ideation

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 28-year-old woman develops severe depression two weeks after delivery of her first child. She is admitted and started on the first FDA-approved drug specifically indicated for Postpartum Depression (PPD). What is this drug and its route of administration?
options: Brexanolone (Zulresso); continuous 60-hour IV infusion in hospital
Esketamine; intranasal in outpatient REMS setting
explanation: Brexanolone (Zulresso) is the first FDA-approved drug specifically for Postpartum Depression (PPD). It is a synthetic allopregnanolone (a neuroactive steroid) that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors. It is administered as a continuous 60-hour IV infusion in an inpatient hospital setting. Zuranolone is also a neuroactive steroid GABA-A modulator approved for both MDD and PPD but is an oral agent given over 14 days. Esketamine is for TRD/suicidal ideation, not specifically PPD. Sertraline is commonly used off-label for PPD but was not the first specifically approved drug.
highYield: Brexanolone = first drug specifically FDA-approved for PPD; allopregnanolone (neuroactive steroid), GABA-A modulator, 60-hour IV infusion

subject: Psychiatry
stem: Zuranolone is a recently approved oral antidepressant that works through a novel mechanism. Which of the following correctly describes its mechanism and dosing schedule?
options: Selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor; continuous daily dosing indefinitely
Positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors (neuroactive steroid); 14-day course taken once daily
NMDA receptor antagonist; intranasal twice weekly
explanation: Zuranolone is a neuroactive steroid (synthetic neurosteroid) that acts as a positive allosteric modulator of GABA-A receptors, modulating both synaptic and extrasynaptic GABA-A receptor subtypes. Uniquely, it is taken once daily for a 14-day treatment course (not continuous long-term dosing like SSRIs). It is FDA-approved for both MDD and Postpartum Depression. Its short course and rapid onset distinguish it from traditional antidepressants. The GABA-A modulation mechanism is shared with brexanolone (IV) but zuranolone is oral.
highYield: Zuranolone: oral neuroactive steroid, GABA-A positive allosteric modulator, 14-day course for MDD and PPD

subject: Psychiatry
stem: Dextromethorphan + Bupropion (Auvelity) is approved for MDD. It has a faster onset than traditional antidepressants. What are the combined mechanisms of action of its two components?
options: SSRI + SNRI combination targeting serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake
NMDA receptor antagonist (dextromethorphan) + dopamine-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (bupropion)
explanation: Auvelity (dextromethorphan + bupropion) combines two distinct mechanisms: dextromethorphan is an NMDA receptor antagonist (similar to ketamine/esketamine, explaining the rapid onset within ~1 week), and bupropion is a dopamine-norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor (DNRI) that also inhibits CYP2D6, slowing dextromethorphan metabolism to maintain therapeutic levels. This combination is approved for MDD with notably faster onset than SSRIs/SNRIs. The NMDA antagonism mechanism links it to the ketamine family of rapid-acting antidepressants.
highYield: Auvelity = dextromethorphan (NMDA antagonist) + bupropion (DNRI); faster onset ~1 week; bupropion also inhibits CYP2D6 to maintain DXM levels

subject: Psychiatry
stem: An elderly patient with Parkinson's disease develops visual hallucinations and delusions. The neurologist wants to treat the psychosis without worsening the motor symptoms of Parkinsonism. Which antipsychotic is specifically FDA-approved for Parkinson's Disease Psychosis and has no dopamine D2 blockade?
explanation: Pimavanserin (Nuplazid) is a selective 5-HT2A inverse agonist (and partial 5-HT2C inverse agonist) with no D2 receptor blockade. This unique mechanism means it treats psychosis without blocking dopamine receptors, so it does not worsen the motor symptoms of Parkinson's disease. It is the only drug specifically FDA-approved for Parkinson's Disease Psychosis (PDP). Clozapine and quetiapine are sometimes used off-label in PD psychosis (lowest D2 affinity among antipsychotics), but only pimavanserin is specifically approved. Olanzapine worsens motor symptoms significantly.
highYield: Pimavanserin = selective 5-HT2A inverse agonist, NO D2 blockade, specifically approved for Parkinson's Disease Psychosis

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 30-year-old patient with schizophrenia has prominent negative symptoms (flat affect, alogia, avolition) and cognitive impairment in addition to positive symptoms. Which newer antipsychotic has preferential D3 receptor affinity that targets negative symptoms and cognition, and is also approved for bipolar depression?
explanation: Cariprazine (Vraylar) is a D2/D3 partial agonist with preferential D3 affinity. D3 receptors are implicated in negative symptoms and cognitive dysfunction in schizophrenia. Cariprazine is approved for schizophrenia, bipolar mania, bipolar depression, and as an adjunct in MDD. Its D3 selectivity distinguishes it from aripiprazole (D2 partial agonist, minimal D3). Brexpiprazole is a D2 partial agonist + 5-HT1A partial agonist + 5-HT2A antagonist, approved for schizophrenia, adjunct MDD, and Alzheimer's agitation. Lumateperone is a serotonin-dopamine activity modulator.
highYield: Cariprazine: D2/D3 partial agonist with preferential D3 affinity; targets negative symptoms/cognition; approved for schizophrenia, bipolar depression

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A patient with schizophrenia is started on brexpiprazole (Rexulti). Compared to aripiprazole, which is its major advantage regarding extrapyramidal side effects?
options: Brexpiprazole has higher D2 affinity leading to fewer EPS
Brexpiprazole has lower akathisia risk than aripiprazole due to balanced 5-HT1A and 5-HT2A activity
Brexpiprazole does not block D2 at all, eliminating all EPS risk
Brexpiprazole causes more tardive dyskinesia but less akathisia
explanation: Brexpiprazole (Rexulti) is a D2 partial agonist + 5-HT1A partial agonist + 5-HT2A antagonist. Compared to aripiprazole (which is also a D2 partial agonist), brexpiprazole has lower akathisia risk due to its more balanced serotonergic and dopaminergic modulation. It is FDA-approved for schizophrenia, as an adjunct in MDD, and for agitation associated with Alzheimer's dementia. It does have D2 activity (so EPS risk is not eliminated). The 5-HT1A partial agonism and 5-HT2A antagonism contribute to its more favorable tolerability profile compared to aripiprazole.
highYield: Brexpiprazole vs aripiprazole: lower akathisia risk; D2 partial agonist + 5-HT1A partial agonist + 5-HT2A antagonist; approved for Alzheimer's agitation

subject: Psychiatry
stem: An image shows a dopaminergic pathway diagram where a drug acts on 5-HT2A receptors, D1 receptors, D2 receptors, and the serotonin transporter simultaneously. The drug is described as a 'serotonin-dopamine activity modulator' with low metabolic side effects, approved for both schizophrenia and bipolar depression. Which drug is this?
explanation: Lumateperone (Caplyta) is described as a serotonin-dopamine activity modulator that acts simultaneously on 5-HT2A receptors (antagonist), D1 receptors, D2 receptors, and the serotonin transporter. This unique multi-receptor profile results in low metabolic side effects (low weight gain, glucose, lipid changes) compared to other antipsychotics. It is approved for schizophrenia, bipolar depression, and (in 2025) as an adjunct in MDD. Cariprazine has preferential D3 affinity. Brexpiprazole has 5-HT1A partial agonism. Quetiapine has significant metabolic effects and is not described as a 'serotonin-dopamine activity modulator.'
highYield: Lumateperone = serotonin-dopamine activity modulator; acts on 5-HT2A, D1, D2, serotonin transporter; low metabolic side effects; approved for schizophrenia + bipolar depression

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 25-year-old man is brought in with mutism, waxy flexibility, posturing, and negativism for 5 days. He has a history of schizophrenia. IV lorazepam test is partially effective. What is the next best step in management?
options: Increase IV lorazepam dose and add oral antipsychotic
explanation: Catatonia is a neuropsychiatric syndrome characterized by mutism, waxy flexibility, posturing, and negativism. The first-line treatment is benzodiazepines (IV lorazepam is the preferred agent). If benzodiazepine response is partial or inadequate, ECT (Electroconvulsive Therapy) is indicated and is highly effective for catatonia. ECT is one of the primary indications for ECT, alongside treatment-resistant depression, severe suicidality, psychotic depression, and severe mania. Antipsychotics alone are not effective for catatonia and can worsen it (especially with NMS risk). Clozapine is for treatment-resistant schizophrenia, not catatonia per se.
highYield: Catatonia: IV lorazepam (BZD) first-line; if inadequate response → ECT is next step; antipsychotics alone contraindicated in catatonia

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A 35-year-old woman with Bipolar I disorder is started on lithium for long-term prophylaxis. Which of the following is the therapeutic serum level range for lithium in maintenance treatment?
explanation: The therapeutic serum level for lithium in maintenance (prophylaxis) of bipolar disorder is 0.6-1.2 mEq/L. Levels above 1.5 mEq/L are associated with toxicity (tremor, ataxia, diarrhea, confusion), and levels above 2.0 mEq/L can be life-threatening. Lithium is the drug of choice for bipolar disorder prophylaxis and is the most evidence-based agent for reducing suicidality. It has a narrow therapeutic index. Monitoring thyroid function (hypothyroidism risk) and renal function is mandatory during lithium therapy. Lithium is a teratogen associated with Ebstein's anomaly.
highYield: Lithium therapeutic range: 0.6-1.2 mEq/L; drug of choice for bipolar prophylaxis and suicide prevention; monitor thyroid and renal function

subject: Psychiatry
stem: A patient on lithium for bipolar disorder presents with coarse tremor, ataxia, polyuria, polydipsia, and confusion. Serum lithium level is 1.8 mEq/L. Which of the following adverse effects of lithium accounts for the polyuria and polydipsia in this patient?
options: Psychogenic polydipsia from the underlying bipolar disorder
explanation: Lithium causes nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (NDI) by inhibiting the action of ADH (antidiuretic hormone) on the collecting tubule, leading to reduced aquaporin-2 expression and inability to concentrate urine. This results in polyuria and compensatory polydipsia. It is distinct from SIADH, which would cause hyponatremia and reduced urine output. Lithium also causes hypothyroidism but this does not directly cause polyuria/polydipsia. This is a chronic effect of lithium even at therapeutic levels, worsening with toxicity. Amiloride can be used to treat lithium-induced NDI.
highYield: Lithium causes nephrogenic DI (inhibits ADH action on collecting duct → polyuria/polydipsia); also causes hypothyroidism and Ebstein anomaly (teratogen)

subject: Surgery
stem: A 55-year-old man is scheduled for elective laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy. According to the Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) protocol, what is the recommended preoperative carbohydrate loading regimen?
options: 100 g carbohydrate drink the night before and 50 g two hours before surgery
Clear liquids only until midnight; solid food prohibited after dinner
50 g carbohydrate drink the night before and 100 g two hours before surgery
No oral intake for 12 hours before surgery to reduce aspiration risk
explanation: ERAS protocol mandates preoperative carbohydrate loading: 100 g drink the evening before surgery and 50 g drink 2 hours before surgery. Clear liquids are allowed up to 2 hours before the procedure. This regimen reduces insulin resistance, attenuates the catabolic stress response, and improves postoperative recovery. Traditional 'nil by mouth from midnight' is outdated and harmful. Options B and D represent old practice. Option C reverses the doses incorrectly.
highYield: ERAS carbohydrate loading: 100 g night before + 50 g two hours pre-op; clear liquids allowed up to 2 hours pre-surgery.

subject: Surgery
stem: Which analgesic modality is the preferred method in ERAS protocol for open colorectal surgery to reduce opioid use, attenuate the stress response, and promote gut recovery?
explanation: Mid-thoracic epidural analgesia is the preferred analgesic modality in ERAS for open colorectal surgery. It reduces opioid consumption, attenuates the neurohumoral stress response, promotes earlier return of gut function, and decreases pulmonary complications. Opioids (options A and C) impair gut motility and delay recovery. Oral tramadol (option D) is part of multimodal analgesia but not the preferred primary modality for open surgery.
highYield: ERAS preferred analgesia for open colorectal = mid-thoracic epidural; reduces opioids, promotes gut recovery.

subject: Surgery
stem: A patient undergoes open hemicolectomy under ERAS protocol. Regarding routine nasogastric tube (NGT) use in ERAS, which statement is correct?
options: NGT should be routinely placed and kept for 48 hours postoperatively
NGT should be used selectively only if the patient develops ileus
Routine postoperative NGT is NOT recommended in ERAS protocol
NGT is mandatory in all open colorectal surgeries for bowel decompression
explanation: ERAS protocol specifically recommends AGAINST routine postoperative nasogastric tube use. Routine NGT increases pulmonary complications (aspiration, atelectasis) and delays the return of oral feeding. NGT may be used selectively for specific indications (e.g., intraoperative complications, severe ileus), but routine placement is not endorsed. This is a key ERAS principle that frequently appears in exams.
highYield: ERAS: NO routine postoperative NGT; routine use increases pulmonary complications and delays oral feeding.

subject: Surgery
stem: According to the ERAS protocol for colorectal surgery, which statement best describes the role of mechanical bowel preparation (MBP)?
options: MBP is mandatory before all colorectal surgeries to reduce anastomotic leak risk
MBP should be combined with oral antibiotics before elective left-sided colectomy
MBP is NOT recommended routinely in ERAS; it may cause dehydration and electrolyte imbalance
MBP is required only for right-sided colonic resections
explanation: ERAS protocol does not recommend routine mechanical bowel preparation before colorectal surgery. MBP has been shown to cause dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, and patient discomfort without a proven reduction in anastomotic leak or infectious complications. While some guidelines allow oral antibiotic bowel preparation combined with MBP for select cases (left-sided colectomy), the default ERAS position is that routine MBP is NOT mandatory. Option B combines two interventions in a way that may be acceptable in some protocols but is not the ERAS default position.
highYield: ERAS: Mechanical bowel preparation is NOT routinely recommended; causes dehydration and electrolyte imbalance.

subject: Surgery
stem: An ERAS protocol for elective colonic resection is being implemented. What is the recommended timeline for early patient mobilization postoperatively?
options: On postoperative day 3 after drain removal
Only after the patient passes flatus
explanation: ERAS mandates early mobilization within 24 hours postoperatively. Early ambulation reduces DVT, hospital-acquired pneumonia, postoperative ileus, and muscle wasting. Waiting for flatus (option D) is outdated practice. Mobilizing within 6 hours (option A) may be feasible in some cases but the ERAS recommendation is 'within 24 hours' as the standard. Option C represents delayed mobilization inconsistent with ERAS principles.
highYield: ERAS early mobilization: within 24 hours postoperatively; reduces DVT, pneumonia, ileus, and muscle wasting.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 68-year-old man with severe aortic stenosis has an STS score of 2.5% (low surgical risk). Based on current evidence from PARTNER 2 and Evolut Low Risk trials, which statement about TAVR is correct?
options: TAVR is contraindicated in low-risk surgical patients; SAVR is the only option
TAVR is now indicated for intermediate-risk and low-risk surgical patients
TAVR is reserved only for patients with STS score greater than 8% (high risk)
TAVR should be offered only when transfemoral access is unavailable
explanation: Based on PARTNER 2 (intermediate risk) and Evolut Low Risk trials, TAVR has expanded its indication to include intermediate-risk AND low-risk surgical patients, not just high-risk patients. The initial TAVR indication was limited to high-risk or inoperable patients, but evidence now supports its use across all risk categories. Options A and C are outdated. Option D is incorrect as transfemoral access is the preferred route, not a contraindication.
highYield: TAVR is now indicated for low-risk patients (not just high-risk); supported by PARTNER 2 and Evolut Low Risk trials.

subject: Surgery
stem: Comparing TAVR versus SAVR in a low-risk patient with severe aortic stenosis, which complication profile is correct?
options: TAVR: higher atrial fibrillation + higher AKI; SAVR: higher pacemaker implantation + paravalvular leak
TAVR: higher pacemaker implantation + higher paravalvular leak; SAVR: higher AKI + higher atrial fibrillation
TAVR: higher stroke + higher mortality; SAVR: higher pacemaker + higher paravalvular leak
TAVR and SAVR have identical complication rates in low-risk patients
explanation: TAVR has higher rates of permanent pacemaker implantation (due to proximity of aortic annulus to conduction system) and paravalvular leak (incomplete sealing around the prosthesis). SAVR has higher rates of acute kidney injury (cardiopulmonary bypass effect), atrial fibrillation (direct cardiac manipulation), and blood transfusion. This distinction is classic for exams. Stroke rates are comparable or even lower with TAVR in experienced centers.
highYield: TAVR: more pacemakers + paravalvular leak. SAVR: more AKI + atrial fibrillation + blood transfusion.

subject: Surgery
stem: In a patient requiring TAVR, which access route is preferred, and what alternatives are used when this route is unsuitable?
options: Transapical is preferred; alternatives are transfemoral and subclavian
Transfemoral is preferred; alternatives include transapical, transaortic, and subclavian
Transaortic is preferred; transfemoral used only if transaortic fails
Subclavian is preferred in all cases to reduce vascular complications
explanation: Transfemoral access is the preferred and most commonly used route for TAVR due to the least invasiveness, feasibility under local anesthesia, and good outcomes data. When femoral access is unsuitable (severe peripheral vascular disease, small caliber vessels, excessive calcification), alternative routes include transapical (left ventricular apex via mini-thoracotomy), transaortic (ascending aorta), and subclavian/axillary approaches.
highYield: TAVR preferred route: transfemoral. Alternatives: transapical, transaortic, subclavian when femoral unsuitable.

subject: Surgery
stem: A surgical trainee is learning about the da Vinci robotic system. Which statement correctly describes its components and instrument capabilities?
options: Consists of a surgeon console, patient-side cart with 4 arms, vision cart; instruments have 4 degrees of freedom
Consists of a surgeon console, patient-side cart with 4 arms, vision cart with 3D HD camera; instruments have 7 degrees of freedom
The surgeon stands at the patient-side cart and controls a 2D camera with laparoscopic instruments
Robotic instruments provide superior haptic feedback compared to conventional laparoscopy
explanation: The da Vinci robotic system consists of three components: (1) surgeon console where the surgeon sits, (2) patient-side cart with 4 robotic arms, and (3) vision cart with a 3D HD camera system. The wristed instruments provide 7 degrees of freedom, far exceeding the 4 degrees available with standard laparoscopic instruments. A key DISADVANTAGE is the LOSS of haptic feedback (option D is incorrect). The surgeon operates remotely from the console, not at the patient-side cart.
highYield: da Vinci: surgeon console + 4-arm patient cart + 3D HD vision cart; wristed instruments = 7 degrees of freedom.

subject: Surgery
stem: Which of the following correctly lists the advantages AND disadvantages of robotic surgery compared to conventional laparoscopy?
options: Advantages: haptic feedback, low cost; Disadvantages: limited visualization, fewer degrees of freedom
Advantages: tremor filtration, motion scaling, magnified 3D field, superior ergonomics; Disadvantages: loss of haptic feedback, high cost
Advantages: haptic feedback, lower operating time; Disadvantages: 3D visualization, motion scaling
Advantages: cost-effectiveness, ease of training; Disadvantages: tremor amplification, loss of 3D vision
explanation: Robotic surgery advantages include: tremor filtration, motion scaling (large movements converted to precise micro-movements), magnified 3D HD visualization, and superior ergonomics for the surgeon during prolonged procedures. The key DISADVANTAGES are loss of haptic (tactile) feedback - the surgeon cannot feel tissue resistance - and high capital and maintenance costs. Options A, C, and D incorrectly assign advantages and disadvantages.
highYield: Robotic advantages: tremor filter, motion scaling, 3D magnified field, ergonomics. Disadvantages: no haptic feedback, high cost.

subject: Surgery
stem: A 45-year-old woman with early-stage breast cancer (T1N0) is undergoing sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB). What is the standard dual-mapping technique used for this procedure?
options: Methylene blue dye alone detected visually
Blue dye (isosulfan blue or patent blue V) plus Tc-99m radiolabeled colloid detected by gamma probe
Indocyanine green (ICG) with CT fluoroscopy intraoperatively
explanation: The standard dual-mapping technique for SLNB in breast cancer uses: (1) blue dye - isosulfan blue or patent blue V - detected visually as stained lymphatic channels and nodes, combined with (2) Tc-99m radiolabeled colloid (usually sulfur colloid) detected by a hand-held gamma probe intraoperatively. This dual technique increases detection rates compared to either alone. ICG with near-infrared fluorescence (option C) is a newer validated alternative but not the traditional standard. Tc-99m alone (option D) misses nodes detectable only by blue dye.
highYield: SLNB dual mapping: blue dye (isosulfan blue/patent blue V) + Tc-99m radiolabeled colloid detected by gamma probe.

subject: Surgery
topic: Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy - ICG and Histopathology
stem: Regarding sentinel lymph node biopsy in breast cancer: which newer imaging agent avoids radiation, and what histopathological technique detects micrometastases missed on routine H&E staining?
options: Methylene blue avoids radiation; PAS staining detects micrometastases
Indocyanine green (ICG) with near-infrared camera avoids radiation; serial sectioning plus IHC for cytokeratin detects micrometastases
Tc-99m with shielded injection avoids radiation; Congo red staining detects micrometastases
Patent blue V avoids radiation; Ziehl-Neelsen staining detects micrometastases
explanation: Indocyanine green (ICG) with near-infrared fluorescence imaging is a validated alternative for SLNB that avoids radiation exposure (unlike Tc-99m). It is detected intraoperatively using a near-infrared camera. For histopathological assessment, serial sectioning of the sentinel node combined with immunohistochemistry (IHC) using cytokeratin markers identifies micrometastases that are missed on routine H&E staining. Up to 4 sentinel nodes are typically removed. Other staining methods listed are not used for this purpose.
highYield: SLNB: ICG + near-infrared camera avoids radiation. Micrometastases detected by serial sectioning + IHC for cytokeratin.

subject: Surgery
topic: Laparoscopic Cholecystectomy - Critical View of Safety
stem: During laparoscopic cholecystectomy, what constitutes the Critical View of Safety (CVS) that must be achieved before clipping and dividing any structures?
options: Visualization of the common bile duct with a cholangiogram confirming anatomy
Two structures (cystic duct and cystic artery) seen entering the gallbladder with the lower third of the gallbladder dissected free from the liver bed
Complete dissection of Calot's triangle with visualization of the cystic duct, cystic artery, and common hepatic duct
Intraoperative ultrasound confirming absence of CBD stones before clipping the cystic duct
explanation: The Critical View of Safety (CVS) requires: (1) only TWO structures - the cystic duct and cystic artery - are seen entering the gallbladder, and (2) the lower third of the gallbladder is dissected free from the liver bed (hepatocystic triangle cleared). CVS does NOT require visualization of the CBD (option A) - in fact, positively identifying the CBD before clip application risks injury. CVS prevents misidentification of the CBD as the cystic duct, which is the most common cause of bile duct injury.
highYield: Critical View of Safety (CVS): 2 structures only (cystic duct + cystic artery) into GB + lower third GB free from liver.

subject: Surgery
stem: A patient undergoing laparoscopic cholecystectomy develops rising end-tidal CO2 and increasing heart rate during pneumoperitoneum. Which physiological effect of CO2 pneumoperitoneum is responsible?
options: Decreased intra-abdominal pressure causing improved venous return
Increased intra-abdominal pressure causing reduced venous return plus CO2 absorption causing hypercapnia
Nitrogen absorption from N2O pneumoperitoneum causing hypercarbia
Oxygen embolism from CO2 dissociation in the bloodstream
explanation: CO2 pneumoperitoneum causes two main physiological effects: (1) increased intra-abdominal pressure which reduces venous return (preload reduction) and can cause cardiovascular compromise, and (2) CO2 absorption across the peritoneum causing hypercapnia (elevated pCO2 and end-tidal CO2). This stimulates the sympathetic system causing tachycardia. CO2 embolism (not oxygen embolism - option D) is a rare but serious complication. N2O (option C) is rarely used for pneumoperitoneum today.
highYield: CO2 pneumoperitoneum: increased intra-abdominal pressure (reduced venous return) + CO2 absorption (hypercapnia, risk of CO2 embolism).

subject: Orthopedics
stem: A 70-year-old postmenopausal woman with severe osteoporosis has been on denosumab 60 mg SC every 6 months for 3 years. Her physician wants to discontinue the drug. What is the most important precaution before stopping denosumab?
options: Gradually taper the denosumab dose over 6 months before stopping
Stop denosumab abruptly; no precaution is required as the effect wears off slowly
Transition to a bisphosphonate before stopping denosumab to prevent rebound vertebral fractures
Switch to teriparatide immediately before stopping denosumab
explanation: Denosumab discontinuation causes a rapid rebound increase in bone resorption (rebound vertebral fractures) due to reversal of RANK-L inhibition. This rebound is unique to denosumab and does NOT occur with bisphosphonates. Therefore, transitioning to a bisphosphonate (e.g., alendronate or zoledronic acid) before stopping denosumab is mandatory to maintain bone protection. Abrupt stopping (option B) is dangerous. Teriparatide (option D) is an anabolic agent and does not prevent the resorption rebound. Tapering (option A) is not feasible as denosumab is a biologic given as fixed-dose injections.
highYield: Denosumab discontinuation = rebound vertebral fractures. MUST transition to bisphosphonate before stopping. Classic exam trap.

subject: Orthopedics
stem: A 65-year-old woman with glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis has failed bisphosphonate therapy. Teriparatide is considered. Which statement about teriparatide is correct?
options: Teriparatide inhibits osteoclasts and is an antiresorptive agent; maximum duration is 36 months
Teriparatide (PTH 1-34) stimulates osteoblasts (anabolic); maximum approved duration is 24 months
Teriparatide should be given after bisphosphonate therapy for maximum efficacy
Teriparatide is a monoclonal antibody targeting sclerostin with dual anabolic-antiresorptive action
explanation: Teriparatide is recombinant PTH (1-34) and is an ANABOLIC bone agent - it stimulates osteoblasts to form new bone, unlike antiresorptive agents (bisphosphonates, denosumab) which inhibit osteoclasts. Maximum approved duration is 24 months lifetime. It is used in severe osteoporosis, glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis, and when antiresorptives fail. Importantly, giving bisphosphonate BEFORE teriparatide blunts the anabolic response - so anabolic therapy should ideally come FIRST (option C is incorrect). Option D describes romosozumab (anti-sclerostin), not teriparatide.
highYield: Teriparatide = PTH 1-34, anabolic (stimulates osteoblasts), max 24 months. Give anabolics BEFORE bisphosphonates for best effect.

subject: Orthopedics
stem: Regarding zoledronic acid for osteoporosis, which of the following statements is correct?
options: Zoledronic acid is given as weekly oral tablets; inhibits COX-2 enzyme pathway
Zoledronic acid is an annual IV infusion (5 mg); inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase; side effects include acute phase reaction and osteonecrosis of the jaw
Zoledronic acid is a monthly SC injection; acts by inhibiting RANK-L binding to RANK receptor
Zoledronic acid can be safely used in patients with eGFR less than 30 mL/min without dose adjustment
explanation: Zoledronic acid is the most potent bisphosphonate, administered as an annual IV infusion (5 mg over 15 minutes). Its mechanism is inhibition of farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in the mevalonate pathway, causing osteoclast apoptosis. Key side effects: acute phase reaction (flu-like symptoms within 24-72 hours of first dose), osteonecrosis of the jaw (ONJ), and atypical subtrochanteric fractures with long-term use. It is contraindicated in severe renal impairment (eGFR <35 mL/min). RANK-L inhibition (option C) is the mechanism of denosumab, not bisphosphonates.
highYield: Zoledronic acid: annual IV 5 mg; inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase; SE: acute phase reaction, ONJ, atypical fractures.

subject: Orthopedics
stem: Which anti-osteoporosis drug has a dual mechanism of both increasing bone formation AND decreasing bone resorption simultaneously, and has a specific cardiovascular precaution?
options: Denosumab: dual RANK-L and sclerostin blockade; precaution in renal failure
Romosozumab: anti-sclerostin antibody with dual anabolic-antiresorptive action; avoid in patients with recent MI or stroke
Teriparatide: stimulates both osteoblasts and osteoclasts; precaution in Paget's disease
Alendronate: dual inhibition of osteoclast and osteoblast; precaution in esophageal stricture
explanation: Romosozumab is a monoclonal antibody against sclerostin (a Wnt pathway inhibitor). By blocking sclerostin, it simultaneously increases bone formation (anabolic) AND decreases bone resorption (antiresorptive) - a unique dual mechanism. It is given as monthly subcutaneous injections for 12 months, followed by transition to an antiresorptive. The key cardiovascular caution: it is avoided in patients with recent MI or stroke due to increased cardiovascular risk observed in trials. Denosumab (option A) targets RANK-L only, not sclerostin. Teriparatide (option C) is purely anabolic.
highYield: Romosozumab: anti-sclerostin, dual anabolic + antiresorptive; 12 monthly SC doses; avoid in recent MI/stroke.

subject: Orthopedics
stem: An 11-year-old boy has fever, elevated ESR, and pain in the diaphysis of the right femur. X-ray shows onion peel periosteal layering. Biopsy reveals small round blue cells. What is the chromosomal translocation associated with this tumor and its treatment?
options: t(9;22) Philadelphia chromosome; treated with imatinib
t(11;22) translocation; treated with chemotherapy plus radiation (radiosensitive tumor)
t(8;14) Burkitt translocation; treated with chemotherapy alone
t(11;22) translocation; treated with surgery alone as radiation causes secondary malignancy
explanation: This is Ewing's sarcoma: second most common primary bone malignancy in children, diaphysis of long bones, 'onion peel' periosteal layering on X-ray, small round blue cells on biopsy. The characteristic translocation is t(11;22) resulting in EWS-FLI1 fusion gene. Treatment: chemotherapy (vincristine, actinomycin, cyclophosphamide, ifosfamide, etoposide) PLUS radiation, because Ewing's is radiosensitive (unlike osteosarcoma which is radioresistant). Surgery may also be used for local control. t(9;22) is Philadelphia chromosome (CML). t(8;14) is Burkitt lymphoma.
highYield: Ewing's sarcoma: t(11;22), diaphysis, onion peel periosteum. Radiosensitive - treat with chemo + radiation.

subject: Orthopedics
stem: A 28-year-old woman presents with knee pain. X-ray shows a soap-bubble lytic lesion at the distal femur epiphysis with no sclerotic rim. Skeletal survey confirms skeletal maturity. Which drug is now approved for unresectable giant cell tumor (GCT) and what is its mechanism?
options: Imatinib; inhibits BCR-ABL tyrosine kinase in GCT stromal cells
Denosumab; inhibits RANK-L expressed by GCT stromal cells
Zoledronic acid; inhibits farnesyl pyrophosphate synthase in osteoclast-like GCT cells
Teriparatide; stimulates osteoblastic response to fill the lytic lesion
explanation: Giant cell tumor (GCT) is locally aggressive, occurring at the epiphysis of long bones after skeletal maturity, with soap-bubble lytic appearance and no sclerotic rim on X-ray. GCT stromal cells highly express RANK-L, which drives the osteoclast-like giant cell proliferation. Denosumab, a monoclonal antibody inhibiting RANK-L, is approved for unresectable or metastatic GCT. This creates an overlap between its use in osteoporosis and bone tumor management - a common exam trick. Standard treatment for resectable GCT is curettage with bone cement or grafting.
highYield: GCT: soap-bubble lytic epiphyseal lesion. Denosumab (anti-RANK-L) approved for unresectable GCT. Standard: curettage + bone cement.

subject: Orthopedics
stem: A 22-year-old man sustains a closed tibial shaft fracture. Six hours later he complains of severe pain disproportionate to injury and paresthesias in the foot, not relieved by opioids. What is the indication threshold for fasciotomy and the critical time window?
options: Compartment pressure >50 mmHg; fasciotomy within 12 hours
Compartment pressure >30 mmHg or within 30 mmHg of diastolic BP; fasciotomy within 6 hours
Absent distal pulse (compartment pressure >60 mmHg); fasciotomy within 8 hours
Pain + paresthesia alone without pressure measurement; fasciotomy within 24 hours
explanation: Compartment syndrome: fasciotomy is indicated when compartment pressure exceeds 30 mmHg or is within 30 mmHg of the patient's diastolic blood pressure (delta pressure <30 mmHg). The critical time window is within 6 hours to prevent irreversible muscle necrosis and Volkmann's ischemic contracture. The earliest signs are pain out of proportion and paresthesia (5 Ps: pain, pressure, paresthesia, paralysis, pallor/pulselessness - the last two are LATE signs). Option A uses incorrect threshold. Option C relying on absent pulse is too late. Option D lacks the pressure criterion.
highYield: Compartment syndrome: fasciotomy when pressure >30 mmHg or within 30 mmHg of diastolic. Within 6 hours to prevent Volkmann's contracture.

subject: Orthopedics
stem: A 40-year-old man on long-term prednisolone for rheumatoid arthritis develops progressive hip pain. MRI shows abnormal signal in the femoral head with normal X-ray. What is the most common cause of AVN of femoral head in India, the gold standard investigation, and the treatment for this early stage?
options: Alcohol is most common; CT scan is gold standard; total hip replacement is treatment
Corticosteroids are most common in India; MRI is gold standard; core decompression for early stage
Trauma is most common; bone scan is gold standard; conservative management always sufficient
Sickle cell disease is most common; X-ray is gold standard; bisphosphonate therapy
explanation: In India, corticosteroids (iatrogenic) are the most common cause of AVN of the femoral head. Other causes include alcohol, sickle cell disease, and trauma (transcervical fractures). MRI is the gold standard for early diagnosis - it detects marrow edema and early ischemia before X-ray changes appear (Ficat Stage 1). For early stages (I-II), the treatment is core decompression (drilling to reduce intraosseous pressure and stimulate revascularization). Advanced stages (III-IV with collapse and OA) require total hip arthroplasty. Bone scan is sensitive but not the gold standard.
highYield: AVN femoral head: corticosteroids = most common in India. MRI = gold standard. Early stage = core decompression.

subject: Orthopedics
stem: A 25-year-old man falls on an outstretched hand. X-ray of the wrist is normal. He has tenderness in the anatomical snuffbox. Which statement is correct regarding further management?
options: Normal X-ray rules out scaphoid fracture; discharge with bandage
Normal X-ray does not exclude scaphoid fracture; MRI or CT required if clinical suspicion is high
Perform a bone scan immediately as it is the gold standard for scaphoid fracture
Repeat X-ray in 6 weeks; if still normal, fracture is definitively ruled out
explanation: Scaphoid fracture is the most common carpal bone fracture following fall on an outstretched hand (FOOSH). Anatomical snuffbox tenderness is the key clinical sign. Crucially, X-rays are normal in up to 20% of scaphoid fractures initially. A normal X-ray does NOT exclude fracture. MRI (most sensitive and specific) or CT scan should be performed when clinical suspicion is high despite a normal X-ray. Proximal pole fractures carry the highest risk of AVN due to retrograde blood supply. Delayed diagnosis and untreated fractures lead to non-union and AVN - serious consequences in young patients.
highYield: Scaphoid fracture: normal X-ray does NOT exclude; MRI or CT needed if snuffbox tenderness present. Proximal pole = highest AVN risk.

subject: Orthopedics
stem: A 45-year-old man presents with sudden-onset bilateral leg weakness, saddle anesthesia, and urinary retention following lifting a heavy weight. MRI shows a massive central disc prolapse at L4-L5. What is this diagnosis and the required management?
options: Lumbar radiculopathy; manage with physiotherapy and NSAIDs
Cauda equina syndrome; emergency surgical decompression within 24-48 hours
Cauda equina syndrome; high-dose IV corticosteroids followed by delayed surgery
explanation: Cauda equina syndrome (CES) is a surgical emergency caused by massive central disc prolapse (most commonly L4-L5 or L5-S1). Classic features are the tetrad: bilateral leg weakness, saddle anesthesia (perianal area numbness), urinary retention (overflow incontinence), and loss of anal sphincter tone. Treatment: emergency surgical decompression (discectomy/laminectomy) within 24-48 hours. Delay beyond this window causes permanent sphincter dysfunction. Steroids (option D) have no proven role in CES. This is NOT lumbar radiculopathy (which is unilateral dermatomal). Cervical myelopathy (option C) causes UMN signs.
highYield: Cauda equina syndrome: saddle anesthesia + urinary retention + bilateral leg weakness = surgical emergency, decompress within 24-48 hours.

subject: Orthopedics
topic: Periprosthetic Joint Infection - Diagnosis and Management
stem: A 68-year-old man with a total knee replacement 4 years ago presents with chronic knee pain, elevated CRP/ESR, and synovial fluid WBC of 4500/mm3. Culture grows Staphylococcus epidermidis. What is the gold standard treatment for this chronic periprosthetic joint infection (PJI)?
options: Two-stage revision: Stage 1 = implant removal + antibiotic-loaded cement spacer; Stage 2 = reimplantation after 6-8 weeks of IV antibiotics
Single-stage revision with immediate reimplantation and intravenous antibiotics
Arthroscopic lavage and debridement without implant removal
explanation: Two-stage revision is the gold standard for chronic PJI. Stage 1: remove the infected implant, thorough surgical debridement, and insertion of an antibiotic-loaded cement spacer (static or articulating). Stage 2: after 6-8 weeks of IV antibiotics (confirmed by negative cultures and normalized inflammatory markers), reimplant a new prosthesis. The most common organism in chronic PJI is Staphylococcus epidermidis (coagulase-negative staph, biofilm-forming). Acute PJI (within 3 months) can be managed by DAIR (Debridement, Antibiotics, Implant Retention). Single-stage revision (option C) is emerging for acute infections with sensitive organisms.
highYield: Chronic PJI gold standard: two-stage revision (removal + antibiotic spacer → 6-8 weeks IV antibiotics → reimplantation). Staph epidermidis = most common chronic PJI organism.

subject: Orthopedics
stem: An X-ray image shows widened, cupped, and frayed metaphyses in a 3-year-old child with bow legs and a rachitic rosary on examination. Which biochemical profile best confirms this diagnosis?
options: High calcium, high phosphate, low ALP, normal 25-OH Vitamin D
Low calcium, low phosphate, elevated ALP, elevated PTH, low 25-OH Vitamin D
Low calcium, high phosphate, elevated ALP, low PTH
Normal calcium, normal phosphate, elevated ALP, normal 25-OH Vitamin D
explanation: Rickets (in children) results from defective bone mineralization at the growth plate. X-ray shows widened, cupped, frayed metaphyses. Clinical features include rachitic rosary (costochondral junction enlargement), Harrison's sulcus, bow legs (genu varum). Biochemical profile: low serum calcium, low phosphate (impaired renal tubular reabsorption), elevated ALP (increased osteoblastic activity), secondary hyperparathyroidism (elevated PTH trying to correct hypocalcemia), and low 25-OH Vitamin D (in nutritional rickets). Treatment is Vitamin D plus calcium supplementation.
highYield: Rickets labs: low Ca, low PO4, high ALP, high PTH, low 25-OH Vit D. X-ray: widened cupped frayed metaphyses, rachitic rosary.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A patient received cisatracurium for muscle relaxation during surgery. At the end of the procedure, the anesthesiologist wants to reverse the neuromuscular blockade. Which statement regarding reversal agents is correct?
options: Sugammadex can be used to reverse cisatracurium by encapsulating the drug
Sugammadex cannot reverse cisatracurium; neostigmine with glycopyrrolate should be used
Sugammadex reverses all neuromuscular blocking agents including succinylcholine
Neostigmine alone (without anticholinergic) is sufficient for safe reversal of cisatracurium
explanation: Sugammadex reverses ONLY aminosteroid NMBAs: rocuronium (highest affinity) and vecuronium. It does NOT reverse benzylisoquinolinium NMBAs such as cisatracurium, atracurium, or mivacurium, and does NOT reverse the depolarizing agent succinylcholine. Cisatracurium undergoes spontaneous Hofmann elimination and does not require reversal in most cases, but if reversal is needed, neostigmine (cholinesterase inhibitor) with glycopyrrolate (anticholinergic to prevent muscarinic side effects) is used. Neostigmine alone (option D) without anticholinergic causes dangerous bradycardia and bronchospasm.
highYield: Sugammadex reverses ONLY aminosteroids (rocuronium > vecuronium). Does NOT reverse benzylisoquinoliniums (cisatracurium, atracurium) or succinylcholine.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: During rapid sequence intubation, a patient received rocuronium 1.2 mg/kg. Immediately after intubation the surgeon cancels the operation. What dose of sugammadex is required for immediate full reversal?
options: Sugammadex cannot be used immediately; must wait for T2 return
explanation: Sugammadex dosing is based on depth of blockade: 2 mg/kg for moderate block (T2 reappearance on TOF), 4 mg/kg for deep block (post-tetanic count 1-2), and 16 mg/kg for immediate reversal after a full intubating dose of rocuronium with no waiting required. This 16 mg/kg 'rescue dose' is a unique advantage of sugammadex, allowing rocuronium to be used for RSI as an alternative to succinylcholine, especially when succinylcholine is contraindicated (burns, denervation injury, etc.). Option D is incorrect - sugammadex can reverse rocuronium at any depth immediately.
highYield: Sugammadex doses: 2 mg/kg (moderate block), 4 mg/kg (deep block), 16 mg/kg (immediate reversal after full RSI dose of rocuronium).

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: Remimazolam is a new ultra-short-acting sedative agent. Which statement correctly describes its mechanism of metabolism and receptor action?
options: Metabolized by CYP3A4 in the liver to an active metabolite; acts on NMDA receptors
Metabolized by tissue carboxylesterase-1 (CES1) via ester hydrolysis to an inactive metabolite (CNS 7054); acts at GABA-A benzodiazepine allosteric site
Metabolized by plasma cholinesterase; acts on GABA-A receptor at the barbiturate binding site
Metabolized by Hofmann elimination at physiological pH; acts on opioid mu receptors
explanation: Remimazolam's key pharmacological innovation is organ-independent metabolism: it contains a methyl propionate ester group cleaved by tissue carboxylesterase-1 (CES1) to produce CNS 7054, which is an inactive metabolite. This means metabolism is independent of hepatic or renal function. Its mechanism of action is identical to other benzodiazepines: enhancement of GABA-A receptor activity at the benzodiazepine allosteric site (alpha-gamma subunit interface), increasing chloride influx. Hofmann elimination (option D) is the mechanism of atracurium/cisatracurium. Plasma cholinesterase (option C) metabolizes succinylcholine and mivacurium.
highYield: Remimazolam: metabolized by tissue CES1 (organ-independent) to inactive CNS 7054; acts at GABA-A benzodiazepine site.

subject: Anaesthesia
topic: Remimazolam - Reversal and Comparison with Propofol
stem: An anesthesiologist is choosing between propofol and remimazolam for procedural sedation. Which statement correctly distinguishes these two agents regarding reversibility?
options: Both propofol and remimazolam can be reversed with flumazenil
Propofol has no reversal agent; remimazolam is reversible with flumazenil
Remimazolam has no reversal agent; propofol is reversed with sugammadex
Both agents are irreversible; naloxone is used for both
explanation: This is a key exam distinction: propofol has NO reversal agent - once administered, its effects must wear off spontaneously (though propofol has a short context-sensitive half-life). Remimazolam is a benzodiazepine and is reversible with flumazenil (competitive antagonist at the benzodiazepine site on GABA-A receptor). However, routine reversal of remimazolam with flumazenil is not recommended due to the risk of rebound sedation if the flumazenil wears off first. Sugammadex (option C) reverses neuromuscular blockers, not sedatives. Naloxone (option D) reverses opioids.
highYield: Key distinction: propofol = no reversal agent. Remimazolam = reversible with flumazenil. Routine flumazenil reversal not recommended (rebound sedation risk).

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: During nerve stimulator monitoring, a patient who received succinylcholine shows no fade on train-of-four (TOF) testing and no post-tetanic facilitation. What block is this, and how does it differ from a non-depolarizing block?
options: Phase II (non-depolarizing) block; characterized by no fade and no post-tetanic facilitation
Phase I (depolarizing) block; characterized by no fade on TOF and no post-tetanic facilitation; opposite of non-depolarizing block
Phase II (depolarizing) block; characterized by fade on TOF and post-tetanic facilitation
Non-depolarizing block; succinylcholine causes fade on TOF
explanation: Succinylcholine causes Phase I depolarizing block: characterized by NO fade on TOF (all four twitches equally reduced) and NO post-tetanic facilitation. This is because depolarizing blockade involves persistent receptor depolarization without receptor antagonism. Non-depolarizing (Phase II) block shows FADE on TOF (progressive reduction T1>T2>T3>T4) AND post-tetanic facilitation (brief augmentation after tetanic stimulation). A TOF ratio >0.9 is required to confirm complete recovery from any block before extubation.
highYield: Depolarizing (succinylcholine): no TOF fade, no post-tetanic facilitation. Non-depolarizing: fade on TOF + post-tetanic facilitation.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: During anesthesia, a patient develops acute hypoxemia. POCUS of the chest shows multiple hyperechoic vertical lines arising from the pleural line, extending to the bottom of the screen without fading. What are these artifacts called and what do they indicate?
options: A-lines; indicate normal aerated lung with air reverberation artifact
B-lines (comet-tail artifacts); indicate interstitial syndrome (pulmonary edema or ARDS)
Z-lines; indicate pleural effusion on the dependent side
E-lines; indicate emphysema with air trapping
explanation: B-lines (also called comet-tail artifacts) are hyperechoic vertical lines arising from the pleural line and extending to the bottom of the ultrasound screen without fading. Multiple B-lines (3 or more per intercostal space) indicate interstitial syndrome, which occurs in pulmonary edema, ARDS, or interstitial pneumonia - all causing thickened interlobular septa. A-lines (option A) are horizontal reverberation artifacts from the pleural line, indicating normal aerated lung. In pneumothorax: absent lung sliding + absent B-lines + only A-lines are seen.
highYield: B-lines (comet-tail) = vertical, reach screen bottom without fading = interstitial syndrome (pulmonary edema/ARDS). 3+ per space = abnormal.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: After central venous catheter insertion, a mechanically ventilated patient develops sudden hemodynamic deterioration. POCUS of the left chest shows absent lung sliding and only A-lines. What additional finding is pathognomonic for pneumothorax on lung ultrasound?
options: Presence of B-lines bilaterally confirming bilateral pulmonary edema
Identification of the lung point (junction of sliding and non-sliding pleura) on ultrasound
D-sign on parasternal short-axis cardiac view indicating tension pneumothorax
Pericardial effusion (echo-free space around heart) indicating concurrent hemothorax
explanation: For pneumothorax on POCUS: absent lung sliding + absent B-lines + presence of A-lines strongly suggest pneumothorax, but the pathognomonic finding is the LUNG POINT - the junction between the sliding pleura (normal lung) and non-sliding pleura (pneumothorax) seen in real-time during breathing. The lung point is 100% specific for pneumothorax when identified. B-lines (option A) would indicate fluid, not pneumothorax. The D-sign (option C) is RV pressure overload from PE. Pericardial effusion (option D) indicates cardiac tamponade, not pneumothorax.
highYield: Pneumothorax POCUS: absent lung sliding + A-lines. Pathognomonic = LUNG POINT (sliding meets non-sliding pleura). 100% specific.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 30-year-old man with burns involving 40% body surface area sustained 48 hours ago requires emergency surgery. Which statement about succinylcholine use is correct in this patient?
options: Succinylcholine is safe to use in burn patients at any time after injury
Succinylcholine is contraindicated from 24 hours post-burn onward due to risk of life-threatening hyperkalemia
Succinylcholine can be used in burns patients for the first 7 days only
Succinylcholine is contraindicated only if the patient has pre-existing renal failure
explanation: Succinylcholine is CONTRAINDICATED in burns patients after 24 hours from the time of injury. Burns (as well as crush injuries, upper/lower motor neuron lesions, prolonged immobilization, and denervation) cause upregulation of extrajunctional acetylcholine receptors throughout the muscle membrane. When succinylcholine is given, massive potassium efflux from these upregulated receptors causes life-threatening hyperkalemia (potentially fatal cardiac arrest). This risk persists until healing is complete (weeks to months). Rocuronium is the preferred alternative for RSI in these patients.
highYield: Succinylcholine contraindicated from 24 hours post-burn (also: crush injury, motor neuron lesions, prolonged immobilization) due to upregulation of extrajunctional ACh receptors causing hyperkalemia.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 60-year-old man with end-stage renal failure and Child-Pugh C liver cirrhosis requires muscle relaxation for elective surgery. Which non-depolarizing neuromuscular blocking agent is most appropriate?
options: Vecuronium; minimal renal and hepatic dependence for elimination
Rocuronium; eliminated primarily by kidneys so safe in liver failure
Cisatracurium; undergoes organ-independent Hofmann elimination and ester hydrolysis
Pancuronium; longest duration provides best relaxation in high-risk patients
explanation: Cisatracurium (and atracurium) undergoes Hofmann elimination - spontaneous non-enzymatic degradation at physiological pH and temperature - plus ester hydrolysis. This metabolism is completely organ-independent, making cisatracurium the drug of choice in patients with hepatic AND/OR renal failure. Atracurium also undergoes Hofmann elimination but releases histamine (a disadvantage); cisatracurium does NOT release histamine. Vecuronium is primarily hepatically eliminated. Rocuronium is hepatically eliminated and renally excreted. Pancuronium has significant renal excretion.
highYield: Cisatracurium: Hofmann elimination (organ-independent) = drug of choice in combined hepatic + renal failure. Does NOT release histamine (unlike atracurium).

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: During general anesthesia with sevoflurane and succinylcholine, a 25-year-old man develops rapidly rising temperature, muscle rigidity, tachycardia, and increasing end-tidal CO2. What is the diagnosis, its molecular basis, and the specific antidote?
options: Neuroleptic malignant syndrome; dopamine receptor blockade; bromocriptine
Malignant hyperthermia; uncontrolled Ca2+ release from sarcoplasmic reticulum (RYR1 mutation); dantrolene
Serotonin syndrome; excess serotonergic activity; cyproheptadine
Thyroid storm; excess thyroid hormone; propylthiouracil
explanation: Malignant hyperthermia (MH) is triggered by volatile halogenated agents (halothane, sevoflurane, desflurane) and/or succinylcholine. It is caused by a mutation in the RYR1 (ryanodine receptor 1) gene causing uncontrolled calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum of skeletal muscle, leading to hypermetabolism, rigidity, hyperthermia, rhabdomyolysis, and acidosis. Specific treatment: dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV bolus (blocks RYR1 channel), repeated as needed. Neuroleptic malignant syndrome (option A) is caused by antipsychotics. Serotonin syndrome (option C) is caused by serotonergic drugs.
highYield: Malignant hyperthermia: volatile agents + succinylcholine triggers; RYR1 mutation; uncontrolled SR Ca2+ release. Rx: dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: Which induction agent uniquely causes cardiovascular stimulation (increased HR and BP), bronchodilation, and preservation of airway reflexes while producing anesthesia?
options: Propofol; causes cardiovascular stimulation and bronchodilation via GABA-A potentiation
Ketamine; causes sympathomimetic stimulation via NMDA receptor antagonism and central catecholamine release
Thiopentone; increases cardiac output and maintains airway reflexes via barbiturate mechanism
Etomidate; causes cardiovascular stimulation and is the preferred agent in asthma
explanation: Ketamine is unique among induction agents for several reasons: (1) cardiovascular stimulation - increases HR, BP, and cardiac output via central catecholamine release (sympathomimetic), making it useful in hemorrhagic shock; (2) bronchodilation - useful in asthmatic patients; (3) preserves airway reflexes (unlike other agents) - though aspiration is still possible; (4) mechanism: dissociative anesthesia via NMDA receptor antagonism. Emergence reactions (hallucinations, vivid dreams) are prevented by co-administering benzodiazepines (e.g., midazolam). Propofol (option A) causes cardiovascular DEPRESSION. Etomidate (option D) is cardiovascularly neutral, not stimulating.
highYield: Ketamine: only induction agent causing cardiovascular stimulation (NMDA antagonist + catecholamines), bronchodilation, airway reflex preservation. Emergence reactions prevented by benzodiazepines.

subject: Anaesthesia
stem: A 28-year-old woman develops a severe frontal headache that worsens on sitting up and is relieved on lying flat, one day after spinal anesthesia for cesarean section. What is the definitive treatment for this complication?
options: Oral paracetamol and bed rest for 7 days
Epidural blood patch using 15-20 mL of autologous blood
IV caffeine infusion as definitive treatment
Repeat spinal injection to equalize CSF pressure
explanation: Post-dural puncture headache (PDPH) is the classic positional headache after spinal/epidural anesthesia: worse when upright (sitting/standing), relieved in supine position. Caused by CSF leakage through the dural puncture causing intracranial hypotension and traction on meninges. Management is stepwise: conservative first (hydration, bed rest, analgesics, caffeine). DEFINITIVE treatment is epidural blood patch (EBP): 15-20 mL of the patient's own autologous blood injected into the epidural space at the level of the puncture; the blood clots and seals the dural tear. Success rate >90%. Caffeine (option C) is adjunctive, not definitive. Repeating the spinal (option D) would worsen the situation.
highYield: PDPH: positional headache post-spinal (upright = worse, supine = better). Definitive Rx = epidural blood patch (15-20 mL autologous blood).

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: A 32-year-old pregnant woman at 11 weeks gestation undergoes a blood test that detects fetal chromosomal aneuploidies by analysing fragments of fetal DNA in maternal circulation. Which of the following statements best describes this test?
options: It is a diagnostic test that confirms chromosomal abnormality
It is a screening test that requires confirmatory invasive testing if positive
It cannot detect sex chromosome aneuploidies
It requires amniocentesis to be performed simultaneously
explanation: Non-Invasive Prenatal Testing (NIPT) uses cell-free fetal DNA (cffDNA) circulating in maternal blood and is a SCREENING test, not diagnostic. A positive (high-chance) result MUST be confirmed by invasive testing such as CVS (11-14 weeks) or amniocentesis (15-20 weeks). NIPT can detect trisomy 21, 18, 13, and sex chromosome aneuploidies, so option C is wrong. It does not require simultaneous amniocentesis.
highYield: NIPT is a screening test — a positive result always requires confirmation by CVS or amniocentesis

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: From what minimum gestational age can Non-Invasive Prenatal Testing (NIPT) be reliably performed due to adequate fetal fraction of cell-free fetal DNA in maternal circulation?
explanation: NIPT can be performed from 10 weeks of gestation when the fetal fraction of cffDNA in maternal circulation is adequate for reliable analysis. Before 10 weeks, the fetal fraction is too low and may lead to test failure or inaccurate results. It should not be confused with CVS (11-14 weeks) or amniocentesis (15-20 weeks), which are diagnostic procedures.
highYield: NIPT earliest at 10 weeks gestation — fetal fraction adequate from this point

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: A 28-year-old woman at 13 weeks gestation receives a high-chance NIPT result for trisomy 21. She is counselled about the need for confirmatory testing. Which of the following procedures is most appropriate at this gestational age?
explanation: At 13 weeks gestation, Chorionic Villus Sampling (CVS) is the appropriate confirmatory invasive test. CVS is performed between 11-14 weeks. Amniocentesis is performed at 15-20 weeks and would not be done at 13 weeks. Repeat NIPT would not provide diagnostic certainty as NIPT remains a screening test. Cordocentesis is not the first-line confirmatory test for aneuploidy.
highYield: CVS at 11-14 weeks; amniocentesis at 15-20 weeks — choose based on gestational age

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: NIPT returns a high-chance result for trisomy 13 in a 35-year-old woman. Subsequent CVS reveals a normal fetal karyotype. What is the most likely explanation for this discordant result?
explanation: Confined placental mosaicism (CPM) is the most common cause of false-positive NIPT results. Because cffDNA in maternal blood is predominantly of placental (trophoblastic) origin rather than fetal origin, chromosomal abnormalities confined to the placenta can produce a discordant high-chance NIPT result with a normal fetal karyotype on CVS. This is a critical exam concept.
highYield: False positive NIPT = confined placental mosaicism (CPM) — placental DNA, not true fetal DNA

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: Which of the following chromosomal conditions is NOT routinely screened by standard Non-Invasive Prenatal Testing (NIPT)?
explanation: Standard NIPT screens for trisomy 21 (Down syndrome), trisomy 18 (Edwards syndrome), trisomy 13 (Patau syndrome), and sex chromosome aneuploidies including monosomy X (Turner). Trisomy 16 is the most common trisomy in spontaneous abortions but is NOT part of standard NIPT panels. Extended NIPT panels may include microdeletions but trisomy 16 as a live-born entity is extremely rare and not in the standard screen.
highYield: Standard NIPT screens: trisomy 21, 18, 13, and sex chromosome aneuploidies — NOT trisomy 16

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: A transvaginal ultrasound at 22 weeks in a singleton pregnancy shows a cervical length of 22 mm. The patient has no prior history of preterm birth. What is the most appropriate management?
explanation: A cervical length of less than 25 mm on transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) at 18-24 weeks is a major risk factor for spontaneous preterm birth. In a singleton pregnancy with cervical length <25 mm, vaginal progesterone (micronised, 200 mg/night) is the recommended intervention. Cervical cerclage is reserved for women with cervical length <25 mm who also have a prior history of preterm birth or cervical insufficiency. 17-OHPC is used in women with prior spontaneous preterm birth.
highYield: Cervical length <25 mm at 18-24 weeks in singleton with no prior PTB = vaginal progesterone 200 mg/night

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: A 26-year-old woman with a history of spontaneous preterm birth at 28 weeks in her previous pregnancy presents at 20 weeks of her current singleton pregnancy. Transvaginal ultrasound shows cervical length of 18 mm. What is the most appropriate management?
explanation: Cervical cerclage is indicated when cervical length is less than 25 mm in a patient with a prior history of spontaneous preterm birth or cervical insufficiency. This patient has both risk factors: a prior spontaneous preterm birth and a short cervix (18 mm). While vaginal progesterone is used for cervical shortening without prior PTB history, the combination of prior PTB plus short cervix makes cerclage the preferred intervention. 17-OHPC is used when cervical length is normal in patients with prior PTB.
highYield: Cervical length <25 mm + prior PTB history = cervical cerclage (not just progesterone alone)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: 17-alpha hydroxyprogesterone caproate (17-OHPC) is administered weekly by intramuscular injection to prevent preterm birth. Which patient profile best describes the correct indication for this drug?
options: Twin pregnancy with cervical length 20 mm
Singleton pregnancy with no prior preterm birth and cervical length 18 mm
Singleton pregnancy with prior spontaneous preterm birth and normal cervical length
Cervical insufficiency detected on ultrasound for the first time
explanation: 17-alpha hydroxyprogesterone caproate (17-OHPC) is specifically indicated for prevention of preterm birth in women with a singleton pregnancy who have a history of prior spontaneous preterm birth. It is given weekly IM starting at 16-20 weeks. It has NOT been shown effective in twin pregnancies. Vaginal progesterone, not 17-OHPC, is the intervention for short cervix without prior PTB. Cervical cerclage addresses cervical insufficiency.
highYield: 17-OHPC = singleton pregnancy + prior spontaneous preterm birth (not for twins, not first-line for short cervix alone)

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: Relugolix combination therapy (relugolix + estradiol + norethindrone acetate) is approved for treatment of heavy menstrual bleeding due to uterine fibroids. What is the mechanism of action of relugolix?
options: GnRH agonist causing initial flare followed by down-regulation
Oral non-peptide GnRH antagonist causing immediate receptor blockade
Progestogen receptor modulator reducing fibroid size
explanation: Relugolix is an oral non-peptide GnRH antagonist. It acts by immediate competitive blockade of GnRH receptors in the pituitary, rapidly suppressing LH and FSH without an initial flare. This is the key distinction from GnRH agonists (e.g., leuprolide) which cause an initial testosterone/estrogen surge (flare) before desensitisation. The combination formulation adds estradiol and norethindrone acetate to manage vasomotor symptoms and protect bone mineral density.
highYield: Relugolix = oral GnRH antagonist, immediate blockade, NO flare — approved for uterine fibroids as Myfembree

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: A 38-year-old woman with symptomatic uterine fibroids and heavy menstrual bleeding is started on an oral medication that requires no injections, immediately suppresses gonadotropins without an initial hormonal surge, and is co-administered with estradiol and norethindrone to preserve bone density. Which drug is she most likely receiving?
explanation: The described drug profile exactly matches relugolix combination therapy (Myfembree): oral administration (no injections, unlike leuprolide), immediate GnRH receptor blockade without a flare, and co-administration with estradiol + norethindrone acetate for add-back therapy. Leuprolide is injectable and causes an initial flare. Elagolix is an oral GnRH antagonist approved for endometriosis, not fibroids primarily, and does not require add-back therapy in its standard formulation. Mifepristone is a progesterone receptor antagonist.
highYield: Relugolix (Myfembree) = oral, no injection, no flare, add-back estradiol+norethindrone, for uterine fibroids

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: Elagolix (Orilissa) 200 mg twice daily is prescribed for endometriosis-associated pain. A patient asks why the higher dose is limited to only 6 months. What is the primary reason for this duration restriction?
options: Risk of hepatotoxicity with prolonged use
Risk of bone mineral density loss with prolonged hypoestrogenic state
Tachyphylaxis and loss of efficacy after 6 months
Risk of thromboembolism with long-term use
explanation: Elagolix causes hypoestrogenism by suppressing GnRH-mediated gonadotropin release, which results in reduced estrogen production. Prolonged hypoestrogenic state leads to bone mineral density (BMD) loss. The 200 mg twice-daily dose causes greater suppression and is therefore limited to 6 months to prevent significant BMD loss. The lower dose (150 mg once daily) causes partial suppression and can be used for up to 24 months. This is why duration limits differ between the two doses.
highYield: Elagolix 200 mg BD = max 6 months; 150 mg OD = max 24 months; duration limited by bone mineral density loss

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: Which of the following correctly differentiates GnRH antagonists from GnRH agonists in clinical practice?
options: GnRH antagonists cause an initial flare of LH and testosterone before suppression
GnRH agonists work by immediate competitive receptor blockade without initial surge
GnRH antagonists provide immediate gonadotropin suppression without an initial hormonal flare
Both GnRH agonists and antagonists produce identical patterns of gonadotropin suppression
explanation: GnRH antagonists (e.g., relugolix, elagolix, ganirelix, cetrorelix) work by immediate competitive blockade of GnRH receptors, suppressing LH and FSH rapidly without any initial hormonal surge (flare). In contrast, GnRH agonists (e.g., leuprolide, buserelin) initially stimulate GnRH receptors causing a flare (surge of LH, FSH, testosterone/estrogen) before receptor down-regulation leads to suppression. This flare can cause tumour flare in prostate cancer or worsening symptoms initially in endometriosis.
highYield: GnRH antagonist = immediate suppression, NO flare; GnRH agonist = initial flare then suppression

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: A gynaecologist is choosing between elagolix and relugolix for a patient. The key differentiating indication is the primary diagnosis. Elagolix is approved for which condition while relugolix is approved for which condition respectively?
explanation: Both elagolix and relugolix are oral non-peptide GnRH antagonists, but they have different primary FDA approvals. Elagolix (Orilissa) is approved for endometriosis-associated pain. Relugolix combination therapy (Myfembree) is approved for heavy menstrual bleeding due to uterine fibroids. This is a high-yield differentiating fact frequently tested in pharmacology and gynaecology exams.
highYield: Elagolix = endometriosis; Relugolix = uterine fibroids — both are oral GnRH antagonists

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: In assisted reproductive technology, which embryo transfer strategy is currently preferred in good-prognosis IVF patients to minimise multiple pregnancy risk without significantly reducing cumulative live birth rates?
options: Double embryo transfer to maximise success per cycle
Triple embryo transfer in women over 38 years
Transfer of all available embryos in the fresh cycle
explanation: Elective single embryo transfer (eSET) is the preferred strategy in good-prognosis IVF patients (young age, good-quality blastocyst available). While single cycle success may be slightly lower, cumulative live birth rates with vitrified-warmed frozen embryo transfer cycles are comparable, while multiple pregnancy rates (and associated risks) are dramatically reduced. Double or triple embryo transfer significantly increases twin/triplet rates with associated maternal and neonatal morbidity.
highYield: eSET (elective single embryo transfer) = preferred in good-prognosis IVF to reduce multiples without reducing cumulative success

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: Following oocyte retrieval in an IVF cycle, which hormonal supplementation is mandatory to support the luteal phase and ensure endometrial receptivity for embryo implantation?
explanation: Progesterone luteal phase support is mandatory in all IVF cycles after oocyte retrieval. Oocyte retrieval removes granulosa cells which normally produce progesterone, creating a luteal phase defect. Exogenous progesterone (vaginal, intramuscular, or oral micronised) is given to support endometrial receptivity and early embryo implantation. Estrogen alone is insufficient. hCG can be used but has a higher risk of OHSS. GnRH agonist for luteal support is used in specific protocols.
highYield: Progesterone luteal support is mandatory in ALL IVF cycles after oocyte retrieval

subject: Obstetrics & Gynaecology
stem: The GnRH antagonist protocol for controlled ovarian stimulation in IVF offers which advantage over the long GnRH agonist protocol?
options: Avoidance of the initial hormonal flare and flexible scheduling
Lower cost due to fewer monitoring visits
Elimination of the need for luteal phase progesterone support
explanation: The GnRH antagonist protocol (using ganirelix or cetrorelix) offers the advantage of immediate pituitary suppression without an initial hormonal flare, and allows flexible scheduling as antagonists are started mid-stimulation (usually on day 5-6 or when the leading follicle reaches 13-14 mm). In contrast, the long GnRH agonist protocol requires a prolonged down-regulation phase and causes an initial flare. Oocyte numbers are comparable. Luteal support is still mandatory in both protocols. Cost may actually be higher due to antagonist medication costs.
highYield: GnRH antagonist IVF protocol = no flare, flexible start, ganirelix/cetrorelix used mid-stimulation

subject: Pediatrics
stem: Onasemnogene abeparvovec (Zolgensma) is a gene therapy approved for spinal muscular atrophy (SMA). Which viral vector is used to deliver the functional SMN1 gene in this therapy?
explanation: Onasemnogene abeparvovec (Zolgensma) uses AAV9 (adeno-associated virus serotype 9) as the viral vector to deliver a functional copy of the SMN1 gene. AAV9 is chosen because of its ability to cross the blood-brain barrier and transduce motor neurons effectively. It is given as a single intravenous infusion. This is a one-time gene replacement therapy — it replaces the defective SMN1 gene but does not modify SMN2.
highYield: Zolgensma (onasemnogene abeparvovec) = AAV9 vector, single IV infusion, replaces SMN1 gene

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 14-month-old child with SMA Type 1 is being considered for onasemnogene abeparvovec (Zolgensma). Which pre-treatment prerequisite is mandatory before administering this gene therapy?
options: Intrathecal nusinersen loading doses must be completed first
Prednisolone cover must be given to prevent hepatotoxicity
Anti-AAV9 antibody titre must be >1:50 to confirm seroconversion
MRI spine must confirm normal cord morphology
explanation: Prednisolone (corticosteroid) cover is mandatory before and after onasemnogene abeparvovec infusion to prevent hepatotoxicity, which is a known serious adverse effect of AAV9 gene therapy. Liver function tests are monitored closely. The drug is contraindicated if anti-AAV9 antibody titre is greater than 1:50 (not indicated by high titre — high titre means the patient already has neutralising antibodies that will destroy the vector). Nusinersen pretreatment is not required.
highYield: Zolgensma pre-treatment: prednisolone mandatory (hepatotoxicity prevention); contraindicated if anti-AAV9 titre >1:50

subject: Pediatrics
stem: Risdiplam (Evrysdi) is an oral liquid approved for SMA from 2 months of age. What is its mechanism of action?
options: Delivers a functional SMN1 gene copy via AAV9 vector
Blocks pre-mRNA splicing of SMN2 exon 7 to reduce toxic protein
Small molecule that promotes SMN2 exon 7 inclusion in mRNA to increase functional SMN protein
Antisense oligonucleotide delivered intrathecally to modify SMN2 splicing
explanation: Risdiplam is a small molecule SMN2 splicing modifier. It stabilises the U1:5'ss duplex at SMN2 exon 7, promoting inclusion of exon 7 in the mRNA transcript. This increases production of full-length, functional SMN protein from the SMN2 backup gene. Unlike nusinersen (intrathecal antisense oligonucleotide), risdiplam is taken orally and distributes systemically, reaching both CNS and peripheral motor neurons. Onasemnogene abeparvovec is the AAV9 gene therapy.
